On ShapeNet point-cloud generation (N=2048, 26M model), OGPP achieves 1-NNA EMD scores of 58.77 (airplane), 58.38 (chair), 55.39 (car) at 200 steps, matching NSOT (61.85/57.63/53.55) with 5× fewer steps and achieving airplane EMD comparable to DiT-3D(XL) (675M params, 1000 steps) at 26× lower parameter count. Baselines include strong recent particle-flow methods (EqFM, NSOT, Minibatch OT) and large diffusion models (DiT-3D). Notably, some two-stage latent diffusion methods (LION, DiT-3D XL, FrePoLad) remain competitive or better on chair and car, so OGPP does not uniformly dominate across all ShapeNet categories. Normal estimation: ATV achieves median unoriented angular error of 7.6° versus 10.9° for NTV; OGPP reaches average cosine similarity 0.92 versus 0.91 (3D-only sorting), with 1-NNA accuracy 0.61 versus 0.78. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The combination of one-sided orbit-space canonicalization, identity-aware particle index embeddings, and Hermite geometric paths is genuinely new — prior flow-matching work for particles uses OT coupling (expensive, Eulerian) or ignores permutation symmetry. The energy-driven benchmark suite (blue noise, minimal surfaces, DLA, Thomson problem, each with physics-grounded intrinsic metrics) is a new contribution to generative modeling evaluation. The Lagrangian reformulation treating flow matching as particle dynamics with identity-conditioned trajectories is a conceptually novel framing. Adapting Hilbert-curve canonicalization from Transformer positional encoding to flow-matching probability path design is the key non-obvious transfer. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are thorough: the paper isolates canonicalization strategy (Table 8), identity embedding (Figure 25), Hermite degree (Table 9), canonicalization dimension (6D vs 3D), noise distribution, and terminal velocity magnitude (Figure 9). The theoretical claims (covariance reduction by canonicalization, Lipschitz ratio inflation by two-sided canonicalization) are supported by proofs in appendices and empirical verification in Section 7.4. Error is quantified across 100–1000 generated samples with multiple metrics per task. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported for individual table entries, which is a gap given the stochasticity of generative models. Code is not mentioned as released in the paper. The paper is published in ACM Transactions on Graphics (peer-reviewed, Vol. 45, No. 4, July 2026). The EqFM baseline is trained with batch size 8 (due to O(B²N³) cost) but for the same wall-clock time as other methods, which is a reasonable fairness choice but slightly disadvantages EqFM. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Models are trained on NVIDIA RTX 4090 and H200 SXM GPUs with 5M–26M parameters; the plain Transformer architecture with Hilbert sorting is straightforwardly implementable on commercially available hardware. - Efficient: Training uses batch size 200 for most tasks; the canonicalization step (Hilbert sort) is O(N log N) per sample and negligible relative to network evaluation; inference requires K ODE steps at the cost of K Transformer forward passes, with the paper showing 5–26× step reductions relative to baselines, which is a genuine efficiency gain. - Effective: For energy-driven tasks the method shows clear order-of-magnitude improvements over baselines on intrinsic physics metrics at low step counts; ShapeNet results are competitive but not universally dominant (two-stage latent methods win on chair and car categories). - Scalable: Experiments test N up to 2048 particles; the method has no asymptotically bad scaling (unlike EqFM's O(B²N³)), but behavior at N=10K–100K is untested. The paper does not evaluate on variable-N settings except for the conditional minimal-surface task (3–8 anchors, fixed N=256). The paper delivers a method and results; no software artifact or pretrained model release is announced. Strengths: - The one-sided canonicalization insight (canonicalize X1 only, not X0) is theoretically grounded with a clean proof that two-sided canonicalization inflates Lipschitz ratios, and the ablation (Table 8, Figure 28) confirms this prediction empirically — this is a rare case of theory-driven design validated by experiment. - Encoding surface normals as terminal tangents of geometric probability paths is elegant and practically impactful: it reduces a separate normal-estimation problem to a zero-cost byproduct of the same flow, with results competitive with 6D direct generation. - The energy-driven evaluation suite is a genuine contribution independent of the main method — physics-grounded metrics (spectral correlation, fractal dimension, residual forces, area deviation) are more meaningful for particle quality than distribution-matching statistics alone. - The ablation study in Section 7.3 is unusually complete, testing five design axes (canonicalization dimension, Hermite degree, initial tangent, noise shape, terminal velocity magnitude) with quantitative results, giving a reader confidence in which choices actually matter. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper notes that the Hilbert-curve canonicalization occasionally produces point pairs that are too close to each other under Z-order ordering, motivating the choice of Hilbert curves; this is a minor acknowledged edge case. The authors observe that the toroidal path improvement is marginal and can introduce close-point artifacts, so they retain linear paths for baseline comparisons. The paper does not explicitly discuss the fixed-N requirement as a limitation, which is perhaps the most significant unstated gap. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) All experiments fix particle count N at training and inference time; the method provides no mechanism for variable-N generation without retraining, which is a major practical constraint for geometry processing where mesh complexity varies. (2) The ShapeNet comparison includes two-stage latent diffusion methods that remain superior on chair and car categories (e.g., DiT-3D XL: 49.11 vs OGPP 61.93 on chair CD), so the method is not uniformly SOTA; the paper's framing of the comparison partially obscures this. (3) No code or pretrained models are released, making independent reproducibility difficult. (4) Error bars are absent from quantitative tables — given the stochasticity of flow-matching inference, reporting variance over multiple runs would strengthen confidence in the reported differences. (5) The energy-driven datasets are generated by classical solvers on small 2D or simple 3D grids; whether the method scales to physically realistic particle counts (e.g., N=10^5 SPH particles or molecular dynamics systems) is entirely untested and unaddressed. (6) The identity embedding approach requires each canonical index to correspond to a stable and consistent spatial role across the training set, which holds for sorted blue-noise or minimal-surface particles but may break for shape categories with high topological variability. Bottom Line: This is a substantive methodological contribution to particle-native generative modeling with clean theory, strong ablations, and impressive empirical results on physically grounded tasks — ML researchers working on flow matching for 3D geometry, particle systems, or graphics will find it well worth reading, while practitioners needing variable-N or very large-N generation should note those gaps remain open.
Recommended 2605.02222 PDF
#7 — Highly Innovative (78)

HierMine: Accelerating Graph Pattern Mining via Hierarchical Sampling

dl.acm.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HierMine, an approximate graph pattern mining (AGPM) system that combines hierarchical variance-adaptive sampler allocation, online recursive partitioning, CLT-based per-region convergence detection, and a NUMA-aware data layout to dramatically reduce redundant computation. - Why it's important: Exact graph pattern mining requires over ten hours for size-5 patterns on million-vertex graphs, and existing approximate systems waste most of their sampling budget on low-contribution regions while being blind to NUMA memory latency — both of which HierMine directly attacks. - How it does it: HierMine partitions the edge-list sampling space by vertex degree thresholds and dynamically reallocates threads toward high-variance regions using a greedy strategy that provably converges to Neyman optimal allocation, while the NUMA-aware layout replicates high-degree vertex adjacency lists across NUMA nodes. - The good: HierMine requires 21.2× fewer samples than ScaleGPM to reach 1% relative error on the 4-diamond pattern in the 105M-vertex Uk graph, translating to a 28.9× average end-to-end speedup over the strongest prior AGPM baseline. - The missing: The paper omits direct comparison against AGIS, a concurrently published competitor the authors cite but explicitly exclude from experiments, and all evaluations run on a single vendor-specific Chinese server (Hygon C86), leaving portability to mainstream Intel/AMD NUMA hardware undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners working on subgraph counting or high-performance graph analytics systems; the core sampling insight (adaptive Neyman allocation via online partitioning) transfers beyond AGPM.
Why this rank: Systems profile, where Impact is weighted 30%. Practical Impact (85) is high — peer-reviewed (TACO 2026), real measured 28.9× speedup on a real implementation, although code repo isn't stated public. Novelty (70) — adaptive Neyman allocation via online partitioning + CLT-based per-region termination is a principled and non-trivial integration. Rigor (75) — formal proofs of unbiasedness and CLT correctness, clean ablation isolating each layer; weakened by single-platform Hygon hardware and no multi-seed runs. Resilience (70) — AGIS comparison conspicuously omitted, distributed-cluster claim unevaluated. Completeness (75) — author limitations confronted directly. Weighted ≈ 0.15*70+0.15*75+0.10*75+0.15*75+0.15*70+0.30*85 = 76.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 85
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Graph pattern mining — counting subgraph isomorphisms for patterns like triangles, cliques, and cycles — is fundamental to social network analysis, bioinformatics, and fraud detection, but exact solvers are computationally intractable for large or complex patterns. Approximate solvers (AGPM) offer statistical guarantees at lower cost, but two structural problems hobble all existing systems. First, they allocate samplers uniformly across the entire edge list, yet the sampling space is highly non-uniform: on the Lj graph, fewer than 2% of the edge-list region (high-degree vertices) accounts for more than 80% of estimated pattern count and 90% of estimation error for the 5-house pattern, meaning the other 98% of samples contribute negligibly. Second, existing error-control methods either use offline Chernoff-based bounds whose required sample count scales exponentially with pattern size (making them impractical for 5-cliques and larger), or use ScaleGPM's coarse-grained online detection that terminates globally but cannot redirect computation adaptively. A third overlooked issue: none of these systems account for NUMA memory latency, which is a dominant cost when random walks hop across irregular graph adjacency lists. Key Idea: The core insight is that the additive decomposability of pattern counts enables unbiased estimation by partitioning the edge-list sampling space into regions and estimating each region's contribution independently. Because pattern occurrences cluster in high-degree vertex neighborhoods, the sampling variance is highly non-uniform across regions. Allocating samples proportionally to each region's weighted variance (Neyman allocation) minimizes total estimator variance for a fixed sample budget. Since the optimal allocation is unknown upfront, HierMine starts from uniform sampling, online-detects high-variance tail regions via degree-sorted recursive splitting, and dynamically shifts threads toward them — converging to near-Neyman allocation without any offline profiling. The NUMA-aware data layout supports this by ensuring that sampled high-degree vertices (the most frequently accessed during neighborhood expansion) reside in local memory. Methodology: The system has four interlocking components. (1) NUMA-aware data layout: edges are partitioned hierarchically — the top 0.0001 fraction of highest-degree vertices are replicated across all eight NUMA nodes; vertices with local degree ≥ 100 are replicated to the primary node of the other socket; remaining vertices are uniformly distributed. This targets the observation that random-walk access probability is proportional to vertex degree. (2) Hierarchical sampling: worker threads per NUMA node sample from both the replicated (global hotspot) region and their disjoint partition. The master thread aggregates statistics periodically, using region-scaled estimators (Ĉ = Σ_r (Σ_{s∈S_r^+} Π|C_i^{r,s}| / |S_r|) × |R_r|) that are proven unbiased (Lemma 4.1). (3) Online grouping-based convergence detection: rather than Chernoff bounds, HierMine applies the CLT: each region's per-sample count has bounded variance, so the regional mean converges normally; the global sum of independent regional normals is also normal. The global relative error is computed from per-region sample means and variances via Equation 1, invoking Φ^{-1}(1−δ/2). Sampling terminates when the estimated error falls below ε with confidence 1−δ (Lemma 4.2). (4) Online partition and adjustment (Algorithm 1): within each NUMA node, the disjoint sampling space is recursively split at the median degree boundary. Threads are reassigned via a greedy quarter-allocation rule (largest-variance-contribution region gets 1/4 of threads, then 1/4 of remaining, etc.), which is shown to converge to Neyman allocation at equilibrium. Three critical design decisions are well-justified: (a) starting with uniform sampling before partitioning avoids committing to an uninformed allocation when variance structure is unknown; (b) the estimator stores raw candidate-size products (not scaled by m) to remain valid after subsequent repartitioning; (c) high-degree vertex replication is capped at 0.0001 global fraction because benefits exhibit clear diminishing returns beyond that threshold. Key Results: On 8 real-world graphs (0.1M to 105.9M vertices, 2M to 6.6B edges) and six 4-node patterns (4-star, 4-path, 4-tailed-triangle, 4-cycle, 4-diamond, 4-clique) at 1% error / 99% confidence, HierMine achieves an average 28.9× speedup over ScaleGPM (the prior best AGPM system for arbitrary patterns); ASAP and Arya time out on most large-pattern / dense-graph combinations. Sample efficiency: 21.2× reduction vs. ScaleGPM for 4-diamond on Uk, 12× for 4-tailed-triangle on Sk, 9.2× for 5-house on Yo. Against exact systems (GLumin, AutoMine), HierMine consistently wins, often by two or more orders of magnitude, while achieving actual errors well below the 5% bound (actual errors on Mi and Pa are almost always < 2%). NUMA-aware layout delivers up to 2× speedup over non-NUMA-aware baseline on Fr and Uk. Thread/NUMA scaling is near-linear within a socket but degrades slightly across sockets due to cross-socket memory traffic. Preprocessing overhead: 370 seconds for Uk (105M vertices), but the preprocessed layout can be cached to disk. Memory overhead: 1.22× for Uk (an additional ~12.5 GB on a 32 GB/node system, within budget). HierMine successfully mines 7-clique patterns on Pk and Pa within hundreds of seconds, a task that would be infeasible for exact methods. Baselines are current and strong (ScaleGPM: VLDB 2024; Arya: NSDI 2023; GLumin: PPoPP 2025). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The combination of (a) online adaptive Neyman allocation via degree-sorted recursive partitioning, (b) CLT-based per-region convergence detection replacing Chernoff bounds, and (c) NUMA-aware hierarchical data layout for graph mining is genuinely novel. Each component individually adapts known ideas (Neyman allocation from 1934 statistics; CLT-based interval estimation; NUMA-aware graph systems) to the specific structure of neighborhood sampling for pattern mining, and the integration is non-trivial. The contrast with AGIS (which uniformizes sample distribution) versus HierMine (which concentrates samples in high-variance regions) clarifies that the conceptual direction is distinct from the concurrent work. Rigor & Reproducibility: Formal proofs are provided for unbiasedness of the hierarchical estimator (Lemma 4.1) and correctness of the CLT-based convergence criterion (Lemma 4.2); both proofs are complete and the assumptions (i.i.d. samples within each region, CLT applicability as |S_r| → ∞) are explicit. The ablation study (Figure 13) isolates the contribution of hierarchical sampling vs. online partitioning vs. dynamic adjustment — hierarchical sampling provides the largest single gain, enabling 4-clique convergence that fails under uniform sampling within the time limit. The estimated-vs.-actual error comparison (Figure 9) confirms that the CLT-based bound is conservative (upper-bounds actual error after stabilization). Parameter sensitivity for replication ratios is evaluated (Figure 12). No error bars or multiple-seed repetitions are reported across the main experiments, which is a gap — single runs may not reflect variance in convergence behavior across random seeds. Code and data are not stated to be publicly released. The paper is peer-reviewed (ACM Trans. Arch. Code Optim., accepted April 2026), not a preprint. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — HierMine is an implemented and deployed system evaluated on real hardware. - Practical: Runs on a dual-socket NUMA server (Hygon C86, 64 cores, 256 GB DDR4) using OpenMP with NUMA-aware pinning; any comparable dual-socket x86 server is sufficient, though NUMA topology-specific replication thresholds may need retuning. - Efficient: Preprocessing cost (up to 370 seconds and 1.22× memory overhead for Uk at 6.6B edges) is non-trivial but cacheable; at runtime, HierMine uses up to 2× more memory than the original graph for hotspot replication, well within 32 GB/node budgets tested. - Effective: Achieves the target (ε, δ) guarantee with actual errors consistently tighter than the bound, outperforming all baselines on all tested pattern/graph combinations except Arya on some simple patterns on Sk. - Scalable: Tested up to 105.9M vertices / 6.6B edges (Uk) and 7-node patterns; near-linear NUMA and thread scaling within a socket, with modest degradation across sockets; no distributed multi-node evaluation is performed despite the paper's claim that the hierarchical layout generalizes to clusters. HierMine delivers an open-source-ready system artifact (implemented, compilable), but the paper does not state a public repository URL. Strengths: - The core statistical argument — that the additive decomposability of pattern counts permits region-wise estimation, enabling Neyman-optimal allocation via online feedback — is correct, non-trivial, and well-formalized with two complete lemmas. - Replacing exponentially-loose Chernoff bounds with CLT-based convergence detection is a meaningful theoretical upgrade that avoids the exponential scaling-factor blowup that renders ASAP and Arya impractical for size-5+ patterns. - The ablation study (Figure 13) is unusually clean: it isolates three successive optimization layers and shows each contributes measurably, with hierarchical sampling as the dominant term. - The sample-efficiency analysis (Table 3) provides a mechanistic explanation of the speedup that goes beyond black-box timing comparisons. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note that neighborhood-based sampling is sensitive to highly skewed degree distributions: on the Sk dataset, ScaleGPM fails entirely and HierMine still underperforms Arya on simpler patterns (e.g., 4-diamond). They frame a hybrid neighborhood/disjoint sampling strategy as future work. They also note that preprocessing large graphs takes up to ~6 minutes (Uk) but is amortizable. These limitations are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The authors cite AGIS (Lee & Lee, VLDB 2026) — a concurrent AGPM system that accelerates ScaleGPM via weight-sampling — as a competitor but explicitly exclude it from the experimental comparison without adequate justification, which is the most significant omission. All NUMA experiments are on a single Hygon C86 platform; Intel Xeon (with UPI interconnect) and AMD EPYC (with Infinity Fabric) have different NUMA topologies and latency ratios, so the 2× NUMA benefit may not transfer uniformly. The scalability claim that the hierarchical layout extends to distributed clusters (Section 4.1) is stated but never evaluated. No multi-seed statistical variance of convergence time is reported — for a randomized algorithm this matters, since a single run's result may not be representative. The memory overhead measurement excludes working memory for sampler state; actual peak memory consumption for large graphs with high replication ratios is not fully characterized. Bottom Line: HierMine makes a genuine systems contribution — the Neyman-adaptive online partitioning plus CLT-based termination is a principled and well-validated solution to a real bottleneck in approximate graph mining — and a practitioner building or studying AGPM systems should read it, though they should be aware the AGIS comparison is absent and the hardware generalizability is untested.
Search Results
#8 — Highly Innovative (78)

HiL-Bench (Human-in-Loop Benchmark): Do Agents Know When to Ask for Help? (2 ▲ )

Tu Trinh, Mohamed Elfeki, Guangze Luo, Kelvin Luu, Nathan Hunt, Ernesto Hernandez, Nandan Marwaha, Yannis Yiming He, Charles Wang, Fernando Carabedo, Alessa Castillo, Bing Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 5 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HIL-BENCH, a 300-task benchmark (150 SWE, 150 SQL) with human-validated information blockers, the ASK-F1 metric for measuring selective escalation quality, and an RLVR training recipe that improves help-seeking judgment in a 32B model. - Why it's important: Frontier agents deployed in enterprise settings fail >90% of the time partly because they confidently fill specification gaps with wrong assumptions rather than asking clarifying questions, and no existing benchmark penalizes this behavior. - How it does it: Tasks derived from SWE-Bench Pro and BIRD are modified to embed 3–5 independent, human-validated blockers that surface only through progressive execution-time exploration, not upfront reading; an ask_human() tool backed by a frozen Llama-3.3-70B judge returns resolutions only when questions specifically target a registered blocker. - The good: The cross-domain transfer result — a Qwen3 32B model trained exclusively on SQL tasks gains +17pp Ask-F1 on held-out SWE tasks — is the strongest evidence that help-seeking is a general, trainable skill rather than a domain heuristic. - The missing: The benchmark covers only SWE and SQL; no evaluation on other high-stakes agentic domains (web navigation, GUI tasks, scientific reasoning) is provided, and the training set of 120 tasks per domain is small enough that the RL gains could reflect task-set overfitting rather than generalization. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on agent evaluation, RLHF/RLVR for agentic systems, or enterprise agent deployment — HIL-BENCH identifies a concrete, measurable failure mode that current benchmarks structurally ignore.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (80) is the strongest dimension — 5–6 audit rounds, judge validated at 97%/91% precision/recall, necessity/sufficiency gates, κ=0.928 taxonomy agreement, public+private split, dataset released. Novelty (80) high — progressive-discovery design and bidirectional ASK-F1 metric are genuine contributions, plus a non-obvious empirical finding that judgment dissociates from capability. Non-Obviousness (80) — the model-archetype failure signatures and SQL→SWE cross-domain transfer (+17pp) are surprising. Completeness (75) and Resilience (70) solid but tempered by 120-task training set, no error bars on RL, only 2 domains, and unexplained GPT-5.5 leaderboard outlier. Impact (80) — released benchmark + harness directly usable. Composite ≈ 0.20*80+0.25*80+0.15*80+0.15*75+0.15*70+0.10*80 = 77.75.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Coding and SQL agents deployed in production are increasingly given incomplete or ambiguous specifications. The core failure mode is not inability to execute — frontier models reach 75–89% pass@3 on fully specified versions of the same tasks — but an unwillingness to escalate: agents assume away missing requirements, produce plausible but wrong outputs, and never signal uncertainty. Enterprise agent pilots report >90% failure rates, and practitioners (Karpathy, Ng) attribute a significant share to this silent-wrongness pattern. Existing benchmarks (SWE-Bench, BIRD, HumanEval) supply complete specifications and reward confident execution, creating a feedback loop where high benchmark scores encourage deployment of agents that fail precisely when specifications are incomplete — the normal condition in production. Key Idea: The central insight is that help-seeking judgment must be measured as a bidirectional precision-recall problem (over-asking is as costly as under-asking), and that blockers must surface through execution rather than upfront inspection. Hiding information that only becomes visible during environment exploration prevents the degenerate strategy of front-loading all questions before starting; the ASK-F1 harmonic mean architecturally prevents gaming through question spam because high recall via many questions collapses precision and kills the score. Methodology: HIL-BENCH construction proceeds in four phases: (1) task selection from SWE-Bench Pro and BIRD, filtering to tasks where top models achieve ~85% pass@3 with full information; (2) blocker injection by domain-expert annotators who remove or obscure 3–5 pieces of critical information per task, classified as missing (42%), ambiguous (36%), or contradictory (22%); (3) quality validation against seven strict criteria (realism, criticality, objectivity, vast search space, independence, no contamination, non-contrived) through 5–6 rounds of human review plus automated checks; (4) dataset assembly with necessity (baseline pass rate ≤5%) and sufficiency (full-info pass rate ≥90% for at least one model) gates. The ask_human() tool uses Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct as a frozen semantic judge, achieving 97% precision and 91% recall against human-annotated question-blocker pairs. ASK-F1 = harmonic mean of question precision (fraction of asked questions that target a registered blocker) and blocker recall (fraction of registered blockers addressed). The RLVR training uses a shaped reward: +0.3 per relevant ask_human() invocation, −0.1 per irrelevant/duplicate invocation, plus a terminal reward of |B_discovered|/|B| gated on finding at least one blocker. Qwen3 32B is fine-tuned with LoRA via the SkyRL framework on 120 tasks per domain, evaluated on 30 held-out tasks per domain. Failure taxonomy classifies 3,600+ traces across three capability dimensions (tool use, alignment, logic) and four failure modes each (completion, accuracy, strategy, self-assessment) using an LLM judge (Sonnet-4) achieving κ=0.928 inter-run self-agreement. Key Results: Under full information, frontier models reach 86–91% pass@3 on SQL and 67–85% on SWE. With ask_human() available, performance collapses to 5–39% on SQL and 2–9% on SWE — gaps of 51–81pp. ASK-F1 averages 40.5% on SQL and 37.4% on SWE; no model reaches the well-judged quadrant (high precision AND high recall). Model failure signatures are stable across domains: GPT-5.4/5.3-Codex are accuracy-dominant (73–93% of tool-use failures involve calling the right tool with wrong parameters), rarely detecting blockers at all; Claude Opus 4.6 has high self-assessment rate (45% of alignment failures involve the agent explicitly verbalizing infeasibility yet submitting anyway) and is completion-heavy in tool use (82.5%), exploring extensively without executing; Gemini 3.1 Pro is domain-sensitive, achieving the highest SQL blocker recall (59.2%) at low precision (35.6%) and showing dramatic inversion with ask_human() on SQL (tool-use completion −38pp, accuracy +44pp). RLVR on Qwen3 32B: in-domain SQL training yields +28pp Ask-F1 (18%→46%) and +13pp pass@3 (11%→24%); cross-domain transfer to SWE yields +17pp Ask-F1 (4%→21%) and +6pp pass@3 (1%→7%). This preprint is from Scale.AI and has not been peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK + NEW METRIC + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The progressive-discovery design (blockers surface through execution, not upfront reading) and the bidirectional ASK-F1 metric that penalizes over-asking are genuinely new contributions. The failure taxonomy methodology and the RLVR training approach are standard adaptations of existing techniques (RLVR, failure trace classification) applied to a new problem framing. The core empirical finding — that judgment and capability are dissociable, and that no frontier model achieves calibrated help-seeking — is substantive and not previously documented at this scale. Rigor & Reproducibility: The benchmark has solid construction rigor: 5–6 rounds of independent human audits, automated structural invariant checks, and a judge validated at 97%/91% precision/recall against human labels. The necessity and sufficiency gates are well-designed. Ablations support the progressive-discovery claim (blocker recall drops from 61% to 11% without environment access). The failure taxonomy uses an LLM judge with κ=0.928 self-agreement and iterative human calibration, which is reasonable but not independently verified. The RL training uses only 120 tasks per domain — a small training set that limits confidence in generalization claims; no error bars or multiple seeds are reported for the RL results. Dataset and harness are linked from the paper. The benchmark includes a 100-task private held-out set to prevent gaming. The use of a frozen open-source judge (Llama-3.3-70B) for evaluation guarantees reproducibility. The full leaderboard (Appendix I) lists additional models not discussed in the main text, suggesting ongoing evaluation. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile — the paper runs experiments on real agents over real tasks. - Practical: The evaluation harness runs on commercially available infrastructure; RLVR fine-tuning of a 32B model with LoRA is feasible on standard multi-GPU setups. - Efficient: Claude uses 2–5× more tokens than other models (up to ~2.4M tokens per SWE task), suggesting token cost is a real concern for deployment; the benchmark evaluation cost is non-trivial at 300 tasks × multiple frontier model API calls. - Effective: The evidence that improved Ask-F1 (process metric) correlates with improved pass@3 (outcome metric) validates that the metric measures something real, not a proxy; the cross-domain transfer result is the strongest effectiveness signal. - Scalable: The benchmark is fixed at 300 tasks in two domains; the RL training uses 120 tasks — both are small scales. Whether the judgment skill learned at 32B transfers to larger models or broader task distributions is not tested. The paper delivers a benchmark dataset (200 public tasks, 100 private), an evaluation harness, and a trained model checkpoint is implied but not explicitly released. Strengths: - The progressive-discovery design is a genuine methodological advance over prior clarification benchmarks that present all ambiguity upfront; the ablation showing 61%→11% recall drop without environment access quantitatively validates this design choice. - ASK-F1's precision-recall decomposition produces a diagnostic that reveals structurally distinct model failure signatures, not just aggregate scores — the GPT/Claude/Gemini archetypes are empirically grounded and non-obvious. - The cross-domain RL transfer result (SQL→SWE +17pp Ask-F1) is the strongest claim in the paper and provides concrete evidence that selective escalation is a general skill, not a domain heuristic. - The seven-criteria blocker quality framework, with explicit rejection criteria and multiple independent audits, is unusually rigorous for a benchmark construction paper. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: Not explicitly stated in a limitations section. The paper notes that the benchmark is limited to SWE and SQL domains, and that progressive discovery means agents must work before gaps surface. The conclusion acknowledges that tribal/organizational knowledge will always require human-in-the-loop, framing this as a permanent constraint rather than a solvable problem. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The RL training results use 120 tasks per domain — a small set that risks task-set overfitting, and the gains (especially on SWE pass@3: 1%→7%) are modest enough that confidence intervals would materially affect interpretation; none are reported. The leaderboard in Appendix I shows GPT-5.5 achieving 29% combined pass@3 and 53% Ask-F1 — substantially better than the frontier models featured in the main text — without any discussion of what GPT-5.5 does differently, which is a notable omission. The benchmark covers only two domains and only coding/SQL agent tasks; whether the judgment gap generalizes to web navigation, tool-use agents, or scientific workflows is asserted but not demonstrated. The ask_human() judge's 9% false-negative rate means ~9% of valid blocker questions are rejected as irrelevant, systematically deflating recall scores — the magnitude of this effect on rankings is not analyzed. The training data for blockers comes from the same distribution as the evaluation (SWE-Bench Pro and BIRD), which may inflate sufficiency rates compared to genuinely novel production tasks. Bottom Line: HIL-BENCH fills a real and important gap in agent evaluation infrastructure, and any team building or benchmarking coding/SQL agents for production deployment should use it — the ASK-F1 metric and progressive-discovery design are both worth adopting, and the failure taxonomy gives immediate diagnostic value.
Recommended 2604.09408 PDF
#9 — Highly Innovative (78)

StraTA: Incentivizing Agentic Reinforcement Learning with Strategic Trajectory Abstraction (10 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces StraTA, a hierarchical GRPO-style RL training framework that generates a compact natural-language strategy at episode start and conditions all subsequent LLM agent actions on it, jointly training strategy generation and action execution through hierarchical group comparisons, diverse farthest-point sampling, and self-judgment step penalties. - Why it's important: Purely reactive agentic RL forces the policy to simultaneously plan and act at every step, producing short-sighted exploration, unnecessary backtracking, and poor credit assignment under sparse terminal rewards — bottlenecks that prevent small open-source models from matching frontier closed-source agents on long-horizon tasks. - How it does it: For each task, N=4 diverse strategies are selected via farthest-point sampling in MiniLM embedding space from σ×N=32 oversampled candidates, M=8 rollouts are executed per strategy forming hierarchical GRPO groups, strategy rewards derive from the top-50% of rollout outcomes, and a self-judgment pass by the same LLM assigns step-level penalties to actions that neither follow the strategy nor advance task progress. - The good: At 7B scale on WebShop, StraTA achieves 84.2% success rate versus GiGPO's 72.8% (+11.4 pp) and versus Claude-4-Sonnet's 18.4%, with results averaged over 3 seeds; on SciWorld, StraTA scores 63.5% overall, surpassing all closed-source baselines and achieving a perfect 100.0% on the Lifespan subset. - The missing: The strategy is fixed at episode start and never revised mid-episode, so no experiment tests environments with substantial state changes mid-trajectory; no cross-task or out-of-distribution generalization experiments are reported. - Read it or not: Researchers building RL-trained LLM agents for text-based long-horizon interactive tasks should read this; those working on single-turn reasoning, RLHF alignment, or multimodal computer-use agents will find limited direct applicability.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Strong Rigor (82) — 3 seeds with std reported throughout, hyperparameter sensitivity studies on σ/δ/κ, ablations isolating both core components, code+models released. Novelty (72) is a compositional novelty rather than algorithmic invention but the hierarchical GRPO design is well-motivated. Non-Obviousness (75) — the strategy-decoupling framing and farthest-point semantic sampling produce concrete mechanistic gains. Completeness (78) and Resilience (75) — strong multi-seed evidence; self-judgment circularity flagged but not analyzed. Impact (80) — released code, models, and a usable training framework on rLLM. The 1.5B-beats-frontier results are striking. Composite ~78.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 82 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 78 Resilience 75 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM agents deployed on long-horizon interactive tasks — embodied household navigation (ALFWorld), web shopping (WebShop), scientific experimentation (SciWorld) — must maintain coherent behavior over many steps while recovering from earlier mistakes, under sparse rewards delivered only at episode termination. Current agentic RL methods are purely reactive: at each step the policy maps the current state directly to an action, conflating the simultaneous demands of global planning and local execution. This coupling produces short-sighted action choices that optimize locally without a coherent course of action, unnecessary backtracking when the agent revisits states, and internally inconsistent decisions across an episode. The credit assignment problem is further compounded because terminal rewards provide no signal about which intermediate actions contributed to success. Prompting-based planning approaches (e.g., ReAct) add reasoning traces but do not optimize the trajectory-level plan through learning. Key Idea: The core insight is to separate strategy generation from action execution via a fixed natural-language "global strategy" sampled once from the initial state and prepended to every subsequent step prompt. This decomposes the otherwise monolithic reactive policy into two learnable sub-objectives — produce a good plan, then follow it — and enables two distinct comparison signals within a GRPO framework: across different strategies for the same task (which plans are better?), and across rollouts under the same strategy (which executions are better?). The hierarchical group structure is the mechanism that makes joint training of both levels tractable without a separate critic. Methodology: StraTA is built on GRPO-style group advantage estimation and operates in four stages. (1) Strategy-guided execution: given initial state s₁, the LLM generates a compact strategy z via a structured prompt, then each step action aₜ is sampled conditioned on both z and local state sₜ. (2) Hierarchical group construction: N=4 strategies per task, M=8 rollouts per strategy yield N×M=32 trajectories. One strategy-level GRPO group compares N strategies; N action-level groups each compare M rollouts under one strategy. Strategy reward is defined as the mean of the top-δ=50% rollout outcomes under that strategy, reducing noise from early-training execution errors. Terminal rewards are broadcast to all steps within an episode. Both levels receive soft length penalties and hard format penalties, with total reward clipped to [-1,1]. The training objective (Eq. 14) sums GRPO clipped-surrogate losses across both levels with a shared KL penalty to a reference policy. (3) Diverse strategy rollout via farthest-point sampling: σ×N=32 candidates are oversampled, encoded by MiniLM-L6, and N maximally-distant strategies are greedily selected by cosine dissimilarity. Runtime is O(σN²d), taking a few milliseconds per task. (4) Critical self-judgment: after each rollout, the same LLM reviews the trajectory and flags step indices that neither follow the strategy nor advance task progress; flagged steps receive an additional reward of -κ=-0.1. Training used AdamW (lr=1e-6), 150 steps, batch size 16, on 8×H100 GPUs with Qwen2.5-1.5B and 7B-Instruct backbones. The non-cumulative setting (each step from individual state-based prompts rather than full conversation history) is adopted from GiGPO. Critical design choices: (a) top-δ strategy reward rather than average — well-justified to isolate strategy quality from execution noise; (b) self-judgment by the same model being trained rather than a separate judge — not evaluated for reliability; (c) fixing the strategy for the full episode — a strong simplification that limits adaptability. Key Results: On ALFWorld: StraTA-7B achieves 93.1% overall success (vs. GiGPO-7B 90.8%, PPO-7B 87.4%, Claude-4-Sonnet 72.9%); StraTA-1.5B achieves 90.7% (vs. GiGPO-1.5B 86.7%, a +4.0 pp gain). On WebShop: StraTA-7B reaches 84.2% success and 91.2 task score (vs. GiGPO-7B 72.8%/84.4, a +11.4 pp success gain); StraTA-1.5B reaches 82.5% (vs. GiGPO-1.5B 65.0%, a +17.5 pp gain). On SciWorld: StraTA-7B scores 63.5% overall (vs. Claude-4-Sonnet 57.4%, ScalingInter-7B 57.0%, GRPO-7B 41.8%), with 100.0% on Lifespan. Ablations on Qwen2.5-3B show the Vanilla base (hierarchical rollout without diversity or judgment) already reaches 79.0%/64.0% on ALFWorld/WebShop; adding Diverse raises ALFWorld to 87.9%; adding Judgment raises WebShop to 66.7%; the full StraTA reaches 88.6%/73.4%, confirming largely orthogonal contributions. Oversampling ratio σ=8 consistently outperforms σ=1 (which degrades to a no-diversity baseline). Counterintuitively, the closed-source frontier models (GPT-5.1 at 55.7% ALFWorld, Gemini-2.5-Flash at 38.6%) are substantially outperformed by RL-trained 1.5B open-source models on these benchmarks. Wall-clock per training step: PPO ≈1750s, GRPO ≈419s, StraTA ≈378s — StraTA is marginally faster than GRPO and 4.6× faster than PPO. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The core novelty is the integration of hierarchical GRPO rollout (strategy-level and action-level group advantages computed jointly), farthest-point semantic diversity sampling for strategy exploration, and self-judgment step penalties — applied together to agentic RL. Each individual component draws on existing techniques: GRPO (Shao et al., 2024), LLM-as-judge, hierarchical RL, diversity sampling. The novelty is the compositional design that cleanly addresses both exploration and credit assignment for the long-horizon agentic setting, not any single algorithmic invention. Rigor & Reproducibility: Results are averaged over 3 random seeds with standard deviations reported in all tables — a meaningful rigor signal for an RL paper. Ablations systematically isolate the two core components on a held-out 3B model and show additive gains. Hyperparameter sensitivity studies cover σ ∈ {1,2,4,8}, δ ∈ {0.1,0.5,1.0}, and κ ∈ {0.01,0.1,1.0} with quantitative outcomes, not just best-configuration reporting. Code and trained models are publicly released (GitHub: xxyQwQ/StraTA; HuggingFace collection linked on the title page). Evaluation uses the AgentGym unified interface, matching GiGPO's protocol. One methodological concern: the self-judgment reward is generated by the model undergoing training, creating a self-referential feedback loop — the paper provides no analysis of whether judgment quality improves over training or whether early noisy judgments harm learning. A second concern: the non-cumulative per-step prompt design (adopted from GiGPO) means each action is generated without explicit memory of prior actions, which may inflate performance on benchmarks but diverge from realistic deployment. This is a preprint; peer-reviewed status is not stated. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile): - Practical: Requires 8×H100 GPUs for training; inference runs on the Qwen2.5 series which is commercially available, so deployment after fine-tuning is feasible on standard GPU hardware. - Efficient: Per-step training time ≈378s (≈StraTA) vs. 419s (GRPO) and 1750s (PPO); the diversity and self-judgment components account for only ~12s per step — negligible overhead despite running 32 total rollouts per task. - Effective: Consistently beats all prompting and RL baselines across three diverse benchmarks with statistically meaningful margins; the +17.5 pp over GiGPO on WebShop at 1.5B scale is particularly credible given multi-seed reporting. - Scalable: Tested only at 1.5B and 7B parameter scales, and on episodes of ≤50 steps. No experiments test longer horizons, larger models (70B+), or environments requiring visual perception; scalability beyond these settings is claimed but not measured. The paper delivers a training framework (built on rLLM) with released code and trained models — a concrete software artifact. Strengths: - The hierarchical rollout design that generates separate GRPO groups at strategy and action levels is a clean architectural choice that addresses exploration and credit assignment simultaneously without adding a separate critic or reward model. - The farthest-point semantic sampling is a principled method for enforcing strategy diversity that demonstrably prevents strategy collapse; the ablation clearly isolates its contribution (+8.9 pp on ALFWorld for 3B). - The empirical results are the strongest reported on all three benchmarks (at the evaluated model scales), with a particularly striking result on WebShop where a 1.5B RL-trained model surpasses closed-source frontier models by >60 pp success. - Hyperparameter sensitivity analysis and multi-seed ablations go beyond minimum-viable experimental validation and provide enough information to implement and tune the method. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note two limitations in the conclusion: (1) effectiveness depends on the quality of the initial generated strategy — a poor strategy degrades all subsequent actions; (2) a fixed strategy may become restrictive when the environment changes substantially mid-episode. They propose adaptive strategy revision as future work. These are stated directly without minimization, which is a credibility positive; however, neither limitation is tested empirically (e.g., no analysis of failure modes attributable to poor strategies). Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The self-judgment step penalty uses the same model being trained as the judge, creating a potentially degenerate feedback loop: if early-training strategies are poor, the same model's judgment of strategy adherence is also unreliable. No diagnostic of judgment quality or its correlation with task success is provided, making it unclear whether the improvement from the Judgment component reflects accurate credit assignment or some other regularization effect. - All three benchmarks are text-only with short horizons (≤50 steps) and fully deterministic environments. There is no evaluation on multimodal computer-use tasks (OSWorld, WebArena) where visual grounding and longer trajectories are required; the claim of general applicability to "long-horizon agentic tasks" is extrapolation beyond evidence. - No cross-task or cross-environment transfer experiments are run; StraTA is trained and evaluated within the same benchmark distribution, leaving the question of whether strategy-generation generalizes across task families unanswered. - The non-cumulative per-step prompt design (each step is generated from a fresh prompt encoding current state, not from the full interaction history) simplifies training but differs from real conversational agents; its impact on the results relative to a full-history baseline is not analyzed. - The case studies (Appendix C) show only successful episodes, providing no qualitative analysis of failure modes or of what distinguishes good from bad strategies. Bottom Line: StraTA delivers a well-executed, computationally efficient upgrade to agentic GRPO training with credible multi-seed results and thorough ablations; practitioners training open-source LLM agents on text-based interactive benchmarks will find it a meaningfully better starting point than GiGPO, though claims of broad generality await evaluation on multimodal and longer-horizon environments.
Recommended 2605.06642 PDF
#10 — Highly Innovative (78)

End-to-End Autoregressive Image Generation with 1D Semantic Tokenizer (4 ▲ )

Wenda Chu, Bingliang Zhang, Jiaqi Han, Yizhuo Li, Linjie Yang, Yisong Yue, Qiushan Guo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 4 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces EOSTok, an end-to-end single-stage training framework that jointly optimizes a 1D discrete visual tokenizer and an autoregressive generative model, achieving a gFID of 1.48 without guidance on ImageNet-1K 256×256. - Why it's important: The standard two-stage pipeline (tokenizer trained first for reconstruction, then frozen while an AR model is trained) prevents the tokenizer from learning representations tuned for generative modeling, creating a quality ceiling that end-to-end feedback can break. - How it does it: The key mechanism is an Autoregressive Prediction Reconstruction (APR) loss that decodes the AR model's teacher-forcing predictions back to pixel space and computes a reconstruction loss, providing pixel-level generative feedback to the tokenizer while preventing the codebook collapse that naive NTP-only supervision causes; combined with an implicit VFM alignment that regularizes encoder hidden patch embeddings (not latent tokens) against DINOv2 features. - The good: EOSTok-H (644M AR + 388M tokenizer) achieves gFID 1.48 without guidance, surpassing all AR and masked AR baselines and matching or beating guidance-augmented diffusion models, with a 10× compute advantage at inference versus DiT-XL/2. - The missing: The entire evaluation is on ImageNet-1K class-conditional generation at 256×256 — there is no text-to-image, no out-of-distribution data, and no evaluation beyond FID/IS, so generalization of the end-to-end framework to open-domain generation is undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers working on discrete visual tokenizers or AR image generation; the APR loss insight and the codebook collapse diagnosis are immediately useful for anyone designing joint tokenizer-generator pipelines.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (72) — APR loss and implicit-vs-direct VFM alignment are genuine method contributions, but components (TiTok, IBQ, LlamaGen, REPA-style alignment) are largely prior art. Rigor (80) — extensive ablations (APR contribution, ordering test, sequence/codebook sweeps, VFM choice) and standard FID code, but no seeds/error bars, no released code/weights, preprint status. Non-Obviousness (80) — codebook collapse diagnosis and the implicit alignment finding (direct alignment hurts 1D tokenizers) are genuinely surprising and well-evidenced. Completeness (75) and Resilience (75) — clean ablations and an internal ordering experiment validate mechanism, but limitations section absent and AutoGuidance comparison is non-standard. Impact (80) — leading gFID 1.48 w/o guidance and 10–100× inference advantage over DiT, though no weights released and ImageNet-only. Composite: 0.20*72 + 0.25*80 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*80 = 76.85 ≈ 77–78.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 75 Resilience 75 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Autoregressive image generation requires visual tokens whose sequential structure supports causal (left-to-right) prediction. Standard 2D grid tokenizers impose a spatial prior that creates bidirectional dependencies among tokens, fundamentally misaligned with raster-order AR modeling. Recent 1D tokenizers (e.g., TiTok) address this alignment but are conventionally trained in two separate stages: first the tokenizer is optimized for reconstruction, then frozen while the AR model trains on the resulting discrete tokens. The frozen tokenizer has no opportunity to adapt its latent space to the generative task, creating a mismatch between what the tokenizer represents and what the AR model needs. Additionally, naively back-propagating the next-token prediction (NTP) loss into the tokenizer causes catastrophic codebook collapse, where the tokenizer learns to use fewer than 2% of its codes to hack the NTP objective while reconstruction quality degrades sharply. Key Idea: The core insight is that the NTP loss is a poor proxy for pixel-space generation quality because it operates on a discrete latent space that shifts throughout training. The fix is the APR loss: during training, the AR model's teacher-forcing predictions (sequences of predicted token probabilities) are decoded directly to pixels using the shared decoder, and the resulting images are compared to ground-truth images with L2 + LPIPS loss. This creates a differentiable path from generation quality in pixel space back through the decoder and codebook to the encoder, giving the tokenizer direct feedback about whether its representations actually support good image generation — not just whether they are easy to predict in discrete code space. Methodology: EOSTok adopts a 1D ViT tokenizer architecture following TiTok: image patches are flattened and concatenated with L learnable query tokens, passed through a causal ViT encoder (bidirectional attention over patches, causal attention over queries), and quantized with IBQ using a K-dimensional codebook on the unit sphere. The decoder mirrors the encoder, concatenating 1D latent codes with 2D mask tokens and outputting reconstructed images. The AR model is based on LlamaGen with AdaLN modulation and learnable positional embeddings (replacing 2D RoPE since input is 1D). The full training objective combines four losses: (1) VQ-VAE reconstruction (L2 + LPIPS + GAN), (2) NTP on discrete tokens (with gradient propagated via soft IBQ embeddings weighted by the softmax probability distribution, bypassing the non-differentiable argmax), (3) APR loss decoding AR teacher-forcing predictions to pixel space, and (4) implicit alignment aligning encoder hidden patch embeddings h_Enc to DINOv2-ViT-L features via a cosine-similarity loss through a learnable MLP projector. A symmetric decoder alignment also aligns decoder hidden states to DINOv2 features. Three critical design decisions: (a) the IBQ straight-through estimation is modified so that the embedding layer computes h = Ind^T Embed (using soft probabilities) rather than a hard lookup, enabling NTP gradients to reach the encoder — well-justified as necessary for the whole framework; (b) implicit rather than direct alignment of latent codes to VFM features avoids leaking 2D spatial structure into the 1D latent space — ablated and clearly supported; (c) the APR loss uses both L2 and LPIPS on teacher-forced pixel reconstructions — ablated across weights, with λ=1.0 optimal. Training runs for 400 epochs (~2M iterations) on 8 H100 GPUs with batch size 256 and BF16 precision. Key Results: On ImageNet-1K 256×256 class-conditional generation without guidance: EOSTok-L (312M AR + 165M tokenizer) achieves gFID 1.74, IS 210.2; EOSTok-H (644M AR + 388M tokenizer) achieves gFID 1.48, IS 239.5. With guidance, EOSTok-L achieves gFID 1.35. EOSTok-H outperforms all prior AR/masked-AR models, including AliTok-L (gFID 1.98 w/o guidance), RAR-L (5.39 w/o), and VFMTok (2.11 w/o). It also beats diffusion models REPA-XL/2 (gFID 5.90 w/o, 1.42 w/ guidance) and Lightning-DiT-XL (gFID 2.17 w/o, 1.35 w/). Reconstruction quality: EOSTok-L achieves rFID 0.73 with a 165M tokenizer, substantially better than TiTok-L-32 (rFID 2.21 with 641M params). On ImageNet 512×256 (appendix), EOSTok-L achieves gFID 1.98 w/o guidance, outperforming all compared methods. Ablation table 1 quantifies the APR loss contribution: Vanilla E2E raises AR accuracy from 10.8% to 30.2% but causes codebook collapse (code usage drops from 99.8% to 51.8%) and gFID degrades from 3.82 to 8.01; adding APR loss restores code usage to 99.7% and improves gFID to 3.32. Sampling efficiency: EOSTok-H generates ~10.5 images/second on a single H100, estimated 20–100× faster than DiT-XL/2 (which requires 250 diffusion steps). The end-to-end training adds only 15–19% overhead in training FLOPs compared to two-stage training. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The APR loss for bridging NTP and pixel-space generation quality is a genuine contribution — the specific failure mode (codebook collapse under NTP feedback) is diagnosed clearly and the fix is directly motivated. The implicit VFM alignment strategy (aligning encoder hidden patch embeddings rather than latent tokens) is a meaningful distinction from VA-VAE / REPA and is ablated to show that direct alignment actually harms 1D tokenizers by re-introducing 2D spatial priors. However, the 1D tokenizer architecture is largely taken from TiTok, IBQ is a prior method, the LlamaGen AR backbone is prior work, and the DINOv2 alignment inspiration comes directly from REPA — the contribution is in how these components are combined and trained, not in the components themselves. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and appropriate: APR loss contribution (Table 1), three VFM injection strategies vs. baseline (Table 2), sequence length sweep (Table 5), codebook size sweep (Table 6), APR weight sweep (Table 10), nested dropout rate (Table 11), VFM choice: DINOv2 vs. SigLIP2 (Table 12). The ordering experiment (Table 4 — reversing or randomizing token order degrades gFID substantially) is a creative test that directly validates the end-to-end framework's effect on latent structure. Error bars and multiple seeds are not reported. No code or model weights release is mentioned in the paper. This is an arXiv preprint (v2 dated May 4, 2026) and has not undergone peer review. Evaluation uses the standard Dhariwal & Nichol FID code, a positive for reproducibility. The AutoGuidance approach (using a separately trained lightweight AR model as the unconditional baseline) is a methodology detail that complicates direct FID comparison with CFG-based methods at the same guidance budget. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (new training method with a system built and evaluated at multiple scales). - Practical: Training requires 8 H100 GPUs for 400 epochs, which is within reach of academic ML groups; inference requires a single GPU and generates 10.5 images/second on an H100. - Efficient: Training overhead versus two-stage is 15–19% in FLOPs; inference is 20–100× cheaper than DiT-XL/2 diffusion sampling because AR decoding with KV cache replaces iterative diffusion steps. - Effective: The evidence shows gFID improvement over prior AR methods on ImageNet class-conditional 256×256 and 512×256 generation, with ablations confirming the contribution of each loss component; however, evaluation is restricted to FID/IS on a single benchmark. - Scalable: The paper tests four model sizes (93M–644M AR parameters) and observes consistent FID improvement with scale; the claim of scalability is supported by Figure 5a across the tested range, but no experiments beyond 644M AR / 388M tokenizer (approximately 1B total) are run, and text-to-image or larger-scale behavior is untested. The work delivers a training framework rather than a released software artifact; no code or weights are publicly available as stated in the paper. Strengths: - The diagnosis of the codebook collapse failure mode under NTP-only joint training is precise and well-evidenced (PCA visualization + token frequency distribution + quantitative FID degradation), making the APR loss motivation compelling rather than ad hoc. - The implicit vs. direct alignment distinction is non-obvious and empirically validated: direct alignment of 1D latent tokens to 2D DINOv2 features actually degrades generation quality (gFID 5.98 vs. 3.32 for implicit), directly falsifying the naive application of REPA to 1D tokenizers. - The ordering experiment (Table 4) provides a clean internal test that the end-to-end framework induces a directional structure in the latent space, validating the mechanism rather than just the outcome. - Competitive results at the largest scale (gFID 1.48 w/o guidance) with an inference throughput advantage over diffusion models make the practical case for AR-based generation concrete. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors do not include a formal limitations section. The conclusion notes that the NTP loss cannot determine generation quality, which motivates APR, but does not acknowledge where the framework may fail. Implicitly, the reconstruction-generation tradeoff as a function of sequence length and codebook size is presented as an open design choice (Tables 5–6), not framed as a limitation. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The entire evaluation is on ImageNet-1K class-conditional generation; there is no evidence that the end-to-end framework generalizes to text-to-image or uncurated image datasets, which are the operationally important settings. The FID metric is known to be gameable and sensitive to implementation details; no perceptual user study or diversity metric (e.g., recall) is reported. The AutoGuidance scheme (using a separately trained lightweight AR model as unconditional sampler) is non-standard and makes the guided gFID numbers not directly comparable to CFG-based competitors using the same guidance budget. The paper does not compare reconstruction quality on out-of-distribution images (e.g., COCO), so whether the 1D tokenizer generalizes beyond ImageNet is unknown. Code and model weights are not released, making independent reproduction difficult. The 15–19% training FLOP overhead is stated but the wall-clock overhead from running two decoders in parallel (one for reconstruction, one for APR) is not explicitly reported in the main training cost table, which lists only FLOPs not time. Bottom Line: EOSTok delivers a concrete, well-motivated fix (APR loss) to the codebook collapse failure of naive end-to-end AR+tokenizer training, and the resulting system achieves leading FID on ImageNet class-conditional generation — practitioners building discrete AR image generation pipelines should read this work, keeping in mind that results are on a single benchmark from a preprint with no released code.
Recommended 2605.00503 PDF
#11 — Highly Innovative (78)

D3-Gym: Constructing Real-World Verifiable Environments for Data-Driven Discovery

Hanane Nour Moussa, Yifei Li, Zhuoyang Li, Yankai Yang, Cheng Tang, Tianshu Zhang, Nesreen K. Ahmed, Ali Payani, Ziru Chen, Huan Sun · arxiv.org · Nesreen K. Ahmed - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces D3-Gym, an automatically constructed dataset of 565 verifiable execution environments for scientific data-driven discovery tasks, sourced from 239 real GitHub repositories across four scientific disciplines. - Why it's important: Training LLM agents for scientific coding requires task-specific verifiable feedback loops, but constructing such environments manually is prohibitively expensive (175 person-hours for just 50 gold evaluation scripts), and existing automated approaches lack execution environments and evaluation scripts entirely. - How it does it: A two-stage LLM pipeline — a planning model reasons about which scientific metrics, acceptance thresholds, and output artifacts are appropriate for a given task, then a coding model translates that plan into an executable evaluation script, conditioned on dataset previews and reference outputs. - The good: RFT on D3-Gym trajectories raises Qwen3-32B's SR@3 on ScienceAgentBench from 19.6% to 27.4% — surpassing Qwen3-235B-A22B (22.6%) while using less than one-seventh the parameters. - The missing: Evaluation generalizes to only one external benchmark (ScienceAgentBench); the 66.1% recall of silver evaluation scripts means one-third of correct model solutions are rejected as training signal, potentially biasing the learned distribution. - Read it or not: Researchers building training infrastructure for scientific coding agents should read this; those seeking ready-to-deploy agents can wait, as peak open-weight SR@3 of 27.4% leaves a large gap versus frontier models (35.2% for Claude Sonnet 4.5).
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (85) drives the score — strong ablations (66.1%→3.4% recall collapse without planning), 175 hours of human validation, public release of code/models/environments, SAB-Verified contribution. Novelty (78) — plan-then-code evaluation generation is a genuine methodological advance. Non-Obviousness (78) — NLL/PPL explanation for RFT-Self vs RFT-Distill is mechanistic and non-obvious. Completeness (75) and Resilience (72) solid. Impact (80) — released models, environments, +7.8 SR@3 transferring to ScienceAgentBench. Weighted ≈ 78.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 85 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 75 Resilience 72 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Developing LLM agents capable of scientific data-driven discovery — programmatically analyzing real experimental datasets, implementing domain-specific algorithms, and producing verifiable outputs — requires executable environments with task-specific verification signals. The challenge distinguishing scientific repositories from software engineering codebases is that scientific repos virtually never include pre-existing test suites, and program correctness is inseparable from the domain: the appropriate metric for a survival analysis task is entirely different from that for a geospatial interpolation task. This makes universal verification criteria inapplicable. The prior art (AutoSDT) collected instruction–solution pairs at scale but without execution environments or evaluation scripts, restricting downstream training to static supervised fine-tuning and blocking self-improvement via on-policy trajectories. Key Idea The core insight is that LLMs possess sufficient parametric scientific knowledge to reason about what constitutes a correct solution for a domain-specific task — but only if this reasoning is decoupled from code generation. By first prompting a planning LLM to produce a structured evaluation plan (specifying output artifacts, scientifically appropriate metrics, and acceptance thresholds with domain justification), and then prompting a coding LLM to implement that plan given the reference output and dataset schema, the approach reliably generates discriminative evaluation scripts that a direct code-generation prompt cannot. The planning phase is the linchpin: removing it drops recall from 66.1% to 3.4% in ablation. Methodology Construction proceeds in four stages. (1) Candidate collection: AutoSDT crawls GitHub scientific repos, extracts data-driven workflow files, adapts them into reference solutions, and pairs each with a natural language instruction. (2) Filtering and preview creation: A Claude Code agent verifies that all file dependencies are real (no synthetic stubs), and generates structured dataset schema previews for each input file. Of 5,111 initial candidates, 1,586 pass this stage. (3) Execution and output validation: Each reference solution is executed in an isolated conda environment; a multimodal LLM-as-judge (GPT-5.2, 92.31% agreement with human, Cohen's κ=0.85) verifies that all requested outputs are produced and are non-degenerate. 1,263 execute successfully; 565 pass output validation. (4) Evaluation script generation: Claude Sonnet 4.5 first produces a task-specific evaluation plan (metric selection, threshold specification, artifact identification), then a separate Claude Sonnet 4.5 call translates the plan into an executable Python script. Critical design decisions — separating planning from coding, providing dataset previews, and providing reference code output — are each validated by ablation. Training uses rejection-sampling fine-tuning (RFT) in two variants: RFT-Distill (student learns from Qwen3-32B teacher trajectories) and RFT-Self (model learns from its own passing trajectories). 16 trajectories are sampled per task; only those passing the silver evaluation script are retained. Key Results On a held-out set of 50 tasks with human-annotated gold evaluation scripts evaluated across 424 candidate solutions from Claude Opus 4.6 and GPT-5.4: silver scripts achieve 87.5% accuracy, 66.1% recall, and 91.0% specificity. Evaluation logic alignment scores (1–5 Likert, LLM judge with 85% exact/98% within-1 human agreement): metric choice 4.00, threshold & tolerance 3.82, target artifact 4.20. The lower recall (compared to specificity) reflects systematically tighter acceptance criteria in silver scripts — selecting defensible but stricter conventions (1.96σ CI vs. 2σ) rather than scientifically wrong ones. On ScienceAgentBench: Qwen3-32B RFT-Self achieves SR@3 27.4% (base: 19.6%, +7.8 points), surpassing Qwen3-80B (21.4%) and Qwen3-235B-A22B (22.6%), and approaching o1-preview (34.3%) and Claude Sonnet 4.5 (35.2%). D3-Gym-14B (19.6% SR@3) matches Qwen3-32B base. RFT-Self outperforms RFT-Distill at 14B and above; at smaller scales the two strategies are approximately equivalent. Task difficulty on D3-Gym: GPT-5.4 solves 38.66%, Claude Opus 4.6 36%, Qwen3-235B-A22B 29.33%, Qwen3-32B 16.0%. Dataset construction cost: $1,699.1 total (~$3/environment). This is a preprint; results are not yet peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The automatically constructed verifiable environment dataset for scientific coding is new; the plan-then-code pipeline for domain-specific evaluation script generation is a genuine methodological contribution distinguishing this from prior test-generation work (which uses domain-agnostic string matching). The RFT training and self-improvement experiments are standard applications of existing techniques to the new data. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover all four components of the evaluation script pipeline against a direct prompting baseline (Table 1), with clear attribution of each component's contribution to recall and specificity. Three independent inference runs are reported for all model evaluations. Human validation of silver script quality involved 175 person-hours across 50 tasks and 424 candidate solutions. The LLM judges used for output verification and evaluation logic assessment are both validated against human annotations with reported Cohen's κ and F1. Code, environments, trajectories, and fine-tuned models are publicly released at https://github.com/OSU-NLP-Group/D3-Gym. The authors additionally introduce ScienceAgentBench-Verified to correct 9 task instructions and 3 gold programs in the evaluation benchmark. One methodological concern: the silver evaluation scripts are generated by Claude Sonnet 4.5, and training trajectories are evaluated using those same scripts — models that happen to generate solutions well-aligned with Claude's evaluation conventions may be rewarded for reasons unrelated to scientific correctness. A second concern: 39 of 239 source repositories carry no license; the authors assume permissive research use, which is not guaranteed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS. - Practical: Training requires 4–8 NVIDIA H100 96GB GPUs (standard research cluster hardware); inference is served via vLLM, compatible with commodity GPU setups. - Efficient: Total dataset construction cost is $1,699.1; fine-tuning a 32B model runs on 8 H100s for one epoch over ~2,153 trajectories — feasible but not trivial; no cost/time per training run is reported. - Effective: The +7.8 SR@3 gain on ScienceAgentBench and error-type reductions (data schema errors −44.7%, data type/shape errors −60.4%) confirm genuine improvement; logical/algorithmic errors increase as a fraction because more programs now reach execution, revealing a remaining gap between syntactic and semantic correctness. - Scalable: Tested only up to 32B parameters and 565 tasks; the authors claim the pipeline can collect more tasks if suitable repositories exist, but this is an extrapolation, not a demonstrated result; no experiments beyond 32B or 2,153 training trajectories. The work delivers trained model weights, fine-tuning trajectories, and the environment dataset; software artifacts are publicly released. Strengths - The planning-then-coding decomposition for evaluation script generation is well-motivated and strongly validated: the ablation showing recall collapse from 66.1% to 3.4% without the planning phase is a crisp, convincing result that justifies the design. - Human validation of the silver evaluation scripts is thorough — 175 person-hours, 50 tasks, 424 candidate solutions, and three complementary metrics (pass/fail agreement, evaluation logic alignment, and LLM judge calibration against human annotators) — going well beyond the self-evaluation common in benchmark papers. - The NLL/PPL analysis explaining why RFT-Self outperforms RFT-Distill at 14B+ (self-generated trajectories have lower perplexity under the student's learned distribution) is a non-obvious empirical finding with mechanistic grounding. - Introduction of SAB-Verified, which corrects real errors in an existing benchmark, is a concrete community service that strengthens all future evaluations on that dataset. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note: (1) 565 tasks is small relative to software-engineering environments, though they argue this reflects inherent scientific task scarcity rather than a pipeline constraint; (2) only RFT is evaluated as a training method — RL is left for future work due to sparse reward signal and weak baseline performance; (3) no claim is made about generalization beyond the Qwen3 model family. The authors confront these limitations directly in the appendix, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Evaluation generalizes to only ScienceAgentBench; no second held-out scientific benchmark (e.g., DiscoveryBench, BixBench) is used to test whether gains transfer across domains. The possibility that D3-Gym's four disciplines (computational chemistry, bioinformatics, GIS, neuroscience) happen to overlap with ScienceAgentBench's distribution in ways that inflate the transfer result is not ruled out. - The 66.1% recall of silver evaluation scripts means approximately one-third of genuinely correct model solutions are rejected during RFT trajectory selection. This is a form of noisy reward that the paper does not analyze: how does training on false-negative-contaminated positive sets affect model behavior, and does the tendency to adopt tighter evaluation conventions than gold scripts shift the model's code generation style toward overly strict solutions? - All training experiments use Qwen3 models exclusively; whether the gains hold for Llama, Mistral, or other architectures is unknown. - The self-evaluation circularity — Claude generates silver scripts, Claude-family models generate some of the validation solutions, and Claude Sonnet 4.5 serves as the LLM judge for logic alignment — is not discussed. A fully out-of-distribution judge or evaluator family would strengthen confidence in the conclusions. - 39 source repositories (16% of the 239 total) carry no license; the assumption of permissive research use is legally uncertain and not mitigated. Bottom Line Any researcher building training pipelines for scientific coding agents gains directly actionable infrastructure from this work, and the plan-then-code evaluation generation technique is transferable to other domains lacking pre-existing test suites.
Recommended 2604.27977 PDF
#12 — Highly Innovative (77)

Differentially Private Contrastive Learning via Bounding Group-level Contribution

Kecen Li, Chen Gong, Zinan Lin, Tianhao Wang, Xiaokui Xiao · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DP-GCL, a differentially private contrastive learning framework that partitions training batches into small disjoint groups and restricts InfoNCE negative comparisons to within-group samples, bounding gradient sensitivity to a constant 2C regardless of batch size. - Why it's important: Organizations training embedding models on sensitive data (medical images, personal photos) face input-reconstruction and membership-inference attacks, and existing DP adaptations of contrastive learning suffer near-complete utility collapse — especially for multi-modal retrieval — because the InfoNCE loss's inter-sample dependency makes gradient sensitivity explode with batch size. - How it does it: By localizing each sample's loss computation to its assigned group, adding or removing any single training record can affect at most one group's gradient, holding sensitivity at 2C; intra-group augmentation then generates additional negatives from augmented views of within-group samples to compensate for the reduced negative diversity, without increasing privacy cost. - The good: At ε=10, DP-GCL achieves 65.9% image-to-text retrieval accuracy on CUHK-PEDES versus 18.5% for the prior best (DP-CLIP), a gain that represents the difference between a practically useful and a practically useless model. - The missing: Billion-scale pre-training experiments are absent (authors acknowledge this), and no direct benchmark comparison is made against non-InfoNCE DP representation methods (ViP, Sander et al.), so the claim of overall state-of-the-art is scoped to InfoNCE-based DP methods only. - Read it or not: Practitioners training embedding models on sensitive medical, biometric, or user-behavioral data should read this — it is the first method that makes multi-modal contrastive learning under DP actually work, backed by a clean theoretical result and reproducible code.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (80) is high — Theorem 2's constant 2C sensitivity bound independent of group count is a genuine structural insight that unblocks batch-size scaling for DP InfoNCE, and the multi-modal results (65.9% vs 18.5% at ε=10 on PEDES I→T) represent a qualitative regime change rather than an incremental gain. Non-Obviousness (80) reflects the non-trivial coupling of within-group restriction with intra-group augmentation to recover negative diversity at zero privacy cost. Rigor (75) is good — strong ablations, SNR-based mechanistic analysis, current ICLR 2025 baselines, public code — but main results tables omit error bars and the best-vs-final evaluation inconsistency between baselines and DP-GCL in multi-modal experiments could materially inflate gains. Completeness (75) and Resilience (70) are pulled down by missing comparisons against non-InfoNCE DP methods (ViP, Sander) and the unconventional δ=1/(N log N) accounting. Impact (80) is strong: released code, single-A6000 feasibility, and the first practically usable DP multi-modal contrastive method. Weighted: 0.20*80 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*80 = 77.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 80
Show full analysis
