Problem & Motivation Organizations deploying embedding models trained on private user content face serious privacy risks: input reconstruction attacks can recover original images from learned embeddings, and membership inference attacks exploit cosine similarity statistics to determine whether a specific record was used in training. Differential privacy (DP) is the principled remedy, but applying DP to contrastive learning is fundamentally harder than to supervised learning. In supervised learning, each sample's loss and gradient depend only on its own label — gradients decompose cleanly over training examples. In InfoNCE-based contrastive learning, every sample's gradient depends on all other samples in the batch through the softmax denominator. Applying per-sample gradient clipping (sample-level DP-SGD) causes sensitivity to scale as (2B+1)C, which is prohibitively large even at moderate batch sizes. The prior alternative, batch-level clipping (DP-CLIP), caps sensitivity at 2C but destroys the gradient accumulation benefit of large batches, blocking the privacy-utility improvements that larger batches normally provide. Neither approach closes the gap between private and non-private contrastive learning, particularly in the multi-modal setting where gradient quality requirements are stricter. Key Idea The core insight is that sensitivity and gradient accumulation can be achieved simultaneously by partitioning each mini-batch into small disjoint groups and restricting each sample's InfoNCE negative set to within-group members only. This decoupling is the key: because the loss for every sample in group k depends only on other samples in group k, adding or removing a single training record can perturb at most one group's gradient computation. The triangle inequality then bounds total sensitivity at 2C regardless of how many groups K there are (Theorem 2), meaning group size S can be tuned freely to optimize the privacy-utility tradeoff without incurring a sensitivity penalty. Intra-group augmentation then recovers the negative diversity lost by grouping, expanding the effective negative count from (S−1) to (Na+1)(S−1) per sample without adding new records to the sensitivity calculation. Methodology DP-GCL operates as a modified DP-SGD loop. At each iteration: (1) Poisson-subsample a mini-batch B from the training dataset; (2) randomly partition indices into K = ⌈B/S⌉ disjoint groups of at most S samples; (3) for each group k, compute the group-level InfoNCE loss restricting the denominator to within-group similarities only (Eq. 9), with Na additional augmented variants of each negative sample added to the denominator (Eq. 12); (4) compute the gradient of each group's loss separately; (5) clip each per-group gradient to ℓ2 norm C; (6) aggregate all K clipped gradients and add Gaussian noise N(0, 4C²σ²I); (7) update model parameters via Adam or SGD. Privacy analysis uses the RDP accountant and standard subsampling amplification. The sensitivity proofs (Theorems 1–3) use the triangle inequality and the structural property that group membership of non-added samples is identical between neighboring datasets. Three critical design decisions: (a) group size S=16 is chosen empirically, justified by the SNR analysis showing an optimal tradeoff between negative diversity and gradient quality at S≈4–16; (b) Na=1 augmentation in main experiments (conservative; ablations show Na=3–4 improves further); (c) LoRA (rank=16) fine-tuning of query and value attention layers for multi-modal ViT-B32 encoders, keeping the base model frozen. Extension to dual-modality (Eq. 15–16) is a direct adaptation of the group-level loss to bidirectional image-text comparison. Key Results Uni-modal image classification (ResNet-18, scratch training, 4 datasets, ε∈{1,10}): DP-GCL achieves an average 5.6% classification accuracy improvement over DP-CLIP. Representative numbers at ε=10: CIFAR-10 linear probing 42.3% vs. 35.1% (+7.2 pp), EuroSAT linear probing 59.1% vs. 52.3% (+6.8 pp), Camelyon k-NN 76.7% vs. 69.9% (+6.8 pp). Multi-modal image-text retrieval (ViT-B32 with LoRA, 4 datasets, ε∈{1,10}): DP-GCL achieves an average 20.1% retrieval accuracy improvement over DP-CLIP. At ε=1, PEDES I→T: 55.8% vs. 15.1% (DP-CLIP); at ε=10, PEDES I→T: 65.9% vs. 18.5%; Fashion I→T: 26.3% vs. 3.6%. The three baselines (DP-SGD, Logit-DP, DP-CLIP) frequently collapse near the untrained baseline for multi-modal tasks. Batch scaling (Figure 3): DP-GCL is the only method that consistently improves with larger batch sizes (tested up to 128×16=2048 for multi-modal), matching non-private contrastive learning behavior. Logit-DP is computationally impractical at large batch sizes (O(B²) complexity: 60–72 hours vs. under 3 hours for DP-GCL). Baselines include ICLR 2025 Logit-DP, making comparisons current. Experiments run on a single NVIDIA A6000 GPU. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is the theoretical result linking group-level negative restriction to constant sensitivity (Theorem 2) and the resulting ability to scale batch size without sensitivity penalty — a property no prior DP contrastive method achieves. The intra-group augmentation strategy is a non-obvious design choice that recovers negative diversity without privacy cost. Grouping and data augmentation are individually known; the combination architected specifically to solve the DP sensitivity problem for InfoNCE is the contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation study (Table 4, 5 runs with std) confirms both BGC and ISA contribute independently, with BGC delivering the majority of gains. Group-size sensitivity analysis (Figures 4 and 9) provides mechanistic SNR-based justification for the optimal range S≈4–16. Augmentation count ablation (Table 6, 5 runs) and augmentation type ablation (Table 7, 9 combinations) demonstrate robustness. Main results tables (Tables 1–3) do not report error bars or standard deviations, which is a weakness. A methodological concern: for multi-modal baselines, the paper reports the best test result observed during training (citing instability), while DP-GCL results are final-iteration values; this inconsistency could systematically favor DP-GCL. Code is publicly released (github.com/SunnierLee/DP-GCL). Privacy accounting uses Opacus with RDP accountant, with δ=1/(N log N), which differs from the common δ=10⁻⁵ convention and complicates cross-paper budget comparisons. The paper is accepted at ACM CCS 2026 (peer-reviewed), but this is a preprint (arXiv:2604.26467v1). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA A6000 (33.2 GB peak for uni-modal, 22.6 GB for multi-modal); commercially available hardware is sufficient. - Efficient: Marginally slower than DP-CLIP and DP-SGD (0.99H vs. 0.80H uni-modal, 2.89H vs. 2.43H multi-modal); DP-GCL's O(B/S) groups with per-group gradient computation is tractable, unlike Logit-DP's O(B²) which requires 60–72H for the same tasks. - Effective: At ε=10, achieves 65.9% I→T on PEDES (vs. 87.9% non-private), demonstrating the method substantially solves the stated problem; a meaningful gap to non-private performance remains, and the Fashion dataset (26.3% vs. 51.9% non-private) shows the gap widens on small, fine-grained datasets. - Scalable: Demonstrated up to batch size 65,536 on ImageNet (1.4M images) and 8,192 for multi-modal; the method uniquely improves with larger batches. Billion-scale web corpus training is not tested and explicitly deferred to future work. Delivers a software artifact: code, training scripts, evaluation code, and configuration files released on GitHub. Strengths - The Theorem 2 sensitivity bound (2C independent of group count K) is the intellectual core of the paper and is both correct and practically significant — it is the property that unblocks batch-size scaling under DP for contrastive learning. - The multi-modal results represent a qualitative advance: prior methods essentially fail at DP multi-modal retrieval (DP-CLIP achieves 18.5% I→T vs. 15.5% untrained baseline on PEDES at ε=10), while DP-GCL achieves 65.9%, making the task practically viable for the first time. - The gradient SNR analysis (Figure 4) offers causal evidence for the observed U-shaped performance curve over group sizes, going beyond hyperparameter sweeps to mechanistic understanding. - The method is architecturally agnostic (tested on ViT-B32, B16, L14, L14-H) and compatible with LoRA PEFT, making it straightforwardly deployable in standard production pipelines. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify two limitations: (1) absence of large-scale pre-training experiments on billion-scale image-text corpora due to compute constraints, despite promising batch-scaling behavior; (2) restriction to InfoNCE-based contrastive learning, with BYOL and SigLIP extensions left for future work. These are confronted directly in Section 6, which is a credibility-positive signal. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The best-vs-final evaluation inconsistency between baselines and DP-GCL in multi-modal experiments is not acknowledged. Given that baselines collapse during training, reporting their best checkpoint inflates the comparison in DP-GCL's favor; reporting all methods at their best checkpoint would be the correct comparison. - Non-InfoNCE DP representation methods (ViP, ICML 2024; Sander et al., ICML 2024) are discussed in related work but excluded from benchmarks, so the claim of state-of-the-art is scoped only to InfoNCE-based DP methods. These alternatives achieve DP by replacing the contrastive objective entirely rather than modifying it. - All multi-modal experiments fine-tune a pre-trained CLIP (ViT-B32); training multi-modal encoders from scratch under DP is not evaluated, leaving unclear how much of the gain comes from the pre-trained initialization versus the DP training procedure itself. - The δ=1/(N log N) privacy accounting convention makes direct comparison to papers using δ=10⁻⁵ non-trivial; the authors do not provide equivalent ε values under the common convention. Bottom Line DP-GCL is the first method to make differentially private multi-modal contrastive learning practically viable, and any practitioner building privacy-preserving embedding models on sensitive data should read it.
Citations 2604.26467 PDF
#13 — Highly Innovative (76)

Weisfeiler Lehman Test on Combinatorial Complexes: Generalized Expressive Power of Topological Neural Networks

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the Combinatorial Complex Weisfeiler-Lehman (CCWL) test, an axiomatic color-refinement framework over combinatorial complexes that unifies and strictly subsumes WL variants for graphs, hypergraphs, simplicial complexes, and cellular complexes, together with a neural instantiation CCIN. - Why it's important: Existing higher-order topological neural networks use mutually incomparable WL variants tied to specific structures; the absence of a unified expressive-power theory prevents principled design and comparison across topological domains. - How it does it: The key move is proving that upper adjacency (N↑) and lower adjacency (N↓) alone suffice to recover all information from boundary and co-boundary operators, so the four-relation CCWL collapses to a two-relation update without loss of discriminative power. - The good: Theorem 4.8 (that N↑+N↓ match full CCWL) is non-obvious and practically valuable: CCIN trained with two neighborhoods outperforms the four-neighborhood version on most benchmarks while running ~7% faster per epoch, validating the theory. - The missing: All real-world benchmarks are standard graph datasets lifted via a cyclic procedure into cell complexes, so CCIN receives strictly richer structure than graph-only baselines — the comparisons do not isolate expressivity gains from information gains due to lifting. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on topological deep learning or GNN expressivity theory; practitioners focused on graph tasks should note that CCIN's gains partly reflect the lifting procedure rather than architectural innovation alone.
Why this rank: Theoretical profile. Novelty (75) and Non-Obviousness (80) are the load-bearing dimensions: the two-neighborhood-suffices theorem is a genuinely non-trivial result that resolves a redundancy question, with empirical confirmation. Rigor (75) — proofs are complete with explicit assumptions, ablations are unusually thorough, but inconsistent MUTAG std (±2.1 vs ±5.24) is unexplained and the lifting-vs-architecture confound on graph benchmarks isn't isolated. Resilience (70) — strict-superiority proofs follow standard CFI templates. Impact (60) — no code released but the theorem points to a concrete two-neighborhood algorithm that's faster and competitive. Weighted: 0.25*75+0.20*75+0.25*80+0.10*70+0.15*70+0.05*60 = 75.
theoretical Novelty 75 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Higher-order graph learning has produced a proliferation of neural architectures — hypergraph networks, simplicial networks, cellular networks — each with its own WL-style test and neighborhood system. These tests (Hypergraph WL, MPSN for simplicial, CWN for cellular) are defined for specific structures and are not mutually comparable. Combinatorial complexes (CCs), which subsume graphs, hypergraphs, simplicial complexes, and cellular complexes under a single rank-ordered incidence framework, provide a natural setting for a unified theory. The gap addressed is the absence of a single WL refinement defined on CCs, which prevents understanding how expressivity relates across topological domains and which neighborhood relations are truly necessary. Key Idea The central insight is a subsumption result: upper adjacency colors subsume boundary colors (c_B ⊑ c_{N↑}) and lower adjacency colors subsume co-boundary colors (c_C ⊑ c_{N↓}), because the bridge cells mediating same-dimensional adjacency explicitly encode the cross-dimensional incidence information. Consequently, a two-relation update rule HASH(c^t_σ, c^t_{N↓}, c^t_{N↑}) achieves the same distinguishing power as the full four-relation rule, resolving redundancy without sacrificing expressivity. Methodology CCWL is defined as an iterative injective hashing over multisets of (color, bridge-color) pairs drawn from four neighborhoods of each cell: boundary B(σ), co-boundary C(σ), lower adjacency N↓(σ), and upper adjacency N↑(σ). The key theoretical pipeline is: (1) define CCWL with four relations; (2) prove via induction that boundary colors are recoverable from N↓ and co-boundary colors from N↑ (Lemma 4.7); (3) prove equivalence of four-relation and two-relation schemes (Theorem 4.8); (4) show CCWL is at least as powerful as 1-WL (Lemma 4.9); (5) prove strict superiority over each domain-specific WL via CFI-style lifting constructions (Propositions B.5–B.8). CCIN is the neural realization: a GIN-style summation aggregator with rank-adaptive MLPs operating on N↑ and N↓, with readout pooling across all cell dimensions. Real-world graph benchmarks are lifted to cell complexes via a cyclic lifting algorithm (extracting rings up to a configurable maximum size). Critical design decisions: (a) opaque rank-aware initialization encoding |B(σ)| and |C(σ)|, which is well-motivated; (b) the cyclic lifting procedure, whose impact on performance is partially addressed but not fully disentangled from the model's architectural contribution; (c) summation aggregation for injectivity, standard following GIN. Key Results On Strongly Regular Graphs (synthetic isomorphism benchmark where 3-WL fails), CCIN achieves near-zero failure rates across all 9 families at ring size ≥ 6, outperforming GIN, MPSN, and 3-WL. On TUDataset graph classification, CCIN achieves 96.4±2.1% on MUTAG (vs. GIN 89.4±5.6%, CWN 90.0±7.4%), 78.3±4.5% on IMDB-B (vs. GIN 75.1±5.1%), and 83.2±1.6% on NCI1 (vs. GIN 82.7±1.7%). On REDDIT-B, CCIN achieves 93.4±1.1% (best among all baselines including CWN 93.1±1.0%). On MOLHIV, CCIN achieves 80.45±1.38 ROC-AUC (vs. CWN 78.58±0.57). On ZINC-FULL (250K molecules), CCIN achieves 0.033±0.004 MAE (best reported, vs. MGNN 0.041±0.001, MPSN 0.044±0.003). On Peptides-func, CCIN achieves 0.6493±0.0262 AP (vs. GPS 0.6435±0.0041). Baselines include strong recent methods (CWN, MPSN, GPS, Subgraphormer). High variance on MUTAG (±2.1 in the bold column, but ±5.24 in ablation tables) suggests sensitivity to random seeds on small datasets. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The CCWL framework is genuinely novel as a unified axiomatic WL test for the full CC hierarchy; the two-neighborhoods-suffice result (Theorem 4.8) is a non-trivial theoretical contribution. The strict superiority proofs (CCWL ≻ each domain WL) follow a known CFI-lifting template applied to a new setting — technically sound but not conceptually surprising given the richer structure of CCs. CCIN is a straightforward GIN-style adaptation; the architectural novelty is limited. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation studies cover four neighborhood types (Table 6), maximum cell dimension (Table 7), ring size (Table D.6), layer count (Tables D.9–D.10), and aggregation functions (Tables D.11–D.13) — the ablation coverage is thorough. Results are reported over 9 seeds with mean ± std. However, no code repository is mentioned or linked in the paper. The main results in Table 2 and the expanded Table D.5 show slightly different numbers for the same model (e.g., MUTAG CCIN: 96.4±2.1 vs. 96.30±5.24), likely from different seeds or configuration variants — this inconsistency is not explained. A key methodological concern: the real-world graph benchmarks all apply cyclic lifting before training, giving CCIN access to topological features unavailable to pure-graph baselines; improvement attributable to this information injection versus the CCWL architecture is not isolated. The ZINC-FULL and Peptides results are compared against transformers and positional-encoding methods that may have different parameter counts. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.00725v1, May 2026); it has not undergone peer review. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Runs on 2× NVIDIA RTX A6000 (48 GB each); experiments use standard batch sizes (32–128) and hidden dims (48–128), deployable on any high-end research GPU. - Efficient: The two-neighborhood simplification reduces training time by ~7% on average versus the four-neighborhood full model; the lower adjacency aggregation carries O(Σ_{τ∈X_{k-1}} |S_k(τ)|²) complexity, which can grow quadratically in ring size and is not characterized at graph scale beyond what benchmarks reflect. - Effective: Achieves best or near-best results on 14 of ~18 benchmarks evaluated; the ablation confirms that the performance gain over full CCWL is real, not a regression. - Scalable: Tested on ZINC-FULL (250K molecular graphs, up to ring size 18) and OGB benchmarks (MOLHIV: 41K, MOLPCBA: 437K); scalability to large individual graphs or meshes with high-order cells is not addressed. The paper delivers results in paper form; no software library or trained models are released. Strengths - The two-neighborhoods-suffice theorem (4.8) is the most intellectually valuable result: it resolves a practical redundancy question that prior work left open, and the experimental ablation confirms it holds empirically across diverse datasets. - The proof architecture is systematic: each domain (graph, hypergraph, simplicial, cellular) gets its own lifting proposition and CFI-style separation example, closing the generalization argument completely. - The unified framework subsumes all four major topological structures under one formalism, and CCIN is the first model shown to match expressivity across all four simultaneously (Table 1). - Ablations are unusually thorough for a theory paper: ring size, dimension, layer count, and aggregation function are all examined. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors note that three-dimensional volumetric datasets are lacking, limiting existing research to two-dimensional geometric structures (faces). They acknowledge that the boundary equality for top-dimensional cells (Case 4, tetrahedra) fails when no additional higher-dimensional cells share boundary faces, and treat this as a special case rather than a flaw. The lack of dedicated CC benchmarks is noted, and the authors rely on lifting as a workaround. These are stated directly and honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The comparison on real-world benchmarks is structurally unfair: CCIN receives lifted higher-order information while baselines (GIN, WL kernel, etc.) operate on the original graphs. A fair comparison would require baselines with the same lifted input, or reporting CCIN performance on the original graphs before lifting. - The ablation showing that CCIN (N↑+N↓ only) outperforms CCIN-Full (all four neighborhoods) on many datasets raises a mild concern: in theory they should be equivalent; in practice the difference likely reflects over-parameterization or optimization landscape differences, but this is not analyzed. - The standard deviation on MUTAG (±2.1 in Table 2, ±5.24 in Table D.5 for the same configuration) is unexplained; MUTAG has 188 graphs and is known to be noisy under 10-fold CV — the reported improvement over CWN (96.4 vs. 90.0) is large enough to be robust, but the inconsistency across tables should be addressed. - The lifting procedure (maximum ring size, cyclic vs. clique) is a free hyperparameter that affects performance significantly (Table D.6 shows up to 3.6% swings on NCI1 across ring sizes 4–13); the best ring size is tuned per dataset without cross-validation over separate test splits, which risks leaking. - No comparison to TopoX / TopoBenchmarkX on the same lifted inputs, which would provide a fairer evaluation of the CCWL architecture independent of the lifting choice. Bottom Line The CCWL framework and its two-neighborhood reduction theorem are a genuine theoretical contribution that practitioners in topological deep learning should read, but the empirical claims must be interpreted cautiously given that performance gains on graph benchmarks conflate richer topological input (from lifting) with architectural improvement.
Search Results 2605.00725 PDF
#14 — Highly Innovative (76)

Adaptive Sigmoid Clipping for Balancing the Direction–Magnitude Mismatch Trade-off in Differentially Private Learning

proceedings.neurips.cc · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AdaSig, a gradient clipping method for DP-SGD that replaces hard clipping with a sigmoid function whose saturation slope α is adaptively updated each iteration via a differentially private sign-gradient step, explicitly balancing the trade-off between direction and magnitude deviation in the clipped batch gradient. - Why it's important: Every existing DP-SGD clipping method either suffers severe direction mismatch (vanilla clipping) or excessive magnitude suppression (PSAC), and no prior method jointly adapts to the evolving gradient statistics that shift this trade-off across training iterations. - How it does it: The clipping map ψ_α(x) = C(2/(1+e^{−αx}) − 1) has a linear region whose width is controlled by α; treating α as a learnable scalar updated privately via a sign-gradient rule (using a self-bounding sensitivity of 0.448/α_t proved analytically) allows the method to track gradient statistics without extra clipping of the slope update itself. - The good: AdaSig achieves statistically significant accuracy gains on 7 of 8 benchmarks over all four baselines, with the largest margin on ImageNette (+2.35pp over PSAC at 64.65%) and SST-2 (+0.96pp over PSAC at 88.44%), and a convergence proof matching the best-known O(√(d log(1/δ))/(Nε)) rate for non-convex losses. - The missing: All experiments use ε ∈ {3, 8} — tight privacy regimes (ε < 1) where clipping bias most dominates training are untested — and the largest model is 125M parameters, leaving the method unvalidated for the billion-parameter DP fine-tuning settings where the contribution would matter most. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners working on DP training of models up to ~100M parameters — the method is computationally free, the theory is complete, and the gains are consistent; those targeting large-scale DP LLM fine-tuning should note the scale gap.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (85) is the standout — 5 seeds with 95% CIs and t-tests, code released, full hardware/software disclosure, complete convergence proof matching SOTA rate, and a clean ablation isolating adaptivity from sigmoid shape. Completeness (80) and Resilience (75) are solid: limitations on ε<1 and >125M scale are scoped honestly, though the α_{t-1}≈α_t approximation lacks formal error bounds and the σ_s=1.01σ heuristic is unablated. Novelty (70) is moderate — a new method (adaptive sigmoid clipping with private sign-gradient slope update) rather than a new problem, and the self-bounding sensitivity (0.448/α_t) is elegant but incremental over PSAC. Non-Obviousness (70) reflects the direction-magnitude trade-off framing. Practical Impact (70) is supported by a released GitHub artifact integrable with Opacus, but gains are modest on easy tasks and untested at the foundation-model scale where DP fine-tuning matters most. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*85 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*70 = 76.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 85 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 80 Resilience 75 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation DP-SGD clips per-sample gradients before aggregation to bound ℓ₂-sensitivity for privacy accounting. Vanilla clipping (Abadi et al., 2016) sets each gradient's norm to min(‖g‖, C), assigning unequal scaling factors across samples and producing a direction mismatch between the aggregated clipped gradient and the true batch gradient. PSAC (Xia et al., 2023) widens the "linear region" of the clipping curve so more gradients receive approximately equal scaling, reducing direction deviation, but its fixed linear-region span suppresses small-norm gradients excessively, lowering the effective signal-to-noise ratio (magnitude deviation). Neither PSAC nor any other prior method adapts the direction–magnitude balance during training despite gradient norm statistics changing substantially across iterations. No existing clipping method was designed to explicitly manage both deviations simultaneously. Key Idea Replace hard clipping with a scaled and shifted sigmoid ψ_α(x) = C(2/(1+e^{−αx}) − 1) applied to each gradient's norm. The parameter α controls the width of the approximately linear region of this map: small α expands the linear region (more equal scaling → less direction deviation, more magnitude suppression); large α contracts it toward hard clipping (less magnitude suppression, more direction deviation). Treating α as a scalar learnable parameter and updating it each iteration via a differentially private sign-gradient step allows the clipping curve to track gradient statistics automatically. The sensitivity of the auxiliary term r(·) used in the α update is self-bounding at 0.448/α_t, so no explicit clipping of that term is required. Methodology DP-SGD-AdaSig runs two differentially private mechanisms per iteration. (1) Model update: compute per-sample clipped gradients g̃_{i,t} = ψ_{α_t}(‖g_{i,t}‖) · g_{i,t}/‖g_{i,t}‖, aggregate, add Gaussian noise with multiplier σ_s, and update θ via SGD. (2) Slope update: approximate ∂h^i(θ_t)/∂α_t ≈ −(λ/B) g_{i,t}^T r(θ_{t−1}, α_{t−1}; B_{t−1}), where r(·) sums the sigmoid derivative terms across the batch (Eq. 12). Lemma 5.1 proves ‖r‖₂-sensitivity ≤ 0.448/α_t; Gaussian noise with this sensitivity and multiplier σ_r yields a private estimate r̂. The slope update is then α_{t+1} = α_t · exp(λ_α · sign(ŝ^T r̂)), a sign-based exponential rule that is robust to noise in both estimates and keeps α strictly positive. By Proposition 6.1, the two parallel Gaussian mechanisms per iteration are equivalent to a single mechanism with combined multiplier σ = (σ_r^{−2} + σ_s^{−2})^{−1/2}, so standard privacy accounting (Opacus) applies unchanged. The objective is the empirical loss; no new loss term is introduced. Critical design decisions: (a) using the sign of the product ŝ^T r̂ rather than the full product, motivated by noise robustness — well-justified; (b) setting σ_s = 1.01σ in practice (prioritizing model-parameter accuracy over slope accuracy) — justified heuristically but not ablated; (c) using a delayed batch for r (from iteration t−1) based on the approximation α_{t−1} ≈ α_t for small λ_α — mathematically convenient but the approximation error is not formally bounded. Key Results All comparisons are under equal (ε, δ)-DP budget, 5 runs with 95% CIs and t-tests. Image classification: MNIST 98.14% (vs. 98.13% best baseline), FashionMNIST 86.68% (vs. 86.35%), CIFAR-10 62.54% (vs. 61.93%), ImageNette 64.65% (vs. 62.30%), CelebA multi-label 88.78% (vs. 88.53%), CelebA smiling 90.95% (vs. 90.90%). Statistically significant (p < 0.05) on 4/6 image datasets. NLP fine-tuning (RoBERTa-base, 125M params): SST-2 88.44% (vs. 87.48%, p < 0.05), QNLI 84.92% (vs. 84.58%, p < 0.05). Cosine similarity histograms confirm AdaSig concentrates mass at higher values than all baselines on ImageNette and CIFAR-10. Ablation on CIFAR-10: best fixed-α accuracy is 62.13% (α=1.0) vs. AdaSig's 62.54%, confirming adaptive α outperforms any fixed choice. Baselines are current and strong (Andrew et al. 2021, Bu et al. 2023, Xia et al. 2023). The method is not tested at ε < 1 or with models beyond 125M parameters. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The sigmoid function itself is not novel, but the specific formulation as a differentially private, adaptively-parameterized clipping operation that treats α as a learnable scalar with a privacy-preserving gradient update is original. The convergence analysis for non-convex losses under a varying, non-standard clipping function required new technical lemmas (E.1–E.9) beyond those applicable to vanilla or PSAC clipping. The framing of gradient clipping bias as an explicit direction–magnitude trade-off with an adjustable control parameter is a genuine conceptual contribution. The method is primarily a new algorithmic component rather than a new problem or dataset. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations over fixed vs. adaptive α confirm the adaptive component adds value. Five seeds, 95% CIs, and t-tests across all reported numbers exceed the field norm for this type of work. Code is released at github.com/faezemoradik/AdaptiveSigmoidClipping.git; all datasets are standard public benchmarks. Full hyperparameter tables and hardware/software disclosure (V100 GPUs, PyTorch 2.3.0, Opacus 1.4.1) are provided. Methodological concern: Assumption 7.4 (symmetric per-sample gradient distribution Δ_t =^D −Δ_t) is empirically supported in prior work for specific settings (Chen et al. 2020) but is a non-standard assumption whose violation is not analyzed. The convergence bound's LHS is weighted by Pr{‖Δ_t‖ ≤ r}, making the guarantee vacuous when gradient variance is large relative to r. The α-gradient approximation α_{t−1} ≈ α_t is used without formal error bounds, relying on the claim that λ_α is small. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile) - Practical: Fully compatible with PyTorch/Opacus on standard GPU hardware; tested at up to 125M parameters with full fine-tuning. - Efficient: Per-iteration overhead vs. vanilla DP-SGD is one additional vector sum (identical cost to the model gradient aggregation) plus one scalar update; memory overhead is one stored d-dimensional vector (r̂ from the previous iteration), negligible relative to per-sample gradient storage. - Effective: Accuracy improvements are statistically significant and consistent in direction across all 8 tasks, though the magnitude is modest on simpler datasets (≤0.4pp on MNIST/FashionMNIST/CelebA-Smiling) and more substantial on harder ones (2.35pp on ImageNette, 0.96pp on SST-2). - Scalable: Tested up to 125M parameters; performance at billions of parameters or under very tight privacy budgets (ε < 1) is untested and extrapolation is unsupported. The work delivers a GitHub software artifact (code + implementation) in paper form; no pretrained model weights are released. Strengths - The self-bounding sensitivity of r(·) at 0.448/α_t (Lemma 5.1) is an elegant result that eliminates one hyperparameter and one clipping operation from the slope update, making the algorithm simpler than it could have been. - The proof that PSAC's linear region is fundamentally constrained between two fixed limiting curves (Appendix A) is a clean analytical argument that motivates the sigmoid choice over extending existing methods. - The convergence proof addresses the joint non-stationarity of θ and α in non-convex settings via a carefully constructed chain of lemmas (E.6 monotonicity properties, E.7–E.8 lower bounds on the expected gradient alignment), achieving a rate that matches the fixed-clipping state of the art. - Experimental discipline is high: matched hyperparameter tuning across baselines, same batch sizes and epochs, cosine similarity and SNR analysis in addition to accuracy, and a direct ablation separating the benefit of adaptivity from the benefit of the sigmoid shape. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that direction and magnitude deviations cannot be eliminated simultaneously — AdaSig balances rather than removes the trade-off. They scope the work to DP training, declaring non-DP training out of scope. The paper does not discuss failure modes when α drifts to extreme values, sensitivity of results to initial α_0, or the impact of the σ_s = 1.01σ simplification. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The approximation α_{t−1} ≈ α_t in the α gradient derivation is unanalyzed; for larger λ_α or rapid gradient statistic changes, this could introduce non-negligible bias not reflected in the convergence bound. - Experiments are restricted to ε ∈ {3, 8}; strong privacy regimes (ε ≤ 1) where clipping bias most severely degrades utility are entirely absent, despite being the most practically relevant. - No experiment combines AdaSig with error-feedback methods (Zhang et al. 2024 [41]), which the paper notes are orthogonal; without this, it is unknown whether AdaSig + error-feedback is competitive with or superior to error-feedback applied to PSAC or vanilla clipping. - The σ_s = 1.01σ heuristic allocates almost all noise budget to the model update (σ_r ≈ ∞), making the r̂ estimate nearly noise-free in practice; this is an implicit design choice whose effect on results relative to other (σ_s, σ_r) splits is unexamined. - All tasks are classification; regression or generation tasks where gradient magnitudes and distributions differ substantially are not evaluated. Bottom Line Practitioners running DP training on models up to ~125M parameters should integrate AdaSig — the computational cost is negligible, the implementation is simple, gains are statistically consistent across a diverse benchmark set, and the convergence theory is complete; the unresolved question is whether the same gains persist at the scale and privacy budgets relevant to current foundation model fine-tuning.
Search Results
#15 — Highly Innovative (76)

Skill1: Unified Evolution of Skill-Augmented Agents via Reinforcement Learning (53 ▲ )

Yaorui Shi, Yuxin Chen, Zhengxi Lu, Yuchun Miao, Shugui Liu, Qi GU, Xunliang Cai, Xiang Wang, An Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Skill1, a framework that trains a single LLM policy to jointly optimize skill selection, utilization, and distillation via a shared binary task-outcome RL signal. - Why it's important: Prior skill-augmented agent methods either leave one stage of the skill lifecycle unoptimized or train each stage with inconsistent reward sources, creating conflicting gradients that cap agent performance. - How it does it: Decomposes the single task-outcome signal into a low-frequency per-skill exponential moving average (which supervises re-ranking via NDCG) and a high-frequency deviation from that trend (which rewards distillation only when a new skill genuinely exceeds the library's current frontier), while the raw outcome supervises utilization. - The good: Achieves 97.5% average success on ALFWorld (±0.6, 3 seeds), statistically significantly better than the prior best RetroAgent at 94.9% (p=0.021, Welch's t-test), with ablations showing that removing any single credit signal degrades all three capabilities simultaneously. - The missing: Evaluation is restricted to two text-only environments (ALFWorld and WebShop); the WebShop improvement is under 1 point with no reported significance test; no test on visual, continuous-action, or long-horizon web environments. - Read it or not: Read if you work on LLM agent training or skill library design; the credit-assignment decomposition is a clean idea with convincing mechanistic evidence, though practitioners should note the narrow evaluation scope.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (85) is a clear strength — 3 seeds with Welch's t-test, systematic ablations isolating each credit signal, public code, lower variance than baseline. Novelty (75) — frequency-decomposition credit assignment from a single binary reward without auxiliary models is a clean technical contribution. Non-Obviousness (80) — the cascade insight (selection precision accelerates distillation) is non-obvious and mechanistically supported. Completeness (75) and Resilience (70) — WebShop gain (<1pt) lacks significance test, EMA over co-retrieved skills not ablated, narrow two-environment evaluation. Impact (70) — open-source code, but ALFWorld is near-ceiling. Weighted composite ~76.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 85 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation RL-trained LLM agents interacting with interactive environments absorb successful strategies only implicitly into model weights; those strategies cannot be explicitly retrieved and reused on future tasks that require similar sub-procedures. A persistent skill library addresses this, but its usefulness depends on three tightly coupled capabilities: selecting the right skill from the library, executing effectively conditioned on it, and distilling new reusable skills from completed trajectories. Prior work exposes two structural gaps: (1) at least one stage is always left without a policy gradient update — SkillRL freezes selection after SFT cold-start, RetroAgent provides no selection gradient — creating optimization bottlenecks; and (2) when multiple stages are trained, they typically receive heterogeneous reward signals (e.g., task outcome for utilization, self-assessed quality for distillation), which creates conflicting pressures because all three stages jointly determine task success. Key Idea The single core insight is that one binary task-outcome signal r(τ) encodes information about all three temporal scopes if decomposed by frequency. Its low-frequency component — a per-skill exponential moving average U(s) — accumulates each skill's long-term effectiveness and serves as a proxy for skill quality that can supervise re-ranking decisions via NDCG. Its high-frequency component — the deviation of a rollout's outcome from the current library ceiling Û — measures whether a newly distilled skill is genuinely novel (positive variation) or redundant (negative), providing a distillation credit signal without any auxiliary model or additional rollouts. Methodology Skill1 frames the problem as a POMDP where state includes a persistent skill library B. Each complete rollout τ = (q, z, a₁:T, sₙₑw) concatenates four generation segments all produced by the same policy πθ: (1) Selection — policy generates a natural-language query q, a frozen MiniLM encoder retrieves top-K=5 candidates BK by semantic similarity, and the policy generates a permutation σ to re-rank them; top-ranked skill z is provided for utilization. (2) Utilization — multi-turn interaction conditioned on z.strat for up to T steps. (3) Distillation — policy reflects on τ to produce (sₙₑw.strat, sₙₑw.desc); new skills enter B only when r(τ)=1; library capacity is capped at 5,000 with retirement by lowest U(s)·log(n(s)) score. Reward assignment: Rutil = r(τ); Rrerank = NDCG(σ, argsort(−U(BK))); Rdistill = r(τ) − Û where Û = max U(s) over retrieved candidates; U(s) updated via EMA with α=0.05 over all co-retrieved skills. Optimization uses GRPO (G=16 rollouts) for utilization and distillation, and REINFORCE for re-ranking (GRPO's group normalization is inapplicable because different queries produce different candidate sets). Total objective: J = Jutil + 0.3·Jrerank + 0.3·Jdistill. Three critical design decisions: (a) updating all co-retrieved skills' utility — justified as evidence of relevance to the same task distribution; (b) high-frequency variation rather than raw reward for distillation — prevents rewarding redundant skills; (c) REINFORCE not GRPO for re-ranking — sound given heterogeneous candidate sets across rollouts. Key Results Base model is Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct for all methods; all RL baselines use the same training budget (150 epochs). On ALFWorld (6 household task types), Skill1 achieves 97.5% average success rate, surpassing the prior best RetroAgent (94.9%, p=0.021) and the strongest RL-only method GiGPO (90.8%, +6.5 points). Per-task gains are largest on Look (+7.7 pts vs. RetroAgent), Pick2 (+5.0 pts), and Heat (+6.3 pts). On WebShop, Skill1 achieves 82.9% success vs. RetroAgent's 82.3% — a marginal gap without a reported significance test. Ablations on ALFWorld: removing the skill library drops 16.6 points to 80.9%; removing distillation costs 5.1 points; removing selection costs 5.7 points; removing both auxiliary loss weights (λ1=λ2=0) drops to 90.2%, worse than removing either individually (94.0% and 94.9%), confirming cross-stage dependence. Training dynamics show selection precision reaches 0.95 by step 20, accelerating utilization and distillation to 0.8 by step 60. Task-skill similarity improves from 0.51 to 0.60; library quality Û reaches 0.91 by step 85. Compute overhead: 387–494s/step vs. 290s for plain GRPO (1.3–1.7×), on 8 H800 80GB GPUs. Training converges in ~30 hours on ALFWorld. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The individual components — GRPO, skill libraries, EMA utility tracking, NDCG ranking, REINFORCE — are established. What is genuinely novel is the frequency-decomposition formulation that derives three complementary, stage-specific credit signals from a single binary outcome without auxiliary models, combined with the joint optimization objective that co-evolves all three stages through a single policy. The insight that selection precision cascades forward to accelerate distillation quality (evidenced by training dynamics) is a non-obvious empirical finding that the ablation corroborates. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are systematic: both workflow components (library, selection stage, distillation stage) and training objective weights (λ1, λ2) are individually zeroed. Three independent seeds with Welch's t-test for the primary ALFWorld comparison; standard deviation (0.6) is smaller than RetroAgent's (0.9). Code is publicly released (https://github.com/AlphaLab-USTC/Skill1). Full hyperparameters appear in Appendix C; RetroAgent is re-run from its official implementation; other baseline numbers are borrowed from prior papers under the same base-model constraint. One methodological concern: the EMA updates all co-retrieved candidates based on any rollout's outcome, not just the selected skill's — this conflates retrieval co-occurrence with actual causal contribution and is never ablated against a selected-skill-only update. A second concern: many baseline results are self-reported from authors of those methods; the RetroAgent reproduction match is encouraging but not established for all methods. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.06130v1, May 2026) submitted to NeurIPS (checklist included); not yet peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The trained agent runs on any hardware supporting Qwen2.5-7B inference; training requires 8× H800 80GB GPUs (~30 hours per environment). - Efficient: Training overhead is 1.3–1.7× over plain GRPO; inference cost scales with library context length, which the paper shows reaches 5,000 skills and causes ~70% slowdown in the no-distillation ablation; distillation bounds this cost in practice. - Effective: On ALFWorld the method achieves 97.5% success, near ceiling, with statistically significant improvement over the best baseline; on WebShop the gain is <1 point, and effectiveness is less convincingly demonstrated. - Scalable: The library cap of 5,000 is acknowledged as a potential bottleneck; no experiment tests behavior beyond 100 training steps or with more diverse task distributions; scale claims rest on extrapolation, not experiment. The work delivers a software artifact (GitHub repository). Strengths - The frequency-decomposition credit assignment is technically clean: extracting three signals from one binary reward without auxiliary models or rollouts is a non-trivial formulation with clear mechanistic justification. - Ablations are unusually thorough — cross-capability degradation when any single credit signal is removed is the strongest piece of evidence that the three capabilities genuinely co-evolve rather than improve independently. - Training dynamics analysis (Figures 3–6) provides interpretable mechanistic support: task-skill similarity, library quality, and skill diversity all improve coherently, not just aggregate success rate. - Statistical rigor is above average for this subfield: 3 seeds, Welch's t-test, per-task p-values, and lower variance than the main baseline. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note: (1) evaluation limited to two text-based environments; generalization to visual observations or deep search environments is unexplored; (2) library capacity is fixed at 5,000 with no hierarchical organization, and this may become a bottleneck as task diversity grows. The authors confront these directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The WebShop gain (82.9% vs. 82.3%) is sub-1-point and no significance test is reported; the claim that Skill1 "also demonstrates the best performance" on WebShop is not statistically supported. - The EMA update applied to all co-retrieved skills (not only the selected skill) is a design choice that could introduce bias — a skill retrieved alongside a bad skill gets its utility downgraded even if it would have succeeded — and this choice is never ablated. - The retirement heuristic U(s)·log(n(s)) is neither ablated against alternatives (FIFO, pure utility) nor analyzed for potential skill-type bias (rare but high-value skills may be retired prematurely due to low n(s)). - All experiments use Qwen2.5-7B; whether the co-evolution advantage persists at 32B or 70B, where the base policy is already stronger, is untested. - ALFWorld has only 6 task types with finite templates; 97.5% near-ceiling performance limits the ability to distinguish methods — the 2.6-point gain over RetroAgent, while statistically significant, may not reflect real-world difficulty. Bottom Line Researchers working on RL-trained skill-augmented agents should read this paper for the frequency-decomposition credit assignment idea and the mechanistic ablation evidence of co-evolution; practitioners should weigh that the evaluation is restricted to two constrained text-only environments and the WebShop numbers do not support the paper's claims there.
Recommended 2605.06130 PDF
#16 — Highly Innovative (76)

OpenSearch-VL: An Open Recipe for Frontier Multimodal Search Agents (80 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OpenSearch-VL, a fully open-source recipe — comprising a data curation pipeline, a 7-tool multimodal environment, and a fatal-aware GRPO training algorithm — for training frontier multimodal deep search agents via agentic reinforcement learning. - Why it's important: Top-tier multimodal search agents are dominated by proprietary systems whose training data, trajectories, and recipes are closed, making it impossible for the research community to reproduce, study, or build on frontier-level agentic search behavior. - How it does it: Wikipedia hyperlink-graph sampling produces multi-hop VQA instances where entity names are progressively fuzzified and the visual anchor is decoupled from the answer entity, forcing genuine multi-hop tool use; a GRPO variant then masks post-failure tokens and applies one-sided advantage clamping to preserve credit for valid pre-failure reasoning. - The good: OpenSearch-VL-32B achieves an average of 63.7 across 7 multimodal deep search benchmarks, exceeding proprietary direct-reasoning models including Gemini-2.5-Pro (46.0) and Claude-4-Sonnet (33.1), with ablations showing each data pipeline stage and each RL component contributes measurably. - The missing: The process reward (rquery) is scored by a proprietary GPT-5.4 judge that ignores intermediate visual operations (Crop, Sharpen, etc.), the accuracy judge uses GPT-4o, no multi-seed error bars are reported, and the tool environment's reliance on externally hosted APIs (Serper, PaddleX) makes exact reproducibility impossible. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on multimodal agent training, agentic RL, or open reproducibility of search agents; the fatal-aware GRPO algorithm and the Wikipedia-path data construction pipeline are the most technically transferable contributions.
Why this rank: Mixed empirical/systems — applying empirical weights. Novelty (78) — the Wikipedia-path data construction with anchor-answer decoupling and the formally proved fatal-aware GRPO with one-sided clamping are genuinely new contributions. Rigor (70) — meaningful ablations on data and RL components but no multi-seed error bars (acknowledged), proprietary GPT-5.4 judge introduces non-stationarity, training curves don't show clear convergence. Non-Obviousness (78) — the gradient-dominance proof and source-anchor grounding insight (worth 11.5 points alone) are non-obvious. Completeness (75) and Resilience (70) — limitations honestly confronted; reliance on external APIs makes exact reproducibility impossible. Impact (85) — full open release of data, code, and 3 model scales, addressing a genuine community gap. Composite ~76.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 85
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal large language models capable of actively searching external evidence, verifying facts across modalities, and reasoning over long-horizon tool-use trajectories represent a critical capability gap: the strongest systems (Gemini, Claude, GPT-5 variants) remain proprietary. Practitioners who want to build or study such agents face three simultaneous bottlenecks: absence of open high-quality training data that requires genuine multi-hop visual+textual reasoning rather than single-hop lookup; absence of a tool environment that addresses imperfect real-world visual inputs (blur, low resolution, skew) before retrieval; and absence of an RL algorithm that handles the cascading tool failures endemic to long-horizon agentic rollouts without either discarding useful partial trajectories or injecting gradient noise from meaningless post-failure tokens. Key Idea: The central insight is that shortcut prevention in training data and gradient correctness in RL must be co-designed: grounding the visual anchor at the start of the reasoning chain rather than near the answer entity forces multi-hop search, while one-sided advantage clamping (Â_i = max(r̃_i, 0) for fatal trajectories) lets the GRPO objective selectively harvest positive credit from the viable prefix of a failed rollout without penalizing it — something that hard-masking, which simply zeros out the entire fatal trajectory gradient, fails to do. Methodology: The system is trained in two sequential stages on Qwen3-VL base models (8B, 30B-A3B MoE, 32B). Stage 1 (SFT): a Wikipedia hyperlink graph is traversed via constrained random walks of length h ∈ {2,3,4} under hub-avoidance (τ_hub = 10,000 in-degree), producing paths with explicit anchor/bridge/answer roles. Canonical questions are generated by GPT-4o, then iteratively rewritten to remove entity names via fuzzy descriptors (three invariants enforced: answer invariance, uniqueness, non-leakage). A CLIP-filtered Wikipedia image grounds the anchor, and the anchor mention is replaced with a visual referring expression. Two staged filters (using frozen Qwen3-VL-32B) discard examples solvable without tools or with a single ImageSearch call. A 10% degradation subset (blur/downsample/distortion + enhancement tools) induces think-with-image behavior. Claude Opus 4.6 generates K=5 rollouts per instance in the real tool environment; two-stage rejection sampling (GPT-4o accuracy judge + GPT-5.4 process judge) yields 36,592 expert trajectories averaging 6.3 tool turns. This SFT corpus is trained with full-parameter finetuning on 256 H20 GPUs with DeepSpeed ZeRO-3. Stage 2 (RL): a composite reward r(τ) = r_fmt · (0.8·r_acc + 0.2·r_query) is evaluated over G sampled rollouts; r_fmt is deterministic structural compliance; r_acc ∈ {0,1} from a GPT-4o judge; r_query ∈ [0,1] from a GPT-5.4 judge scoring query relevance, progression, SNR, and cross-modal complementarity. Fatal step detection triggers after K=3 consecutive tool errors; post-failure tokens are masked; fatal trajectories receive advantage Â_i = max(r̃_i, 0) rather than the signed group-normalized score. RL runs ~200 steps on 64 H20 GPUs. Key Results: On 7 knowledge-intensive benchmarks (SimpleVQA, VDR, MMSearch, LiveVQA, BrowseComp-VL, FVQA, InfoSeek), OpenSearch-VL-8B achieves avg 56.6 vs. the previous best open 8B agent SenseNova-MARS-8B at 52.7. OpenSearch-VL-30B-A3B achieves 61.6 vs. its Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B agentic baseline of 47.8 (+13.8). OpenSearch-VL-32B achieves 63.7 avg, exceeding Gemini-2.5-Pro direct-reasoning at 46.0 and GPT-5 direct-reasoning at 45.1. On MMSearch, the 30B-A3B model improves from 44.2 to 68.7 (+24.5); on InfoSeek from 56.2 to 72.4 (+16.2). Data pipeline ablation on 8B (three benchmarks avg): removing source-anchor grounding drops 11.5 points; removing fuzzy entity rewriting drops 10.3; removing staged filtering drops 8.2; removing enhancement subset drops 1.3. RL recipe ablation: SFT-only baseline 64.6 avg; +vanilla GRPO 67.6; +hard masking 67.7 (+0.1 over vanilla); +fatal masking only 69.1 (+1.5); +fatal masking + one-sided clamp 71.8 (+4.2). Training confirms fatal-aware GRPO sustains more tool turns (avg ~12 vs. ~8 at 200 steps) and higher batch accuracy than vanilla GRPO. Baselines are current and strong (GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Claude-4-Sonnet, Qwen3-VL, multiple open agentic agents). GPT-5, GPT-5.4, and Gemini-2.5-Pro comparisons are sourced from their own technical reports — vendor self-reported numbers. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The data curation pipeline (Wikipedia path sampling with functional role assignment, fuzzy rewriting with formal invariants, source-vs-answer anchor decoupling) is a genuinely novel construction not replicated by prior work. The fatal-aware GRPO with one-sided clamping formalizes and proves gradient dominance over hard-masking. The datasets (SearchVL-SFT-36k, SearchVL-RL-8k) are new open resources. The combination of visual enhancement tools (SuperResolution, PerspectiveCorrect, Sharpen) with retrieval in a unified agentic RL framework distinguishes this from retrieval-only multimodal agents; however, the individual components (GRPO, Wikipedia path walking, SFT→RL staging, CLIP grounding) are standard techniques composed in a novel configuration. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and meaningful: both data pipeline stages and RL components are individually ablated with quantitative results on three benchmarks. No multi-seed error bars are reported; the authors explicitly acknowledge this as a limitation of compute cost. Code, models, and datasets are promised for release at GitHub and HuggingFace (as of the preprint date, exact availability should be verified). The process reward judge is GPT-5.4 — a proprietary, version-dependent model — and the accuracy judge is GPT-4o; both introduce potential non-stationarity across evaluation runs. The tool environment relies on Serper (web search), Polaris Lens (image search), JINA Reader, and PaddleX OCR — all external hosted APIs whose behavior can drift, making exact numerical reproducibility impossible by construction (the authors acknowledge this). The evaluation protocol for benchmark numbers follows Vision-DeepResearch's GPT-4o judge prompt, enabling cross-system comparability. Some benchmarks in Table 2 have missing entries for baselines (DeepMMSearch-R1-7B, WebWatcher), limiting pairwise comparison breadth. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS. - Practical: The system runs on Nvidia H20 GPUs (commercial hardware); SFT requires 256 GPUs for 2–4 days and RL 64 GPUs for 10 days, placing it in the industrial-research tier rather than academic lab feasibility. - Efficient: RL adds ~4.2 average accuracy points over the SFT-only baseline at the cost of 10 additional GPU-days on 64 H20s; inference depends on live external APIs (Serper, Polaris Lens, PaddleX), adding latency and cost per query that are not quantified. - Effective: The evidence shows genuine improvement over strong agentic baselines on 7 diverse benchmarks, including substantial gains on MMSearch (+24.5) and InfoSeek (+16.2) relative to the Qwen3-VL agentic baseline, strongly supporting that the method solves the stated problem at measured scale. - Scalable: The method is tested at 8B, 30B-A3B, and 32B model scales and shows consistent improvement at all three; scaling to larger models or longer-horizon trajectories beyond the tested range is not evaluated and not claimed. The work delivers trained model checkpoints, two open datasets, and training code — all three software artifacts are promised for release. Strengths: - The anchor-answer decoupling design in data construction is well-motivated and empirically validated: removing source-anchor grounding alone costs 11.5 average accuracy points, making this the single highest-leverage design decision in the pipeline. - The formal proof (Proposition 1) that one-sided advantage clamping dominates hard-masking in gradient informativeness is a non-trivial theoretical result grounded in the GRPO framework, not just empirical heuristic. - The counterfactual ablation section (Appendix D.3) traces a concrete example to show exactly how removing each design choice would enable shortcut retrieval, providing genuine mechanistic insight rather than only benchmark deltas. - The commitment to full open release (data, code, models) addresses the field's core reproducibility problem, which the paper correctly identifies as the central gap relative to proprietary systems. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state: (1) tool environment instability (search ranking drift, fetch failures, summarization hallucinations in TextSearch and ImageSearch) inflates reward variance; (2) the composite reward depends on proprietary GPT-4o and GPT-5.4 judges that are costly, version-dependent, and score only textual queries while ignoring intermediate visual operations like Crop; (3) exact numerical reproducibility is blocked by reliance on externally hosted APIs and prohibitive multi-seed evaluation cost. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The query-quality reward r_query uses GPT-5.4 — a model released after most baselines were trained and not available to competing systems — as a training signal during RL. This is not a standard baseline and may confer an advantage that is entangled with the training algorithm. The improvement from hard-masking to fatal-masking+one-sided-clamp (+4.2 points) is the key RL contribution, but this is only ablated on the 8B model on three benchmarks; whether the gain holds consistently at 32B scale is not shown. The 10-day RL phase is run for only ~200 optimization steps, and the training curves in Figure 3 do not show clear convergence, raising questions about whether longer training would further improve or overfit. The enhancement subset (image degradation + visual tool training) contributes only 1.3 average accuracy points in ablation, yet this is presented as a major capability; the paper does not report results on a benchmark specifically targeting degraded-input scenarios, where this contribution would most clearly appear. The SFT trajectory synthesis uses Claude Opus 4.6 as the expert, meaning the training distribution is partially determined by a proprietary external model whose behavior may shift across API versions. Bottom Line: Researchers building or studying multimodal search agents will find concrete, well-ablated recipes here — particularly the Wikipedia-path data construction and the fatal-aware GRPO algorithm — that represent genuine advances over the current open baseline, though the reliance on proprietary judge models and external APIs means "fully open" is an aspiration rather than a complete reality.
Recommended 2605.05185 PDF
#17 — Highly Innovative (76)

Learning while Deploying: Fleet-Scale Reinforcement Learning for Generalist Robot Policies (10 ▲ )

Yi Wang, Xinchen Li, Pengwei Xie, Pu Yang, Buqing Nie, Yunuo Cai, Qinglin Zhang, Chendi Qu, Jeffrey Wu, Jianheng Song, Xinlin Ren, Jingshun Huang, Mingjie Pan, Siyuan Feng, Zhi Chen, Jianlan Luo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 4 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Learning While Deploying (LWD), a fleet-scale offline-to-online reinforcement learning framework that post-trains a single generalist Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policy continuously during real-world deployment across a fleet of 16 dual-arm robots. - Why it's important: Deployed robots encounter distribution shifts, long-tail failures, and task variations that no static offline dataset can fully capture, yet existing RL methods either train task-specific specialists or cannot stably fine-tune large generative VLA policies from heterogeneous off-policy fleet data. - How it does it: LWD pairs Distributional Implicit Value Learning (DIVL) — a categorical distributional extension of IQL that preserves multi-modal return structure — with Q-learning via Adjoint Matching (QAM), which converts critic gradients at denoised actions into stable step-wise regression targets for the flow-based action head, using the same RL objective across both offline pretraining and online fine-tuning stages. - The good: On four 3–5 minute long-horizon manipulation tasks (tea, juice, cocktail, shoebox), LWD (Online) achieves a 0.91 step-wise score versus 0.68 for SFT and 0.77 for RECAP, with the DIVL ablation alone showing a 16.7% gain over scalar expectile regression in the online stage. - The missing: No ablation isolates QAM against alternative policy-extraction methods (e.g., AWAC), no analysis of whether the generalist policy degrades on tasks absent from the online replay stream, and the fleet size (16 robots) is modest — the claimed scalability beyond this is asserted, not measured. - Read it or not: Researchers building real-world continual robot learning systems should read this; practitioners focused on simulation or single-task RL can skip it, as the contribution is tightly coupled to the fleet deployment setting.
Why this rank: Systems profile — fleet-scale deployed system on 16 real dual-arm robots with 3–5 minute long-horizon manipulation tasks. Novelty (70) — DIVL combines C51-style distributional RL with IQL bootstrap plus entropy-adaptive τ; QAM is adopted from prior work. Rigor (65) — DIVL ablations are clean but no QAM ablation, no error bars, human-rated long-horizon scoring without inter-rater reliability, SFT baseline confounded by smaller training data than LWD. Non-Obviousness (75) — the multi-modal-returns critique of scalar critics and the unified offline/online objective are intellectually substantive. Completeness (70) — direct limitations. Resilience (70) — would survive most reviewer attacks except the data-confound and missing QAM ablation. Impact (90) — deployed system at fleet scale on contact-rich long-horizon tasks, substantial real-world demonstration, even though no public artifact is released. Composite ≈ 0.15·70+0.15·65+0.10·75+0.15·70+0.15·70+0.30·90 = 75.75 → 76.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 90
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large-scale pretraining of VLA policies acquires broad manipulation competence, but real-world deployment is not a fixed test distribution. Robots deployed in homes, stores, and workspaces encounter unseen object configurations, user preferences, rare failure modes, and distribution shifts that accumulate with use. Static offline datasets cannot cover this deployment tail. Interactive imitation learning (DAgger-style) treats deployment data as a source of supervised action labels, discarding the information in failed trajectories and autonomous recoveries. Existing RL approaches for robotics either train task-specific policies from scratch or fine-tune specialists, not a shared generalist policy. The gap is an offline-to-online RL algorithm that (a) works on large, off-policy, heterogeneous fleet data with sparse binary rewards, (b) stably updates a large generative VLA policy without destabilizing its pretrained representations, and (c) preserves generality across diverse tasks while continuously improving from deployment experience. Key Idea: The core insight is that the distribution of returns for a given state-action pair in fleet deployment data is multi-modal and heavy-tailed — the same action in the same state can lead to success or failure depending on object configuration, policy version, or intervention timing. A scalar expectile critic collapses this into an average and obscures rare but reproducible high-return modes. Replacing the scalar expectile with a categorical distributional value model (DIVL) retains these modes; the τ-quantile of the distribution then serves as the in-distribution optimistic bootstrap target without requiring explicit action maximization. An entropy-based adaptive τ schedule further calibrates bootstrap optimism: uncertain states (high-entropy distributions) receive conservative targets, confident states receive optimistic ones. This distributional treatment is then paired with QAM for policy extraction: rather than backpropagating critic gradients through the full multi-step ODE of the flow policy (expensive and numerically fragile), QAM uses the critic gradient at the denoised endpoint to initialize an adjoint state, which propagates supervision locally along the reference flow trajectory, yielding stable update targets for the flow model. Methodology: LWD proceeds in two stages sharing the same objective. Stage 1 (offline pretraining) trains the policy πθ, critic Qϕ, and distributional value model Vψ on a static buffer Boff containing 652.5 hours of expert demonstrations (336.6h), successful rollouts (88.8h), failed rollouts (39.2h), and human-guided play data (187.9h). All data is converted to chunked transitions (H=30 action steps) with sparse binary terminal rewards. For long-horizon tasks, 10-step chunk-level TD targets accelerate sparse reward propagation through the fixed buffer; short-horizon tasks use 1-step TD. Stage 2 (online post-training) deploys the offline-initialized policy to a 16-robot fleet, which asynchronously streams policy transitions and optional human intervention segments into an online buffer Bon. The central learner trains on 50/50 mixed replay from Boff ∪ Bon, using 1-step TD for all tasks online (longer backups are unstable when human interventions cross TD paths), and broadcasts updated checkpoints every 50 steps. The actor uses the π0.5 architecture (PaliGemma backbone: Gemma-2B + SigLIP-So400M, plus a Gemma-300M flow action expert). The critic/value network uses a separate Gemma 3-270M-IT + SigLIP-So400M backbone with a distributional value head (201 categorical atoms over [-0.1, 1.1] following C51) and a clipped double-Q critic head. During online updates, the policy VLM backbone is frozen and only the action expert is updated, while the critic and value networks are fully fine-tuned. The reference flow fβ is fixed at the behavior-cloned initialization throughout. Key design decisions: (1) unified RL objective across offline and online stages avoids the conservative critic mismatch at stage transition; (2) architectural separation of actor and critic/value networks prevents interference between action generation and value optimization; (3) the critic conditions on both state readout tokens and action chunks encoded with temporal attention pooling. Key Results: On eight real-world manipulation tasks (four grocery restocking tasks scored by binary success, four long-horizon tasks scored by human-evaluated sub-step completion), LWD (Online) achieves an average of 0.95, versus SFT 0.76, RECAP 0.85, HG-DAgger 0.85, and LWD (Offline) 0.88. The advantage concentrates on long-horizon tasks: LWD (Online) 0.91 versus SFT 0.68, RECAP 0.77, HG-DAgger 0.73, LWD (Offline) 0.79. Individual long-horizon scores: Gongfu Tea 0.89 (vs. SFT 0.64), Fruit Juice 0.90 (vs. 0.66), Cocktail 0.93 (vs. 0.70), Shoebox 0.92 (vs. 0.70). Grocery restocking tasks are near-saturated across methods (LWD Online 0.97–1.00). Mean cycle time on long-horizon tasks decreases by 23.75 seconds versus SFT, consistent with critic-guided reduction in hesitations and retries. Ablation of DIVL vs. scalar expectile: +9.7% offline and +16.7% online on long-horizon tasks. Ablation of adaptive τ: +4% average offline score (0.84→0.88). The distributed infrastructure handles 1,604 episodes across an 8-hour 16-actor run with 100% end-to-end delivery; P50 episode-to-learner latency is 41 s, model-to-actor 38 s. Wall-clock budget per online experiment is 4 hours ≈ 60 robot-hours across the fleet. Baselines are current (RECAP from π*0.6, HG-DAgger, SFT as reference), but no comparison against concurrent online VLA RL methods (VLA-RL, RIPT) is provided in the real-world setting. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM. The genuine novelty is threefold: (1) applying distributional RL (C51-style categorical value model) within the IQL asymmetric bootstrap framework for heterogeneous fleet data, with entropy-based adaptive τ — this combination is new even if the components individually are not; (2) integrating QAM (Li & Levine 2026) as the policy-extraction mechanism for a large flow-based VLA in a real-world fleet post-training setting, which had not been validated at this scale or architecture size; (3) the end-to-end system closing the offline-to-online loop for a shared generalist policy across diverse real-world tasks. DIVL is a straightforward extension of IQL with C51, and QAM is adopted from prior work; the combination and application constitute the novel contribution rather than either component alone. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations isolate two DIVL design choices (distributional vs. scalar value, adaptive vs. fixed τ) but do not ablate QAM against alternatives (AWAC, advantage-weighted regression), leaving the policy-extraction contribution empirically unsupported. No error bars or significance tests appear in Table I; only cycle-time means and standard errors are reported in Fig. 5, and the number of evaluation episodes per task is not stated. Long-horizon scores are assigned by trained human evaluators using a rubric — interrater reliability is not characterized. The offline pretraining data (652.5 hours) is large; no ablation examines data volume sensitivity, making it unclear how much of the gain is attributable to the RL objective vs. the richer offline data coverage compared to the SFT baseline. Code, model weights, and data are not released (no repository URL mentioned). The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.00416v1, May 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. One methodological concern: RECAP is adapted to the multi-task generalist setting rather than run in its original single-task form, introducing an implementation choice that may disadvantage the baseline. Deployability (SYSTEMS): Profile: SYSTEMS — the paper evaluates a built, deployed system on 16 real robots. - Practical: The system runs on commercially available dual-arm robots (Agibot G1) at 30 Hz joint-position control, and the cloud learner uses standard distributed JAX infrastructure, with no exotic hardware beyond the robot fleet itself. - Efficient: 4 hours of wall-clock online training (≈60 robot-hours across 16 actors) suffices to reach 0.95 average from a 0.88 offline baseline; offline pretraining consumes 652.5 hours of curated data, which is a substantial but one-time cost. - Effective: The policy demonstrably solves 3–5 minute manipulation tasks requiring contact-rich skills (pouring, grasping, tool use) with 0.91 average step-wise score, and the value function tracks task progress through sparse terminal rewards as visualized qualitatively. - Scalable: The fleet size of 16 robots is relatively modest; the actor-learner architecture is claimed to scale independently, but no experiment validates performance or stability at 100+ robots or across a broader task distribution; scalability is asserted via architectural design, not empirically demonstrated. The work delivers a deployed system and algorithm but not a public software artifact, library, or pretrained model. Strengths: - The empirical demonstration on 8 real-world tasks with 3–5 minute long-horizon episodes is unusually demanding for real-world robot RL; most prior fleet-scale RL work (QT-Opt, MT-Opt) focused on short-horizon grasping. - The insight that multi-modal fleet returns are poorly served by scalar critics is well-motivated and supported by the 16.7% online DIVL gain; Proposition 1 provides a formal equivalence showing DIVL is a strict generalization of IQL rather than an ad hoc modification. - Using a unified RL objective across offline and online stages directly addresses the known offline-to-online mismatch (conservative critics that miscalibrate for online extrapolation), and the design is validated by the LWD (Offline) → LWD (Online) improvement chain. - The distributed infrastructure design — versioned snapshots, at-least-once delivery, SPMD JAX multi-host learner — is described at enough detail to be reproducible; the P50/P99 latency characterization is a practical engineering contribution for fleet RL. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state three limitations: (1) the online update schedule is a straightforward real-time design not optimized for larger-scale or long-term continual deployment; (2) long-horizon tasks use a single short language instruction, which is insufficient for complex tasks requiring VL reasoning and task decomposition; (3) the framework lacks explicit safety modeling, which is required for reliable real-world deployment. These are confronted directly and honestly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most significant unaddressed gap is the absence of any QAM ablation — the paper adopts it as the policy-extraction mechanism without comparing against AWAC, advantage-weighted regression, or direct critic backpropagation, so the claim that QAM is necessary is unsupported empirically. Second, there is no evaluation of catastrophic forgetting: as the shared policy improves on online tasks, the paper does not assess whether performance degrades on tasks not present in the current deployment stream, which is critical for a generalist policy. Third, the massive offline data buffer (652.5h) is shared by all methods, but SFT trains only on demonstrations (336.6h) while LWD also uses rollouts and play data — the performance gap between SFT and LWD conflates the RL objective with superior data utilization; an SFT baseline trained on the full offline buffer would isolate the RL contribution. Fourth, the long-horizon scoring rubric is human-evaluated with no inter-rater reliability measurement, introducing potential evaluation noise. Fifth, fleet size scaling is completely unevaluated — the claim of fleet scalability rests on architectural arguments, not experiments. Bottom Line: A practitioner building real-world continual learning systems for manipulation robots will find LWD's combination of distributional value estimation, flow-policy-compatible critic gradients, and distributed infrastructure concretely useful — but should treat the scalability and generalization claims with caution until the missing ablations (QAM alternatives, fleet size scaling, forgetting) are addressed.
Recommended 2605.00416 PDF
#18 — Highly Innovative (75)

UniVidX: A Unified Multimodal Framework for Versatile Video Generation via Diffusion Priors (69 ▲ )

Houyuan Chen, Hong Li, Xianghao Kong, Tianrui Zhu, Shaocong Xu, Weiqing Xiao, Yuwei Guo, Chongjie Ye, Lvmin Zhang, Hao Zhao, Anyi Rao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 4 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces UniVidX, a unified framework that repurposes a 14B-parameter video diffusion model (Wan2.1-T2V) to perform any-to-any generation across multiple visual modalities (RGB, albedo, irradiance, normal, alpha, foreground, background) within a single model, instantiated as UniVid-Intrinsic and UniVid-Alpha. - Why it's important: Prior approaches train a separate model for each input-output modality pair, which ignores cross-modal correlations and prevents flexible conditioning; a single unified model that can freely exchange conditions and targets across modalities would collapse this proliferation of specialists into one reusable artifact. - How it does it: Three mechanisms cooperate: Stochastic Condition Masking (SCM) randomly partitions modalities into noisy targets vs. clean conditions each training step; Decoupled Gated LoRA (DGL) activates per-modality LoRA adapters only when that modality is being generated; and Cross-Modal Self-Attention (CMSA) shares keys and values across modalities while keeping queries modality-specific. - The good: UniVid-Intrinsic matches or beats dedicated specialist methods on inverse rendering while using only 19K training frames versus NormalCrafter's 860K (a 45× reduction), credibly demonstrating that VDM priors do the heavy lifting. - The missing: The primary inverse rendering benchmark (InteriorVid-Test) is 24 clips constructed by the authors from the same synthetic pipeline as InteriorVid-Train, making it an in-distribution self-evaluation; an independent held-out benchmark would substantially strengthen the generalization claims. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on video intrinsic decomposition, video matting, or unified multimodal generation — the data-efficiency result is striking and the three-component architecture is worth studying; skip it if you need out-of-distribution robustness guarantees backed by independent benchmarks.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) — stochastic condition masking + decoupled gated LoRA in the video setting is new; CMSA is adapted from image-domain cross-domain attention.
