The VQA metric is augmented with a non-standard CoT prompting strategy developed by the authors (VQA+CoT), which substantially shifts scores (e.g., Prox·E: 0.28 → 0.71 with Qwen2.5-VL-7B); this improvement is validated qualitatively but introduces evaluator-selection bias since the authors both propose and evaluate the metric. The user study is modest at 26 participants and 80 samples (two splits of 40). Code is not explicitly released in the paper (the method uses publicly available implementations of SuperDec, TRELLIS, VoxHammer inversion, and FLUX.1-Kontext). The paper is peer-reviewed at SIGGRAPH 2026. A robustness analysis on 90 non-curated samples found only 14 failures (2 from VLM editing errors, 0 from LLM parsing errors), which is a meaningful empirical reliability signal. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (a built system evaluated by running edits on a benchmark). - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA A100 80GB GPU; the SLAT inversion component alone requires 4m 18s and is the dominant cost, but can be omitted for structure-only edits — the A100 requirement is realistic for research labs and cloud inference but not consumer hardware. - Efficient: Total runtime of ~10m 28s per edit is significantly slower than TRELLIS+FLUX (1m 27s) and substantially slower than training-based methods at inference (Spice-E 32s, BlendedPC 48s), though those methods require days of training; SLAT inversion accounts for ~41% of total runtime. - Effective: The ablation and user study provide direct evidence that the method achieves both higher edit fidelity (VQA 0.71) and better identity preservation (LPIPS 0.10) simultaneously, which competing methods trade off — the evidence supports the stated claim. - Scalable: Not tested beyond single-object scenes at TRELLIS's native 64³ voxel resolution; the scene-editing demonstration (four composed assets) is qualitative only, and the paper explicitly acknowledges the voxel resolution ceiling for large or detailed scenes. The work delivers a runnable pipeline built on publicly available components, but does not release a packaged software artifact or trained model. Strengths: - The proxy-induced denoising mechanism is technically clean: the three-region spatial masking with distinct timestep injection schedules is a principled solution to the identity-preservation vs. edit-fidelity tradeoff that competing methods fail to balance. - Training-free generalization across arbitrary input shapes and edit types (geometric transformation, part addition/removal, appearance change) without any category-specific retraining is a genuine practical advantage over ChangeIt3D/BlendedPC/Spice-E. - The color-coded JSON + four-view VLM interface is a well-designed grounding mechanism that lets the VLM reason about spatial relationships without hallucinating primitive IDs — a non-trivial engineering insight for VLM-in-the-loop geometry pipelines. - The failure rate analysis on 90 non-curated samples (14 total failures, only 2 from VLM proxy editing) provides a rare, honest empirical reliability characterization that most 3D editing papers omit. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge two limitations: (1) the method's performance is bounded by the quality of SuperDec's initial decomposition — when components are merged into a single primitive (e.g., chair spindles absorbed into the backrest frame, lamp handle merged with shade), fine-grained control over those components is lost; and (2) the pipeline requires a highly capable VLM for the abstraction editing step, and only the most capable current models reliably support the pipeline, creating dependence on proprietary frontier models. Both limitations are confronted honestly with illustrative failure cases. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The quantitative benchmark is exclusively ShapeTalk/ShapeNet furniture (three categories). Edit3D-bench results are qualitative only. There is no quantitative test on real-captured 3D assets (e.g., from scans or 3D Gaussian reconstructions), leaving open whether SuperDec's abstraction quality degrades for non-synthetic objects. - The VQA+CoT metric was designed and tuned by the authors and used as a primary evaluation criterion; the strong improvements from CoT (e.g., 0.28 → 0.71 for Prox·E on Qwen) versus weaker improvements for thinking-model variants suggest sensitivity to prompt engineering that is not independently validated. - No per-category breakdown of the main quantitative results is reported; given that Lamp, Chair, and Table have very different structural complexity, aggregated metrics may obscure category-specific failure modes. - The user study asks participants to rate two images without controlling for rendering quality differences (textured vs. point-cloud rendering across baselines), which may introduce a confound favoring appearance-generating methods. - The method currently supports one edit at a time; sequential multi-edit scenarios (applying edit 1, then edit 2 on the result) are demonstrated qualitatively in Figure 1 but not evaluated quantitatively or analyzed for error accumulation. Bottom Line: Prox·E presents a technically well-motivated and practically useful solution to a real gap in 3D editing — the inability of pixel-space methods to execute metric structural instructions — and any practitioner building text-controlled 3D shape editing pipelines will find the proxy-induced denoising mechanism and VLM grounding strategy directly actionable; the narrow ShapeNet evaluation and the non-standard VQA metric are real caveats but do not undermine the core contribution.
Recommended 2604.23774 PDF
#24 — Innovative (74)

Hierarchical Abstract Tree for Cross-Document Retrieval-Augmented Generation (3 ▲ )

Ziwen Zhao, Menglin Yang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 5 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Ψ-RAG, a tree-RAG framework for corpus-level cross-document multi-hop retrieval that replaces k-means clustering with an agglomerative hierarchical clustering-inspired "merging and collapse" index and augments retrieval with a multi-turn hybrid agentic retriever. - Why it's important: Existing tree-RAG methods (e.g., RAPTOR) degrade severely as corpus scale grows — RAPTOR's F1 on 2Wiki drops from ~77% at single-document scale to ~22% at corpus scale — blocking practical deployment for multi-hop enterprise search. - How it does it: The tree index is built greedily by sorting all pairwise chunk similarities and iteratively merging or collapsing nodes, a process proven via Dasgupta's cost to resist the k-means "uniform effect" that contaminates minority clusters; a ReAct-style agent then performs multi-turn hybrid (tree + BM25) retrieval with query enrichment (inserting appositions to anchor named entities). - The good: Ψ-RAG is the first tree-RAG to surpass a state-of-the-art Graph-RAG method, outperforming HippoRAG 2 by 7.4% in average F1 across six QA benchmarks while indexing 6.5× faster than RAPTOR and ~576× faster than HippoRAG 2. - The missing: Base indexing complexity is O(n²), which is impractical for industrial-scale corpora without the HNSW/bucketing extensions that are validated only on indexing speed, not end-to-end QA quality at scale. - Read it or not: Worth reading for RAG practitioners who need multi-hop cross-document retrieval without the OpenIE overhead of graph approaches; the theoretical grounding and open-sourced code make it actionable.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (75) is the headline strength — formal Dasgupta-cost proofs (Theorems 4.3/4.4) underpin the engineering choice, evaluation spans 4 task types and 10 datasets, code is released, and ablations isolate each component. Novelty (75) and Non-Obviousness (75) come from the merging-and-collapse algorithm and the theoretical demonstration that AHC-based trees avoid the k-means uniform effect, producing the first tree-RAG to surpass HippoRAG 2. Completeness (70) is helped by direct acknowledgment of the O(n²) limitation and BM25 entity-pollution failure mode; Resilience (70) holds despite the proofs being formally tight only for 3-layer equi-depth trees and HippoRAG 2 being re-run with a non-native embedder. Impact (75) reflects released GitHub code and 6.5×–576× indexing speedups. Weighted: 0.20·75+0.25·75+0.15·75+0.15·70+0.15·70+0.10·75 ≈ 74.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Enterprise and knowledge-intensive NLP applications require retrieving from large multi-document corpora and answering questions that chain across multiple passages (multi-hop QA). Flat retrieval methods (BM25, DPR) cannot follow causal chains between documents. Graph-RAG (e.g., Microsoft GraphRAG, HippoRAG 2) handles multi-hop well but requires expensive OpenIE-based graph construction (137,000–148,000 seconds on HotpotQA/MuSiQue). Tree-RAG (e.g., RAPTOR) is fast to index but fails at corpus scale: RAPTOR's Recall@5 on PopQA degrades by >25% compared to DPR alone, and its F1 on 2Wiki collapses from ~77% to ~22% when the index scales from a single document to a full corpus. Three root causes are identified: (1) k-means-type clustering enforces a uniform cluster-size bias (the "uniform effect"), corrupting minority-topic abstract nodes with majority-topic content; (2) tree indexes lack explicit cross-document connections, so multi-hop causal chains cannot be followed; (3) coarse upper-layer abstracts block fine-grained entity matching needed for factual QA. Key Idea The key insight is that agglomerative hierarchical clustering's greedy local-similarity merging minimizes Dasgupta's cost in a way that naturally preserves skewed corpus distributions, whereas k-means' global centroid optimization forces uniform cluster sizes by design (proven via the equivalence of the k-means objective to maximizing Σ nᵢnⱼ‖μᵢ−μⱼ‖²). Equipping the resulting non-uniform, distribution-adaptive tree with an agentic multi-turn retriever that reformulates sub-queries and fuses sparse and dense retrieval per step simultaneously resolves structural isolation and coarse abstraction. Methodology Ψ-RAG follows a two-phase indexing/retrieval pipeline. Abstract Tree Indexing: All n document chunks are encoded into dense vectors; an O(n²) symmetric similarity matrix S is computed. Chunk pairs are sorted by descending similarity and processed in order: (i) if neither chunk has a parent, both are linked under a new abstract node (merge); (ii) if one chunk has a parent, the other collapses under that parent (leaf-node collapse); (iii) if both have parents in separate subtrees of equal depth, their roots are merged; (iv) if depths differ, the shallower root is re-attached at the matching depth in the deeper path (abstract-node collapse). The algorithm runs for exactly n−1 steps, producing a single connected multifurcating tree. Nodes with >40 children are rebalanced by splitting in half. An abstraction LLM (Llama-3.3-70B by default) then generates either a summative paragraph or a keyword list for each internal node. A BM25 sparse index is also built during this phase. Time complexity: O(n² log n) for sorting, O(n²) for merging; O(n²) space. Multi-granular Agentic Retrieval: Top-down beam search on the tree selects top-k children at each layer by cosine similarity, producing O(log n) traversal. A Retrieval-and-Answering (R&A) LLM agent receives the initial top-k chunks (from both tree and BM25), outputs either ⟨answer⟩ or ⟨retrieve, q'⟩; if the latter, q' is an enriched sub-query (e.g., "Who is the wife of the American film producer David Gest?" from "Who is David Gest's wife?"). This iterates up to imax times, with tree and sparse retrieval fused by a reranker (Qwen3-Reranker-8B) or RRF. Critical design decisions: (a) greedy local merging vs. global k-means — justified by Theorems 4.3/4.4; (b) keyword vs. summative abstract — empirically comparable, keyword marginally weaker on multi-hop; (c) reranker vs. RRF — reranker superior (+5.5% F1 on 2Wiki in ablation) though not the core contribution. Key Results On six cross-document QA benchmarks (NQ, PopQA, HotpotQA, 2Wiki, MuSiQue, MultiHop-RAG; 1,000 queries each, corpora of 609–11,656 documents), Ψ-RAG achieves 62.77% average F1 vs. 36.69% for RAPTOR+Q3R (+25.9%), 55.41% for HippoRAG 2 (+7.4%), 52.87% for HippoRAG 2+Q3R (+9.9%), and 53.55% for BM25+DPR+Q3R (+9.2%). On the hardest multi-hop dataset 2Wiki, Ψ-RAG reaches 76.94% F1 vs. RAPTOR's 22.16% (+54.78%) and HippoRAG 2's 71.35% (+7.7%). On single-hop (NQ, PopQA), Ψ-RAG matches or marginally beats HippoRAG 2 but the gap over flat retrieval closes, consistent with the design focus on multi-hop. On narrative QA and summarization, Ψ-RAG outperforms RAPTOR by 9.7% F1 average on NarrativeQA and ∞-LongBook, and exceeds the graph-based GoR by 3.2% ROUGE-L on QMSum/WCEP. Indexing takes 258s on MuSiQue (1.3M tokens) vs. RAPTOR's 9,254s (6.5× faster) and HippoRAG 2's 148,860s (~576× faster). Ablation shows the R&A agent contributes ~20% average F1 gain on multi-hop datasets; sparse retrieval contributes +41.21% F1 on 2Wiki. With lightweight backbones (L3-8B abstraction, BGE reranker), indexing drops to 2,487s with only minor accuracy loss. Closed-source R&A agents (Gemini-2.5-Flash: 51.86% F1 on MuSiQue) improve further over L3-70B (47.83%). Baselines are current and strong; the HippoRAG 2 comparison is reported both in its original embedding setting (NV-Embed-V2) and the paper's unified Q3-8B setting (a fair practice). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuine novelty is threefold: (1) the first formal proof that AHC-based tree construction avoids k-means' uniform effect via Dasgupta's cost (Theorems 4.3/4.4), grounding an engineering choice in theory; (2) the specific "merging and collapse" algorithm that produces equi-depth multifurcating trees from pairwise similarity rankings without requiring k or depth as hyperparameters; (3) the demonstration that a tree-RAG can first match then surpass state-of-the-art Graph-RAG on multi-hop QA. Individual components — AHC, BM25 hybrid retrieval, agentic ReAct-style retrieval, RRF/reranker fusion — are standard ideas applied in a new integrated configuration. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative (Figure 6, Tables 5–7, Figure 11), isolating the R&A agent, sparse retrieval, reranker, query reorganization, and backbone choices. However, error bars and significance tests are absent, and all experiments appear to be single runs. Code is released at https://github.com/Newiz430/Psi-RAG. Benchmarks are standard; baselines cover traditional, graph, and tree RAG paradigms. One methodological concern: the paper re-runs HippoRAG 2 with a different embedding model (Q3-8B vs. its native NV-Embed-V2); HippoRAG 2 was validated with NV-Embed-V2, so the Q3-8B setting may understate its performance, and the comparison for a subset of datasets using the original setting shows HippoRAG 2 is indeed stronger in its native setting on most benchmarks. The theoretical analysis (Theorems 4.3/4.4) is formally proved only for 3-layer equi-depth trees; the paper argues generalizability via Dasgupta's cost modularity but does not prove this for the actual 5–6-layer non-equi-depth trees used in experiments. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on 3× RTX 4090 GPUs (48GB VRAM each); a lightweight configuration (L3-8B abstraction, Qwen3-0.6B embedding, BGE reranker) reduces indexing to ~2,500s and average query latency to ~5.5s on a 1.3M-token corpus, feasible on single-GPU setups. - Efficient: O(n²) similarity ranking is the bottleneck; HNSW reduces this to O(kn log kn) and bucketing further to O(n²/b²) at the cost of index quality (not evaluated end-to-end on QA); LLM abstraction dominates wall-clock time at 50M+ token scale (~1.4M seconds estimated). - Effective: Strong evidence the method solves cross-document multi-hop QA; false rates of 10–21% on single/multi-hop datasets and 30–35% not-mentioned rates on the hardest multi-hop questions (MuSiQue, MultiHop-RAG) indicate meaningful failure modes at the current scale. - Scalable: Empirically validated up to 50M tokens (350k chunks) for indexing speed only; QA quality at that scale is not reported; the claim that HNSW+bucketing preserves accuracy is asserted but unvalidated against QA benchmarks. Delivers a software artifact (GitHub repository with full pipeline) at paper form. Strengths - Formal theoretical grounding via Dasgupta's cost (Theorems 4.1, 4.3, 4.4 with full proofs) distinguishes this from typical engineering RAG papers; the theorems non-trivially show that the collapsing process in Ψ-RAG, not raw AHC, carries the non-uniformity preference. - Unusually broad evaluation: four task types, ten datasets, covering single-hop QA, multi-hop QA, narrative QA, and summarization — most prior structured-RAG papers focus on one or two task types. - Strong headline result: first tree-RAG to exceed a state-of-the-art Graph-RAG system on multi-hop QA benchmarks while being orders of magnitude faster to index, resolving a previously accepted trade-off. - Backbone flexibility is validated quantitatively (Table 6) rather than just claimed, showing the framework remains competitive with 1B-parameter abstraction models and open-source rerankers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) O(n²) base complexity limits applicability to industrial-scale corpora, motivating the HNSW/bucketing appendix; (2) LLM abstraction becomes the dominant cost bottleneck at scale; (3) BM25 is vulnerable to high-frequency entity names that pollute sparse retrieval (the "David" vs. "David Gest" problem); (4) when the initial retrieval attempt for a reorganized query fails, subsequent attempts typically also fail because the high-level semantic focus of the tree retriever does not shift; (5) the imax setting requires knowing the hop count in advance. The authors confront these directly and propose partial mitigations (query hop discriminator, HNSW extensions), which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The theoretical proofs cover 3-layer equi-depth trees; actual deployed trees are 5–6 layers deep and not equi-depth. The generalization argument relies on Dasgupta's cost modularity, but this is asserted rather than proven for multifurcating trees of arbitrary depth — a motivated reader should verify whether the proof technique extends cleanly. - All HippoRAG 2 comparisons in the main table use Q3-8B rather than NV-Embed-V2 (the setting in which HippoRAG 2 was optimized); the paper reports this in a footnote but does not provide full cross-dataset numbers for the original HippoRAG 2 setting, making the magnitude of the improvement harder to verify independently. - The quality of HNSW and bucketing extensions under QA evaluation is absent — Table 14 reports only time/memory, not whether QA accuracy degrades; practitioners adopting these extensions for large corpora have no evidence of quality preservation. - LightRAG (published EMNLP 2025, cited in the paper) is not included as a baseline despite being a relevant lightweight structured RAG competitor. - Latency distribution is not reported; the agentic retrieval's per-query processing time can vary greatly (1–4 hops), and tail-latency characteristics are unexplored for production settings. Bottom Line A practitioner building a production RAG system for cross-document multi-hop QA should read this paper: it provides the first tree-RAG with peer-reviewed evidence that it matches or beats Graph-RAG accuracy at a fraction of the indexing cost, with released code and a theoretically grounded explanation for why the indexing design works.
Recommended 2605.00529 PDF
#25 — Innovative (73)

Lightning Unified Video Editing via In-Context Sparse Attention (11 ▲ )

Shitong Shao, Zikai Zhou, Haopeng Li, Yingwei Song, Wenliang Zhong, Lichen Bai, Zeke Xie · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces In-Context Sparse Attention (ISA), a trainable sparse attention mechanism for video editing under In-Context Learning (ICL) paradigms, along with LIVEditor, a video editing model built atop ISA trained on a curated 1.7M-sample dataset. - Why it's important: ICL-based video editing concatenates source and context token sequences, quadrupling standard attention cost and making long-sequence video editing at 64K+ tokens computationally intractable on single-GPU inference. - How it does it: ISA selectively prunes low-saliency context tokens via coarse pooled attention scores, then splits queries by a sharpness metric (proxy for 0th-order Taylor approximation error) to route high-sharpness queries to full attention and low-sharpness queries to a block-wise 0th-order Taylor sparse attention kernel reducing per-block complexity from O(LQLKD) to O(LQD). - The good: ISA achieves ~1.47x wallclock speedup over FlashAttention 3 in training-free mode and, after fine-tuning, actually outperforms a full-attention baseline on all EditVerseBench metrics, suggesting the noise-pruning effect is beneficial beyond mere approximation. - The missing: The three benchmarks used (EditVerseBench, VIE-Bench, IVE-Bench) are themselves recent preprints or unpublished works from closely overlapping author groups, raising concerns about circular or favorably tuned evaluation; no human study is reported, and hardware scale (GPU count for training) is not clearly stated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners and researchers designing efficient attention for video diffusion models — the sharpness-guided query routing is a reusable idea; less essential for those outside the video generation / sparse attention space.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) — sharpness-guided query routing tied to a Taylor-error bound and ICL-specific token bimodality is a genuinely new sparse-attention design. Rigor (70) — dense hyperparameter sweeps, formal Theorem 3.1 with proof, cross-model transfer, fully differentiable Triton kernels; weakened by VLM-only evaluation, no human study, no error bars, and benchmark circularity (EditVerseBench shares author lineage with a baseline). Non-Obviousness (80) — the empirical finding that ISA outperforms full attention after training (implicit regularization via context pruning) is counter-intuitive. Completeness (70) — most limitations honestly acknowledged, but benchmark circularity not addressed and GPU count for training undisclosed. Resilience (70) — theoretical grounding helps but VLM-evaluator and benchmark-overlap risks would draw reviewer fire. Impact (75) — Triton kernel with backward pass is directly drop-in deployable; 1.47×–4.87× speedups are real. Composite ≈ 0.20·70+0.25·70+0.15·80+0.15·70+0.15·70+0.10·75 = 72.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: In-Context Learning (ICL) for video editing concatenates source tokens and context tokens and feeds the combined sequence into a transformer with full attention. Because video tokens at 544×960 resolution on Wan-2.1/2.2 reach approximately 64K tokens, and context length roughly equals source length, the total sequence length doubles, quadrupling the O(N²) attention cost. Existing sparse attention methods (STA, SWA, VSA, Sparge, Radial) were designed for general video generation without accounting for the structural distinction between source and context token populations in ICL, and they therefore miss the opportunity to exploit the fact that context tokens contribute far less to the total attention score than source tokens. Key Idea: The core insight is that context and source tokens have measurably different saliency in the ICL attention matrix (Fig. 4–5 show source→source scores are markedly higher than source→context scores, especially in deeper layers), so context tokens can be aggressively pruned and the interaction with the remaining non-selected key blocks can be approximated via block means (0th-order Taylor expansion). The approximation error is upper-bounded by a Query sharpness metric — the variance of the coarse block-level attention distribution — making sharpness a cheap and effective routing signal for deciding which queries need full versus approximate attention. Methodology: ISA operates in five stages. Stage 1: Block-wise compression — query, key, and value tensors are mean-pooled into compressed block representations (block size 64 default), and a coarse attention map is computed from these. Stage 2: Context token pre-selection — the coarse score matrix is summed along the query dimension to score each context key block; a Top-k selection (select ratio αs, default 0.125) retains only the most salient context blocks, reducing context KV from Lctx to αs·Lctx. Stage 3: Sharpness-aware query splitting — sharpness per query block is computed as the variance of the coarse attention distribution over source keys; queries are sorted and split into a "sharp" subset (fraction 1−αf routed to FlashAttention 2/3) and a "flat" subset (fraction αf routed to the Taylor kernel). Stage 4: Decoupled kernel execution — flat queries use the block-wise 0th-order Taylor kernel, which loads per-block key and value means instead of full key-value pairs for non-selected blocks, reducing FLOPs from O(LQLKD) to O(LQD) per non-selected block; selected blocks use exact sparse attention. Stage 5: Reconstruction — outputs are scattered back and a coarse residual (scaled by γ) is added. Both forward and backward passes are implemented in Triton, making ISA fully differentiable. Three hyperparameters control sparsity: αs (context selection ratio, default 0.125), αns (no-sparsity ratio for Taylor kernel, default 0.0625), and αf (flat ratio, default 0.5). LIVEditor adds a two-stage training regime on Wan 2.2 (1.7M coarse samples → 0.089M high-quality fine-tune) and a decoupled RoPE strategy that resets positional indices independently for source and context token groups. Key Results: All experiments are on peer-reviewed ICML 2026 work, but the benchmarks cited are themselves recent preprints. On EditVerseBench: LIVEditor (ISA) scores 7.89 Quality / 20.09 Text Align / 27.19 Temporal Consistency / 24.55 Editing Quality, surpassing the previous best (EditVerse: 7.65 / 20.07 / 26.73 / 23.93) and outperforming its own full-attention twin LIVEditor (full-attn: 7.62 / 19.98 / 27.13 / 23.80). In training-free sparse attention comparison on EditVerseBench, ISA achieves 1.47x speedup over FA3 with quality metrics above all other sparse methods (Radial, Sparge, STA, SWA, VSA) and even above full attention; STA achieves 2.09x but with severe quality collapse (Quality 4.45 vs 7.78). On VIE-Bench, LIVEditor (ISA) leads across all five task types (Add, Remove, Swap, Style, Hybrid). Cross-model transfer to HunyuanVideo-1.5 (training-free, hyperparameters tuned on Wan-2.2) yields 1.39x speedup with a slight quality improvement over full attention (7.04 vs 6.89), suggesting some generalization. At the kernel level (Fig. 2), ISA reduces attention-module latency by ~60% vs. SDPA and FA2 at 131K sequence length with head dim 128 (735ms → 199ms). Combined with TeaCache temporal caching (threshold δ=0.20), overall speedup reaches 4.87x but with visible quality degradation. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The sharpness-guided query routing combined with block-mean 0th-order Taylor approximation specifically tailored to ICL token bimodality is genuinely novel versus general-purpose sparse attention methods. The 1.7M data pipeline and LIVEditor model constitute a new system. The mathematical framing (Theorem 3.1 bounding Taylor error by sharpness×intra-block variance) provides theoretical grounding not present in prior sparse attention work. Applying sparse attention to ICL video editing is a new setting, but the underlying components (pooling attention, block sparse, Top-k selection) are well-established. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations over all three hyperparameters (αs, αns, αf) are reported with dense sweeps (Fig. 9), revealing ISA is most sensitive to Flat Ratio αf and robust to the other two. Stage I vs. Stage II training is ablated (Table 3–4). Block size and source sequence length robustness are ablated (Tables 15–16). Cross-model transfer is tested on one additional backbone (HunyuanVideo-1.5, Table 17). Theorem 3.1 is formally proved (Appendix F), though the Lipschitz assumption on the softmax energy function is moderate. Error bars / multiple seeds are not reported. The benchmark evaluations rely on VLM-based scoring (Gemini/GPT) rather than human ratings, which is the field norm but introduces evaluator bias. The benchmarks (EditVerseBench, VIE-Bench, IVE-Bench) are largely concurrent preprints, some from overlapping author groups — EditVerse/EditVerseBench (Ju et al., 2025) is used both as a baseline and as the primary benchmark, a potential conflict of interest. Code is not explicitly stated as released; neither model weights nor dataset are confirmed public at time of submission. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (a built system evaluated on video editing benchmarks). - Practical: ISA is implemented with Triton kernels that run on standard NVIDIA GPUs; training uses DeepSpeed ZeRO-3 Offload, which targets multi-GPU clusters (exact GPU count and model size not stated). - Efficient: At 64K sequence length and head dim 128, ISA reduces attention latency by ~60% vs. SDPA/FA2; 1.47x end-to-end speedup over FA3 in training-free mode; time per full video editing run ≈7m09s vs. 10m30s for FA3 at default resolution. - Effective: ISA-trained LIVEditor outperforms full-attention LIVEditor on all VLM evaluation metrics across three benchmarks, suggesting the quality improvement is not merely a speedup-quality tradeoff artifact. - Scalable: Tested at up to 131K sequence length in kernel microbenchmarks; speedup grows with sequence length (log-linear) and no degradation reported, but training-scale GPU count is unstated, and 100× scale testing is not performed. No public software artifact, dataset release, or trained model checkpoint is confirmed. Strengths: - The observation that context tokens systematically contribute less to ICL video editing attention (documented empirically in Figs. 4–5 and exploited algorithmically) is a precise, non-obvious insight that motivates the design cleanly. - The sharpness proxy for Taylor approximation error is theoretically grounded (Theorem 3.1) and empirically validated (Fig. 6 shows strong positive correlation), making the query routing decision principled rather than heuristic. - ISA outperforming full attention after training (not just matching it at a lower compute cost) is a counter-intuitive and compelling empirical finding, suggesting the method acts as implicit regularization by pruning noise tokens. - The Triton implementation covering both forward and backward passes makes ISA a fully trainable, drop-in replacement — practically deployable rather than inference-only. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note exclusion of MLLM-based methods (EditVerse, UniVideo, InstructX) due to unavailability of public implementations and their distinct paradigm (massive MLLM capacity vs. efficiency focus). They acknowledge that concurrent preprints (LoVoRA, ReCo) are not included in comparisons. They note ISA performance is sensitive to Flat Ratio αf, requiring it to be set to 0.5 (a high value), limiting realized sparsity on the query side. No acknowledgment is made of the benchmark circularity risk. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Benchmark circularity: EditVerseBench is authored by a group (Ju et al.) that also appears as a baseline (EditVerse); LIVEditor is compared on a benchmark created by authors with potential publication incentives to favor ICL-paradigm methods — independent benchmark validation is absent. - VLM-based evaluation (Gemini scoring) is used as the primary quality metric without any human study, and no inter-rater reliability or calibration against human judgments is reported. - GPU count and total training compute are not disclosed; the claim that this is deployable is hard to verify without knowing whether training required 8, 64, or 256 GPUs. - The 1.7M dataset is generated using proprietary models (Gemini 2.5 Flash and Gemini 2.5 Image Preview), meaning the training distribution is bound by a closed-source generator whose biases are not characterized. - Cross-model generalization is tested on only one additional backbone (HunyuanVideo-1.5), and hyperparameters were tuned on Wan-2.2, so the 1.39x speedup result may not hold under in-distribution tuning for HunyuanVideo-1.5. - No evaluation on failure modes: e.g., what happens when source and context videos are very dissimilar or when context tokens are semantically dense and should not be pruned. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or fine-tuning ICL-based video editing systems on Wan or HunyuanVideo should read this for the ISA attention design and the sharpness-routing idea, which is practically usable and theoretically grounded, even though the evaluation setup carries circular benchmark risks that a rigorous committee would probe.
Recommended 2605.04569 PDF
#26 — Innovative (73)

When Do Diffusion Models learn to Generate Multiple Objects? (4 ▲ )

Yujin Jeong, Arnas Uselis, Iro Laina, Seong Joon Oh, Anna Rohrbach · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 4 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MOSAIC, a controlled synthetic dataset generation framework, and uses it to causally identify how training data properties — scale, concept imbalance, and compositional holdout — drive multi-object generation failures in text-to-image diffusion models. - Why it's important: Diffusion models fall below 50% accuracy on multi-object compositional tasks while exceeding 80% on single-object tasks; without causal analysis separating data-driven causes, engineering fixes for this failure remain ad hoc. - How it does it: Small (~90M-parameter) U-Net and DiT diffusion models are trained from scratch on fully parameterized synthetic 3D datasets that independently vary dataset size, concept imbalance distributions, and the number of compositionally held-out concept pairs across three tasks (Attribution, Counting, Spatial Relations). - The good: The diagonal leave-out compositional generalization protocol produces clean, quantitative characterization of when and how generalization collapses, and the encoder-disentanglement ablation is a genuine negative result that redirects attribution away from the text encoder and toward the diffusion model's binding mechanism. - The missing: All controlled experiments use one-hot structured conditioning over 128×128 synthetic rendered images rather than natural language over real photographs — the gap between this setting and the text-conditioned real-world failures that motivate the work is asserted but not experimentally bridged. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers studying compositional generalization, counting, or spatial reasoning in diffusion models; practitioners seeking actionable training improvements should note the study is diagnostic and prescriptions remain incomplete.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (80) is the strongest dimension — causal experimental design via training-from-scratch on fully controlled synthetic data, thorough ablations across architectures, sizes, distributions, holdout levels, auxiliary losses, and SD3 fine-tuning extension, code released. Non-Obviousness (80) counting's collapse-while-loss-decreases dynamics and the encoder-disentanglement negative result are genuinely surprising and redirect the field. Novelty (70) MOSAIC is the first controlled framework parameterizing three compositional dimensions for diffusion analysis, and the diagonal leave-out adaptation is novel for generative settings. Completeness (75) limitations acknowledged honestly, especially the gap from one-hot to natural-language conditioning. Resilience (65) the conditioning-modality gap is the main reviewer-attack surface, and no multiple seeds. Impact (60) diagnostic, not prescriptive — no training recipe that solves the failure, but the framework and findings will inform follow-up. Composite ≈ 0.20*70+0.25*80+0.15*80+0.15*75+0.15*65+0.10*60 = 73.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 75 Resilience 65 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Text-to-image diffusion models consistently fail on multi-object prompts: GenEval scores for state-of-the-art models drop below 50% on multi-object tasks despite exceeding 80% on single-object tasks. The failures span three dimensions — correctly counting object instances, binding attributes to the right instance, and preserving spatial relations. Despite extensive empirical documentation, the root causes are unclear. Two data-driven hypotheses are plausible: (1) models may fail to learn individual concepts reliably due to imbalanced training data (e.g., high-count phrases are rare in LAION-2B); (2) models may fail to recombine seen concepts when specific compositions were absent from training. Real-world datasets entangle both effects with visual noise, making causal analysis infeasible without controlled data. Key Idea The core insight is to cleanly separate concept generalization — whether a model that has seen a concept at least once can reliably generate it under imbalance — from compositional generalization — whether a model can recombine fully observed concepts into novel pairings — by training diffusion models from scratch on synthetically parameterized datasets. The diagonal leave-out scheme (removing 0/1/3/5/8 diagonals from a 10×10 concept-pair matrix) provides fine-grained, monotonic control over compositional difficulty while guaranteeing all individual concepts remain fully observed, enabling direct isolation of the combinatorial difficulty from the single-concept difficulty. Methodology MOSAIC generates photorealistic 3D scenes at 128×128 resolution (built on COMFORT-BALL assets) with three task-specific subsets: Attribution (sphere/cube color binding, 10×10=100 combinations), Spatial Relations (10 angular bins of 18° around a reference sphere), and Counting (1–10 spheres). Both a U-Net (~90M params, score-matching objective) and a DiT (~90M params, rectified-flow objective) are trained; conditioning is via one-hot vectors encoded with a small MLP and injected through cross-attention. Concept generalization experiments vary dataset size (2k/10k/50k/100k) under uniform and power-law-skewed distributions. Compositional generalization experiments vary holdout level (0–8 diagonals) across three dataset sizes, adding a shared color factor to Counting and Spatial Relations to form two-concept compositions. Evaluation uses task-specific discriminative classifiers (CNN for counting, ResNet for attribution/spatial relations) trained on up to 1M synthetic samples; VAE reconstruction accuracy exceeds 0.999 on all tasks, validating classifier reliability. Extensions: LoRA fine-tuning of SD3-medium on the SPEC benchmark (1.5K image-text pairs), and an object co-occurrence variant using Comfort-Car assets with realistic occlusions. Critical design decisions — avoiding occlusions in the base setting for isolation, using a top-down fixed camera, and one-hot conditioning — are justified as diagnostic choices, not practical deployment choices. Key Results Concept generalization: Attribution and Spatial Relations achieve >90% accuracy across all dataset sizes (2k–100k) and both imbalance regimes for both U-Net and DiT. Counting behaves differently: near-perfect at 2k (memorization), drops to 64% at 10k uniform and 72% skewed for U-Net, recovering to 86% (50k) and 95% (100k). Adding scene complexity to Attribution and Spatial Relations causes comparable accuracy drops at 10k (from ~99% to 80–97%), confirming that scene complexity, not concept identity, drives counting's failure. Imposing a radial grid layout raises counting accuracy from 0.655 (random, 10k, U-Net) to 0.994 (grid, 10k, U-Net), a 51-point improvement. Model size (40M/90M/200M) does not change the failure pattern. Compositional generalization: with 8 of 10 diagonals held out at 100k data, Attribution unseen accuracy is 0.61, Counting drops to 0.02, Spatial Relations drops to 0.03; with 5 diagonals held out, they are 0.61, 0.39/0.02, and 0.52/0.03 respectively across dataset sizes, establishing a clear hierarchy Attribution < Counting < Spatial Relations. Extended experiments: SD3 LoRA fine-tuning on SPEC consistently degrades counting accuracy regardless of rank (r=4/16/32) or learning rate (1e-4/1e-5), while spatial relation accuracy improves — matching controlled findings. Disentangled encoder ablation: replacing jointly trained encoder with a frozen cross-entropy-pretrained encoder yields only marginal improvement in compositional accuracy on unseen compositions, ruling out encoder composition failure as the primary cause. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. MOSAIC is the first controlled dataset generation framework explicitly parameterizing three multi-object compositional dimensions for diffusion model analysis. The empirical finding that scene complexity dominates over concept imbalance in explaining multi-object failures is genuinely new. The diagonal leave-out protocol is adapted from discriminative compositional generalization literature (Uselis et al., 2025), but its application to generative diffusion models and the resulting task-difficulty hierarchy is novel. The encoder disentanglement negative result is an important original contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough: two architectures, four dataset sizes, two distributions, five holdout levels, model sizes (40M/90M/200M), auxiliary losses (InfoNCE, cross-entropy, frozen encoder), LoRA rank and learning rate sweeps, and spatial priors — all reported. Evaluation classifiers are externally trained and validated with near-perfect accuracy on VAE reconstructions. Code is released at https://github.com/eugene6923/MOSAIC.git. However, no confidence intervals, error bars, or multiple random seeds are reported for the main accuracy figures — important given counting's non-monotonic training dynamics. Per-class accuracy and pixel-distance memorization analyses are provided in the appendix and add mechanistic credibility. The finding that the same counting collapse occurs across all model sizes strengthens the generality claim. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Training the controlled experiments requires 4×A100 GPUs for ~5 hours per model; MOSAIC dataset generation uses publicly released 3D assets, so replication is feasible. - Efficient: Models are intentionally small (90M, 128×128) as a diagnostic choice; the framework does not claim to be an efficient production system. - Effective: The findings identify data-driven failure modes causally and show that imposing a spatial prior (grid layout) substantially improves counting accuracy; they do not deliver a trained model or training recipe that solves compositional generalization. - Scalable: Controlled experiments stop at 100k samples and 90M parameters; SD3 LoRA extension at real scale shows counting fragility persists, but the compositional generalization result at natural-language-conditioned production scale is not tested. Software artifact: MOSAIC dataset generation framework is publicly released; no trained model weights are released. Strengths - Causal experimental design: training from scratch on fully controlled synthetic data enables causal attribution of failures to data properties — a level of rigor unavailable to correlation-based analyses of real datasets. - Counting's anomalous learning dynamics: the discovery that counting accuracy peaks and then collapses while training loss decreases monotonically, paired with condition embedding PCA showing collapse at 10k but separation at 100k, is a specific mechanistic finding with non-obvious implications for training and architecture design. - Encoder disentanglement negative result: demonstrating that a frozen, well-supervised disentangled encoder provides only marginal benefit for compositional generalization shifts the research target from text encoder design to the diffusion model's binding mechanism — a meaningful redirection. - Extension to realistic conditions: verifying that the controlled findings hold under SD3 fine-tuning and object co-occurrence scenarios with occlusions meaningfully strengthens external validity. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge that MOSAIC is a diagnostic benchmark, not a realistic generative benchmark — objects are simplified 3D assets with no occlusions, fixed camera, and limited visual diversity. They note that the SD3 fine-tuning experiments differ in conditioning mechanism and data scale from the controlled setting. They acknowledge the gap to real-world text-conditioned generation. These are confronted directly, which is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The most significant gap is the conditioning modality: all controlled experiments use one-hot vectors, not language tokens. The paper's framing targets real T2I failures, but the mechanistic findings (condition embedding collapse, encoder disentanglement ineffectiveness) are specific to structured one-hot conditioning — whether they transfer to CLIP or T5 embeddings is undemonstrated. - No multiple seeds or confidence intervals are reported; given the non-monotonic accuracy behavior observed in counting, variance across random initializations is material to interpreting the results. - The counting collapse mechanism is identified (embedding collapse) but not resolved — the paper shows that InfoNCE loss, cross-entropy auxiliary loss, and frozen pretrained encoders all fail to prevent collapse, leaving the root cause unexplained. - The object co-occurrence extension evaluates only object pairs; the practically important failure regime of 3+ object compositions is not tested. - This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Bottom Line A rigorous diagnostic study with causal experimental design that produces specific, falsifiable findings about data-driven limits of multi-object compositional generation — the controlled methodology and negative encoder result are genuine contributions — but the unresolved gap between one-hot synthetic conditioning and real text-to-image conditioning limits immediate applicability to practitioners.
Recommended 2605.00273 PDF
#27 — Innovative (73)

SkillOS: Learning Skill Curation for Self-Evolving Agents (21 ▲ )

Siru Ouyang, Jun Yan, Yanfei Chen, Rujun Han, Zifeng Wang, Bhavana Dalvi Mishra, Rui Meng, Chun-Liang Li, Yizhu Jiao, Kaiwen Zha, Maohao Shen, Vishy Tirumalashetty, George Lee, Jiawei Han, Tomas Pfister, Chen-Yu Lee · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SkillOS, a GRPO-based RL training recipe that teaches a dedicated "skill curator" LLM to insert, update, and delete Markdown skill files in an external repository, enabling a separate frozen agent executor to improve on streaming tasks over time. - Why it's important: LLM agents deployed across sequential task streams waste accumulated experience because current heuristic or manual skill curation approaches lack downstream executor feedback and cannot learn complex management operations like skill revision and deletion. - How it does it: Related tasks are grouped into training instances so that skill updates after early tasks are directly evaluated by their effect on later tasks in the same group, converting a long-horizon credit-assignment problem into a dense, tractable within-group RL signal with a composite reward covering task success, function-call validity, content quality, and repository compression. - The good: A trained Qwen3-8B curator outperforms zero-shot Gemini-2.5-Pro used as curator on every agentic benchmark, demonstrating that small targeted RL training outweighs raw frontier-model scale for this specific curation role. - The missing: The evaluation uses fixed-size test splits (140 ALFWorld tasks, 500 WebShop tasks) rather than an open-ended streaming deployment; whether SkillRepo degrades, bloats, or suffers harmful curation errors over thousands of tasks is entirely untested. - Read it or not: Researchers building self-evolving or continual-learning agent systems should read this; it is less valuable to those primarily interested in ALFWorld or WebShop performance per se, where the absolute numbers remain modest.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) — grouped task stream formulation cleanly converts long-horizon credit assignment into tractable within-group RL signal; the finding that an 8B trained curator beats zero-shot Gemini-2.5-Pro is concrete and surprising. Rigor (70) — 3 seeds with std reported, ablations cover main components but reward weights never ablated, no code/weights released, content-quality judge potentially gameable. Non-Obviousness (75) — the curation-as-learnable-skill framing and behavioral analyses (insert→update transition) provide genuine insight. Completeness (70) and Resilience (65) limited by absence of true streaming evaluation despite the streaming motivation. Impact (60) — no released artifact. Composite ~73.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM-based agents deployed in streaming settings — where tasks arrive sequentially — must handle each task without retaining procedural lessons from prior interactions. The dominant "one-off" paradigm wastes accumulated experience. Reusable skills extracted from trajectories provide a natural memory substrate, but three classes of prior work all fall short: (1) manually curated skill repositories (e.g., Anthropic's skills) require expert effort and cannot scale; (2) prompting/heuristic-based memory systems prescribe fixed curation operations that cannot adapt to what the executor actually needs; (3) recent RL approaches either teach agents to use skills provided by others, or optimize curation only within short task horizons where learning signals for skill update and deletion are sparse. The gap is a training recipe that learns a long-horizon curation policy from indirect, delayed executor performance feedback. Key Idea The key insight is that skill curation decisions have delayed, indirect effects — a skill inserted after task i proves its worth only when it helps task i+k. SkillOS makes this feedback dense and trainable by grouping semantically related tasks into training instances of size ~10, so that the curator's decisions on early tasks directly determine the skill context available for later tasks in the same group. This converts a credit-assignment problem that is intractable across an unbounded stream into a tractable within-group RL problem, enabling the curator to learn that a skill update is valuable not because it looks good locally, but because it causally improves executor performance on related future tasks. Methodology The system is modular: a frozen agent executor (πL) handles task execution; a trainable skill curator (πS, initialized from Qwen3-8B) manages an external SkillRepo. Each skill is a Markdown file with YAML frontmatter (name + natural-language description for retrieval) and a Markdown body (workflow, constraints, heuristics). Skill retrieval at inference time uses BM25 (top-5). Training data construction is a two-stage pipeline. Stage 1 annotates each task with 5-dimensional phrase tuples (topic, required skills, math concepts/theorems, heuristic strategies, common pitfalls) using Gemini-2.5-Pro with structured decoding. Stage 2 assembles groups via a 6-condition dependency gate: shared foundational concepts (≥1 matched concept pair, ≥1 matched skill pair), shared reasoning strategy, non-duplication (topic soft-Jaccard ≤ 0.65, overall similarity ≤ 0.85), non-triviality (overall similarity ≥ 0.30), progression (successor introduces at least one new concept or skill), and forward curriculum (difficulty delta ≥ 0). Phrase similarity uses soft-Jaccard with greedy one-to-one matching under sentence-embedding cosine (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, threshold 0.60). The training loop (Algorithm 1) samples one task group per step, initializes SkillRepo empty, then processes tasks sequentially: BM25 retrieval → frozen executor runs task → curator observes trajectory and generates structured function calls (insert_skill, update_skill, delete_skill) → SkillRepo updated → next task. The composite reward is: r = r_task + 1.0·r_fc + 0.1·r_cnt + 0.05·r_comp where r_task is average binary success over tasks 2...|G| (first task has empty repo), r_fc is the fraction of valid executed function calls, r_cnt is a Qwen3-32B judge score for skill abstraction/reusability/actionability/faithfulness, and r_comp = 1 − |SkillRepo| / |curator_input| (discourages verbatim trajectory copying). GRPO is used with group size 8, batch size 32, learning rate 1×10⁻⁶; the KL term is dropped to encourage exploration. Training runs on 16 H100 GPUs for 3 days (ALFWorld), 2.5 days (reasoning), 5 days (WebShop). Two design decisions are well-justified: the grouped formulation is directly validated by the w/o grouping ablation (−3.9 SR points). The content quality reward is validated by the w/o r_cnt ablation (−2.6 SR points). Reward weight hyperparameters (1.0, 0.1, 0.05) are stated but not ablated, leaving their sensitivity uncharacterized. Key Results On ALFWorld (140 test tasks, 6 task types), with Qwen3-8B executor: SkillOS achieves 61.2% average SR vs. 55.7% for ReasoningBank (strongest external baseline) and 47.9% No Memory, while reducing average interaction steps from 21.1 to 18.9. With Gemini-2.5-Pro executor: 80.2% SR vs. 74.3% (MemP), 14.8 steps vs. 17.7. On WebShop (500 test instructions), Qwen3-8B executor: SR 16.5% vs. 12.0% (MemP), score 40.6 vs. 35.7, steps 19.4 vs. 21.3 (WebShop absolute SRs are low across all methods). On reasoning tasks (Qwen3-8B executor): average accuracy 73.8% (SkillOS) vs. 69.6% (No Memory), with AIME24 reaching 80.0% vs. 76.0%. The most striking finding: the RL-trained 8B curator outperforms SkillOS-gemini (zero-shot Gemini-2.5-Pro as curator) on all agentic benchmarks — e.g., ALFWorld/Qwen3-8B executor: 61.2% vs. 50.7%. This holds even when Gemini-2.5-Pro is the executor. Cross-task generalization (Figure 3) shows reasoning-trained curators transfer well to agentic tasks (+13.8–14.1% on ALFWorld), while environment-specific skills (WebShop, ALFWorld) transfer less to reasoning. Baselines are contemporaneous 2025–2026 preprints (ReasoningBank, MemP); the comparison is appropriate. Results are reported as mean ± std across 3 seeds. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The grouped task stream training formulation for learning long-horizon curation policies is the primary contribution; it is a non-obvious re-framing of the credit-assignment problem in agent memory. The composite reward combining delayed outcome with intermediate quality signals is incremental but practically important. GRPO, BM25 retrieval, Markdown skill format, and the modular executor/curator separation are all established ideas applied to a new problem formulation. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover the two main design decisions (grouped training, content quality reward, compression reward), which is minimally sufficient. Standard deviations over 3 seeds are reported throughout. However, the reward weights (λ_f=1.0, λ_u=0.1, λ_c=0.05) are not ablated. The content quality judge (Qwen3-32B) is itself a pretrained model; its calibration as a training reward is not independently validated and could be gameable. The grouping pipeline hyperparameters were tuned on 200 held-out tasks — a small validation set relative to the complexity of the 6-condition gate. No code, model weights, or processed datasets are released. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.06614v1, May 2026); results have not been peer-reviewed. A minor circularity: task success during training uses LLM-as-a-judge with the same frozen executor backbone used in evaluation, sharing potential biases. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Training requires 16 H100 GPUs (2.5–5 days); once trained, inference adds one Qwen3-8B call per task for curation plus BM25 lookup — feasible on commercially available hardware for practitioners with moderate compute budgets. - Efficient: Training cost is substantial but one-time; runtime overhead per task is approximately one additional LLM call (8B scale) on top of the executor, which is modest relative to executor inference for larger models. - Effective: Gains are consistent across three benchmarks and four executor scales (Qwen3-8B/32B, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite), and the ablation confirms that both key components contribute; the evidence credibly supports the stated contribution. - Scalable: Tested on fixed evaluation splits of 140–500 tasks; the paper makes no claim about and does not test behavior as SkillRepo grows across thousands of tasks or how performance evolves over a truly long streaming deployment. The work delivers no software artifact; it is paper-form only. Strengths - The grouped task stream formulation is a clean, well-motivated solution to the credit-assignment problem in skill curation: turning delayed, stream-wide feedback into dense within-group RL signal without requiring access to future tasks at test time. - The finding that an RL-trained 8B curator outperforms zero-shot Gemini-2.5-Pro across all agentic benchmarks is concrete and surprising, establishing that curation is a learnable skill that benefits more from targeted training than from frontier-model reasoning ability alone. - Behavioral analysis (Figure 4: operation distribution over training showing insert→update transition; Figure 5: emergence of meta-strategy skills) provides genuine interpretability into what the learned policy acquires, distinguishing this from a pure empirical paper. - The cross-task generalization experiments (Figure 3) test a practically important question — can a curator trained on one task type transfer to others — and give a nuanced, quantified answer. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge three limitations in Appendix D: (1) BM25-only retrieval is a simplification that may limit skill-matching quality; joint optimization of retrieval and curation is deferred. (2) Flat Markdown skills cannot express executable procedures or hierarchical sub-skill composition. (3) The frozen executor means the curator must compensate alone for any curator-executor mismatch; joint optimization would be stronger but more expensive. The authors confront these directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The reward weights (λ_f=1.0, λ_u=0.1, λ_c=0.05) are stated but never ablated; it is unknown how sensitive results are to these choices, and the relative magnitudes (task outcome vs. content quality 10:1) are arbitrary. - Long-horizon streaming reliability is entirely untested: evaluation on 140 ALFWorld or 500 WebShop tasks is not a streaming deployment — there is no assessment of SkillRepo bloat, harmful skill accumulation, catastrophic overwrites of useful skills, or performance degradation as the repo grows. - The grouping pipeline is complex (6-condition gate, phrase encoder, 15+ hyperparameters) and was tuned on only 200 held-out tasks; its robustness to different domains and its contribution to results relative to simpler grouping alternatives is unexamined. - The compression reward uses raw token-length ratio (|SkillRepo|/|curator_input|), which rewards brevity but not information density — a curator could game this by inserting very short, low-quality skills. - WebShop absolute SRs are low across all methods (9.8–16.5% with Qwen3-8B), and the SkillOS improvement (+4.5 percentage points) overlaps with variance across runs; the practical significance of this gain is unclear. - Delete operations remain a small fraction throughout training (Figure 4); whether the few deletion decisions made by the trained curator are accurate (removing genuinely harmful skills vs. useful ones) is never measured. Bottom Line SkillOS makes a well-supported and practically motivated contribution to learned skill curation in streaming agent settings; the grouped task stream training formulation and the demonstration that targeted 8B-scale training beats frontier zero-shot curation are the most valuable takeaways for researchers and practitioners building self-evolving agent infrastructure.

#28 — Innovative (72)

Unifying Sparse Attention with Hierarchical Memory for Scalable Long-Context LLM Serving

Zihan Zhao, Baotong Lu, Shengjie Lin, Yizou Chen, Jing Liu, Yanqi Zhang, Ziming Miao, Ming-Chang Yang, Haiying Shen, Qi Chen, Fan Yang · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Spin, a sparse-attention-aware LLM inference framework that co-designs a unified five-stage execution pipeline with a locality-aware, hierarchically-managed KV cache to close the gap between the theoretical and realized performance of dynamic sparse attention. - Why it's important: Dynamic sparse attention algorithms promise to reduce the GPU memory and bandwidth bottleneck of long-context decoding, but existing implementations are ad-hoc, per-algorithm prototypes that fail to translate algorithmic sparsity into system-level throughput — and can even underperform optimized dense serving at low load. - How it does it: Spin introduces a partition abstraction that decouples algorithm-defined sparsity granularities from hardware-efficient paged memory, then builds a GPU-friendly bucketed LRU manager with dynamic buffer sizing and two-level demand-allocated page tables on top of that abstraction. - The good: Spin delivers 2.34–5.66× higher online throughput than vLLM on A100 at high request rates, reduces metadata HBM consumption by 49–78×, and improves per-token decode latency by up to 58% over the original sparse-attention implementations — all while supporting three structurally different sparse algorithms through a shared runtime. - The missing: The paper evaluates serving efficiency but not model accuracy under the sparse attention approximations; end-to-end accuracy degradation on LongBench-v2 / LongGenBench is never reported, so the throughput gains are unanchored to quality. - Read it or not: Essential reading for ML systems researchers and inference engineers building or evaluating long-context serving stacks; practitioners who only care about algorithmic accuracy improvements can skip it.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Impact (80) — weighted 30% — drives the score: a real, integrated 7k C++ + 3k Python artifact on vLLM, three sparse algorithms unified, 2.3–5.7× online throughput on A100 measured against current strong baselines (vLLM, vLLM-Offload, LServe), and 49–78× metadata reduction. Rigor (70) is solid for a systems paper — multi-model, multi-GPU-generation, online + offline, with informative incremental ablations; docked for no error bars, no code release URL, and the 5× buffer heuristic and 64-bucket count lacking sensitivity analysis. Non-Obviousness (70) — the partition abstraction unifying three structurally different algorithms and the GPU-resident bucketed-LRU approximation are non-trivial design moves. Resilience (65) and Completeness (65) — the most material gap is the missing accuracy verification under sparse approximation, and the million-token framing is not experimentally supported beyond 120K. Weighted: 0.15*70+0.15*70+0.10*70+0.15*65+0.15*65+0.30*80 ≈ 72.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 65 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Long-context LLM serving is jointly bottlenecked by GPU memory capacity (KV caches grow linearly with sequence length) and GPU memory bandwidth (every decode step reads all historical KV state). Dynamic sparse attention — which selects only a query-dependent subset of the KV cache each step and keeps the full cache in CPU DRAM — offers a principled remedy, but the algorithmic savings do not flow through to system throughput for two reasons. First, sparse algorithms are diverse and operate at incompatible granularities (fixed blocks, variable-size clusters, individual tokens), so existing support has fragmented into per-algorithm prototypes that cannot share kernels, schedulers, or memory managers. Second, keeping the full KV cache in CPU memory means that retrieving fine-grained, non-contiguous KV subsets over PCIe becomes a new bottleneck — irregular transfers underutilize PCIe bandwidth, and without caching, temporal locality across decoding steps is wasted. The authors quantify the gap by constructing an analytical ideal-sparse-serving envelope (Belady-optimal caching + peak PCIe utilization) and showing that ShadowKV, a state-of-the-art sparse method, incurs 2× higher retrieval overhead than the ideal at batch size 1 and fails to scale to large batch sizes at all. Key Idea: The central insight is that all dynamic sparse attention algorithms share the same five-operation execution pattern (Index → Select → Offload ↔ Retrieve → Attention) and differ only in partition granularity — the logical unit of KV organization and retrieval. By elevating partition to a first-class abstraction that sits above hardware-efficient pages, Spin can expose a clean algorithm customization interface (Index and Select) while keeping all data movement (Offload and Retrieve) under unified, optimized control. Temporal locality across decoding steps — the observation that critical token sets overlap between consecutive steps — then motivates a GPU-resident LRU cache over partitions, approximated with bounded-range timestamps to avoid expensive global sorting. Methodology: Spin is implemented in 7k lines of C++/CUDA and 3k lines of Python on top of vLLM. The five-stage decode pipeline is: (1) Index (prefill + periodic decode): consumes prompt KV cache, produces partition specifications and per-partition summaries (e.g., mean keys for ShadowKV, k-means centroids for RetroInfer); (2) Offload: materializes partitioned KV in CPU pinned memory asynchronously; (3) Select (per decode step): scores all partition summaries against the current query and returns top-k partition indices; (4) Retrieve: checks GPU residency, evicts cold pages via bucketed LRU, and issues warp-based vectorized copy kernels to transfer missing partitions over PCIe; (5) Attention: runs FlashAttention (or a custom kernel for algorithms requiring it) over the retrieved subset. Three critical design decisions: (1) Head-wise physical pages — different attention heads attend to different critical tokens, so Spin allocates per-head pages rather than colocating all heads in one page, avoiding unnecessary PCIe traffic. This decision is well-justified by prior observations on head-wise sparsity patterns. (2) Bucketed LRU with 64 timestamp buckets — because sparse attention accesses partitions in bulk per step, strict recency ordering within a batch is unnecessary; approximating LRU with a small histogram scan enables GPU-parallel eviction. The choice of 64 buckets is empirically motivated but the sensitivity to this hyperparameter is not analyzed. (3) Demand-allocated two-level page tables — pre-allocating worst-case flat arrays for head-wise, long-context serving would consume tens to hundreds of GB of HBM; Spin instead maintains a small static top-level directory on GPU and allocates second-level segments from a bounded GPU pool only when physical pages are assigned, so metadata scales with active working set. The buffer elasticity policy allocates a minimum of 5× mandatory pages as the buffering budget, a threshold validated empirically across tasks and algorithms. Key Results: On online serving with Qwen3-14B on a single A100 (LongBench-v2, 1.5 req/s): Spin-ShadowKV achieves 2.34×, Spin-RetroInfer 3.80×, and Spin-SeerAttention 3.49× higher output-token throughput than vLLM. On LongGenBench (1.5 req/s, A100): throughput gains reach 2.62–5.66× over vLLM. At 4 req/s on B200, gains are 1.66–3.72× over vLLM. Spin supports 2.5–3.3× larger average batch sizes than vLLM and vLLM-Offload. TTFT is 7–9× lower than vLLM at 0.5 req/s (due to reduced queuing from higher throughput), while TPOT is 14–107% higher than Spin-ShadowKV depending on the sparse algorithm (retrieval budget differences). In offline decode, Spin supports 4–8× larger max batch sizes than baselines, translating to 1.34–3.49× higher throughput over vLLM. Against the original sparse algorithm implementations directly (offline, controlled setting): Spin reduces per-token decode latency by 21–47% at batch size 1 and 8.4–58% at peak batch size, yielding up to 2.39× throughput improvement. Hierarchical metadata reduces HBM metadata consumption by 13.9–17.4× (two-level indexing alone) and a further 3.5–4.5× by offloading selected tables to CPU, totaling 49–78× over baseline. Bucketed LRU raises decode throughput by 49% over mandatory-only caching. Context lengths tested: 16K, 32K, 64K, and 120K. The B200 at small batch sizes shows weaker advantage because its higher compute and HBM bandwidth relatively alleviate the full-attention bottleneck that Spin exploits. Baselines (vLLM, vLLM-Offload, LServe) are current and competitive; LServe is a 2025 GPU-only sparse serving system. SeerAttention-R is evaluated only on Qwen3-14B because it requires task-specific training and larger-model variants are not publicly available. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (primary), with elements of NEW METHOD in the bucketed LRU kernel and two-level demand-allocated page table applied to head-wise sparse serving. The unification of sparse attention algorithms under a shared execution pipeline is a genuine systems contribution; the individual components (LRU replacement, multi-level page tables, GPU–CPU KV offloading) are established OS and systems concepts applied purposefully to this new problem setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation studies are present and informative: buffer management is decomposed into three incremental configurations (base offload, mandatory-only, full bucketed LRU), cache-size impact on hit ratio is measured across algorithms and tasks, and metadata HBM cost is broken down by component. Multiple models (14B, 32B, 70B), two GPU architectures (A100, B200), two structurally different benchmarks, and three sparse algorithms are tested, providing reasonable coverage. No error bars, confidence intervals, or statistical significance tests are reported — typical for systems papers but limiting for claims about precise speedup magnitudes. No code repository or model weights are mentioned as released. The exclusion of LServe from online experiments (scheduler incompatibility) limits head-to-head comparison in the most practically relevant setting. The 5× buffer heuristic is empirically set but the sensitivity analysis covers only buffer-to-mandatory ratios across algorithms, not across different context lengths or model sizes systematically. Deployability (SYSTEMS profile): - Practical: Runs on standard data-center A100 and B200 GPUs with PCIe Gen4/Gen5 links and commodity CPU DRAM; no exotic hardware required. - Efficient: Metadata HBM overhead is reduced 49–78×; warp-based vectorized copy kernels saturate PCIe bandwidth; bucketed LRU replacement runs entirely on GPU without host-side synchronization; total implementation overhead is 7k C++ + 3k Python on vLLM. - Effective: Spin demonstrably improves end-to-end serving throughput and latency; however, accuracy under the sparse attention approximation is not re-evaluated in the paper — the serving-efficiency gains are real, but whether the quality/throughput tradeoff point is acceptable for a given deployment is left to the reader. - Scalable: Tested up to 120K context length and 4-GPU tensor-parallel configurations; claims generalize to longer contexts by design of the metadata system, but no experiment beyond 120K is reported, leaving million-token context windows (the stated motivation) unvalidated. Spin delivers a software artifact: a modified vLLM runtime with C++/CUDA extensions. No release URL is provided. Strengths: - The ideal sparse-serving envelope construction (Equations 1–3, Figure 1–2) is a crisp analytical tool that precisely frames the problem and allows the paper to measure how much of the theoretical opportunity each design decision captures — a methodologically useful contribution independent of the system itself. - The partition abstraction genuinely unifies three structurally different algorithms (block-based ShadowKV, cluster-based RetroInfer, learned-projection SeerAttention-R) behind a single runtime with only ~10–20 lines of algorithm-specific code per integration (per Figure 4), demonstrating real generality. - The two-level demand-allocated page table reduces metadata HBM by 49–78× — an often-overlooked but critical bottleneck that would block the system from scaling to the large batch sizes where gains are largest. - Evaluation breadth — two GPU generations, three model scales, two workload types with different input/output length ratios, and both online and offline regimes — is significantly more thorough than typical inference systems papers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that LServe is excluded from online experiments because its scheduler does not support online request arrivals, and that SeerAttention-R is restricted to Qwen3-14B because it requires algorithm-specific training that has not been extended to larger models. They note that LServe achieves 2.5–29% lower prefill latency by applying sparse prefill (orthogonal to Spin), and that Spin's TPOT is higher than vLLM due to larger per-step decode batches and GPU–CPU retrieval. On B200 at small batch sizes Spin underperforms vLLM — authors attribute this to B200's higher compute and HBM bandwidth reducing the bottleneck that Spin exploits. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most significant omission is the absence of any end-to-end accuracy evaluation: the paper assumes the sparse attention algorithms maintain model quality, but never verifies that Spin's serving path (including its caching and retrieval decisions) does not degrade output quality relative to full-attention serving on LongBench-v2 or LongGenBench tasks. This matters because retrieval errors (cache evictions of critical partitions) could silently degrade generation quality without appearing in throughput metrics. The 5× mandatory-to-buffer heuristic is empirically derived from a limited set of tasks and algorithms; it is unclear whether it holds under adversarial or out-of-distribution access patterns (e.g., long-range dependencies that span more than 64 decoding steps). The paper tests up to 4 GPUs and 120K context but positions the work as targeting "hundreds of thousands or even millions of tokens" — this extrapolation is unsupported by experiment. The bucketed LRU 64-bucket parameter receives no sensitivity analysis. Finally, energy/power measurements are absent, which is relevant for data-center deployment decisions. Bottom Line: Spin makes a concrete, measurable contribution to the long-context LLM serving stack — any team running sparse attention inference at scale on GPU–CPU hierarchies will find the partition abstraction and bucketed LRU design directly actionable — but the absence of accuracy verification means practitioners must validate quality preservation before deployment.
Recommended 2604.26837 PDF
#29 — Innovative (72)

Exploring the Efficiency of 3D-Stacked AI Chip Architecture for LLM Inference with Voxel

Yiqi Liu, Noelle Crawford, Michael Wang, Jilong Xue, Jian Huang · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Voxel, a compiler-aware, event-driven end-to-end simulation framework for 3D-stacked AI chips (DRAM banks stacked atop AI cores via TSVs) that enables hardware/software co-exploration for LLM inference workloads. - Why it's important: No commodity 3D AI chips exist yet, but industry is actively building them; without a simulation tool that captures their distributed, non-uniform memory architecture and compiler interactions, architects are flying blind on design choices that span a 1.84× performance gap. - How it does it: Voxel translates ML compiler execution plans into an event dependency graph, then simulates AI cores (Scale-sim v3), NoC links (ORION 2.0), and distributed DRAM banks (Ramulator 2.0) concurrently, accelerating DRAM simulation via XOR-based trace fingerprinting that achieves a 99.91% cache hit rate. - The good: Validation against a Graphcore IPU Mk2 emulator shows 0.24%–6.8% error across five LLM/vision models, and the exploration uncovers actionable, non-obvious findings — e.g., naively scaling DRAM bandwidth past ~10 TB/s without software-aware tensor placement actually plateaus or degrades performance due to row-buffer conflicts. - The missing: The paper evaluates only a single batch size (32) and sequence length (2048), leaving decode throughput at larger batch sizes — the regime where memory-bound behavior changes character — uncharacterized. - Read it or not: Essential reading for computer architects designing or evaluating next-generation AI accelerators with 3D-integrated DRAM; less relevant to ML practitioners with no hardware co-design involvement.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (75) is the dominant contributor — Voxel fills a genuine gap (no prior open-source 3D AI chip simulator with compiler-controlled tensor-to-bank mapping) and is committed to open-source release; non-obvious empirical findings (bandwidth plateau, compute-shift dominance, core-group sweet spot at 8) directly inform real chip design decisions. Novelty (70) strong — first joint model of distributed non-uniform DRAM, compiler-controlled placement, and multiple compute paradigms. Non-Obviousness (75) high — the XOR trace coalescing trick (99.91% hit rate, 1100× reuse) is genuinely clever, and the empirical findings overturn naive intuitions. Rigor (70) above average for a simulator paper — validation against Graphcore IPU emulator at 0.24%–6.8% error is the strongest reproducibility signal in the cluster, and the authors honestly expose the SRAM-vs-DRAM emulator discrepancy. Completeness (70) and Resilience (70) good — limitations (thermal model simplification, single batch size) are acknowledged proportionately. Weighted: 0.15*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.30*75 = 71.75 → 72.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM inference is bottlenecked by memory bandwidth: weight tensors for 70B-parameter models must be streamed from DRAM each decode step, and conventional 2.5D-packaged chips (H100, TPU) cannot scale bandwidth proportionally with compute because pin count scales with die perimeter, not area. 3D packaging via TSVs scales bandwidth with die area (up to 400 GB/s per 0.02 mm²) by stacking DRAM banks directly atop AI cores.
