However, this architecture introduces two new problems absent from 2.5D designs: (1) each TSV bus is dedicated to one DRAM bank, so a row-buffer conflict stalls that bus entirely rather than being masked by bus-sharing across banks; and (2) cores access distributed DRAM modules with non-uniform latency, creating serialization hazards when multiple cores share a tensor in a distant bank. No existing open-source simulator handles distributed non-uniform DRAM access, custom compute paradigms, and compiler-controlled tensor-to-bank mapping simultaneously, making it impossible to reason about 3D AI chip efficiency without Voxel or an equivalent tool. Key Idea The central insight is that the performance of a 3D AI chip is not determined by any single hardware parameter in isolation, but by the interaction among compute paradigm, tile-to-core mapping, tensor-to-bank placement, and NoC topology — and that the only way to expose this interaction faithfully is to model the chip's event graph at request granularity while allowing the ML compiler to inject its decisions before simulation. Voxel's key technical trick is DRAM trace coalescing: rather than replaying 500 million memory requests for a 30B-parameter model (weeks of simulation time), it fingerprints per-channel access traces with bitwise XOR of successive addresses, which encodes row/column transition patterns that fully determine DRAM timing, and reuses cached timing results for structurally identical traces. Methodology Voxel operates in three phases. First, a software interface accepts three primitives — `compute(op_tile, core_id)`, `copy_data(src, dst)`, and `sync()` — through which an ML compiler specifies tiling, placement, and compute paradigm. Compound collectives (allReduce, reduceScatter, allGather) are built from these. Second, Voxel constructs a directed event-dependency graph and traverses it chronologically, dispatching events to component simulators: Scale-sim v3 for systolic arrays and SRAM, Ramulator 2.0 for per-channel DRAM request queues, and ORION 2.0 for NoC power/latency. Computation tiles with identical shapes share cached results. DRAM requests are processed with a priority queue per channel; structurally equivalent traces reuse cached latencies with an N=32 request warm-up window for divergent blocks. Third, thermal enforcement adjusts core frequency proportionally when aggregate power density exceeds 0.7 W/mm². Design space exploration uses multi-level area-constrained coordinate descent to approximate the Pareto-optimal frontier across 11 design dimensions. Critical design decisions: (1) the XOR-based trace coalescing is well-justified by the highly repetitive operator structure of transformer models; (2) using Scale-sim v3 for core simulation is appropriate but delegates accuracy of systolic array behavior to a separate tool; (3) the thermal model is explicitly simplified (uniform power density cap, no spatial thermal network), which the authors acknowledge limits fidelity for thermal-constrained scenarios. Key Results All experiments use a default configuration of 256 cores, 32×32 systolic arrays, 12 TB/s DRAM bandwidth, 2D mesh NoC, and 2 MB SRAM/core on a ≤850 mm² die, testing Llama2-13B, Gemma2-27B, OPT-30B, Llama3-70B, and DiT-XL. Compute paradigm: compute-shift outperforms SPMD by 46.73% on prefill and dataflow by 17.74%; across all paradigms the max gap is 1.84×; SPMD's NoC overhead reaches 49.08% of total prefill time. Tile-to-core mapping: dimension-ordered mapping reduces latency by up to 57.48% over sequential mapping on mesh NoC for NoC-bound workloads; all three topologies (mesh, torus, all-to-all) converge to similar performance under dimension-ordered mapping. DRAM placement: row-buffer conflicts reach 43.35% of decode latency at 16 TB/s under uniform placement; software-aware placement reduces this overhead by 80.68% on average and keeps conflict overhead ≤14.8% even at ultra-high bandwidth. Core groups: grouping 8 cores and using a hardware request tracker improves 1,024-core decode performance by up to 58% (42% average); group sizes above 8 yield negligible additional benefit. SRAM: benefit saturates at 8 MB/core for memory-bound decode; 32× SRAM scaling delivers only 35.7% prefill improvement. Energy: scaling DRAM bandwidth improves decode energy efficiency by reducing static energy; excessive core scaling degrades energy efficiency because static power doubles from 512→1024 cores while decode latency barely decreases. Baselines are state-of-the-art for compute paradigms (SPMD/XLA, dataflow/SambaNova, compute-shift/WaferLLM) but the comparison is simulation-internal, not against measured real hardware. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. Voxel is the first simulator to jointly model distributed non-uniform DRAM access, compiler-controlled tensor-to-bank mapping, and multiple compute paradigms for a 3D AI chip architecture. The software-aware tensor-to-bank placement policy and core-group hardware synchronization mechanism are new design proposals. The empirical findings (e.g., DRAM bandwidth scaling plateau, compute-shift dominance, core-group sweet spot at size 8) are non-obvious and not derivable from prior simulators. The XOR trace coalescing is a genuinely clever engineering contribution, not a standard technique. Rigor & Reproducibility Validation against real silicon (via Graphcore IPU Mk2 emulator) is the strongest reproducibility signal: error of 0.24%–6.8% across five models is credible. Ablation structure is thorough: each design dimension is swept independently with the others held at the default configuration, giving clean counterfactual readings. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported, which is typical for deterministic cycle-accurate simulation but means sensitivity to configuration assumptions is not quantified. The paper commits to open-sourcing Voxel but no URL or repository is provided in this preprint (arXiv:2604.26821v1). The thermal model is a known simplification. One methodological concern: the validation emulator uses SRAM to emulate DRAM banks, which structurally eliminates row-buffer conflicts; the authors compensate by replaying DRAM traces on Ramulator separately, but this is a two-step validation rather than a single end-to-end ground truth. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — Voxel is a built simulation tool evaluated through deployment on LLM workloads. - Practical: Voxel runs on a standard multi-core server; the paper reports that naïve explicit simulation of a 30B-parameter model would take weeks, but trace coalescing (99.91% hit rate) makes it tractable on a 64-thread server, though exact runtimes are not stated. - Efficient: Simulation cost is dominated by the number of unique DRAM access patterns, not total request count; for transformer workloads with 40+ repeated layers and 64+ DRAM channels each pattern repeats ~2,560 times, making the approach highly efficient at LLM scale. - Effective: Validation at 0.24%–6.8% error against an IPU-based emulator demonstrates Voxel accurately models end-to-end LLM inference timing; the emulator approximation (SRAM for DRAM) is partially corrected by trace replay. - Scalable: Tested up to 1,024 cores and 12–16 TB/s DRAM bandwidth within a single die; multi-die or wafer-scale configurations are not evaluated, and simulation cost at 10× core count is not characterized. Voxel will be released as a software artifact (open source, code not yet available). Strengths - The DRAM trace coalescing mechanism is an original and well-engineered solution to a genuine scalability wall: a single insight (XOR-encoded transition patterns, not absolute addresses, determine DRAM timing) enables a 1,100× result reuse factor that makes full-LLM simulation tractable. - The simulator architecture cleanly separates compiler decisions from hardware simulation, enabling true software/hardware co-design — a structural advantage no prior open-source tool offers for 3D AI chips. - The core-group hardware mechanism is a concrete, low-overhead design proposal (a simple request tracker) that directly addresses the counter-intuitive finding that adding cores can reduce DRAM bandwidth utilization. - Validation methodology is unusually honest: the authors expose and account for the systematic SRAM-vs-DRAM discrepancy in the emulator rather than reporting raw emulator numbers as ground truth. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify thermal modeling as simplified: Voxel uses a single power-density threshold rather than a spatiotemporal thermal network, and they commit to extending this in future work. They note that no commodity 3D AI chip exists for direct validation, requiring the emulator workaround. The paper does not claim Voxel captures all architectural details of any specific future chip. These limitations are stated directly and proportionately. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single batch size (32) and sequence length (2048) throughout all experiments: the prefill/decode trade-off space, and especially the throughput-vs-latency frontier, is not characterized across batch sizes. This is the dimension most relevant to inference serving operators. - The Pareto frontier exploration (Figure 7) identifies optimal configurations but does not report how long the coordinate descent search takes — making it unclear whether the tool can iterate fast enough to be practically useful during chip design cycles. - Energy modeling relies on analytical component models (OpenRAM, ORION 2.0), not measured silicon; the energy conclusions (e.g., scaling cores degrades efficiency) are plausible but unvalidated. - The comparison of compute paradigms uses "SOTA approaches" for each paradigm's mapping strategy, but different paradigms have different implementation maturity; the 1.84× gap may partially reflect unequal optimization effort across paradigms rather than intrinsic architectural differences. - The thermal constraint (0.7 W/mm²) is applied uniformly across the chip, but real 3D dies have local hot spots; the effect of spatial thermal variation on design recommendations is unknown. Bottom Line Voxel fills a real and urgent gap — no open-source tool could previously model 3D-stacked AI chip efficiency at compiler granularity — and its empirical findings (bandwidth saturation plateau, compute-shift dominance, core-group threshold at 8) are actionable and non-obvious, making this a high-value read for architects working on next-generation AI accelerator design.
Search Results 2604.26821 PDF
#30 — Innovative (72)

Replication in Graph Partitioning and Scheduling Problems

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a comprehensive theoretical and empirical analysis of replication (redundant assignment of operations to multiple processors) in hypergraph partitioning and BSP DAG scheduling, proving new hardness results and developing a practical three-component scheduling heuristic. - Why it's important: Real parallel workloads for MoE LLM inference and sparse matrix-vector multiplication routinely pay unnecessary communication costs because standard partitioning and scheduling models forbid replication — quantifying this gap and providing practical tools to close it directly impacts deployable systems. - How it does it: For partitioning, two ILP formulations (ILP/D allowing duplication to ≤2 processors; ILP/R allowing unlimited replication) are solved with a commercial solver; for scheduling, a local-search heuristic adds three replication strategies — batch replication, superstep merging, and superstep replication — on top of a strong non-replicating baseline. - The good: For P=2, all partitioning ILPs are solved to optimality and show 23–63% mean communication cost reduction across SpMV and MoE benchmarks, including complete elimination of communication on 5 instances; scheduling heuristic improvements of 12–23% at P=8 are cross-validated as near-optimal by ILP on small DAGs. - The missing: No competing replication-aware scheduling algorithm (e.g., Ahmad & Kwok 1998) is compared, and no scalable partitioning heuristic is provided for the large graphs that matter most in practice — leaving the production-scale question unanswered. - Read it or not: Researchers working on distributed MoE serving, SpMV parallelization, or BSP scheduling theory should read this; practitioners needing replication-aware partitioning heuristics for industrial-scale graphs will need to wait for follow-on work.
Why this rank: Scored using Theoretical profile given the prominence of the inapproximability results. Novelty (75) is strong — the finite-factor inapproximability result for partitioning with replication via balanced vertex separator reduction is clean and tight, and the MoE hypergraph benchmark is genuinely new. Non-Obviousness (75) reflects the surprising hardness jump and the counter-intuitive finding that superstep merging (not superstep replication) drives improvement. Rigor (75) is solid — open-source code, proofs with appendix details, ILP-validated heuristic quality on small DAGs — but no comparison against Ahmad & Kwok or Bozdag replication-aware baselines. Completeness (70) and Resilience (65) are credible: authors directly confront the small-scale ILP, P=O(1) assumption, and missing scalable partitioning heuristic. Impact (55) is moderate — released artifacts and benchmarks, but no production-scale partitioning heuristic. Composite ~72.
theoretical Novelty 75 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Parallel execution of large computations requires distributing operations across processors while minimizing inter-processor communication — a fundamental tension in scientific computing, ML inference, and big data. Standard hypergraph partitioning and DAG scheduling models assume each operation is assigned to exactly one processor. This assumption is mathematically convenient but practically wrong: re-executing an operation on multiple processors (replication) can eliminate several communication steps at the cost of modest extra compute. Distributed LLM inference on MoE architectures, SpMV parallelization, and sparse triangular solvers all encounter this setting. Prior work on replication in scheduling exists but is fragmented across different models; for hypergraph partitioning, no prior work explicitly studies the replication extension. No comprehensive study spanning theory, exact computation, and experiments across diverse real-world workloads has been conducted. Key Idea The central theoretical insight is that graph partitioning with replication of cost 0 is equivalent to finding a balanced vertex separator — a connection that immediately explains the dramatic jump in hardness (from O(log n)-approximable without replication to inapproximable to any finite factor with replication) and provides the reduction path. For scheduling, the key algorithmic insight is that the BSP h-relation cost structure means individual greedy communication-to-replication swaps often get stuck at local minima where multiple bottleneck communications must be removed simultaneously; the advanced heuristic escapes these by treating batches of bottleneck communications and entire superstep merges as atomic moves. Methodology For hypergraph partitioning, two ILP formulations extend the standard binary assignment formulation. ILP/D introduces a per-node duplication variable z_v ∈ {0,1} and adds O(P²·κ) constraints to handle coverage semantics under replication, limiting each node to ≤2 processors. ILP/R introduces per-pin-per-processor binary variables z_{(v,e),p} allowing unlimited replication, at the cost of a variable count scaling with κ. The commercial solver COPT is used with a 5-hour time limit on an AMD EPYC 7763 with 1 TB RAM. Partitioning benchmarks include: (1) moe-8 — hypergraphs derived from 8-tuple expert co-occurrence patterns of Qwen3-235B and DeepSeek R1 on MMLU (80–241 nodes, 125–126 hyperedges, 1000–1008 pins per layer, first 5 expert layers); (2) moe-2 — same but using pairwise co-occurrences (80–239 nodes, 500–502 hyperedges); (3) spmv-fg and spmv-rn — fine-grained and row-net models from SuiteSparse matrices (92–494 nodes). For DAG scheduling in the BSP model with parameters P, g (inverse bandwidth), L (synchronization cost), the baseline is a BSPg list scheduler plus hill-climbing local search from Papp et al. [44]. The advanced heuristic iterates three moves: batch replication removes all bottleneck communications saturating the maximum h-relation cost simultaneously; superstep merging collapses consecutive superstep pairs by replicating necessary ancestors recursively; superstep replication copies all nodes of one superstep onto a second processor. The objective is to reduce total BSP cost (compute + g·h-relation + L per superstep). Scheduling benchmarks: HyperDAG database (52 DAGs, 1K–100K nodes), PSDD circuits (29 DAGs, 2.5K–175K nodes), SpTRSV (40 DAGs, 1K–150K nodes). On the theoretical side: Theorem 4.1 proves graph partitioning with replication NP-hard to approximate to any finite factor (reduction from balanced vertex separator via balanced complete bipartite subgraph, requiring a novel gadget for arbitrary ε ∈ (0,1)). Theorem 4.2 extends to n^{2-δ} additive hardness. Lemma 4.3 shows BSP scheduling with replication on chain DAGs is polynomial, NP-hard on in-trees and 2-layer DAGs. Theorem 4.5 proves ETH-hardness of approximating surplus cost (BSP cost minus n/P) to n^{1/(log log n)^δ} via reduction from smallest k-edge subgraph. Key Results Partitioning (P=2, all solved to ILP optimality): mean cost reductions are 59.63% (spmv-fg), 41.31% (spmv-rn), 23.93% (moe-8), 63.14% (moe-2); 5 instances reduced to cost 0. Partitioning (P=4, often not solved to optimality): 65.22%, 21.93%, 16.99%, 42.49%. Cost reduction grows substantially with ε: for spmv-rn at P=2, from 11.79% at ε=1.25% to 56.32% at ε=5%. Scheduling advanced heuristic (P=8, g=4, L=20): 19.17% (hdb), 23.13% (psdd), 11.61% (sptrsv) mean cost reduction over a strong non-replicating baseline; for g=16: 40.97%/56.49%; for L=400: 58.17%/34.53%. Improvement scales monotonically with P across all datasets. The basic heuristic contributes 0.1–6.3% improvement; the advanced heuristic adds 8–52 percentage points. Ablation shows batch replication and superstep merging each contribute substantially; superstep replication is marginal (≤1% additional). Scheduling ILP validation on 40–80 node DAGs gives 12.99% (P=2) and 21.08% (P=4) optimal reductions, closely matching advanced heuristic performance on larger instances. Neither ILP/D nor ILP/R dominates: ILP/R is generally stronger but ILP/D consistently outperforms on moe-8. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD (two ILP formulations for hypergraph partitioning with replication; three-component BSP scheduling heuristic with replication), NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (moe-8 and moe-2 hypergraph benchmarks from MoE profiling), NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (first comprehensive quantification of replication benefits across partitioning and scheduling), NEW FORMAL RESULT (finite-factor inapproximability for partitioning with replication; surplus cost hardness for BSP scheduling). The inapproximability result is genuinely novel and strong — replication converts an O(log n)-approximable problem into one where even constant approximation is NP-hard. The MoE benchmark and the scheduling heuristic are applications of known algorithmic paradigms (ILP, local search) to a new and practically relevant setting, rather than fundamental algorithmic advances. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is released open-source (https://github.com/Algebraic-Programming/OneStopParallel); MoE benchmark data and experimental results are in the artifacts repository. Proofs are sketched in the main text with full details in appendix; they appear technically sound for the partitioning results. The ILP-based scheduling validation against optimal on tiny graphs (12.99% vs ~13%) provides meaningful calibration of heuristic quality. Ablation studies cover both individual component activation (Table 4) and incremental addition (Table 14). However: no competing replication-aware algorithm is compared in scheduling; the moe-8/moe-2 instances are very small (80–241 nodes), far from production MoE scale; for P=4 partitioning, the ILP often fails to prove optimality within 5 hours, and the reported solutions may not reflect true optimum ratios; no significance tests or multiple seeds are reported (both ILP and the deterministic heuristic produce single-run results). The surplus cost inapproximability theorem (Theorem 4.5) holds equally with and without replication — it characterizes BSP complexity generally rather than the impact of replication specifically. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile) - Practical: The scheduling heuristic runs on standard hardware and scales to 175K-node DAGs; it requires only an existing BSP schedule as input. The partitioning ILPs require a commercial solver (COPT) and are limited to P≤4 and ~500 nodes within 5 hours — not practical for production-scale partitioning. - Efficient: Scheduling heuristic runtime is not quantified but inherits the baseline scheduler's scaling; ILP solving takes up to 5 hours for P=4 instances with ~200–500 nodes. The advanced heuristic has cubic worst-case complexity in superstep count but is reported to work on 175K-node instances. - Effective: Scheduling heuristic improvements are consistent with ILP-optimal on tiny graphs. Partitioning improvements at P=2 are provably optimal. The moe-8 results (17–24%) likely understate the benefit at full model scale due to isolated-node removal and the use of only first-layer data. - Scalable: Scheduling heuristic shows consistent improvement percentages across medium/large/huge subsets of hdb (1K–100K nodes). Partitioning ILPs do not scale; no large-scale partitioning heuristic is provided. The paper does not test P beyond 16 for scheduling or beyond 8 for partitioning (where ILP fails entirely). Software artifact: C++ implementation in OneStopParallel; new MoE hypergraph benchmarks. Strengths - The finite-factor inapproximability result for graph partitioning with replication is clean and tight — it reduces to a fundamental combinatorial problem (balanced vertex separator) and uses a non-obvious argument about arbitrary ε that distinguishes it from prior hardness results requiring specific separator sizes. - The moe-8 benchmark derived from real Qwen3-235B and DeepSeek R1 expert access profiles on MMLU is a timely contribution directly relevant to LLM deployment, and will be useful to the community independently of this paper's results. - The identification that superstep merging (not superstep replication) is the key driver of improvement beyond the basic heuristic is counter-intuitive and informs future heuristic design — superstep replication adds ≤1% despite its conceptual appeal. - The cross-validation of scheduling heuristic quality against ILP optima on tiny graphs (12.99% vs 12.33% mean cost reduction) provides credibility to the heuristic results on large graphs where optimality cannot be verified. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note: (1) partitioning ILPs are limited to small graphs (P≤4, ~500 nodes) and fail to prove optimality at P=8; (2) for P=4, replicating ILPs sometimes do not find improvements within 5 hours; (3) isolated experts in MoE graphs are discarded, requiring future work on more complete hypergraph models; (4) developing scalable heuristics for replication in partitioning is flagged as the most important future direction. The paper confronts these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The scheduling experiments compare only against the authors' own non-replicating scheduler [44], not against Ahmad & Kwok [2] or Bozdag et al. [7], which explicitly study replication in communication-delay models. It is unknown whether the proposed heuristic outperforms existing replication-aware methods or simply adds replication capability to a strong baseline. - MoE instances are tiny (80–241 nodes, 5 first layers only); Qwen3-235B has 94 expert layers with 128 experts each, and production routing involves token-batch effects not captured by the 8-tuple approximation. The practical claim about MoE deployment is extrapolated far beyond the experimental scale. - The assumption P = O(1) throughout is foundational to both the theory and experiments, but tensor/expert parallelism in LLM inference commonly uses P=8–512. The model does not address large-P behavior. - No analysis of the extra compute cost incurred by replication as a fraction of total schedule cost is provided. Practitioners need to know when replication's compute overhead becomes prohibitive relative to the communication savings. - Theorem 4.5 (surplus cost hardness) is explicitly called an "outlier" by the authors because it does not differentiate replication from non-replication — including it without this connection weakens its value within the paper's central thesis. Bottom Line This paper provides the first rigorous, broad-scope characterization of replication's impact on partitioning and scheduling — the theoretical results are strong and the empirical evidence is convincing on the instances tested — but it stops short of delivering the scalable partitioning heuristic that would make it immediately actionable for production workloads.
Search Results 2605.00209 PDF
#31 — Innovative (72)

Scaling Properties of Continuous Diffusion Spoken Language Models

Jason Ramapuram, Eeshan Gunesh Dhekane, Amitis Shidani, Dan Busbridge, Bogdan Mazoure, Zijin Gu, Russ Webb, Tatiana Likhomanenko, Navdeep Jaitly · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper establishes the first scaling laws for continuous diffusion (CD) spoken language models (SLMs) trained without text supervision, introduces the phoneme Jensen-Shannon divergence (pJSD) metric for evaluating linguistic quality when exact likelihoods are intractable, and scales a CD SLM to 16B parameters on tens of millions of hours of conversational speech. - Why it's important: Speech-only SLMs operate at the linguistic level of a 3–4-year-old child, and prior work on discrete autoregressive SLMs suggests closing the gap with text LLMs could require orders of magnitude more compute — understanding whether continuous diffusion is a more viable path is essential for the field. - How it does it: An MM-DiT architecture operating directly on 80-dimensional log-mel filterbanks is trained across a grid of model sizes (0.6M–11.5B parameters) and ten compute budgets (10^18–10^21 FLOPs) using isoFLOP analysis, with a novel fused two-stage approach that jointly optimizes both the loss scaling law and a generalized sigmoid downstream metric mapping. - The good: The isoFLOP flattening finding — that at higher compute budgets, the range of (N, D) allocations achieving near-optimal loss expands by approximately 2 orders of magnitude — is a genuinely new, actionable insight that opens an efficient inference frontier not observed in prior AR SLM scaling work. - The missing: The paper never provides a compute-matched direct comparison between CD and AR SLMs; it confirms they follow similar scaling trajectories without demonstrating that CD actually outperforms AR at equivalent compute, leaving the central "more viable" claim unsubstantiated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on speech LM scaling, diffusion model evaluation methodology, or compute-optimal training recipes; less useful for practitioners seeking a system that achieves strong linguistic coherence, since the honest conclusion is that no current approach does.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (78) and Novelty (72) anchor the score — the isoFLOP flattening result and decreasing r*(C) trend are genuinely new and counter to prior AR SLM scaling reports, and pJSD is a useful methodological contribution. Rigor (75) is good (≥3 seeds across grid, muP/completeP, fused two-stage fit with train/test MRE) but undermined by proprietary SpeechCrawl, no code/weights release, and a single 16B run that is architecturally distinct from the grid. Completeness (78) is elevated by honest negative results — authors directly confront that scaling alone does not yield long-form coherence. Resilience (70) is hurt by the absence of a compute-matched CD-vs-AR comparison, which is the paper's central framing. Impact (45) is weak: no artifact released, inference cost (100 NFE) not benchmarked. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*45 ≈ 72.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 78 Resilience 70 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Speech-only spoken language models trained directly from audio without any text supervision remain far behind text-based LLMs in linguistic competence. The dominant paradigm — discretizing continuous SSL speech representations into tokens and training autoregressive (AR) models — faces a structural bottleneck: one second of speech corresponds to ~80 mel frames but only ~4 text tokens, a 20× sequence length inflation that dramatically increases compute demands. Prior SLM scaling work (Cuervo & Marxer, EMNLP 2024) characterized this problem for discrete AR models but found that achieving LLM-level linguistic proficiency could require orders of magnitude more compute. Since speech signals are inherently continuous, applying continuous diffusion directly to mel spectrograms avoids the discretization step. No systematic scaling law analysis existed for this alternative paradigm, leaving open whether it offers a better scaling trajectory than discrete AR. Key Idea The core insight is that continuous diffusion SLMs operating on log-mel filterbanks exhibit power-law scaling analogous to AR SLMs, but with a qualitatively distinct isoFLOP geometry: as compute increases, the optimal loss surface flattens dramatically so that a 2-orders-of-magnitude wider band of (N, D) allocations achieves near-optimal loss. This means at high compute, training a much smaller model on much less data costs almost nothing in loss — opening a practical pathway to fast inference without meaningful quality sacrifice. A secondary methodological contribution is pJSD: measuring the Jensen-Shannon divergence between phoneme n-gram distributions of generated and real speech as a sampling-based surrogate for the exact sequence likelihoods that diffusion models cannot compute tractably. Methodology Data: SpeechCrawl, a proprietary conversational speech dataset filtered to ~7M hours of ≥99% English audio (average clip length 30 minutes), resampled to 24kHz and converted to 80-dim log-mel filterbanks at 80Hz. Architecture: MM-DiT with two parallel streams of log-mel filterbanks (10s context + 30s generation target) interacting through full bidirectional self-attention; velocity parameterization (v-prediction) with min-SNR loss weighting; model size scaled by fixing d_emb/L = 128. CFG: rather than dropping conditioning during training, silence is encoded as the unconditional signal at inference, saving substantial FLOPs. Hyperparameters transferred via muP and completeP, tuned once on a 36M-parameter base model. Scaling grid: 10 compute budgets from 10^18 to 10^21 FLOPs, model sizes 0.6M–11.5B parameters, ≥3 seeds per (N, D) point. Scaling law fit: L(N,D) = E + (A/N^α + B/D^β)^γ with γ retained as a free parameter (necessary for <5% MRE); Huber loss minimized by basin-hopping + L-BFGS-B. Downstream metric scaling: fused two-stage approach jointly optimizes all parameters of both the loss scaling law and a generalized sigmoid M = ℓ + (h−ℓ) / (1 + exp(−k(L−L0))), avoiding error accumulation in sequential two-stage fitting. Evaluation uses pJSD (n=1..5, HifiGAN vocoder + Allosaurus phoneme recognizer), DNSMOS P.808/P.835, NISQA MOS, and Meta Audiobox Aesthetics (content enjoyment, content understanding, production quality, production complexity). 16B model: adds a frozen Whisper-large-v3 encoder and Perceiver (downsampling to 4096 tokens) for richer context conditioning, trained on unfiltered SpeechCrawl at ~tens of millions of hours. Key Results Scaling law for validation loss: L = 0.0055 + (0.0638/N^0.3995 + 29.7667/D^0.5644)^0.7051, train MRE 0.49%, test MRE 0.80%. Optimal tokens-per-parameter ratio r(C) decreases with compute: at C = 10^21 FLOPs, r = 245 (equivalent text-token ratio ≈ 12.25), lower than the ~20 tokens/parameter reported for Chinchilla-optimal text AR LMs — suggesting CD SLMs are more compute-efficient per parameter at high budgets. This contrasts with 25Hz discrete AR SLMs where r*(C) was reported to increase with compute. IsoFLOP flattening: at ε = 10^-3 tolerance, ΔN and ΔD both expand by ~2 orders of magnitude as C increases from 10^18 to 10^21. pJSD follows scaling laws: 5-gram fit achieves 1.04% test MRE; 1-gram achieves 8.21%. Content enjoyment and content understanding (Meta Audiobox) scale predictably; all MOS variants and production quality/complexity do not scale — they saturate near real-data baselines at minimal compute, suggesting acoustic fidelity is easily learned but linguistic structure is not. Extrapolation of the content understanding scaling law suggests the real-data baseline may remain unreachable at any compute budget given the current architecture. 16B model (with Whisper conditioning): validation loss 0.0047, below the irreducible E = 0.0055 estimated for the base architecture, with pJSD = 0.1811 vs. 0.2253 for the best C = 10^21 base run. Despite this improvement, long-form linguistic coherence is not achieved. Ablations: noise schedule dominates perceptual quality; training duration dominates linguistic quality (pJSD); monotonically degrading metrics with increasing temporal patch size across all evaluation axes. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (primary), NEW METHOD (secondary). The isoFLOP flattening behavior and decreasing r*(C) trend for CD SLMs are genuinely new empirical observations not reported in prior AR SLM scaling work. The pJSD metric and fused two-stage downstream scaling law fit are new methodological contributions. The MM-DiT architecture, v-parameterization, min-SNR weighting, muP/completeP, and CFG with silence conditioning are all adapted from prior work applied to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Mean and standard deviation across ≥3 seeds are reported for all scaling law grid points — a meaningful rigor commitment given the compute involved. muP/completeP provides principled hyperparameter transfer. Ablations are systematic across 4 independent axes. The fused two-stage fitting is validated with explicit train/test MRE splits. Critical concerns: SpeechCrawl is proprietary and not released; no code or model weights are stated to be released. The 16B model is a single run with an architecturally distinct system (Whisper encoder + Perceiver), making it incomparable to the scaling grid; seed variance at that scale is unknown. pJSD conflates vocoder reconstruction quality with model quality — a better vocoder mechanically improves the metric. The noise scheduler type and number of inference NFE steps are held fixed across the scaling grid due to compute limits, potentially biasing the optimal allocation estimates. This is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2604.24416v1, 27 Apr 2026), not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The scaling law grid (up to 11.5B) and 16B model require Apple-scale infrastructure; the scaling law fitting methodology is reproducible on standard hardware, but independent replication of training experiments is not feasible without SpeechCrawl and equivalent compute. - Efficient: CD SLMs become more parameter-efficient than text AR LMs above C ≈ 5.64×10^19 FLOPs (r* < 400), but inference requires 100 diffusion NFE steps — inherently slower than single-pass AR decoding; per-token inference latency comparison against AR SLMs is not quantified. - Effective: The 16B model achieves rich prosody, multi-speaker output, and multilingual generation, but fails at long-form linguistic coherence — the stated primary success criterion of SLMs — and the authors conclude further scaling is impractical without new representations. - Scalable: Scaling is characterized up to 10^21 FLOPs and 11.5B parameters; the 16B experiment partially validates extrapolation by showing loss below the estimated irreducible floor, but linguistic quality does not scale to coherence at any tested point, and the extrapolated content understanding metric suggests a hard ceiling. No software artifact (library, model weights, dataset) is released. Strengths - The isoFLOP flattening finding is practically significant and previously unreported: at high compute, near-optimal performance spans 2 orders of magnitude in both model size and data size, giving practitioners wide latitude in trading model size for inference speed without quality loss. - The fused two-stage scaling law fit — jointly optimizing loss scaling law parameters and the downstream sigmoid metric mapping — is a clean methodological improvement over sequential two-stage fitting, demonstrated with quantifiably lower MRE (1.04% vs. 8.21% for 5-gram vs. 1-gram pJSD). - The honest negative result is the paper's most valuable contribution: even at 16B parameters and tens of millions of hours of data, long-form coherence is not achieved, and the authors correctly conclude that scaling alone cannot bridge the gap — a finding that redirects the field toward representation and architecture improvements. - The discovery that perceptual quality metrics (all MOS variants) saturate near real-data baselines immediately with minimal compute, while linguistic quality continues to scale, is a clean empirical dissociation that sharpens what the hard problem actually is. Author-Acknowledged Limitations Long-form linguistic coherence is explicitly flagged as the primary unsolved challenge — confronted directly rather than minimized, which is a credibility positive. pJSD is acknowledged as a stopgap metric and the authors recommend ASR+LM perplexity cascade as a better future measure once SLMs improve enough for ASR to function reliably. The extrapolation conclusion (real-data quality may be unreachable) is presented with explicit caveats about functional form assumptions. Whisper conditioning in the 16B model introduces indirect text supervision, acknowledged but framed as a frozen feature extractor experiment. Vocoder reconstruction error is noted as a potential confound in pJSD. The noise scheduler and NFE are fixed due to compute limits. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The paper's central framing — that CD SLMs are "more viable" than discrete AR SLMs — is never directly tested: no compute-matched comparison against an AR SLM baseline appears. The scaling trajectories are similar, not demonstrably superior, and the r*(C) advantage is theoretical unless paired inference cost (100 NFE vs. single AR pass) is accounted for. - The 16B model is architecturally distinct from the scaling grid models (Whisper encoder, Perceiver, unfiltered data, 300s context window) and is a single seed; attributing its improved pJSD to compute scaling versus architecture change or indirect text supervision leakage is impossible from the paper alone. - No wall-clock or energy cost comparison between the CD and AR paradigms at matched quality levels is provided, despite this being central to the "efficiency" argument. - pJSD is evaluated on samples passed through HifiGAN, whose reconstruction quality is a confound; a vocoder quality ceiling could mask model improvement even as training continues to improve. - The SpeechCrawl dataset is fully proprietary with minimal documentation — deduplication methodology, speaker overlap between train and evaluation sets, and potential test contamination are unaddressed. Bottom Line This is a rigorous, large-scale empirical paper from Apple that contributes the first scaling law characterization for continuous diffusion SLMs, reports a practically useful isoFLOP flattening result, and reaches the honest — if sobering — conclusion that scaling alone cannot produce coherent speech language models, making it essential reading for anyone serious about speech LM scaling or diffusion model evaluation methodology.
Recommended 2604.24416 PDF
#32 — Innovative (72)

HERMES++: Toward a Unified Driving World Model for 3D Scene Understanding and Generation (68 ▲ )

Xin Zhou, Dingkang Liang, Xiwu Chen, Feiyang Tan, Dingyuan Zhang, Hengshuang Zhao, Xiang Bai · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HERMES++, a unified driving world model that simultaneously performs 3D scene understanding (VQA, scene description) and future point cloud prediction from multi-view camera images within a single framework. - Why it's important: Autonomous driving requires both semantic reasoning and geometric foresight, but existing systems either predict future geometry without language grounding or understand scenes without anticipating geometric evolution — a gap that is safety-critical for collision avoidance. - How it does it: Learnable world queries are initialized from the current BEV feature map, passed through the LLM to absorb semantic context, then fed into a Current-to-Future Link that propagates BEV features across time horizons under textual and ego-motion conditioning; a Joint Geometric Optimization strategy adds explicit L1 rendering loss plus implicit cosine and Gram-matrix latent regularization against a frozen self-supervised geometry extractor. - The good: HERMES++ (1.8B) achieves 3s Chamfer Distance of 1.01 vs. specialist DriveX's 1.10 (ICCV 2025) while simultaneously exceeding understanding specialists (CIDEr 0.749 vs. Omni-Q's 0.686), both without auxiliary 3D detection or lane supervision. - The missing: All ablations are run on 25% of training data, and all benchmarks are nuScenes derivatives — the paper does not validate generation quality or understanding on any out-of-distribution dataset (e.g., Waymo, adverse weather), limiting generalization claims. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building unified perception-prediction architectures; the world query mechanism and Joint Geometric Optimization are the transferable insights, but those targeting deployment should note the complete absence of inference latency numbers.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (65) is solid but extends an ICCV 2025 conference paper with incremental additions (JGO, Textual Injection, Ego Modulation, scaling). Rigor (70) — systematic ablations across many tables, but all on 25% training data, no error bars, and a key training dataset is a concurrent preprint from the same lab. Non-Obviousness (75) — the LLM-routed world query insight with bidirectional gradient coupling is genuinely non-obvious and ablation-validated. Completeness (70) — broad benchmark coverage but missing inference timing and OOD evaluation. Resilience (70) — single-dataset (nuScenes) evaluation undermines generalization claims. Impact (70) — code forthcoming, no efficiency numbers. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*70 = 70.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Autonomous driving systems must simultaneously interpret the current scene semantically and predict how that scene will geometrically evolve to enable safe planning. Prior work bifurcates into two camps: driving world models (ViDAR, DriveX, OccWorld) that predict future 3D geometry but cannot answer natural language queries, and VLM-based driving systems (OmniDrive, DriveLM, ORION) that excel at scene understanding but have no mechanism to predict future geometric evolution. This gap is safety-critical — a model that can describe a pedestrian but cannot predict whether they will step into the road is fundamentally incomplete. Existing unified attempts (Doe-1, Epona) operate in 2D single-view space and lack dense 3D geometric constraints intertwined with semantic reasoning. The specific challenge is designing a representation and interaction mechanism that preserves geometric structure (essential for point cloud generation) while remaining compatible with token-based LLMs (essential for language understanding), and that enables semantic reasoning to genuinely guide geometric prediction rather than merely sharing shallow features. Key Idea The key insight is that LLM-processed world queries — learnable tokens initialized from BEV feature max-pooling and enriched by the LLM's full forward pass over both visual and textual tokens — act as bidirectional bridges. Because these queries participate in the LLM's causal attention (rather than bypassing it), gradients from the geometric generation objective backpropagate through the LLM, improving language reasoning while encoding actionable geometric priors for future prediction. This single architectural choice — routing world queries through the LLM instead of around it — is responsible for the performance gap between the proposed method and the separated unification baseline (3s CD: 1.436 vs. 1.634, Table VII). Methodology The pipeline has four main stages. (1) BEV Tokenization: An OpenCLIP ConvNeXt-L backbone extracts multi-scale perspective features; BEVFormer v2 spatial cross-attention lifts them into a 180×180 BEV grid (256 channels); 4× strided downsampling yields 45×45 tokens projected into the LLM embedding dimension. Design decision: downsampling from fine-grained BEV (×4) vs. direct coarse querying reduces 3s CD from 2.012 to 1.436 — well-justified. (2) LLM Understanding + World Query Enrichment: BEV tokens, text instruction tokens, and ∆t×n world queries (n=4; initialized via BEV max-pooling + ego-motion MLP embeddings + learnable frame embeddings) are concatenated and processed jointly by InternVL2 (1.8B default, LoRA fine-tuned). World queries use causal attention to aggregate both BEV observations and textual context. Design decision: processing queries through the LLM vs. just using them to aggregate BEV features yields consistent improvement (Table VII c vs. b); justified by gradient flow argument. (3) Current-to-Future Link: Stacked cross-attention/self-attention/FFN blocks propagate current encoded BEV Bt to future timestamps. Cross-attention uses enriched world queries plus average-pooled text embeddings (Textual Injection) as Key/Value. Ego Modulation (scale+shift affine from ego-motion MLP, zero-initialized, applied to self-attention and FFN only) decouples ego motion from scene dynamics. Output: ∆t predicted BEV maps. (4) BEV-to-Point Render + Joint Geometric Optimization: A differentiable SDF-based neural renderer converts predicted BEV to point clouds via volumetric expansion, SDF prediction per ray sample, and depth-weighted accumulation. Training objective: Ltotal = Llang (autoregressive LM) + 10·Lrender (L1 depth) + Lcos (voxel-wise cosine similarity to frozen geometry extractor output) + Lgram (Gram matrix correlation loss across three orthogonal spatial projections HW, HZ, WZ). The geometry extractor (sparse 3D conv SECOND + Render) is self-supervised in a pre-training stage then frozen; it is discarded at inference, adding no runtime cost. Design decision: implicit Lcos + Lgram jointly vs. explicit-only reduces 3s CD from 1.637 to 1.436 — well-justified by feature visualization showing suppression of ray-shaped ego-centric artifacts. Key Results On nuScenes generation, HERMES++ (1.8B) achieves 3s CD = 1.01 vs. DriveX 1.10 (ICCV 2025, camera-only, the current specialist leader) and ViDAR 1.73 (CVPR 2024). Current-frame (0s) CD = 0.53 vs. DriveX 0.66. With 3.8B LLM: 3s CD = 0.97. On OmniDrive-nuScenes understanding, HERMES++ (1.8B): CIDEr = 0.749, METEOR = 0.385, ROUGE = 0.327, vs. Omni-Q (CVPR 2025) CIDEr = 0.686 (Omni-Q uses 3D box + lane detection supervision; HERMES++ uses none). With 3.8B: CIDEr = 0.772. On NuScenes-QA VQA: 61.3% accuracy vs. Omni-Q 59.2% (camera SOTA) and CenterPoint+MCAN 59.5% (LiDAR-based). On DriveLM leaderboard: Final Score 0.59, matching Team NVIDIA (CVPR 2024 challenge winner). Open-loop motion planning on nuScenes: average L2 = 0.37m, collision = 0.29% — competitive with OmniDrive++ (0.30%) and ORION (0.37%) despite no explicit planning or perception supervision. The ablation confirming the 13.7% improvement over the ICCV 2025 conference version is well-documented. Scaling from 0.8B to 3.8B yields consistent gains across both tasks: 3s CD improves by 12.5% and CIDEr by 4.8%. Baselines (DriveX, Omni-Q, ORION) are drawn from 2024-2025 conference proceedings and represent strong current comparisons. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM. The LLM-processed world query mechanism (bidirectional gradient coupling between understanding and generation) and the Joint Geometric Optimization via frozen self-supervised geometry extractor are genuine contributions. The BEV representation for driving LLMs is established prior work (OmniDrive, BEVFormer); the unified understanding+generation architecture extends the ICCV 2025 conference version (HERMES) with four specific additions: JGO, Textual Injection, Ego Modulation, and the 3.8B scaling study. The underlying rendering approach (SDF-based neural rendering of BEV features) follows prior work (UniPAD, PonderV2). What is genuinely novel is the combination: routing world queries through the LLM backbone and the Gram+cosine implicit latent regularization applied to a frozen self-supervised encoder. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are systematic across BEV scale (Table III), JGO components (Table IV), Current-to-Future Link components (Table V), task interaction (Table VI), world query strategy (Table VII), and hyperparameters (Table VIII). A consistent weakness: all ablation experiments use 25% of training data for efficiency. The authors state this but do not verify that relative ablation trends hold at full data scale. No error bars, multiple seeds, or statistical significance tests are reported. Code and model weights will be publicly released (https://github.com/H-EmbodVis/HERMESV2, not yet available at submission). This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.28196v1, April 30, 2026); the conference version was peer-reviewed at ICCV 2025 [19]. The NuInteract dataset [84] used in training is a concurrent arXiv preprint from the same research group (arXiv:2505.08725), creating a mild concern about the independence of training data from the same lab's concurrent work. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: The model uses a 1.8B–3.8B LLM with LoRA adaptation, BEVFormer v2, and a sparse 3D convolutional encoder — all compatible with standard multi-GPU training on commercially available hardware; the geometry extractor is discarded at inference, reducing runtime overhead. - Efficient: No inference latency, FPS, or memory footprint numbers are reported anywhere in the paper; the computational cost relative to specialist baselines (DriveX, OmniDrive) is not characterized, which is a significant gap for any deployment evaluation. - Effective: Evidence shows competitive or superior performance across four nuScenes-derived benchmarks and motion planning vs. 2024-2025 baselines, including surpassing specialist models that use auxiliary supervision; the competitive motion planning without planning supervision suggests genuine internalization of dynamics rather than task-specific overfitting. - Scalable: Scaling from 0.8B to 3.8B LLM shows consistent gains; the paper tests a 3s prediction horizon only; no evaluation beyond nuScenes (single city, controlled conditions); no stress-scale tests on longer temporal horizons, sensor failure, or distribution shift. The work delivers a software artifact (model + code, forthcoming), but exists in preprint form for this extended version. Strengths - The world query architecture — routing learnable temporal tokens through the LLM rather than as a parallel branch — creates genuine bidirectional coupling confirmed by ablation (Table VII: bypassing the LLM degrades 3s CD from 1.436 to 1.526, even with equivalent BEV feature aggregation), and the gradient flow improvement to the LLM is validated by understanding metric gains. - The Joint Geometric Optimization addresses a real and previously under-addressed failure mode of purely observational rendering supervision: the ray-shaped ego-centric projection artifacts visible in Figure 5(a) are cleanly resolved by the frozen geometry extractor's latent regularization, with an ablation showing 12.3% CD improvement at 3s. - Outperforming dedicated understanding specialists (Omni-Q, ORION) on CIDEr, METEOR, and NuScenes-QA accuracy without auxiliary 3D box or lane detection supervision is a non-trivial result that validates the claim that BEV geometry encodes richer scene context than sparse query-based representations. - The evaluation scope — four benchmarks spanning VQA, graph-based reasoning, open-ended description, and motion planning — provides a broader validation footprint than most related work. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge two limitations explicitly in the conclusion: (1) how to better leverage semantic priors from pre-trained multimodal models for BEV input requires further investigation; (2) expanding generation beyond point clouds to diverse modalities (video, occupancy grids) remains future work. These limitations are framed honestly as open research directions rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No inference timing. The paper reports no FPS, latency, or memory footprint at inference, making it impossible to assess whether the model is usable in real-time autonomous driving systems. - 25%-data ablations. Marginal gains (e.g., Textual Injection: CD 1.542 → 1.506; Ego Modulation: 1.506 → 1.442) are small enough to be unreliable at 25% training scale; full-scale validation of each component is absent. - Single-dataset generalization. All benchmarks derive from nuScenes (Boston/Singapore, good weather, dense urban). No evaluation on Waymo, nuPlan, KITTI, or any adverse-condition split; the paper's generalization claims rest entirely on in-distribution nuScenes splits. - Training data provenance. NuInteract, a key pre-training dataset, is a concurrent arXiv preprint from the same group; its contribution relative to architectural choices is not ablated. - Text metric validity. CIDEr/METEOR/ROUGE measure surface-level text similarity against reference annotations, not factual accuracy of scene descriptions. The paper provides no hallucination rate or factual grounding evaluation. - Motion planning comparison framing. Competing against OmniDrive++ (which uses full perception supervision) while using only text + geometric supervision is presented as an advantage, but the comparison is asymmetric and lacks discussion of what specific supervision choices enable each system's strengths. Bottom Line Practitioners building unified perception-prediction systems for autonomous driving will find actionable value in the world query mechanism and Joint Geometric Optimization strategy — both are well-ablated, transferable ideas — but the complete absence of inference timing, exclusive reliance on nuScenes variants for evaluation, and ablations conducted on 25% training data leave unresolved questions that must be addressed before production consideration.
Recommended 2604.28196 PDF
#33 — Innovative (72)

Continuous-Time Distribution Matching for Few-Step Diffusion Distillation (22 ▲ )

Tao Liu, Hao Yan, Mengting Chen, Taihang Hu, Zhengrong Yue, Zihao Pan, Jinsong Lan, Xiaoyong Zhu, Ming-Ming Cheng, Bo Zheng, Yaxing Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Continuous-Time Distribution Matching (CDM), a diffusion distillation framework that replaces the fixed discrete-timestep training schedule of DMD with a dynamic continuous schedule and adds an off-trajectory supervision loss driven by velocity-field Euler extrapolation. - Why it's important: Existing DMD-family methods are forced to anchor training to the exact inference timesteps, causing sparse gradient coverage, unsmooth velocity fields, and over-smoothed outputs that typically require GAN or reward-model auxiliary losses to fix — each adding implementation complexity and instability. - How it does it: At each training iteration CDM samples a random schedule length and random continuous timesteps instead of fixed anchors, then extrapolates off-trajectory latents via a first-order Euler step along the predicted velocity field and enforces distribution matching on those drifted points to directly penalize inter-anchor truncation error. - The good: CDM achieves HPSv3 9.561 vs. D-DMD's 9.176 on SD3-Medium at 4 NFE without any GAN or reward-model component, with matched inference latency (246 ms/img), and thorough ablations confirm each of the three loss components contributes independently. - The missing: Training cost is 1.8× that of D-DMD and the paper evaluates only two proprietary-style text-to-image backbones (SD3-Medium and Longcat-Image), leaving generalization to class-conditional, video, or smaller-scale models undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers active in diffusion distillation or flow-matching acceleration; the insight that distribution-matching is schedule-independent and the theoretical justification via truncation-error bounds are substantive enough to be useful even if the specific method is superseded.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) moderate — schedule-decoupled DMD is a genuinely new insight within the family but framed as a NEW METHOD, not a new problem. Rigor (75) strong — thorough ablations, code released, but no error bars, DMD2 re-implementation removes its GAN loss which may understate the baseline (Goodhart-adjacent confound). Non-Obviousness (75) — the schedule-independence finding challenges a common assumption and the Tweedie/truncation-error analysis is non-obvious. Completeness (70) — limitations directly confronted (training cost, teacher upper bound, T2I-only). Resilience (70) — solid theory but only two proprietary-style backbones, no user study. Impact (70) — code released, no weights, training requires 16 A100s. Composite ≈ 0.20·70+0.25·75+0.15·75+0.15·70+0.15·70+0.10·70 = 72.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Diffusion and flow-matching models generate high-quality images but require tens to hundreds of network evaluations at inference, making them expensive for real-time or resource-constrained deployment. Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) and its descendants address this by training a student model to match the teacher's output distribution in as few as 1–4 steps. However, all existing DMD variants anchor both forward (backward simulation) and gradient computation to a fixed discrete set of timesteps that mirrors the inference schedule. This rigid alignment deprives the student of gradient supervision between schedule anchors, forces the velocity field to be learned only at a sparse grid of points, and — because the reverse KL divergence is mode-seeking — tends to produce over-smoothed images. Current remedies layer on discriminator networks (DMD2) or reward models (Flash Diffusion), increasing training complexity. CDM asks whether the discrete schedule alignment is actually necessary, and answers empirically and theoretically that it is not. Key Idea: The core insight is that distribution matching is schedule-independent: training with uniformly sampled random continuous timesteps rather than fixed discrete anchors improves quality because it smooths the learned velocity field over the full interval (0,1] rather than overfitting to sparse points. The second, complementary insight is that few-step Euler integration introduces O((Δt)²) truncation error controlled by the material derivative dv_θ/dτ; the CDM loss directly suppresses this quantity by enforcing distribution matching on off-trajectory latents obtained by taking one Euler step along the predicted velocity, forcing the student's material derivative to track the teacher's. Methodology: CDM is built on top of Decoupled DMD (D-DMD) and retains its two-component objective (CFG Augmentation loss L_CA and Distribution Matching loss L_DM), adding a third term L_CDM. The training pipeline has three phases per iteration. (1) Dynamic backward simulation: a random schedule length N ~ U{1, N_max} and a random strictly-decreasing continuous time sequence are sampled; N Euler steps generate the student trajectory, and one anchor x_{t_i} is uniformly selected. (2) On-trajectory losses: L_CA steers the student's clean-data estimate toward higher text-image alignment using CFG-augmented teacher predictions; L_DM minimizes reverse KL divergence between student and teacher distributions at the anchor by comparing frozen real teacher D_φ and online-updated fake teacher D_ψ scores. Both losses are derived using Tweedie's formula for flow-matching to show they regularize the velocity field uniformly over (0,1] rather than at discrete points. (3) Off-trajectory CDM loss: a new timestep t'_i ~ U(0,1] is sampled; the off-trajectory latent x_{t'_i} = x_{t_i} + (t'_i - t_i) v_θ(x_{t_i}, t_i, c) is computed via first-order Euler extrapolation; the student is evaluated at this drifted point and compared against the same real/fake teacher pair. The target for the CDM loss uses the local clean-data estimate D_θ(x_{t'_i}, t'_i, c) rather than the full-trajectory final prediction, which ablations confirm is critical. The full objective is L = L_CA + L_DM + L_CDM with equal weights. For SD3-Medium, training uses AdamW, batch 128, 4K iterations on 16 A100 GPUs (~24 hours); for Longcat-Image, LoRA rank-64 fine-tuning with 2K iterations. Key Results: On SD3-Medium at 4 NFE (vs. 5 baselines): CDM achieves Aesthetic 6.075, DPGBench 85.26, PickScore 21.95, HPSv3 9.561, CLIPScore 27.98 — first place on all five metrics among image-free, continuous-timestep methods. The strongest prior image-free baseline, D-DMD, scores HPSv3 9.176; the gap of 0.385 HPSv3 is meaningful relative to the spread across all methods (6.054–9.561). On Longcat-Image, CDM similarly leads: HPSv3 10.65 vs. D-DMD 9.629 vs. DMD2 8.803. In both settings the 4-NFE CDM student matches or exceeds the 100-NFE teacher on DPGBench and HPSv3. Extended evaluation adds FID 30.30 (CDM) vs. 31.47 (D-DMD) and OCR accuracy 34.82 vs. 33.47, with CDM leading on FID and lagging slightly on OCR. Training overhead is 1.8× D-DMD (same 62.5 GB peak memory); inference latency is identical at 246 ms/img. The ablation over dynamic vs. fixed schedule alone shows HPSv3 improvement from 9.482 to 9.561, and removing L_CDM drops HPSv3 from 9.561 to 9.153. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The continuous-time reformulation of DMD training is genuinely novel — specifically the combination of schedule-decoupled dynamic timestep sampling and velocity-driven off-trajectory distribution matching. The theoretical connection (via Tweedie's formula) showing that the CA and DM gradients uniformly regularize the velocity field under the dynamic schedule, and the formal derivation linking L_CDM to suppression of Euler truncation error M2, are new analytical contributions within the DMD family. The individual components (backward simulation, KL-based matching, Euler extrapolation) are each known; the novelty lies in their specific integration and in the empirical finding that schedule alignment is unnecessary. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are thorough: individual loss components are ablated, pairwise combinations are tested, and each core design choice (dynamic schedule, off-trajectory perturbation type, target latent construction) is individually varied in a right-panel ablation table. However, error bars and significance tests are absent throughout. The paper trains on 200K prompts from five datasets and evaluates on 2K held-out PickScore prompts plus 1K DPGBench prompts — a reasonable scale. Two baselines (DMD2, D-DMD) are re-implemented from scratch because no official SD3-Medium checkpoints exist, introducing a potential confound (the re-implementation explicitly removes GAN losses, which may disadvantage DMD2 relative to its published version). Code is publicly released at https://github.com/byliutao/cdm; no pretrained weights are mentioned as released. This is a preprint — results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability: Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: CDM trains on 16 A100 GPUs over 24 hours and infers at 246 ms/img on a single GPU at 1024×1024; commercially available hardware suffices for inference, though training requires a multi-GPU cluster. - Efficient: Training is 1.8× more expensive than D-DMD (same peak memory at 62.5 GB); inference overhead is zero relative to D-DMD since both use the same backbone and NFE. - Effective: On automated preference metrics (HPSv3, AES, PickScore, DPGBench) CDM consistently outperforms all baselines; qualitative comparisons support the quantitative findings, though all evaluation is on standard automated metrics that are themselves susceptible to gaming. - Scalable: Tested only on two large models at 1024×1024; the paper claims generalization but does not test at larger scales, smaller compute budgets, or video generation — the dynamic schedule's variable N up to N_max=28 already increases average backward simulation length and will become more expensive at higher step budgets. The work delivers a training recipe (code released) but no standalone trained model weights. Strengths: - The empirical finding that distribution matching is schedule-independent (Figure 2, Table 2 right panel) is clearly demonstrated and challenges a widely-held assumption in the DMD literature. - The theoretical analysis is unusually complete for an applied distillation paper: Tweedie-based derivation of CA/DM gradients and a formal Euler truncation-error bound connect the method design directly to the mechanisms being improved. - Competitive results without adversarial training or external reward models simplifies the training pipeline relative to DMD2 and Flash Diffusion, which is a meaningful practical contribution. - Ablations are systematically designed: each design choice is varied in isolation, and the contribution of each is clearly quantified. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state three limitations: (1) increased per-iteration training cost (~1.8× D-DMD) due to longer average backward simulation and extra teacher forward passes on off-trajectory latents; (2) CDM is fundamentally upper-bounded by the teacher, since both DM and CDM losses rely on the teacher's score — the CFG-free analysis shows that failure modes of the teacher propagate to the student; (3) evaluation is restricted to text-to-image tasks (SD3-Medium, Longcat-Image), and extension to text-and-image-to-image editing and video diffusion is left to future work. These limitations are stated directly and confronted honestly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - FID and OCR are only compared against D-DMD in the extended table (Table 3); Hyper-SD, Flash, TDM, and DMD2 are excluded from FID evaluation, making it impossible to rank CDM on distributional fidelity against all baselines. - Both backbones (SD3-Medium, Longcat-Image) are large proprietary-style rectified-flow transformers; there is no evaluation on a standard academic benchmark (e.g., ImageNet class-conditional or CIFAR) or a smaller model, which limits assessment of whether the gains hold generally or are specific to this model family. - The concurrent work SOAR [40] shares the off-trajectory insight but targets post-training alignment; the paper notes this but does not compare against it experimentally, leaving the relative merits unclear. - The claim that the 4-NFE student "matches or surpasses the 100-NFE teacher" rests on HPSv3 and DPGBench — both automated metrics that are known to correlate imperfectly with human preference, especially for photorealism and fine details. No user study is provided. - The re-implementation of DMD2 without its GAN loss (as noted in Appendix C.2) means DMD2's published performance is not replicated; the reported DMD2 numbers in Table 1 understate the published method, potentially overstating CDM's relative improvement. Bottom Line: CDM makes a substantive, well-analyzed contribution to diffusion distillation by showing that the discrete schedule constraint is unnecessary and providing a principled remedy with demonstrable quality gains over the closest baseline — practitioners working on few-step diffusion model deployment will find both the insight and the released code immediately useful.
Recommended 2605.06376 PDF
#34 — Innovative (72)

The Power of Order: Fooling LLMs with Adversarial Table Permutations

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Adversarial Table Permutation (ATP), a gradient-based attack that finds worst-case semantics-preserving row and column rearrangements of tables to maximally degrade LLM performance on Table Question Answering. - Why it's important: LLMs are increasingly deployed on tabular data in high-stakes settings, and a structural fragility that can be triggered by semantics-preserving reorderings — without any content change — represents a reliability risk that random-permutation studies have failed to quantify worst-case. - How it does it: ATP relaxes the intractable discrete permutation search to a continuous problem using doubly stochastic matrices (Birkhoff-von Neumann), optimizes via Adam with information entropy regularization, then projects back to hard permutations via the Hungarian algorithm — applied in a hybrid mode (soft on embeddings, hard on position IDs and attention masks) to maintain differentiability while producing valid attacks. - The good: Joint row-and-column ATP consistently outperforms random and evolutionary search baselines across 10 open-weight LLMs and 3 benchmarks, with drops like Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on TATQA from 0.49 to 0.20 (LLM-as-judge score) and Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct on WTQ from 0.47 to 0.26, confirmed by mechanistic attention analysis showing >4× drop in attention to correct evidence cells. - The missing: ATP requires white-box gradient access, so closed models (Gemini-2.5, GPT-4o) are only evaluated under heuristic attacks, leaving the worst-case vulnerability of the strongest frontier systems unquantified. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on LLM robustness, table reasoning, or structured-data interfaces — the formalization is clean, the coverage broad, and the mechanistic evidence goes beyond a pure empirical report.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) is solid — extends PEARL-style 1D prompt-permutation attacks to 2D row/column with a clean Birkhoff-von Neumann relaxation and entropy-regularized Sinkhorn. Non-Obviousness (75) elevated by the column>row finding plus mechanistic attention evidence. Rigor (70) limited by absent error bars, partial judge/victim circularity (Gemini-2.5), and code-not-yet-released. Completeness (75) and Resilience (70) reasonable but undermined by small tables (~10×5), a deliberately weak evolutionary baseline, and no defense numbers. Impact (65) reflects a diagnostic tool with promised code. Weighted: 0.20*75+0.25*70+0.15*75+0.15*75+0.15*70+0.10*65 = 72.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: The dominant paradigm for LLM-based Table Question Answering (TQA) linearizes tables into text and feeds them as part of a prompt. Tables are inherently permutation-invariant: any rearrangement of data rows or columns that keeps the header row first preserves all relational information. However, decoder-only transformers are order-sensitive: they process input as a strict token sequence, so the serialized representation of a table encodes a specific position for every cell. The gap between permutation-invariant semantics and order-sensitive processing creates a structural vulnerability. Prior work (e.g., TableFormer, TransTab) showed that row/column order influences BERT-style representation models, but those findings do not transfer to the now-dominant generative LLM setting, and none of the prior work characterized worst-case permutations — relying instead on random shuffles. Practitioners deploying LLMs for financial, scientific, or enterprise tabular reasoning have no quantitative bound on how badly a table reordering (whether accidental or adversarial) can degrade correctness. Key Idea: The core insight is that the combinatorial search over permutation matrices (NP-hard, scaling as n!×m! in table shape) can be relaxed to a differentiable continuous problem by replacing permutation matrices with their convex hull — doubly stochastic matrices, per Birkhoff-von Neumann — parameterized via the Sinkhorn algorithm. Entropy regularization forces the soft permutations toward hard ones without the numerical instability of low-temperature Sinkhorn operations. The Hungarian algorithm projects the optimized soft solution back to the nearest hard permutation, and the hybrid application (soft on continuous embeddings, hard on discrete position IDs and attention masks) maintains an end-to-end differentiable pipeline that produces a valid attack on the full model input. Methodology: ATP operates as follows. Given a victim LLM, a table T, a question Q, and a ground-truth answer A, two unconstrained parameter matrices θ_r ∈ ℝⁿˣⁿ and θ_c ∈ ℝᵐˣᵐ are initialized. The Sinkhorn algorithm converts these to doubly stochastic matrices D_r and D_c (with D_r constrained to fix the header row). Soft permutations D_r, D_c are applied to continuous token embeddings; their hard projections Proj(D_r), Proj(D_c) via Hungarian matching are applied to position IDs and attention masks. The optimization objective maximizes the cross-entropy loss of the ground-truth answer under the permuted input, minus entropy regularization terms λ₁H(D_r) + λ₂H(D_c) that encourage the soft matrices to be sharp. Adam is used for optimization over N_attack iterations (default 20). Three critical design decisions: (1) entropy regularization over temperature-based Sinkhorn — well-justified by numerical instability of float16/bfloat16 at τ≤0.05; (2) Hungarian projection over Gumbel sampling — deterministic and avoids stochastic variance; (3) hybrid soft/hard application — necessary because position IDs and attention masks are discrete. A label-free variant replaces cross-entropy with KL divergence between clean and permuted output distributions. Evaluation uses three TQA benchmarks: WTQ (avg 10×5 table, ~145 tokens), TATQA (10×4, ~140 tokens), FeTaQA (12×5, ~160 tokens). Order-sensitive questions (~0–3% of each set) are filtered and excluded. LLM-as-judge scoring uses Gemini-2.5 (temperature 0, seed 0) without exposing the table to the judge, validated against human annotations on 500 examples (Spearman rank correlation 0.847 vs. human, versus 0.120 for ROUGE-L). Baselines include random permutation, best-of-20 random, row reversal, column reversal, and evolutionary search (population 5, 5 generations, 30 model queries/example). Key Results: All 10 evaluated open-weight LLMs are vulnerable even to random permutations. On WTQ, Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct drops from 0.47 to 0.33 under random permutation, and further to 0.26 under joint ATP. On TATQA, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct drops from 0.49 to 0.20 under joint ATP. On FeTaQA, the same model drops from 0.50 to 0.31. Joint row-and-column ATP is universally the strongest attack, and its effect is super-additive: on WTQ with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, row-only ATP reduces by 0.11, column-only by 0.15, and joint by 0.24. Column permutations consistently cause larger drops than row permutations, attributed to column reordering perturbing token order within every row simultaneously. Closed models are also vulnerable via heuristics: Gemini-2.5 drops from 0.88 to 0.72 on WTQ under column reversal alone; GPT-4o from 0.95 to 0.82. Mechanistic analysis (attention heatmaps) shows that ATP reduces attention to the correct evidence cell by >4× and to the correct numeric value by >3×, while increasing attention across artificial row-boundary tokens created by linearization (mean boundary attention 0.026 vs. 0.017 for clean). Runtime is ~10 seconds/example on a single A100 at N_attack=20. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The ATP algorithm is genuinely novel in the tabular attack setting: it extends 1D prompt-permutation attacks (PEARL) to the 2D row-and-column structure of tables, introduces information entropy regularization as a stability-improving alternative to temperature-based Sinkhorn, and uses the Hungarian algorithm for deterministic hard projection. The empirical finding that column permutations are systematically more damaging than row permutations is a non-obvious, novel observation with a plausible mechanistic explanation. The mechanistic attention analysis supporting the structural fragility hypothesis is a meaningful contribution beyond the attack itself. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations isolate two key design choices: (1) entropy weight λ — λ₁=λ₂=10 outperforms no regularization and is superior to both smaller and larger values; (2) N_attack — 5 iterations already beats random, and convergence is essentially complete by 20. No error bars or significance tests are reported across runs, which is a modest concern given the low absolute scores. The LLM-as-judge is Gemini-2.5, which is also one of the victim models evaluated; while the judge does not see the table, this partial circularity should be noted. Code is promised as publicly available but not yet released at submission time. No trained model weights or datasets beyond public benchmarks are involved. The evaluation metric is justified with 500-example human annotation (correlation 0.847). Baselines are reasonable but evolutionary search is weak (population 5, 5 generations) relative to what a more sophisticated search could achieve. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: ATP requires white-box access to model internals (token embeddings, position IDs, attention masks), which means it applies only to open-weight models that can be loaded locally; API-deployed closed models are not directly attackable. - Efficient: ~10 seconds/example on one A100 GPU at N_attack=20, or ~3 seconds at N_attack=5 with moderate performance loss — acceptable for evaluation/diagnostic use, but prohibitive for large-scale online attack. - Effective: ATP consistently finds permutations that cause substantially larger degradation than random or heuristic baselines across all 10 open models and 3 datasets, with mechanistic evidence validating the attack mechanism. - Scalable: Tested on small tables (~10×5); the Sinkhorn/Hungarian pipeline scales quadratically in table dimensions, but larger tables dramatically expand the permutation space — the paper does not test at 50×20 or larger, so scalability claims are extrapolation. The work delivers a software artifact (code promised, not yet released) and a diagnostic method. It stops short of a defense or fine-tuned model.
