TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces an efficient exact-SHAP algorithm for covariate-informed Time Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) that exploits their native input flexibility — variable context length and optional covariate subsets — to compute Shapley values via masked inference, eliminating background-data sampling, demonstrated on day-ahead electrical load forecasting for a German transmission system operator. - Why it's important: Energy grid operators face regulatory pressure (EU AI Act) to deploy interpretable AI in critical infrastructure, but prior SHAP adaptations for time series either require expensive background sampling or depend on specific architectures, making them impractical for general-purpose TSFMs that must earn operational trust. - How it does it: By grouping the TSFM inputs into 7 feature groups (4 temporal windows + 3 covariates) and masking absent groups via context truncation or NaN injection — exploiting the fact that TSFMs natively handle missing or shortened inputs — the method evaluates all 2^7 = 128 coalitions and computes exact Shapley values without imputing absent values from a background distribution. - The good: Zero-shot Chronos-2 achieves MAE 156.2 MW (MAPE 2.44%) on one full year of real TransnetBW TSO data, within 4% of a Transformer trained on 8.75 years of domain data (MAE 150.2 MW), and delivers full SHAP explanations in ~5 seconds per forecast. - The missing: All results use ERA5 reanalysis weather (perfect hindcast, not operational forecasts), which the authors acknowledge overstates accuracy; and there is no validation that the 7-group coarsening produces faithful attributions relative to fine-grained SHAP. - Read it or not: Worth reading for energy-systems ML practitioners and XAI researchers building toward regulated, trustworthy forecasting pipelines — the masking insight is clean, reusable, and immediately applicable to any TSFM with flexible input handling.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The masking-based exact-SHAP adaptation for covariate-informed TSFMs is architecturally clean and broadly reusable (Non-Obviousness 70, Novelty 65 — new method, not a new problem). Rigor (70) benefits from real one-year TSO operational data and a public repo, but is held back by no error bars, no SHAP fidelity validation against fine-grained attributions, and the ERA5 perfect-hindcast assumption. Completeness (75) is strong — limits are confronted directly. Practical Impact (75) reflects the open-source, immediately deployable codebase with full dataset access. Composite: 0.20*65 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*75 ≈ 70.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Transmission system operators and energy grid planners need accurate day-ahead load forecasts to balance supply and demand, schedule redispatch measures, and prevent grid congestion. Deep learning models have improved forecast accuracy but lack transparency, which creates regulatory risk under frameworks like the EU AI Act and practical risk when system operators cannot verify that models respond correctly to known physical relationships (e.g., temperature-load coupling, PV-offset from behind-the-meter solar). TSFMs have emerged as powerful zero-shot forecasters but are opaque. Existing SHAP adaptations for time series — TimeSHAP, WindowSHAP, SHAPformer — either require sampling from background data (expensive for large models with thousands of time-step features) or require manipulating internal attention weights (architecture-specific). No prior work demonstrated efficient exact SHAP on covariate-informed TSFMs applied to real operational energy data. Key Idea Unlike classical supervised models that require all training features at inference, TSFMs natively accept arbitrary-length input contexts and arbitrary subsets of covariates at inference time. This structural property converts the SHAP coalition-evaluation problem — which normally requires imputing absent features from a background distribution — into a simple masking problem: withheld features are handled by the model itself via context truncation or NaN tokens, requiring no external data. Grouping thousands of hourly time steps and multiple covariates into N=7 groups then makes exact Shapley computation tractable with only 2^N = 128 forward passes per forecast. Methodology The pipeline has three components. First, feature grouping partitions inputs into 7 groups: four temporal windows of past load (last day = hours −24 to −1; short-term = hours −168 to −25; intermediate = hours −672 to −169; long-term = all earlier history) plus three individual covariates (binary Sunday/holiday indicator, temperature, solar irradiance). This collapses the exponential coalition space from 2^(thousands of time steps + 3) to 2^7 = 128. Second, model-specific masking implements the absent-group withholding: for Chronos-2, oldest time steps are removed by context-length reduction, intermediate/recent steps are replaced by NaN (Chronos-2 uses a special missing-value token, setting NaN inputs to zero internally), and covariates are dropped entirely; for TabPFN-TS, which cannot handle NaN in the target variable, intermediate/recent steps are row-deleted rather than NaN-filled, while covariate masking is identical. Base predictions (all groups masked) use mean load from the prior 48 weeks (Chronos-2) and one year (TabPFN-TS). Third, standard Shapley values are computed by summing weighted marginal contributions across all 128 coalitions. The exact formula (Equation 1) is used — not an approximation. Evaluation: the forecasting task is 24-hour day-ahead prediction at hourly granularity over the test year (October 2024 – September 2025) for German TSO TransnetBW, with 10 years of historical load (January 2015 – September 2025) from the ENTSO-E Transparency Platform enriched with ERA5 reanalysis temperature and irradiance for Baden-Württemberg. SHAP explanations are generated for forecasts made at midnight (one explanation per hour, covering the full test year without overlap). Three critical design decisions: (1) the 4-bucket temporal grouping is domain-motivated but not ablated against finer granularity; (2) the base-prediction choice differs between models and is not ablated; (3) the use of median of the probabilistic distribution as the point forecast is reasonable for operational use but limits the analysis to point SHAP. Key Results On the one-year held-out test set: Chronos-2 (covariate-informed, 52-week context) achieves MAE 156.2 MW / RMSE 229.1 MW / MAPE 2.44%; TabPFN-TS (covariate-informed) achieves MAE 163.2 MW / RMSE 250.5 MW / MAPE 2.55%; a Transformer trained on 8.75 years of domain data achieves MAE 150.2 MW / RMSE 206.8 MW / MAPE 2.34% (best overall); a Transformer trained on 1 year achieves MAE 213.4 MW / MAPE 3.32%; a same-type-day persistence baseline achieves MAE 325.9 MW / MAPE 5.24%. Covariate inclusion improves zero-shot MAE by 27.0% for Chronos-2 and 31.5% for TabPFN-TS. Explanation runtime: Chronos-2 generates one full daily explanation in ~5.12 seconds; TabPFN-TS in ~367 seconds (~6 minutes). SHAP feature importance assigns past load 89% (Chronos-2) and 87% (TabPFN-TS) of total attribution; among covariates, holiday ranks highest (~4.5–6.5%), followed by temperature (~3.5–3.8%), then irradiance (~2.7%). Covariate dependence plots show physically plausible behavior: load decreases on holidays, increases with temperature drops below 15°C (electric heating), and increases when irradiance drops (PV offset reduction). Context length ablations show Chronos-2 plateaus around 16 weeks (MAE ~156 MW) while TabPFN-TS requires 52 weeks to reach competitive performance (MAE drops from 549 MW at 1 week to 163 MW at 52 weeks). All weather inputs use ERA5 reanalysis (perfect hindcast), so reported accuracy is an upper bound on real operational performance — a caveat the authors state explicitly. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The novel contribution is the architecture-agnostic SHAP adaptation that exploits TSFM input flexibility to eliminate background sampling, yielding exact Shapley values with 128 forward passes regardless of context length or feature count within each group. The temporal grouping idea is adapted from TimeSHAP/WindowSHAP; the Shapley framework is standard. The empirical finding — that zero-shot covariate-informed TSFMs nearly match a domain-trained Transformer on a full year of real TSO operational data — is a genuinely useful validation for the energy forecasting community, not a standard benchmark result. Rigor & Reproducibility Code and dataset are publicly available at https://github.com/KIT-IAI/SHAP4TSFMs. The test set is one full held-out year of real operational data, which is stronger than synthetic or academic benchmark evaluation. However, no ablation isolates the impact of the 4-bucket temporal grouping design — finer-grained bucketing might reveal different attribution patterns. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple seeds are reported; all experiments are single-run. Crucially, there is no validation that the coarse 7-group SHAP attributions are faithful representations of fine-grained feature importance — the paper assumes grouping preserves attributions but does not test this against full-feature SHAP on a small window. The Transformer baseline is deliberately small (2 layers, 128 hidden dim, 4 attention heads); a larger trained baseline might have closed the performance gap. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2604.28149v1, submitted April 2026), not yet peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Both models run on standard hardware without specialized infrastructure; Chronos-2 requires ~66 ms per forecast and ~5 seconds per explanation, suitable for hourly operational scheduling. - Efficient: Chronos-2's 128-call explanation protocol is 80× faster than naive full SHAP on long contexts; TabPFN-TS requires ~6 minutes per daily explanation, borderline for operational use but acceptable for day-ahead scheduling where forecasts are generated hours in advance. - Effective: Chronos-2 achieves near-parity with a fully trained domain Transformer on real TSO data, and SHAP attributions align with known physical relationships, providing plausible evidence of operationally useful behavior; the ERA5 perfect-forecast weather assumption inflates the reported numbers. - Scalable: Only tested on a single TSO region (~14 GW peak); extension to multiple regions, lower-voltage grids, or covariate sets beyond 3 features (which would increase 2^N beyond 128) is claimed but not demonstrated. The work delivers a runnable open-source codebase with full dataset access. Strengths - The masking-based SHAP adaptation is architecturally clean and broadly applicable: any TSFM that accepts variable context length and optional covariates can be instrumented with this approach without modifying model internals or training procedures. - Near-parity of zero-shot Chronos-2 with a trained Transformer on a full year of real TSO operational data — not a standard benchmark — provides credible, practically meaningful evidence for the deployment potential of covariate-informed TSFMs in energy systems. - Covariate dependence plots (Figure 2) are methodologically well-designed: using temperature difference (relative to prior day) rather than absolute temperature as the x-axis correctly captures the change-signal the model responds to, producing interpretable directional attribution patterns consistent with physical domain knowledge. - Full-year local explanation panels (Figures 7/8) enable systematic inspection of how attribution patterns evolve across seasons and holiday events, going beyond cherry-picked examples to demonstrate explanation consistency at operational scale. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: ERA5 reanalysis weather is used as a proxy for perfect weather forecasts, so reported accuracy represents an upper bound on real operational performance. The study is limited to one TSO region. Analysis is restricted to point forecasts; probabilistic forecast explanation is deferred to future work. TabPFN-TS explanation runtime (~6 minutes per day) may be prohibitive for some operational contexts. The current 4-bucket temporal grouping is coarse; finer granularity requires approximate SHAP sampling, not explored here. The authors confront these limitations directly and do not minimize them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No SHAP fidelity validation: the 7-group coarsening could mask important within-bucket heterogeneity (e.g., contributions from a specific anomalous hour within the short-term window) or produce distorted attributions if temporal contributions are non-uniform within a bucket. The paper never verifies that grouped SHAP values correspond to fine-grained attributions computed on a shorter, tractable sub-problem. - The base-prediction definition differs between models (48 weeks vs. 1 year) and is not ablated; since SHAP values are measured relative to the base prediction, this inconsistency makes cross-model SHAP magnitude comparisons less interpretable. - No SHAP comparison against the trained Transformer baseline — it is unknown whether the zero-shot TSFM explanations are more or less consistent with domain knowledge than the supervised model's explanations, which would be the most informative comparison for an XAI paper. - The Transformer baseline is small (128 hidden dim, 2 layers); a state-of-the-art supervised time series Transformer would be a stronger competitor and might close the performance gap more definitively. - The scalability claim — that the method generalizes to larger covariate sets and other TSFMs — is not demonstrated; 10 covariates would require 2^13 = 8192 forward passes for exact SHAP, making approximate methods necessary, but no analysis of this tradeoff is provided. Bottom Line A practically useful and immediately deployable contribution for energy-systems ML practitioners: the masking-based SHAP adaptation is principled, architecture-agnostic, and backed by credible zero-shot performance on real TSO operational data — but the absence of SHAP fidelity validation and the perfect-weather assumption are real limitations that a practitioner must account for before trusting the explanations in a live operational setting.
Search Results 2604.28149 PDF
#47 — Innovative (70)

X2SAM: Any Segmentation in Images and Videos (16 ▲ )

Hao Wang, Limeng Qiao, Chi Zhang, Lin Ma, Guanglu Wan, Xiangyuan Lan, Xiaodan Liang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 6 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces X2SAM, a unified segmentation MLLM that extends image-centric any-segmentation to video by coupling Qwen3-VL-4B with a language-conditioned Mask Memory module, supporting 7 image and 7 video segmentation task types from a single conversational interface that accepts both textual and visual (point/box) prompts. - Why it's important: Existing segmentation MLLMs are structurally siloed — image models cannot handle video, video models lack visual prompt support, and none spans both modalities in one interface — fragmenting development and precluding deployment in applications that require the full task range. - How it does it: The Mask Memory stores MLLM-conditioned guided vision features (not raw pixel features) from preceding frames in a FIFO cache, then cross-attends them to the current frame before mask decoding, propagating semantic grounding temporally rather than relying on low-level appearance matching. - The good: On video reasoning segmentation (ReVOS), X2SAM achieves 69.9 J&F overall, outperforming HyperSeg by +14.2 absolute points; on video referring segmentation it achieves 78.5/79.0 J&F on Ref-YT21/Ref-DV17, exceeding UniPixel-7B by +7.5 and +2.6 J&F respectively. - The missing: The new V-VGD benchmark is derived from the same YT-VIS19 and VIPSeg datasets used in training (reformatted, not held out), making the strong V-VGD numbers an in-distribution measurement rather than evidence of genuine visual grounding transfer. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building unified multimodal segmentation systems, particularly for the Mask Memory design and joint training strategy; less essential for specialists in optimized video object segmentation, where SAM2-H still leads by ~15 J&F.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile given the unified-system contribution and released code. Practical Impact (80) drives the score — broadest task coverage of any segmentation MLLM with public code, and substantial gains over HyperSeg/X-SAM/UniPixel across 12+ tasks. Completeness (70) is helped by direct, unminimized acknowledgment of the FIFO memory and generalist-specialist limitations. Rigor (70) is solid on ablations but the V-VGD benchmark is derived from training data (in-distribution), inference cost is not reported, and there are no error bars. Novelty (60) reflects principled engineering integration rather than a new paradigm. Composite: 0.15*60 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*65 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.30*80 ≈ 70.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Practical visual understanding systems require pixel-level segmentation across both static images and dynamic video, driven by natural conversational instructions and interactive visual cues. Foundation models (SAM, SAM2) produce high-quality masks but cannot interpret language. Segmentation MLLMs like LISA close the language gap but are restricted to images without visual prompt support. Video extensions (VISA, VideoLISA) add temporal text-to-mask generation but lack visual prompts and image-video unification. The result is a fragmented landscape where no single model handles the combined space of (textual + visual prompts) × (images + videos) across diverse segmentation task types — a prerequisite for a practically deployable general-purpose system. Key Idea: The core insight is to condition temporal memory on MLLM-guided features rather than raw visual appearance. After the LLM produces a `<SEG>` token embedding that semantically grounds the target, the Mask Decoder generates a mask and the Memory Encoder encodes the downsampled vision features together with the current mask logits into a guided vision feature. This guided feature — already modulated by semantic grounding — is stored in the FIFO bank and cross-attended by subsequent frames. Unlike SAM2 which propagates appearance features, X2SAM propagates semantically aware features, so temporal consistency is maintained in a space aligned with the language instruction. This allows the same `<SEG>`-token-conditioned decoding pipeline used for images to extend directly to video without a separate temporal architecture. Methodology: Training has two stages. Stage 1 (Agnostic Segmentor Pre-training): the mask decoder (adapted from Mask2Former, with Token-to-Image and Query-to-Image attention modules, zero-initialized T2I weights) is trained on SA-1B with BCE + Dice loss (λ=5.0 each) to learn class-agnostic shape and boundary priors. Stage 2 (Unified Joint Training): the full model — Qwen3-VL-4B backbone (LoRA, r=128, α=256), SAM2 image encoder, projectors, mask decoder, and Mask Memory — is jointly optimized on 14 segmentation tasks plus image and video chat. A dimension-shifting pipeline transposes the B×T×H×W×3 input, processes each frame independently through the mask encoder, and re-assembles the temporal output via the memory module. Modality-aware batching uses B=1 for video (grad accumulation every 4 steps) and 4× multiplier for images; temporal-aware sampling groups same-length clips to minimize padding. The joint loss is Lar for chat tasks and Lar + Lmask + Lcls for segmentation tasks. Three critical design decisions: (1) zero-initialization of T2I attention — random init degrades image metrics; (2) multi-scale mask+class guidance in memory — single-scale flat memory yields only marginal gains (+0.5 J&F on V-Rea. vs. baseline) while the full guided design yields +17.2 J&F; (3) memory size K=6 rather than K=8 — larger caches introduce noisy temporal cues and degrade V-Ref/V-Rea. All three are supported by ablation. Key Results: On video reasoning segmentation (ReVOS), X2SAM achieves 69.9 J&F overall, surpassing HyperSeg (55.7) by +14.2 and VideoLISA (not reported on ReVOS) substantially. On video referring segmentation, Ref-YT21 78.5 J&F and Ref-DV17 79.0 J&F, exceeding UniPixel-7B (71.0/76.4) by +7.5/+2.6. On image reasoning segmentation, 64.5 cIoU / 71.1 gIoU on ReasonSeg validation, exceeding HyperSeg (56.7/59.2) by +7.8/+11.9. Video GCG achieves 75.8 mIoU on V-GLaMM, +21.5 over VideoGLaMM (54.3). Image open-vocabulary (ADE20K A150) 31.2 PQ / 38.2 mIoU, substantially exceeding X-SAM (20.9/28.8) and ODISE (22.6/29.9). Video VGD box prompt (YT-VIS19) 74.4 AP vs. SAM2-H 54.0 AP. Image COCO panoptic: 54.1 PQ, slightly below X-SAM (54.7) and Mask2Former-L (57.8). Video object segmentation (YT-VOS19): 74.0 J&F vs. SAM2-H 88.8 — a persistent 14.8-point gap to specialized VOS. Image chat (MMBench): 83.5, exceeding X-SAM (69.3) and LLaVA-OV (80.8). All main experiments trained for 1 epoch on 32 NVIDIA H800 GPUs (3.3K GPU hours). Baselines are current and include recent CVPR/ECCV/NeurIPS papers. Evaluated on standard public benchmarks. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The primary novelty is an engineered system integration: the language-conditioned Mask Memory module that couples semantic grounding with temporal propagation is a genuine technical contribution distinct from SAM2's low-level memory or X-SAM's frame-wise decoding. The V-VGD benchmark formalizes video visual grounded segmentation with interactive prompts, which was previously unmeasured. The joint training strategy with dimension-shifting and modality-aware batching is a practical engineering contribution. The overall framework is a principled extension of X-SAM to video, not a novel algorithmic paradigm. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation coverage is thorough: T2I attention initialization, joint training variants, Mask Memory design components (progressive ablation of single-scale → mask-guided → class-guided → multi-scale), memory size (1–8 frames), region sampler feature source and kernel size, agnostic segmentor data source, and MLLM backbone choice. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported, consistent with norms in this area but limiting statistical interpretability. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/wanghao9610/X2SAM. Model weights not explicitly confirmed as released in the paper. Preprint (arXiv:2605.00891v1, May 2026), not yet peer-reviewed. Key concern: the V-VGD evaluation benchmark is derived from YT-VIS19 and VIPSeg training data by reformatting annotations — this is a within-distribution evaluation rather than a genuine held-out test of video visual grounding. Deployability: Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS. - Practical: Built on Qwen3-VL-4B + SAM2 components; training requires 32 H800 GPUs; inference hardware requirements (VRAM, latency) are not stated, limiting practical assessment. - Efficient: Training cost 3.3K GPU hours, 36.5% below naive joint training (5.2K hours); per-sample inference FLOPs and latency not reported, which is a meaningful omission for a system paper. - Effective: Strong evidence across 12 of 14 task types; the persistent gap to SAM2-H on pure video object segmentation (74.0 vs. 88.8 J&F) and to specialized image-only models on some image tasks shows the generalist-specialist tradeoff has not been closed. - Scalable: Tested on standard benchmarks with clips of 8–64 frames; the fixed FIFO memory (K=6) is finite and the authors explicitly flag failure risk for long videos with occlusions; no evaluation on temporal scales beyond training. The paper delivers a released software artifact (code repository). Strengths: - Broadest task coverage of any published segmentation MLLM: 7 image + 7 video task types with both textual and visual prompts in one model, where closest competitors (HyperSeg, X-SAM) cover at most 5+4 or 7+0 respectively. - The Mask Memory design is principled: ablations confirm that naive single-scale temporal memory is nearly useless (+0.5–1.1 J&F), while the full mask+class guided multi-scale design yields +17.2 J&F on V-Rea. (36.5→53.5 All), demonstrating that the specific architectural choice drives the video gains. - General capability preservation: X2SAM achieves 83.5 on MMBench and 74.4 on VideoMME — competitive with or exceeding dedicated chat models — demonstrating that segmentation fine-tuning did not degrade the LLM backbone's language understanding, which is non-trivial. - Open-vocabulary segmentation improves markedly over predecessor: 31.2 PQ vs. X-SAM's 20.9 on ADE20K, a 49% relative gain, suggesting the video training data provides useful semantic diversity for open-vocabulary generalization. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly identify three limitations: (1) unified training over heterogeneous image/video data remains computationally expensive; (2) the fixed-size FIFO memory may be insufficient for long videos with prolonged occlusions or large appearance changes; (3) the model may still lag behind specialized models on narrowly focused tasks. These are confronted directly and without minimization, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The V-VGD benchmark is the most notable concern: derived from YT-VIS19 and VIPSeg by annotation reformatting, not from a disjoint video corpus, making V-VGD evaluation an in-distribution measurement rather than generalization evidence. The 74.4 AP on YT-VIS19 V-VGD cannot be interpreted as zero-shot visual grounding performance. Additionally, inference cost (FLOPs, latency, GPU memory) is entirely absent, which is critical for a systems paper claiming practical deployment value. The comparison to Qwen3-VL-4B as a direct baseline on video chat is omitted — since that is the exact backbone, any chat degradation from segmentation training cannot be isolated. Training is for exactly 1 epoch with no convergence analysis; it is unclear whether results would improve substantially with longer training or whether the model is underfit. The 8-frame video training clips make long-video behavior (>64 frames) entirely extrapolated rather than measured. Bottom Line: X2SAM is the most task-complete unified segmentation MLLM published to date, with the Mask Memory design delivering concrete and well-ablated video segmentation improvements; practitioners building general-purpose vision systems should read it for the architecture and training strategy, but should treat the V-VGD benchmark numbers with caution given the benchmark's overlap with training data.
Recommended 2605.00891 PDF
#48 — Innovative (70)

Beyond SFT-to-RL: Pre-alignment via Black-Box On-Policy Distillation for Multimodal RL (35 ▲ )

Sudong Wang, Weiquan Huang, Xiaomin Yu, Zuhao Yang, Hehai Lin, Keming Wu, Chaojun Xiao, Chen Chen, Wenxuan Wang, Beier Zhu, Yunjian Zhang, Chengwei Qin · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 6 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PRISM, a three-stage post-training pipeline (SFT → adversarial on-policy distillation alignment → RLVR) for large multimodal models that inserts an explicit distribution-alignment stage between SFT and RLVR to repair the distributional drift SFT introduces. - Why it's important: SFT degrades strong base models by displacing their native capabilities, and downstream RLVR alone cannot recover from this damage — on Qwen3-VL-8B, standard SFT→GRPO barely restores the pre-SFT Instruct baseline, leaving significant performance on the table. - How it does it: Alignment is formulated as a minimax adversarial game between the policy and a Mixture-of-Experts discriminator with dedicated perception and reasoning experts, trained via Bradley-Terry loss on on-policy rollouts versus supervision demonstrations, without requiring access to teacher logits. - The good: PRISM+GRPO outperforms SFT→GRPO by +4.4 and +6.0 average accuracy points on Qwen3-VL-4B/8B across seven benchmarks, and these gains hold across three RL algorithms (GRPO, DAPO, GSPO), with the additional property that PRISM generates higher accuracy using fewer tokens than SFT+GRPO. - The missing: The entire evaluation is confined to a single model family (Qwen3-VL), and the curated supervision corpus is distilled from Gemini 3 Flash (a proprietary model), making it impossible to cleanly disentangle whether gains come from the alignment mechanism or from superior supervision data quality. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers on multimodal post-training pipelines or on-policy distillation, specifically for the heterogeneous drift diagnosis and the MoE discriminator design; practitioners should replicate with non-proprietary supervision data before drawing general conclusions.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) for repositioning on-policy distillation as standalone alignment stage between SFT and RLVR plus heterogeneous MoE discriminator. Rigor (65) — solid ablation suite (MoE, VL, alignment, SFT, data scale), code/data/checkpoints released, but no error bars/seeds, potential train-eval contamination from shared benchmark sources, and no SFT→RLVR baseline using only the curated 113K data to disentangle data-quality from alignment-mechanism. Non-Obviousness (75) — the heterogeneous drift diagnosis and token-efficiency improvement are non-obvious. Completeness (75) — direct limitations section. Resilience (65) — the un-disentangled supervision-quality confound is a real reviewer attack vector. Impact (75) — released artifacts and consistent gains across three RL algorithms. Composite ≈ 0.20·70+0.25·65+0.15·75+0.15·75+0.15·65+0.10·75 = 70.25 → 70.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 65 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: The standard SFT→RLVR post-training recipe for large multimodal models suffers from a structural problem: SFT on demonstration data using uniform token-level imitation pushes the model away from both its original distribution and toward only surface-level imitation of the supervision distribution. This drift is particularly severe for strong base models where SFT displaces existing capabilities rather than supplementing them, and is uniquely heterogeneous in multimodal settings because visual grounding errors and logical reasoning failures degrade in qualitatively different ways. On Qwen3-VL-8B specifically, SFT drops average accuracy from 63.3 to 58.1 points, and SFT→GRPO only recovers to 63.3 — failing to exceed the pre-SFT baseline. No existing work addresses this distributional gap as a first-class problem; prior RL variants operate only within the RL stage without correcting the degraded SFT initialization. Key Idea: The core insight is that SFT-induced distributional drift in multimodal models is heterogeneous — visual grounding and reasoning fail in qualitatively different ways — and therefore requires disentangled corrective signals before RLVR begins, not within RLVR. PRISM makes alignment a standalone intermediate stage formulated as a black-box adversarial game: a Mixture-of-Experts discriminator with one perception expert and one reasoning expert is trained to distinguish on-policy rollouts from supervision demonstrations via Bradley-Terry pairwise loss, and the policy is trained via GRPO to generate responses the discriminator cannot distinguish from supervision. Because the game operates at the response level rather than the logit level, it requires only samples from the supervision pool, not logit access to any teacher model. Methodology: PRISM proceeds in three sequential stages. Stage 1: full-parameter SFT (freezing vision tower and projector) on a combined corpus of 107K Gemini 3 Flash-curated samples plus 1.26M public demonstrations (~1.37M total), for 1 epoch, peak LR 1e-5 with cosine schedule, using DeepSpeed ZeRO-2. Stage 2 (alignment): 500 steps of adversarial on-policy distillation. The policy samples N=16 rollouts per prompt; an MoE discriminator assembled from four Qwen3-VL-2B models with top-2 routing assigns separate scores to the visual description component (perception expert Dv) and reasoning trace component (reasoning expert Dr). Combined reward is r(x,y) = α·Dv(x,c) + (1−α)·Dr(x,t) with α=0.5. The discriminator minimizes Bradley-Terry loss on supervision/rollout pairs; the policy maximizes group-normalized advantages via GRPO. KL regularization is explicitly disabled so the policy can move freely away from the SFT checkpoint. Both experts are co-optimized with the policy throughout to prevent reward staleness. Stage 3 (RLVR): 1500 steps on 2K difficulty-filtered samples (pass rate 0.2–0.8 under the aligned policy), using verifiable reward rv = racc + rfmt (0.8/0.2 weighting). The RL algorithm is interchangeable; GRPO, DAPO, and GSPO are all evaluated. Three critical design decisions: (1) removing KL regularization is well-justified — it would actively oppose drift correction; (2) MoE vs. single discriminator is ablated and shows −3.4 avg drop without MoE; (3) co-training discriminator with policy is justified to avoid the known reward staleness failure mode of fixed reward models. Key Results: PRISM+GRPO vs. SFT→GRPO on Qwen3-VL-4B: 66.2 vs. 61.8 average (+4.4 pts) across MathVista, MathVerse, MathVision, WeMath, MMMU, MMMU-Pro, and HallusionBench. On Qwen3-VL-8B: 69.3 vs. 63.3 (+6.0 pts). Largest individual gains are on MathVision (4B: 45.4 vs. 35.5; 8B: 52.0 vs. 37.1) and WeMath (4B: 82.9 vs. 77.8; 8B: 86.4 vs. 79.7). Gains are consistent across DAPO (+4.5/+3.7 avg) and GSPO (+4.2/+5.4 avg). On 8B, SFT→GRPO returns exactly to the Instruct baseline (63.3), while PRISM+GRPO exceeds it by 6.0 points. The compute-matched baseline (SFT→RLVR with extra RL steps equal to alignment budget) is used throughout, making gains attributable to the alignment mechanism rather than extra training. A non-obvious token efficiency result: PRISM+GRPO achieves 45.4% on MathVision using 2,889 avg tokens vs. 35.5% at 5,724 tokens for SFT+GRPO, suggesting the alignment stage teaches more concise reasoning rather than inflating chain-of-thought length. No error bars or multiple random seeds are reported. This is a preprint; results are self-reported. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET. The method is genuinely novel in repositioning on-policy distillation as a standalone intermediate alignment stage between SFT and RLVR (prior OPD work treats distillation as the terminal objective), and in the heterogeneous MoE discriminator design for multimodal drift. The 113K Gemini 3 Flash-curated multimodal reasoning corpus is a new dataset contribution. Individual components — adversarial OPD, Bradley-Terry preference loss, MoE routing, GRPO — are all known; the novelty lies in their specific compositional arrangement and the underlying motivating analysis of multimodal distributional drift. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and targeted: MoE vs. dense discriminator (−3.4 avg), VL vs. text-only discriminator (−3.9 avg), removing alignment stage (−4.4 avg), removing SFT (−16.8 avg), SFT data scale 107K vs. 1.37M (−3.7 avg). No error bars, significance tests, or multiple random seeds are reported for any comparison. Code, data, and model checkpoints are released at https://github.com/XIAO4579/PRISM. Methodological concern: the curated supervision data is drawn from the same problem sources that partially overlap with evaluation benchmarks (MathVista, MathVerse, etc. are described as source benchmarks for data collection), which is a potential benchmark contamination risk that is not addressed. The α=0.5 MoE weighting is not ablated despite the paper's own evidence that perception and reasoning experts converge at different rates. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Training requires 8× NVIDIA H100-SXM5-80GB GPUs; the alignment stage additionally maintains four Qwen3-VL-2B discriminator modules in memory simultaneously with the policy, making this feasible for industrial labs but expensive for academic settings. - Efficient: The alignment stage adds 500 joint policy+discriminator training steps and the discriminator requires assembling four 2B models; the paper acknowledges increased memory/compute overhead but does not quantify wall-clock cost or GPU-hour overhead versus standard SFT→RLVR. - Effective: Consistently improves over the compute-matched SFT→RLVR baseline across all three RL algorithms and both model scales; evidence for the core claim is strong within the tested setting. - Scalable: Tested only at 4B and 8B parameter scales; generalization to larger models (e.g., 32B, 72B) is identified as future work but not demonstrated. The work delivers code, data, and trained checkpoints; it is not stop-at-paper-form. Strengths: - The heterogeneous drift diagnosis — showing empirically that SFT degrades strong instruct models and that RLVR alone cannot recover this — is a genuine intellectual contribution that explains a practically significant phenomenon and motivates the entire pipeline. - The compute-matched baseline design (SFT→RLVR with extra RL steps budgeted to equal the alignment stage) is methodologically honest; it prevents attributing gains to extra compute. - The token efficiency result (better accuracy with fewer tokens after PRISM) is non-obvious and suggests a qualitative improvement in reasoning behavior rather than simply longer chain-of-thought generation. - The MoE discriminator ablation (−3.4 avg vs. same-parameter dense discriminator) provides concrete evidence that disentangled perception/reasoning feedback is the mechanism rather than discriminator capacity. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge: (1) additional compute cost from jointly training policy and discriminator; (2) the structured response format requirement (explicit caption + reasoning trace decomposition) may not generalize to tasks without natural decomposition into perception and reasoning components; (3) distribution alignment analysis uses structural proxies (step counts, caption item counts) rather than direct distributional measures such as embedding-space divergences, which they note introduces its own model-selection bias. Future work targets broader task formats, larger base models, and shortening or amortizing the alignment stage. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most serious unacknowledged issue is the conflation of supervision data quality with alignment mechanism: all three stages use Gemini 3 Flash-curated data, and the paper includes no baseline that applies the standard SFT→RLVR pipeline using only the curated 113K high-quality data (without alignment). This would isolate whether gains come from higher-quality Gemini-distilled traces or from the alignment mechanism itself. Additionally: (1) the evaluation is limited to Qwen3-VL exclusively — whether the pipeline transfers to other multimodal architectures is entirely unknown; (2) the source benchmarks for data curation (MathVista, MathVerse, WeMath, etc.) overlap with evaluation benchmarks, and the paper does not confirm that evaluation test splits were excluded from the curated training set, creating a potential benchmark contamination risk; (3) the fixed α=0.5 for combining perception and reasoning rewards is not ablated despite Figure 3 showing that the two experts converge at qualitatively different rates, suggesting task- or domain-adaptive weighting could matter. Bottom Line: Practitioners building post-training pipelines for multimodal reasoning models should read this paper for the alignment-stage insight and MoE discriminator design, but should replicate with non-proprietary supervision data and on a second model family before treating the approach as generally validated.
Recommended 2604.28123 PDF
#49 — Innovative (70)

Evaluating Temporal Consistency in Multi-Turn Language Models

Yash Kumar Atri, Steven L. Johnson, Tom Hartvigsen · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ChronoScope, a 1.47-million-chain diagnostic benchmark built from Wikidata that measures whether language models maintain, override, or transfer implicit temporal scope across multi-turn dialogue. - Why it's important: Interactive deployments (legal research, education, historical analysis) require that a model established in a 2010 context continue reasoning about 2010 facts in follow-up turns — failures silently corrupt downstream reasoning while appearing superficially correct. - How it does it: Chains are constructed deterministically from temporally annotated Wikidata snapshots (2022–2025) using property-specific question templates; drift is measured by checking whether incorrect answers match present-day Wikidata facts rather than the historically correct ones. - The good: The scale–drift paradox finding is the most credible and surprising result: stronger proprietary models (ChatGPT-4, Gemini-2.5-Flash) exhibit higher drift rates than smaller open-weight models, suggesting larger pretraining memorization of present-day facts amplifies resistance to implicit contextual override. - The missing: The paper never establishes a single-turn factual baseline — it does not show what fraction of the historical facts each model actually knows when asked directly with explicit temporal context, making it impossible to disentangle scope-maintenance failure from simple factual ignorance. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on multi-turn reasoning, dialogue state tracking, or LLM evaluation — the benchmark design and scale–drift paradox are genuinely useful; skip if you want a path to fixing the problem, as the paper is purely diagnostic.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) — 1.47M-chain benchmark with well-defined drift metric is new. Rigor (68) — deterministic construction, paraphrase robustness check, public release; reduced for preprint, no single-turn factual baseline, proprietary model uncontrolled. Non-Obviousness (78) — scale-drift paradox (larger models drift more) is genuinely counter-intuitive. Completeness (62) — missing single-turn baseline is acknowledged gap. Resilience (62) — only zero-shot tested, knowledge-vs-scope confound not resolved. Impact (68) — dataset and scripts released. Weighted ≈ 69.5.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 62 Resilience 62 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Language models deployed in chat systems are expected to honor an implicit principle of contextual persistence: once a temporal frame is established ("In 2010, who was the UK Prime Minister?"), follow-up questions ("What policies did they produce?") should be answered within that frame without requiring the user to restate the year. Prior temporal QA benchmarks (TempQuestions, TimeQA, TimeR1, PAT-Questions) all resolve temporal scope independently per query and do not test whether models carry that scope forward. Multi-turn benchmarks (HotpotQA, CoQA, DREAM) assume temporally stable facts. No benchmark simultaneously requires (i) multi-turn interaction, (ii) implicit scope persistence, and (iii) explicit drift annotation. The consequence is a systematic blind spot: a model can appear accurate on single-turn temporal QA while silently substituting present-day facts whenever temporal markers are omitted. Key Idea: The core insight is the separation of two failure modes that existing benchmarks conflate: factual ignorance (the model does not know the historical fact) versus temporal scope drift (the model knows the historical fact but retrieves a present-day version because implicit temporal context is not maintained across turns). By recording both the historically correct answer and the present-day Wikidata answer for each turn, the benchmark can attribute incorrect predictions to scope drift rather than knowledge gaps. The design choice to evaluate under three settings — Gold Context (oracle prior turns), Self-Conditioned (model's own prior outputs), and Questions Only (no history) — cleanly triangulates the source of failure. Methodology: Construction proceeds in three deterministic stages. Stage 1: four annual Wikidata snapshots (2022–2025) are materialized. Stage 2: for each snapshot and quarterly anchor date, truth tables are built from temporally scoped claims (start/end/point-in-time qualifiers), filtered for deprecated statements and self-loops, and deduplicated per (subject, property) pair by Wikidata rank. Stage 3: anchored facts are converted to NL chains via fixed property-specific templates, yielding 11 chain families — Carryover, Carryover-Then, Scope Switch, Cross-Entity Then, Multi-Turn Chain, Change Point, Distinct Count, Interval Reasoning, Interval Change, Temporal Narrative, Bridged Multi-PID — each encoding a distinct pattern of temporal state evolution (fixed, override, cross-turn, aggregative, branched, evolving, interval). Chains are annotated with drift-eligible flags when a distinct present-day answer exists. Evaluation metrics include Acc@1, Final@1 (final turn only), Chain@1 (strict: all turns correct), and Drift@1 (rate of present-day substitution on drift-eligible turns). All 9 models are evaluated zero-shot. A robustness check paraphrases 10,000 chains via GPT-5 while preserving entities, anchors, and gold answers. Critical design decisions: excluding Turn 1 from evaluation (it explicitly states the year, so including it inflates accuracy without testing persistence — well-justified); using QIDs for deduplication rather than surface strings (well-justified); restricting to entity-centric Wikidata properties (justified for reproducibility, but limits scope — see Gaps). Key Results: Under Gold Context (oracle prior turns), ChatGPT-4 achieves the highest Acc@1 at 0.441 and Final@1 at 0.516; Gemini-2.5-Flash reaches 0.384/0.446; open-weight 7B models cluster between 0.234 and 0.306 Acc@1. Strict chain accuracy (Chain@1) is uniformly low: 0.032–0.089 under Gold Context, collapsing to 0.000–0.015 under Self-Conditioned evaluation — a near-total failure for every model tested. Drift rates are highest for proprietary models: ChatGPT-3.5 reaches 0.346 drift in Questions Only, ChatGPT-4 reaches 0.292, while open-weight models typically stay below 0.085 under Gold Context. The scale–drift paradox — larger models drift more — is the most counter-intuitive finding. Under Gold Context, Final@1 improves with chain length up to 4–5 turns (Qwen-2.5-7B: 0.48 at L=2, 0.57 at L=5) but degrades for longer chains; under Self-Conditioned evaluation, Final@1 collapses for L≥4 across all models. Paraphrasing leaves performance nearly unchanged (within 1–2 pp), confirming the failure is not template-exploitation. Per chain family, Scope Switch is by far the hardest (Qwen-2.5-7B Acc@1: 0.027, Drift: 0.187 under Gold Context); Interval Reasoning is relatively tractable (0.675 Acc@1). All results are from a preprint and have not undergone peer review. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The benchmark itself is the primary contribution: no prior dataset combines multi-turn interaction, implicit scope persistence, explicit drift annotation, and knowledge-graph grounding at this scale (1.47M chains versus the next-largest comparable dataset at 200K). The empirical finding that scale amplifies drift (rather than suppressing it) is genuinely novel and non-obvious. The formalization of temporal scope stability as a distinct property from factual accuracy is a useful conceptual contribution, though it builds naturally on discourse theory (Reichenbach 1947, Kamp & Reyle 1993). Rigor & Reproducibility: Dataset, evaluation scripts, prompt templates, and metadata are publicly released at https://github.com/yashkumaratri/ChronoScope. Construction is fully deterministic from fixed Wikidata snapshots, and chain sampling uses fixed seeds — exact reproducibility is claimed and plausible. No fine-tuning or prompt optimization is performed; all models are evaluated zero-shot with identical prompts, which is methodologically clean. No ablations are needed (the benchmark is not a trained system), but the three evaluation settings cleanly isolate different failure modes. The paraphrasing robustness check on 10,000 chains adequately addresses the template-exploitation concern. One methodological concern: proprietary model outputs (ChatGPT, Gemini) are noted as uncontrollable in terms of decoding parameters and internal memory, so those results should be treated as approximate. The paper acknowledges that some models (LLaMA-3 variants, Phi-4, Nemotron) were omitted from the main tables because they failed even Turn 1 — reporting their Turn 1 accuracy separately would have been more transparent than omitting them entirely. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile — the paper runs experiments on existing models against a new benchmark. - Practical: ChronoScope evaluation requires querying existing LLMs via API or local inference; no special hardware beyond what is needed to run the evaluated models. - Efficient: Benchmark construction is a one-time deterministic pipeline from Wikidata dumps; inference cost scales with the number of models and chains evaluated (subset sampling to 100K chains per setting is used). - Effective: The three-setting evaluation design cleanly measures the stated property (temporal scope stability); the drift metric specifically identifies present-biased substitution rather than generic incorrectness, which is the right operationalization. - Scalable: The benchmark is already at 1.47M chains; adding new model families requires only API calls or local inference runs with fixed prompts — straightforward to extend. The paper does not deliver a software library or trained model, only a dataset and evaluation suite. Strengths: - The three-setting evaluation design (Gold/Self-Conditioned/Questions Only) is well-constructed: it isolates scope reasoning from error propagation and from factual ignorance, yielding clean causal attribution. - The scale–drift paradox is a genuine empirical discovery: the counterintuitive finding that GPT-4 and Gemini drift more than 7B open-weight models challenges the common assumption that scale fixes reasoning failures. - The drift metric is precisely defined: by requiring that the present-day answer exist and differ from the historical answer, it distinguishes temporally misaligned retrieval from hallucination. - Deterministic, KG-grounded construction avoids annotation noise and provides unambiguous ground truth across 1.47M examples — a significant engineering advantage over human-annotated benchmarks. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge four limitations honestly: (1) the benchmark covers only entity-centric Wikidata facts and does not address narrative, procedural, counterfactual, or speculative temporal reasoning; (2) template-generated questions lack the pragmatic ambiguity of natural dialogue; (3) present-day answers are fixed to a Wikidata snapshot and may become stale; (4) some models (Phi-4, Nemotron, older LLaMA-3) were excluded from main tables due to low Turn 1 accuracy. The paper explicitly notes that ChronoScope identifies the failure mode without prescribing an architectural fix. These are confronted directly and constitute a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most significant unacknowledged gap is the absence of a single-turn baseline per model: the paper claims failures are not due to missing knowledge, but this rests on the drift metric (matching present-day answers), not on direct measurement of each model's historical factual accuracy in isolation. A Turn-1-only accuracy table broken out by chain family and anchor year would have closed this gap. Second, the paper evaluates only zero-shot prompting; chain-of-thought or explicit temporal anchoring instructions ("always maintain the year stated in Turn 1") might dramatically change results — their absence means the benchmark's contribution to understanding trainable failures is limited. Third, the Wikidata property coverage is not characterized: which properties are included, how many, and whether they skew toward any particular domain or fact type (e.g., positional facts like "head of government" may be overrepresented). Fourth, for proprietary models, the paper cannot control system prompts or internal context-window management, which could cause the results to understate model capability. Fifth, the interaction between Wikidata training data cutoff and snapshot years is not analyzed — models trained on data through 2024 may have memorized the 2022 and 2023 Wikidata facts themselves, which confounds the knowledge-vs-scope-drift distinction. Bottom Line: ChronoScope is a well-engineered, large-scale benchmark that fills a real gap in multi-turn LLM evaluation, and the scale–drift paradox result alone is worth reading about — but practitioners should treat the low absolute accuracy numbers cautiously until a single-turn factual baseline establishes how much of the failure is knowledge-versus-scope, and until non-zero-shot prompting strategies are tested.
Recommended 2604.23051 PDF
#50 — Innovative (70)

LLM -Emu: Native Runtime Emulation of LLM Inference via Profile-Driven Sampling

Wei Da, Evangelia Kalyvianaki · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LLM-Emu, a vLLM plugin that replaces only the GPU forward execution path with a density-aware 2D latency oracle and timer-resolved Future, enabling wall-clock online LLM serving emulation without GPU hardware. - Why it's important: Evaluating LLM serving systems under realistic online conditions (dynamic arrivals, queueing, continuous batching, KV-cache pressure) requires expensive GPU runs, and existing simulators either re-implement scheduler logic (creating drift risk) or operate offline, precluding direct exercise of live HTTP traffic. - How it does it: LLM-Emu hooks at the vLLM executor boundary, intercepts each per-step GPU call, samples a latency from a 2D (total_tokens × concurrency) profile via adaptive Shepard-weighted nearest-neighbor pooling, and resolves a timer-based Future with synthetic token IDs — leaving all other vLLM code untouched. - The good: Across 6 hardware/model/workload cells and 5 request rates, TPOT and ITL stay within 4.8% absolute error, E2E latency within 5.3%, and output throughput within 1.9% of real vLLM, with errors explainable by known oracle limitations. - The missing: The profile is collected from the same workload distribution used for evaluation, making it unclear how well LLM-Emu generalizes to out-of-distribution workloads; no multi-GPU or multi-node validation is provided. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or benchmarking LLM serving infrastructure who want a cheap, reproducible online experimentation harness tied to real vLLM behavior; less relevant to those studying cluster-level or disaggregated serving.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (78) drives the score — open-sourced vLLM plugin enabling cheap reproducible online emulation, 1.9-5.3% error across diverse cells. Novelty (60) — architectural choice (executor-boundary hook) is the novel point in design space; components are standard. Rigor (60) reduced for in-distribution profile/eval workload, no error bars, no comparison to existing simulators on accuracy. Non-Obviousness (55), Completeness (62), Resilience (60). Weighted: 0.15*60+0.15*60+0.10*55+0.15*62+0.15*60+0.30*78 = 64.8 → 65.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 62 Resilience 60 Impact 78
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Evaluating LLM serving systems under realistic online conditions requires running the full serving stack — dynamic HTTP arrivals, queueing, iteration-level scheduling, KV-cache management — all of which are too expensive to exercise repeatedly on real GPUs. Existing simulators (Vidur, LLMServingSim, APEX, Frontier) address cost but operate offline, re-implement serving-engine schedulers (creating drift as vLLM evolves), and rely on operator-level or learned latency models that require calibration. Revati uses CUDA interposition for time-warped emulation but targets accelerated offline emulation rather than wall-clock online serving, and its implementation is not publicly available. The gap is a serving-native, wall-clock online emulator that exercises the actual vLLM HTTP and scheduling code paths without requiring GPU hardware or a parallel scheduler implementation. Key Idea: The core insight is that the GPU forward pass is the only component that requires actual hardware; everything else in vLLM — admission, scheduling, KV-cache management, output processing, HTTP serving — runs on CPU and can execute unchanged. By hooking at the executor boundary and substituting a latency sample from a 2D (total_tokens, concurrency) empirical profile plus synthetic token IDs, LLM-Emu preserves all scheduling dynamics while eliminating GPU dependence. The two-dimensional profile feature set (total tokens and concurrency) intentionally trades generality for low calibration cost and interpretability. Methodology: LLM-Emu is implemented as a lazy-import hook in `vllm/v1/worker/gpu_worker.py` that installs an emulated execution path during worker initialization, bypassing model loading and real GPU setup entirely. At each scheduler step, the hook routes execution to a density-aware oracle that indexes a pre-built JSON profile by (tt, conc) bucket, split into decode-only and prefill-or-mixed step types. For sparse buckets, Algorithm 1 performs adaptive K-nearest-neighbor expansion sorted by range-normalized 2D distance, accumulating neighboring bucket samples until a reliability floor M is met, then draws a Shepard-weighted sample. The oracle then schedules a threading.Timer to resolve a Future after the predicted delay and returns synthetic token IDs for downstream post-processing. The asynchronous path preserves vLLM's scheduler-worker overlap (Figure 2). Critical design decisions: (1) using total_tokens and concurrency rather than per-operator features — well-justified as capturing the dominant latency variation while remaining interpretable; (2) storing raw latency samples per bucket rather than pre-aggregated statistics — justified to preserve empirical variance; (3) splitting decode-only vs. prefill-or-mixed steps — justified by their different latency characteristics. Profile collection runs a sweep from light load to saturation over 3.5–4.5 GPU-hours, producing ~5.9 MB of JSON with ~276K samples across ~7.3K (tt, conc) buckets. The `--num-gpu-blocks-override` flag aligns KV-cache capacity between real and emulated runs to prevent confounding from block-count variance across boots. Key Results: Evaluated across 6 cells (varying GPU: RTX 8000 / A40; model: Qwen3-4B / 8B / 14B / Llama-3.1-8B; attention backend: FlashInfer / FlashAttention 2; arrival pattern: Poisson / bursty γ=0.25) at 5 request rates (r ∈ {2,4,8,16,32}), using the official vllm bench serve ShareGPT workload with 2000 prompts per rate. TPOT and ITL achieve maximum absolute errors of 4.8% and 4.69% respectively; E2E latency maximum error is 5.22%; output throughput maximum error is 1.88%. TTFT is the weakest metric, with maximum absolute error of 10.41% (bursty workload, r=8), attributed to its sensitivity to admission timing, queue state, and startup effects. The largest non-TTFT errors occur under bursty arrivals (γ=0.25), where queue depth volatility creates batch compositions the static profile captures less well. No baseline simulator (Vidur, Revati, etc.) is directly compared in terms of accuracy metrics — the paper compares against real vLLM ground truth only. This is a preprint; results are not peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. LLM-Emu contributes a working open-source tool (2.5K LOC delta on vLLM 0.18.1) that occupies an unstaked point in the design space: wall-clock online serving emulation with a minimal executor-boundary hook. The individual components (profiling-based latency prediction, K-NN interpolation, timer-resolved futures) are standard; the novelty lies in the architectural decision to hook inside vLLM rather than alongside it and in the empirical validation of this approach's adequacy across varied conditions. Rigor & Reproducibility: Evaluation uses matched seeds and 2000 prompts per rate, and the KV-cache alignment safeguard (`--num-gpu-blocks-override`) is a methodological positive. However, no error bars or confidence intervals are reported — each cell appears to be a single paired run. The profile is collected from the same workload (ShareGPT) used for evaluation, raising a Goodhart-Law-adjacent concern: it is unknown whether the oracle generalizes to workloads with substantially different prompt-length or concurrency distributions. No out-of-distribution workload test is included. Code is open-sourced at https://github.com/AKafakA/llm-emu. No comparison against existing simulators on the same accuracy axis is provided. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — LLM-Emu is a built tool evaluated by deployment against a real serving engine. - Practical: After profiling, LLM-Emu runs entirely CPU-side and requires no GPU; profile collection requires one GPU run of 3.5–4.5 hours per model/hardware/config combination. - Efficient: The runtime footprint is ~2.5K LOC and a 5.9 MB JSON profile; the oracle's per-step overhead is not quantified, but the design explicitly avoids CUDA interception and per-operator modeling. - Effective: Evidence shows it closely tracks real vLLM for TPOT, ITL, E2E, and throughput across the tested configurations; TTFT accuracy is weaker and degrades further under bursty arrivals. - Scalable: Validated only on single-node, single-GPU deployments; multi-GPU and multi-node extension is listed as future work with no results. The paper delivers a software artifact (vLLM plugin + profiling tool) open-sourced at the stated repository. Strengths: - The serving-native hook design is architecturally clean: by intervening only at the executor boundary, LLM-Emu inherits all vLLM scheduler and HTTP logic for free, avoiding the scheduler-drift problem that plagues parallel-implementation simulators. - The timer-resolved Future correctly preserves vLLM's asynchronous scheduler-worker pipelining, a subtle correctness requirement that offline simulators cannot satisfy. - Breadth of validation across model families (Qwen3, Llama), model scales (4B–14B), two GPU SKUs, two attention backends, and two arrival distributions is above average for a systems paper of this length. - Open-source release with a documented one-line switch between real and emulated serving lowers the barrier to adoption and reproduction. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge: (1) validation limited to single-node deployments; (2) profile collection requires several hours of GPU time per configuration; (3) TTFT is less stable due to admission and queue-state sensitivity; (4) offline batch inference path is not evaluated; (5) automated API drift detection across vLLM releases is absent. These are confronted honestly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most significant unacknowledged gap is that the profile and evaluation workloads share the same distribution (ShareGPT), making it impossible to assess oracle fidelity under workload shift — a common real-world scenario (e.g., code generation vs. chat). No comparison to any existing simulator on a common accuracy axis is provided, making it impossible to quantify the accuracy advantage (if any) over Vidur or LLMServingSim. Single-run evaluation without repeated seeds means the reported error figures carry unknown variance. The paper does not report emulation speed relative to real serving (wall-clock ratio), which is a key practical metric for a tool whose primary purpose is to reduce experimentation cost. The `--num-gpu-blocks-override` workaround for KV-cache variance is a methodological patch that masks a potential source of real-world divergence when the emulator is used without knowing the target hardware's block count. Bottom Line: A practitioner studying vLLM scheduling, admission, or serving dynamics gains a useful, reproducible, and cheap experimentation harness — but should treat TTFT results with caution and budget 3.5–4.5 GPU-hours upfront per configuration for profiling.
Search Results 2605.00616 PDF
#51 — Innovative (69)

Marco-MoE: Open Multilingual Mixture-of-Expert Language Models with Efficient Upcycling

Fan Jiang, Yu Zhao, Chenyang Lyu, Tianqi Shi, Yichao Du, Feihu Jiang, Longyue Wang, Weihua Luo · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Marco-MoE, a family of fully open-source sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models built by fine-grained upcycling of a dense 0.6B-parameter checkpoint, yielding models (8B and 17B total params) that activate only 0.6B and 0.86B parameters per token and outperform comparably-sized dense and sparse competitors across English and 29–64 language multilingual benchmarks. - Why it's important: Expanding multilingual language model coverage within a fixed activated-parameter budget degrades per-language quality (the "curse of multilinguality"), yet dense alternatives that escape this constraint require 5–55× more training compute than this work uses. - How it does it: The FFN weight matrices of a pretrained dense model are sliced column-wise into fine-grained sub-experts, replicated with Drop-Upcycling noise injection, and scaled by N^(1/3) to correct the output-magnitude mismatch between a dense summation and a softmax-gated MoE, followed by a 5.1T-token four-stage multilingual curriculum and cascaded on-policy distillation post-training. - The good: Marco-Mini-Instruct (0.86B activated) achieves an English average of 75.5 and multilingual cultural average of 71.0, beating Qwen3-4B-Instruct (73.3 / 69.1) and Gemma3-12B-Instruct (65.8 / 67.7) while using 5–14× fewer activated parameters, at 1.56×10²³ FLOPs vs Qwen3-4B's 8.64×10²³. - The missing: No inference latency or throughput benchmarks are reported, so the wall-clock efficiency advantage of sparse activation relative to dense baselines of matched quality is not quantified. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on efficient multilingual LLMs or MoE upcycling — the fine-grained upcycling recipe, weight-scaling fix, and expert-activation linguistic analysis are all concretely useful; skip if you need a peer-reviewed result or primarily care about reasoning-heavy tasks.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (70) is solid — staged 100B-token annealing ablations, weight-scaling ablation, full release of weights, datasets, and recipes — though only single runs and no error bars, plus a non-thinking-mode comparison that flatters the baseline gap. Practical Impact (80) is the strongest dimension: complete open-source release (weights for multiple models, training data mixtures, post-training recipes) is unusually thorough at this quality level. Novelty (65) and Non-Obviousness (70) come from the principled N^(1/3) weight-scaling correction for fine-grained upcycling and the emergent linguistic-family structure in expert routing. Completeness (70) is helped by an honest limitations section confronting language coverage and modularity, though inference latency — central to the efficiency claim — is not measured. Resilience (65) is moderate; the scaling-factor derivation assumes uniform routing and teacher-family bias from Qwen3 is unaddressed.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Building small LLMs that cover many languages without quality degradation is hard because a fixed parameter budget forces a trade-off between linguistic breadth and per-language depth. Existing solutions either train at prohibitive scale (Qwen3-4B: 36T tokens, Gemma3-4B: 36T tokens) or use dense architectures that lack the architectural flexibility to assign distinct capacity to different language families. Meanwhile, MoE upcycling (converting a pretrained dense model into a sparse MoE) is computationally attractive but prior work replicated entire FFN blocks, producing homogeneous experts that fail to specialize — a poor fit for the diverse structural demands of multilingual processing. Key Idea The core trick is sub-matrix splitting: instead of copying the full FFN weight matrices to initialize each expert (coarse-grained upcycling), the paper partitions each FFN weight along the intermediate dimension into N thin column slices, each of which becomes a separate fine-grained expert. This produces experts with smaller hidden dimension than the source dense FFN (e.g., 384 or 768 vs. 3072), so more of them can be held per layer (232 or 256 total experts) while keeping only 8 active. Because the MoE output is a softmax-weighted sum (magnitude ≈ 1/N of the dense output), they apply a weight scaling factor of λ = N^(1/3) to all sliced matrices before replication, restoring numerical parity with the dense initialization. Methodology The pipeline has four components. (1) Architecture: standard decoder-only transformer (GQA, RMSNorm, SwiGLU, RoPE aligned with Qwen3) with FFN layers replaced by granular MoE layers following DeepSeekMoE. (2) Upcycling: dense Qwen3-0.6B FFN weights are partitioned into N slices → N fine-grained experts; each is replicated M times to reach the target total expert count; Drop-Upcycling randomly zeros indices along the intermediate dimension of each copy and re-initializes those weights from a Gaussian matching the dropped weights' statistics; routers are zero-initialized. Weight scaling λ = N^(1/3) is applied to all projection sub-matrices before replication. (3) Pre-training: 5.1T tokens in four stages using Megatron-LM, Warmup-Stable-Decay schedule, AdamW with load balancing loss (0.01) and router Z-loss (0.001). Stages progressively shift from English-heavy to multilingual-heavy data (62.5% multilingual by Stage 3, 87.5% by Stage 4), with synthetic multilingual QA, STEM, and cultural MCQ data added in later stages; ablations confirm each component's contribution. (4) Post-training: SFT with SLIME framework (1 epoch, ~4000 steps, 64 GPUs), then cascaded on-policy distillation (OPD) — first from Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 (~1900 steps), then from Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct (~1000–1900 steps). OPD trains on student-generated trajectories with dense per-token teacher supervision. Key Results Base models evaluated on 28+ benchmarks via the Light-Eval framework. Marco-Mini-Base (0.86B activated, 17.3B total, 1.56×10²³ FLOPs) achieves English average 63.7 (vs. Qwen3-4B 63.3 at 8.64×10²³ FLOPs), multilingual general 50.9 (vs. Qwen3-4B 48.3), and multilingual cultural 65.0 (vs. Qwen3-4B 65.7 — gap driven by CMMLU 68.0 vs 76.2 and C-Eval 66.0 vs 76.6). Marco-Mini-Global-Base extends to 64 languages (+1.4T tokens) with only 0.1 English degradation (63.6) while improving multilingual advantage to +3.6% over Qwen3-4B. Instruct: Marco-Mini-Instruct scores 75.5 English (Qwen3-4B-Instruct: 73.3), 50.8 multilingual general (first), 71.0 cultural (first). Expert activation correlation analysis (100 documents per language, Pearson correlation of per-layer activation vectors) shows Romance, Slavic, Austronesian, and Indic language families form high-correlation clusters mirroring linguistic phylogenies, while typologically isolated languages (Thai, Arabic, Hebrew) remain low-correlation. Cascaded distillation adds +1.8–2.3 English points and +0.5–1.5 multilingual points across categories. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM / NEW METHOD. The fine-grained sub-matrix splitting upcycling and the N^(1/3) weight-scaling correction are technically novel moves within the known paradigm of MoE upcycling; the multilingual focus with four-stage curriculum and synthetic cultural data generation is an engineering contribution. Applying MoE upcycling to multilingual compact models is presented as a first, and the expert-activation linguistic analysis adds an empirical finding. The OPD cascaded distillation pipeline reuses published components but the full integration is original in scope. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: Tables 2–4 isolate the effect of multilingual QA, STEM, and cultural data using 100B-token annealing runs on Qwen3-4B; Figure 5 ablates weight scaling and upcycling vs. from-scratch training over 100B tokens on a 17B-A0.8B model; Table 8 shows per-stage improvements; Table 12 shows cascaded distillation effects. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported — results are single runs on fixed evaluation sets, which is standard for LLM pretraining papers but limits statistical confidence. Code and model weights are publicly released (GitHub: Marco-LLM; HuggingFace: Marco-Nano-Base, Marco-Mini-Base, Marco-Mini-Global-Base, Marco-Nano-Instruct, Marco-Mini-Instruct); training datasets and recipes are also disclosed. This is a preprint and has not undergone peer review. A methodological concern: baseline comparisons for Instruct models compare against Qwen3-4B-Instruct in "non-thinking" mode — the paper notes this explicitly, but Qwen3 models are significantly stronger in thinking mode, which may skew the competitive positioning. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Both models run on commercially available GPUs; the sparse architecture means inference activation cost matches a 0.6B–0.86B dense model, making them suitable for edge and API deployment. - Efficient: Memory footprint is 8B–17B parameters (storage), but the per-token compute cost is equivalent to activating ~0.6B–0.86B parameters; the paper does not report tokens/second or latency vs. baselines, so quantitative throughput comparison is absent. - Effective: Yes — across 28+ benchmarks spanning knowledge, reasoning, translation, and culturally grounded tasks, the models consistently match or exceed baselines with 3–14× more activated parameters; the evidence is strong and multi-dimensional. - Scalable: Demonstrated from 29 to 64 languages with minimal degradation (+1T tokens incremental); no experiments at larger MoE scales (e.g., ≥100B total parameters); the claim that the MoE architecture inherently mitigates the curse of multilinguality beyond 64 languages is asserted but not tested. Trained models are released; no library or inference tool beyond standard HuggingFace is described. Strengths - The N^(1/3) weight-scaling fix is a mathematically well-motivated correction (addressing the dense-sum vs. softmax-weighted-sum mismatch) that demonstrably stabilizes training (Figure 5 shows reduced loss spikes), and will generalize to any fine-grained upcycling scenario. - The empirical expert-activation analysis is a genuine mechanistic finding: language family structure emerges in MoE routing without any explicit linguistic supervision, providing interpretable evidence for why MoE helps multilingual transfer. - Full openness — weights, all training datasets, mixture ratios per stage, and post-training recipes — is rare at this quality level and substantially raises reproducibility compared to contemporaneous releases. - The data ablation methodology (staged 100B-token annealing experiments on Qwen3-4B) is disciplined and produces clean, interpretable attribution of gains to each data component. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) coverage of 29–64 languages is a small fraction of global linguistic diversity compared to 1000-language models; (2) heavy reliance on translation-based and synthetic pipelines for low-resource settings, which may not capture authentic multilingual reasoning; (3) the current framework requires full model retraining to add new languages (not modular/incremental); (4) future work should address more sophisticated routing for extremely long-tail languages. The authors confront these directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The N^(1/3) scaling factor is motivated by assuming uniform routing (p_i ≈ 1/N), but in practice routing is non-uniform; the paper does not verify that this scaling remains optimal or correct under learned non-uniform routing. - Teacher model choice in OPD (Qwen3-30B and Qwen3-Next-80B) is from the same Alibaba model family as the base (Qwen3-0.6B); any inductive biases baked into Qwen3's training distribution will propagate into Marco-MoE-Instruct through distillation. This is not discussed. - No inference latency or throughput numbers are reported, which is a meaningful omission for a paper whose core claim is compute efficiency — a reader cannot verify that the sparse architecture yields actual wall-clock speedup vs. a dense 0.86B model. - The comparison against Qwen3-4B-Instruct uses only non-thinking mode; in thinking mode, Qwen3-4B achieves substantially higher scores on reasoning benchmarks (e.g., MATH, GPQA), making the claimed performance advantage likely narrower than reported for reasoning-centric tasks. - Expert load balance at inference time (expert utilization distribution across layers and languages) is not shown, leaving open whether some experts are underutilized or dead after training. Bottom Line Practitioners building multilingual NLP systems with tight inference-cost constraints will find the Marco-MoE upcycling recipe, the weight-scaling correction, and the released artifacts concretely useful, though the absence of inference latency data and the preprint status merit caution before production deployment.
Recommended 2604.25578 PDF
#52 — Innovative (69)

MiniCPM-o 4.5: Towards Real-Time Full-Duplex Omni-Modal Interaction (3 ▲ )

Junbo Cui, Bokai Xu, Chongyi Wang, Tianyu Yu, Weiyue Sun, Yingjing Xu, Tianran Wang, Zhihui He, Wenshuo Ma, Tianchi Cai, Jiancheng Gui, Luoyuan Zhang, Xian Sun, Fuwei Huang, Moye Chen, Zhuo Lin, Hanyu Liu, Qingxin Gui, Qingzhe Han, Yuyang Wen, Huiping Liu, Rongkang Wang, Yaqi Zhang, Hongliang Wei, Chi Chen, You Li, Kechen Fang, Jie Zhou, Yuxuan Li, Guoyang Zeng, Chaojun Xiao, Yankai Lin, Xu Han, Maosong Sun, Zhiyuan Liu, Yuan Yao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MiniCPM-o 4.5, a 9B open-source multimodal LLM with the Omni-Flow framework that enables simultaneous perception and speech generation across vision, audio, and text streams, replacing turn-based interaction with continuous full-duplex streaming. - Why it's important: Existing omni-modal models serialize perception and response into alternating phases, making it impossible to react to new inputs mid-generation and forcing strictly reactive (never proactive) behavior — a fundamental bottleneck for ambient assistance and live-scene understanding. - How it does it: Omni-Flow partitions interaction into 1-second time windows, serializing visual tokens, audio tokens, and output tokens from each window into a single causal LM sequence;
