(4) Advantage shaping: the advantage is computed over the full G+G' pool rather than just the G selected responses, so the normalization statistics reflect the true difficulty; this amplifies positive advantages by 2.1–5.0× for typical success counts. KL regularization is removed from the objective, motivated empirically by prior work showing it harms multi-prompt training. The full objective (Eq. 7) combines standard GRPO updates on original rollouts with policy-shaped updates on resampled ones. Training uses the OpenR1-Math-46k-8192 dataset, EasyR1 framework, G=8, G'=24, temperature 1.0 for rollout, and 4–8×A100-80GB GPUs. Key Results Three base models are evaluated on MATH-500 (Acc@1), GSM8K (Acc@1), AMC 2022–24 (Acc@1), AIME 2024 (Mean@32), AIME 2025 (Mean@32). LOPE with training signal shaping achieves averages of 39.82 (+2.79 vs. GRPO 37.03) on Qwen3-1.7B-Base, 53.99 (+4.62 vs. GRPO 49.37) on Qwen3-4B-Base, and 53.88 (+6.20 vs. GRPO 47.68) on Qwen2.5-Math-7B. The naive-prompt resampling baseline actually degrades performance on Qwen3-4B (48.95 vs. GRPO 49.37) and is flat on 7B, suggesting the perturbation itself — not merely more rollouts — is responsible for gains. The analysis of 352 hard questions (all failing Pass@8 under naive prompt) shows Lorem-perturbed resampling independently resolves 50 questions that neither naive nor high-temperature (T=1.2) resampling can reach, demonstrating orthogonal coverage. The perturbation study (Table 2) identifies that only methods with mean perplexity below ~100 and Latin-based vocabulary (LOPE: 39.82, Filtered Latin Natural Language: 39.85, Latin Unigram: 39.67) beat the naive resampling baseline; Random Token (perplexity 4.6×10^5) regresses to 37.65, below even standard GRPO. Training-time resample question-level success rate (Pass@24) shows LOPE consistently above naive resampling throughout 200 training steps across all three models. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is applying task-irrelevant, low-perplexity text as a structured prompt-space perturbation to recover GRPO training signal on zero-advantage hard questions, combined with theoretically motivated importance-sampling corrections and advantage re-normalization. The individual components (GRPO, importance sampling, policy shaping from Yan et al. 2025) are prior work; the contribution is their integration with the Lorem Ipsum perturbation insight, plus the systematic empirical characterization of what properties make a perturbation effective. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: LOPE without training signal shaping is reported separately (e.g., 47.32 vs. 53.88 on 7B), isolating the value of policy and advantage shaping. The perturbation comparison (Table 2) covers eight alternatives. Training-time resample success rates are plotted over 200 steps for all three models. However, error bars and multiple seeds are absent — all results appear to be single runs, and the benchmarks are noisy (AIME uses Mean@32 but AMC uses Acc@1 over small sample sets). Code is publicly available at https://github.com/shrango/LoPE. The paper is a preprint under review, so results have not been independently peer-reviewed. One methodological concern: the advantage shaping analysis is entirely analytical (Appendix C.2), not experimentally ablated at the component level in a controlled way; the training signal shaping ablation in Table 1 conflates policy and advantage shaping. English Unigram Model (perplexity ~85) underperforms Latin 3-Gram Model (perplexity ~91), which seems to contradict the low-perplexity rule — the authors attribute this to English interference but do not control for this alternative explanation. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Training runs on 4–8×A100-80GB GPUs, which is commercially available but not lightweight; inference at G'=24 resamples per hard question increases rollout compute roughly 3× for affected questions. - Efficient: Resampling overhead applies only to zero-advantage questions; the authors report consistently higher question-level success rates (Pass@24), which implies fewer wasted rollouts over the training run, though no wall-clock comparison is provided. - Effective: Evidence shows LOPE solves questions that neither naive nor high-temperature resampling can reach, and final benchmark scores consistently exceed GRPO baselines across all three model scales; the claim holds for math reasoning but the mechanism is confirmed only in this domain. - Scalable: Tested at 1.7B, 4B, and 7B; no experiments at 13B, 32B, or 70B, and gains grow with model scale (2.79 → 4.62 → 6.20 average points), leaving open whether the method remains effective as base model capability increases and zero-advantage questions become rarer. The work delivers code and a training recipe but no pretrained model weights or evaluation artifacts beyond benchmark numbers. Strengths - The core observation that prompt-space perturbation and logit-space perturbation explore orthogonal subsets of hard questions (Figure 2, 50 exclusively solved questions in the hard 352-subset) is cleanly demonstrated and genuinely surprising. - The systematic perturbation ablation (8 alternative perturbation types spanning 6 orders of magnitude in perplexity) provides actionable design principles — low perplexity + Latin vocabulary — grounded in interpretable analysis of token entropy and t-SNE question representations. - The policy and advantage shaping derivations (Appendix C) are mathematically precise, correctly relaxing the πθ_old = 1 assumption from prior work and identifying the vanishing-gradient pathology of off-policy importance sampling with a bounded fix. - Results are consistent across all three model families and all five benchmarks, including competition-level AIME, where gains are largest (AIME25: 5.73 vs. 4.47 on 1.7B; 16.51 vs. 9.90 on 7B). Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note in the Ethics Statement that Lorem Ipsum sequences cannot be guaranteed free of potentially toxic or biased words, and that excessively strong perturbations may cause incoherent or harmful model outputs. They frame this as an open safety challenge and commit to future systematic study. They do not explicitly acknowledge the narrow domain coverage (math only) or the single-seed evaluation as limitations in the main text, though the ethics statement is unusually candid for this type of work. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All experiments use mathematical reasoning; the recipe's generality to code generation, scientific QA, or instruction-following is undemonstrated. The English-interference hypothesis assumes models reason in English — non-English base models or multilingual settings are not discussed. - Single-seed training with noisy benchmarks (AMC has small sample count) means observed gains could partially reflect variance; AIME 2024/2025 Mean@32 is more stable but still sensitive at these score levels. - The ablation of policy shaping vs. advantage shaping is not disaggregated: Table 1 reports only the joint "training signal shaping" condition, making it impossible to attribute the performance difference to either component individually. - The naive resampling baseline removes KL regularization (stated in Section 6.1 for fair comparison), conflating the effect of removing KL with the effect of LOPE's perturbation; it is unclear whether LOPE's gains over standard GRPO partially reflect removing KL rather than the perturbation itself. - Computational cost analysis is absent: the extra G'=24 rollouts per zero-advantage question increase total compute for hard questions by 3×, and no estimate of what fraction of training questions trigger resampling is reported. Bottom Line LOPE is a practical, well-motivated technique for recovering GRPO training signal on hard math questions, backed by clean empirical analysis and principled off-policy corrections, though the absence of multi-seed evaluation, lack of ablation disaggregating shaping components, and restriction to sub-7B math settings warrant caution before adopting it as a general RLVR recipe.
Recommended 2605.05566 PDF
#64 — Innovative (68)

Heterogeneous Scientific Foundation Model Collaboration

Zihao Li, Jiaru Zou, Feihao Fang, Xuying Ning, Mengting Ai, Tianxin Wei, Sirui Chen, Xiyuan Yang, Jingrui He · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Eywa, a three-tier agentic framework (EywaAgent / EywaMAS / EywaOrchestra) that integrates domain-specific foundation models into LLM-based pipelines via a bidirectional FM-LLM interface implemented through the Model Context Protocol. - Why it's important: Language-only agents serialize structured scientific data (time series, tabular) into text, an operation the paper proves via mutual information arguments discards task-relevant information and imposes an irreducible Bayes-risk penalty that no amount of downstream LLM reasoning can recover. - How it does it: Each domain-specific FM is wrapped with a query compiler (LLM state → structured FM invocation) and a response adapter (FM output → language-compatible context), letting the LLM decide what to compute while the FM performs the actual domain-specific computation, with the fallback of skipping FM invocation entirely preserving full language-only behavior. - The good: EywaAgent achieves 6.6% higher utility than the single-LLM-agent baseline while simultaneously cutting token usage by ~30% and wall-clock time by ~10% across 9 scientific sub-domains — quality and efficiency improve together. - The missing: The entire evaluation rests on only 2 foundation models (Chronos for time series, TabPFN for tabular) across 200 total tasks (15–28 per sub-domain), with no statistical significance tests, and 41% of benchmark tasks involve natural-language QA where FMs are not invoked — limiting how broadly the "heterogeneous FM collaboration" claim is actually exercised. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners building agentic systems for scientific tasks involving structured non-linguistic data; the MCP-based integration pattern is immediately actionable, but treat the quantitative gains cautiously given the small evaluation scale.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) — combines MCP-based FM-LLM bridge with information-theoretic motivation; the cross-modality heterogeneity finding is genuinely interesting. Rigor (55) pulled down by no significance tests on n=15-28 per sub-domain, self-constructed benchmark with non-standard utility metric, and 41% of tasks not exercising FMs at all. Non-Obviousness (75) — the language-bottleneck framing and finding that FM heterogeneity beats LLM heterogeneity is non-obvious. Completeness (70) and Resilience (60) limited by circular benchmark construction and missing head-to-head FM-vs-serialized-LLM comparison. Impact (80) reflects open-source code+benchmark. Composite ~68.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Agentic LLM systems treat language as a universal interface, which fails for scientific tasks involving structured, non-linguistic data modalities — time series (energy, finance, climate), tabular data (clinical, materials), and domain-specific representations (molecular graphs, crystal structures). Serializing these into text tokens is not lossless: the paper formalizes this as an information bottleneck where I(Y; T(X)) ≤ I(Y; X), with strict inequality whenever the serializer discards task-relevant structure. Meanwhile, domain-specific foundation models — Chronos, TabPFN, AlphaFold, GraphCast — are pretrained precisely to operate over these native representations and outperform any text-based approach on their target domain, but they expose no language interface and cannot participate directly in LLM reasoning chains. Prior tool-use frameworks treat external models as passively-called APIs with manually-specified workflows, which does not generalize across heterogeneous domains or support dynamic model selection per task. Key Idea The core technical insight is the FM-LLM "Tsaheylu" interface: a bidirectional bridge (query compiler φ: S → U, response adapter ψ: O → Z) that allows an LLM to dynamically configure and invoke a domain-specific FM at any reasoning step, receive its output as a language-compatible context, and continue reasoning from there — while retaining the ability to skip FM invocation and fall back to standard language reasoning. This makes the LLM-only agent a strict special case (C always skips), while expanding the function class to include FM-native computation. The framework is then layered: EywaAgent (single coupled FM-LLM unit), EywaMAS (heterogeneous multi-agent system with plug-and-play EywaAgent substitution), and EywaOrchestra (LLM conductor dynamically selects per-task agent configuration, backbone LLMs, FM assignments, and communication topology from a finite pool). Methodology Implementation uses LangChain agents paired with FastMCP servers; each FM is deployed as a local HTTP MCP endpoint. At inference time, an EywaAgent loads task data into server-side storage and invokes the FM on demand. Three pipeline tiers: (1) EywaAgent: at each reasoning step t, the LLM-induced control policy C ∈ {invoke, skip} determines whether to run the Tsaheylu pipeline (u = φ(s), o = F(x,u), z = ψ(o)) or produce a language-only response. C is not pre-specified but emerges from the LLM's reasoning over current state — a flexible but potentially miscalibrated design choice. (2) EywaMAS: any existing MAS topology (sequential, looped, hierarchical, heterogeneous) can incorporate EywaAgents by replacing a subset of LLM agents; communication protocol and topology are unchanged. Default topology is debate. A theorem (analogous to Theorem 3) proves that a single EywaAgent whose messages can reach the output node within the interaction horizon strictly improves solvability over LLM-only MAS. (3) EywaOrchestra: an LLM conductor receives task description, domain, and task type; outputs a JSON configuration specifying setting (single/multi-agent), backbone LLM per agent, FM assignment, and topology. The conductor selects from a finite topology pool; Algorithm 1 shows a 3-step procedure: select config, instantiate system, execute. Theorem 18 proves oracle adaptive routing achieves Roracle ≤ R*_fixed with strict inequality when no fixed configuration is uniformly optimal. Evaluation benchmark EywaBench-V1: 200 tasks across 9 sub-domains (material, energy, space, biology, clinic, drug, economy, business, infrastructure), mixing natural language (78 tasks), time series (82 tasks, Chronos applicable), and tabular (40 tasks, TabPFN applicable) from DeepPrinciple, MMLU-Pro, fev-bench, and TabArena. Unified utility score u ∈ [0,1] per task (soft-match for NL, sMAPE+MAAPE-based for time series and tabular regression, accuracy for tabular classification). Baselines: Single-LLM-Agent, Refine MAS, Debate MAS, MoA, X-MAS — all using the same gpt-5-nano backbone where applicable. Experiments run on 13th Gen Intel i9-13900H CPU, 64GB RAM (no GPU). Key Results All results are from EywaBench-V1 (200 samples), a preprint. - EywaAgent vs. Single-LLM-Agent: utility 0.6558 vs. 0.6154 (+6.6%), tokens 3,137 vs. 4,469 (−30%), latency 22.78s vs. 25.22s (−10%). Gains are consistent across all 9 sub-domains. - EywaMAS vs. best homogeneous MAS (Debate): utility 0.6761 vs. 0.6460 (+4.7%), tokens 11,214 vs. 13,216 (−15%), though EywaMAS is slower (72.11s vs. 78.22s, marginal). - EywaMAS outperforms heterogeneous LLM-only MAS (MoA: 0.6273, X-MAS: 0.6188), establishing that cross-modality FM heterogeneity matters more than language-model heterogeneity alone — a notable finding. - EywaOrchestra: utility 0.6746 (matches EywaMAS at 0.6761), tokens 8,335 (−26% vs. EywaMAS), latency 48.16s (−33%), with no expert configuration required. Outperforms EywaMAS on Space (0.7187 vs. 0.6899) and Drug (0.6319 vs. 0.6248) sub-domains. - Biology subdomain is the weakest for all methods (0.34–0.38 utility across the board), suggesting the 2 FMs don't cover life-science tasks at the FM level effectively. - Backbone ablation: gpt-4.1-nano → gpt-5-nano yields +15.5% utility for EywaAgent; gpt-5-nano → gpt-5-mini yields only +1.25%, consistent with a domain-specific bottleneck dominating over general LLM capability at frontier scales. - Diminishing returns from gpt-5-nano to gpt-5-mini: on Biology, Clinic, Economy, and Business, gpt-5-mini is occasionally slightly worse, suggesting that the remaining performance headroom lies in domain-specific FM quality, not LLM capability. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The FM-LLM interface pattern (LLM invoking external models via structured API calls) is closely related to existing tool-use and MCP frameworks; the genuine novelty is (1) the information-theoretic formalization of the language bottleneck as motivation, (2) the three-tier compositional architecture with formal guarantees, and (3) EywaBench as the first benchmark explicitly spanning heterogeneous data modalities across physical/life/social sciences for agentic evaluation. The theoretical results are novel but rest on a domain advantage assumption that is essentially definitional rather than independently derived. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover LLM sampling temperature, FM softmax temperature, prompt design (default, detailed, CoT, ReAct), and backbone scaling — reasonably thorough. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported in the main tables; with n=15–28 per sub-domain, sub-percent utility differences are within noise. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/Violet24K/Eywa; EywaBench-V1 is released as a Parquet file. The benchmark is self-constructed by the same team evaluating on it, and the soft-match utility metric (including a custom lexical fallback formula combining token F1 and SequenceMatcher ratio, capped at 0.8) is non-standard and not independently validated. gpt-5-nano is used as the default backbone — this is a very recent model and its characteristics are not widely benchmarked by external parties, making reproducibility dependent on OpenAI API access and potential model updates. Deployability (SYSTEMS) - Practical: Runs on consumer-grade CPU hardware (i9-13900H, 64GB RAM) with no GPU; FM inference via local MCP server avoids remote API latency for the FM calls. - Efficient: EywaAgent reduces tokens by 30% and latency by 10% vs. single-agent baseline; EywaMAS is ~3× slower than EywaAgent due to multi-agent communication overhead, which is not always justified (e.g., Biology: 111.92s for EywaMAS vs. 34.68s for EywaAgent for <12% utility gain). - Effective: Consistent utility improvements for EywaAgent and EywaMAS across all 9 sub-domains; life science remains the weakest category and the framework's FM contribution is limited there since neither Chronos nor TabPFN targets biology-specific data. - Scalable: The current system integrates only 2 FMs evaluated on 200 tasks; the paper explicitly flags scaling to a larger FM ecosystem as future work and provides no experiments at larger task volume or with additional modalities. The finite topology pool in EywaOrchestra constrains architectural diversity. The work delivers a software artifact (open-source code + benchmark dataset), not just paper form. Strengths - The information-theoretic formalization of the language bottleneck (Lemma 12: strict Bayes-risk gap R_T > R_X under non-degenerate serialization) provides a principled first-principles justification that is stronger than the usual hand-waving about "LLMs can't handle structured data." - The empirical finding that cross-modality heterogeneity (FM + LLM) consistently outperforms language-model heterogeneity alone (MoA, X-MAS) across 9 sub-domains is a substantive, non-obvious result that directly challenges the design philosophy of current heterogeneous LLM MAS systems. - The simultaneous improvement in both utility and token/latency efficiency — not a quality-efficiency tradeoff but an improvement on both axes — is credibly supported across all 9 sub-domains. - EywaOrchestra's ability to nearly match expert-configured EywaMAS while using 26% fewer tokens and requiring no manual topology specification is a practical contribution for deployment. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge three limitations: (1) performance depends on the quality of underlying LLMs and domain-specific FMs, creating an inherited performance ceiling; (2) EywaBench cannot cover all scientific domains, and some specialized modalities are absent; (3) heterogeneous collaboration can introduce additional computation, latency, and communication overhead compared to single-agent baselines. They also identify learning orchestration policies, tighter FM-LLM coupling (shared representation spaces, differentiable interfaces), and EywaBench extension as future directions. The authors confront limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Statistically underpowered evaluation: 15–28 samples per sub-domain with no significance testing means many reported utility differences (e.g., 0.3798 vs. 0.3545 in Biology, a 7% relative difference on 20 samples) cannot be distinguished from noise. The paper presents all differences as meaningful rankings without this caveat. - FM coverage mismatch with claimed scope: 41% of EywaBench tasks are natural-language QA (DeepPrinciple + MMLU-Pro), where neither Chronos nor TabPFN is invoked. Utility gains on these tasks come from LLM reasoning differences, not FM collaboration. The paper conflates two distinct sources of improvement without isolating them. - Circular benchmark construction: EywaBench is designed, built, and evaluated by the same group; the custom soft-match utility metric is not validated against human judgments or standard evaluation protocols, and natural-language tasks reward the LLM backbone rather than FM integration. - EywaMAS overhead is unjustified in some domains: Biology EywaMAS runs 111.92s vs. EywaAgent at 30.84s for a 0.008 absolute utility gain — the paper does not discuss when multi-agent overhead is not worth incurring, and the conductor in EywaOrchestra sometimes fails to avoid this (Space sub-domain: EywaOrchestra 75.47s vs. EywaAgent 23.12s with lower utility). - No comparison to scientific LLMs fine-tuned for structured data (e.g., Time-LLM, TabPFN with LLM prompting wrappers, domain-adapted models): the baselines are all generic chat LLMs, which understates what language-only approaches can achieve with domain-specific tuning. - Assumption 1 (domain advantage) is not empirically verified in isolation: The paper assumes FMs outperform serialized-LLM reasoning on their target modality but does not include a direct head-to-head comparison of Chronos vs. LLM-as-forecaster or TabPFN vs. LLM-as-regressor on the benchmark tasks, which would have strengthened the motivating premise. Bottom Line A practitioner building agentic pipelines for scientific tasks with time-series or tabular data will find the MCP-based FM-LLM integration architecture immediately usable and the simultaneous quality-efficiency gains credible — but should treat the information-theoretic framing as motivation rather than proof, and validate on a larger, independently-constructed benchmark before drawing broad conclusions about the framework's effectiveness across scientific domains.
Search Results 2604.27351 PDF
#65 — Innovative (68)

Micro Language Models Enable Instant Responses

Wen Cheng, Tuochao Chen, Karim Helwani, Sriram Srinivasan, Luke Zettlemoyer, Shyamnath Gollakota · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces micro language models (µLMs), a family of 8.8M–29.5M parameter decoder-only transformers trained on chat data, paired with a collaborative generation framework in which the µLM irrevocably commits the first 4–8 words of a response on-device while a cloud LLM completes it, eliminating perceptible latency on wearable devices. - Why it's important: Smartwatches, smart glasses, and similar edge devices cannot sustain even 100M-parameter models due to memory and thermal constraints, and cloud-only inference imposes 0.2–5+ second time-to-first-token delays that make ambient AI assistants feel unresponsive — a gap no existing approach resolves without sacrificing either response quality or latency independence. - How it does it: Rather than draft-and-verify (speculative decoding), which keeps TTFT bound to the network round-trip, the µLM immediately displays its output and the cloud model is instruction-prompted to act as a continuator — suppressing its default behavior of restating or responding to the opener — with three structured error-recovery modes (explicit correction, natural recovery, humor-aware recovery) handling the cases where the local prefix goes wrong. - The good: On OrangePi embedded hardware the 28M µLM achieves 45ms TTFT and outputs four words in 55ms, delivering 4.3× higher end-to-end throughput and 4.5× lower energy per token than SmolLM2-135M-Instruct; the collaborative framework outperforms standalone Qwen3-0.6B on LLM-judge stitched-response quality and is rated at least as good as the full standalone cloud LLM in 77.3% of user judgments. - The missing: The key on-device alternative — a locally runnable 100M–300M parameter model generating complete responses — is absent from evaluation; the user study has only 15 participants, and end-to-end latency reduction is never measured by the authors directly (cloud latency figures are borrowed from an external paper). - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners working on wearable AI, edge inference, or latency-aware conversational systems — the commit-and-continue framework is directly deployable; those seeking rigorous preference evidence or a complete competitive analysis should be aware of the study's scale limitations.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (weights 15/15/10/15/15/30). Practical Impact (75) is solid — released model checkpoints, demo, real OrangePi measurements (45ms TTFT, 31 mJ/token), but stops short of production wearable integration. Novelty (70) — commit-and-continue with continuator-prompting and typologized error recovery is genuinely new, though the µLM architecture itself is standard. Rigor (60) is the main drag: the most relevant baseline (a locally-runnable 100M–300M model generating full responses) is absent, cloud latency figures are borrowed from another paper, the user study is n=15, the LLM judge overlaps with the cloud continuators tested, and a safety-eval dataset shares an author — preprint status further reduces Rigor. Non-Obviousness (70) — the continuator-prompting trick is non-obvious. Completeness (65) — limitations honestly acknowledged but the missing on-device baseline hurts. Resilience (60) — claims survive directionally but the 5.3% preference margin and missing baseline are exploitable. Weighted: 0.15·70 + 0.15·60 + 0.10·70 + 0.15·65 + 0.15·60 + 0.30·75 = 67.75 ≈ 68.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Wearable and edge devices — smartwatches, smart glasses, budget smartphones — operate under per-application memory budgets of tens of megabytes and thermal envelopes too tight for sustained inference even at the 100M–1B parameter scale. Cloud offloading, the current workaround, introduces multi-second delays: 50–500ms network RTT, 100–4000ms server queuing, and 50–500ms cloud prefill. Real-time human-AI interaction requires sub-second responsiveness. Scripted speech fillers ("Um", "Let me think...") that hold the floor while awaiting cloud results are repetitive and decrease user affinity. The gap is: how do you give a user the perceptual experience of an instant response when the device cannot run a capable model and the cloud is too slow? Key Idea The core insight is that the on-device model does not need to generate a complete, high-quality response — it only needs to commit enough semantically grounded tokens (~4–8 words, corresponding to ~1–2 seconds of reading at average silent reading speed) to give the user visible forward progress while the cloud generates the rest. By instruction-prompting the cloud model to act as a continuator rather than a responder, the stitched text reads as a single fluent response. Crucially, tokens are irrevocably committed and displayed immediately, making time-to-first-token entirely independent of network conditions. Methodology The µLM family consists of five decoder-only Transformers with pre-norm residual blocks, rotary positional embeddings (RoPE, θ=10⁶), RMSNorm, SwiGLU feed-forward networks, grouped-query attention (fewer KV heads than Q heads), and tied input/output embeddings. Hidden sizes 256/384/512 crossed with 8/16 layers yield 8.79M–29.5M parameters. A custom BPE tokenizer with vocabulary size 12,288 is trained on the pretraining corpus to control embedding overhead. Pretraining uses 1.485B tokens from UltraChat, MOSS, and Instruction_merge_set with a cleaned multi-turn chat format and next-token prediction. SFT uses 323.4M tokens from Magpie-style data. Training compute is FLOP-matched across variants (FLOPs ∝ N×D), using the 28.85M model trained for three epochs as the reference; smaller/larger variants receive proportionally more/fewer optimizer steps. AdamW (β₁=0.9, β₂=0.95), weight decay 0.1, peak LR 5×10⁻⁴, cosine decay to 10%, bfloat16. Three critical design decisions: (1) commit-and-continue vs. draft-and-verify is well-justified for TTFT independence from network; (2) instruction prompting the cloud as a continuator (suppressing meta-commentary and restating behaviors) is justified empirically by the handoff quality scores; (3) structured error correction modes are justified by HCI literature on user preference for integrated vs. visible repair. Evaluation uses lm-eval-harness (zero-shot, length-normalized accuracy) for standard benchmarks and LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4o, five rubrics) for dialogue quality, with human validation (n=10, Pearson r=0.803 for quality, r=0.719 for stitched response). Key Results On lm-eval-harness (HellaSwag, PIQA, BoolQ, ARC-Challenge, OpenBookQA), µLM-28.85M achieves an overall score of 0.390, comparable to Pythia-70M (0.388) and Pythia-160M (0.389), and matching or exceeding LaMini-GPT-124M (0.393), LaMini-Neo-125M (0.406), and LaMini-Cerebras-256M (0.406); SmolLM2-135M-Instruct leads at 0.429. On qualitative dialogue evaluation (GPT-4o judge), µLM-28.85M achieves a mean score of ~3.01 vs. LaMini variants at 1.57–1.88 and SmolLM2-135M-Instruct at 3.60, with all LaMini variants substantially underperforming. On collaborative generation (Table 1), µLM-28M + Qwen2.5-72B at 4 words achieves the best average score of 4.67 across fluency, semantic coherence, redundancy, and style consistency — outperforming standalone µLM-28M (3.72) and standalone Qwen3-0.6B (4.59). Handoff correction rates are 3.7% at 4 committed words, 8.4% at 8 words, and 16.4% at 16 words, confirming the 4–8 word operating point. User study (n=15): collaborative output preferred in 28.0% of comparisons, standalone LLM in 22.7%, tie in 49.3%; the tie-or-prefer rate of 77.3% is significantly above 50% (p < 0.001 binomial test). For error recovery, natural recovery ranked 1st by 44%, humor-aware by 36.7%, and explicit correction ranked 1st by only 19.3%. On OrangePi 5 hardware, µLM-28M achieves TTFT of 45ms and 4-word output in 55ms, 4.3× higher end-to-end throughput and 4.5× lower energy per token vs. SmolLM2-135M-Instruct. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The commit-and-continue collaborative generation framework with structured error recovery is genuinely novel — no prior work combines irrevocable on-device prefix commitment with cloud continuation and typologized recovery modes. The µLM model family itself represents engineering optimization (standard transformer architecture applied at extreme scale) rather than architectural novelty, but the framing of sub-30M models as response initiators in an asymmetric collaboration, with systematic characterization of the 8M–30M quality regime, goes beyond straightforward application of known ideas. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover prefix length (4/8/16 words), local model size (8M vs. 28M), cloud model choice (three models), width-vs-depth allocation (shallower 28.85M outperforms deeper 29.5M at matched FLOPs), and input query length (Appendix G shows stable performance across 3–47 token inputs). LLM judge scores are validated against human annotations. MinHash-based contamination detection is applied between eval and training sets. Model checkpoints and demo are publicly released at GitHub. Concerns: (1) The evaluating LLM judge (GPT-4o) is not independent of the frontier models used as cloud continuators; (2) the primary standalone alternative in Table 1 is Qwen3-0.6B (a cloud model), not a locally deployable 100M–300M on-device model — the most relevant competitive baseline for the deployment scenario is absent; (3) cloud latency figures motivating the paper are borrowed from Agrawal et al. (2024), not measured in the authors' own infrastructure; (4) the user study (n=15) is underpowered for the observed 5.3% preference margin; (5) the safety evaluation in Appendix H uses a prompt set (Cheng et al., 2025) whose lead author overlaps with this paper's authors. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a trained model family deployed on embedded hardware with measured latency and energy. - Practical: Runs on an OrangePi 5 (ARM SBC available for ~$60) with no special accelerator; the 28M model uses memory consistent with wearable application budgets. - Efficient: 45ms TTFT, 55ms to 4 words, 31 mJ/token on OrangePi — 4.3× higher end-to-end throughput and 4.5× lower energy than SmolLM2-135M-Instruct on the same board; however, full response quality still requires cloud inference, so the system's energy budget includes both on-device and cloud costs. - Effective: Stitched response quality (avg 4.65–4.67 on a 1–5 scale) substantially exceeds standalone µLM (3.72) and matches or beats standalone Qwen3-0.6B (4.59); user study confirms perceptual acceptability at n=15. - Scalable: Tested only on short single-turn queries (mean 14 tokens); multi-turn dialogue management is explicitly out of scope; hardware tested on OrangePi 5 as a proxy, not production wearable SoCs (Apple Watch, Meta Ray-Ban glasses). Delivers a software artifact (model checkpoints, demo) and a deployable pipeline; stops short of integration with a production wearable OS or SDK. Strengths - The commit-and-continue insight — that a cloud model can be reliably instruction-prompted into continuator mode, allowing TTFT to be fully decoupled from network latency — is practically significant and non-obvious; Table 1 shows the stitched output consistently outperforms the cloud model's smaller standalone competitor. - Three structurally distinct error recovery modes grounded in HCI literature are well-theorized and the user study confirms the predicted preference ordering (natural > humor > explicit), validating the design taxonomy. - Actual hardware energy and latency measurements on OrangePi with external USB power meter provide ground-truth deployability numbers (31 mJ/token, 45ms TTFT) rather than estimates. - FLOP-matched training across five variants and MinHash contamination filtering reflect careful experimental methodology for a systems paper at this scale. Author-Acknowledged Limitations Authors directly acknowledge: µLMs may underperform on long prompts where critical cues are buried deep; training covers only everyday conversational data, not math or coding; current design handles single-turn only (multi-turn context to be managed by cloud LLM); hardware evaluation uses OrangePi 5 as a first-order proxy, not production wearable SoCs; privacy concern that sensitive queries still reach the cloud via handoff. These are addressed honestly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The most important missing baseline is a locally-runnable 100M–300M model (e.g., SmolLM2-135M-Instruct or MobileLLM-125M) generating complete responses on the edge device — the exact alternative a device manufacturer would evaluate against the µLM+cloud approach. Its absence from Table 1 leaves the core deployment tradeoff unquantified. - End-to-end latency reduction is never measured in the authors' own setup; the cloud latency numbers (0.2–5+ seconds) are sourced from Agrawal et al. (2024) and may not reflect the specific cloud inference stack used for experiments. - The user study has 15 participants from a single institution, which is insufficient to distinguish a 5.3% preference margin (28% vs. 22.7%) from noise, despite the binomial test on the tie-or-prefer aggregate passing. - The 12,288-token BPE vocabulary trained on the same conversational corpus as the model may degrade on out-of-distribution inputs (technical, multilingual, or code-heavy queries) in ways not evaluated. - The safety evaluation (Appendix H, 80 prompts) uses a dataset from Cheng et al. (2025) whose first author is also a co-author of this paper, raising a potential conflict in the adversarial probe selection. Bottom Line This preprint delivers a practically deployable, hardware-validated system that cleanly solves the wearable TTFT problem with real energy numbers and a principled error-recovery framework — practitioners targeting smartwatch or smart glasses AI should read it, but should independently measure end-to-end latency and test against a locally-runnable small model before committing to the architecture.
Recommended 2604.19642 PDF
#66 — Innovative (68)

Repetition over Diversity: High-Signal Data Filtering for Sample-Efficient German Language Modeling (12 ▲ )

Ansar Aynetdinov, Patrick Haller, Alan Akbik · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 5 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper demonstrates that repeated multi-epoch training on a small, aggressively quality-filtered German web corpus (28B tokens × up to 7.2 epochs) consistently outperforms single-pass training on a larger, lightly filtered corpus under the same total token budget. - Why it's important: Practitioners building non-English LLMs face a genuine dilemma because strict quality filtering reduces the available token pool by 20×, and conventional wisdom (Muennighoff et al., 2023) warns that multi-epoch repetition yields diminishing returns after 4 epochs — this paper challenges both assumptions for non-English settings. - How it does it: Three LLM-annotated document-level classifiers (Coherence, Information Value, Educational Quality) are distilled into embedding-model regressors trained on 500K Llama-3.3-70B-scored documents, then applied to 500M German web documents; the intersection (Dense Core, 5.1% yield) serves as the high-density training corpus. - The good: A 350M-parameter model trained on 28B Dense Core tokens for 3.6 epochs scores 39.24 average across six benchmarks versus 34.35 for a 100B-token single-pass Random baseline, and outperforms LLäMmlein-120M (1T tokens), Gemma-3-270M (6T tokens), and Qwen-3-0.6B-Base (36T tokens) despite training on 10–360× fewer tokens. - The missing: All experiments are capped at 1B parameters and 200B tokens; the scaling behavior of the quality-versus-quantity trade-off at 7B+ parameters and trillion-token budgets is entirely untested and only claimed to generalize. - Read it or not: Worth reading for NLP practitioners building small-to-medium language models for high-resource non-English languages who need concrete filtering strategies and evidence about safe multi-epoch repetition budgets; less urgent for researchers focused on large-scale English or multilingual training.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) for genuinely challenging the 4-epoch repetition ceiling in non-English settings — a clear empirical finding, though the filtering pipeline is adapted from FineWeb-Edu. Rigor (60) hurt by zero error bars/seeds, self-corrected benchmarks used for both training-decision and final evaluation (Goodhart risk), and confounded document-length in AA High baseline. Non-Obviousness (70) — the curriculum-underperforms-pure-Dense result is non-obvious. Completeness (75) strong, with direct limitation acknowledgment. Resilience (60) — the self-corrected benchmark and missing significance tests would draw reviewer fire. Impact (75) — released models, datasets, and concretely useful guidance for non-English LLM builders. Composite ≈ 0.20·70+0.25·60+0.15·70+0.15·75+0.15·60+0.10·75 = 67.75 → 68.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 75 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Pre-training data for non-English high-resource languages like German, French, or Japanese is abundant (hundreds of billions of tokens) but not unlimited in the way English is (multi-trillion tokens). Recent English work (FineWeb-Edu, Phi) showed that training on aggressively quality-filtered subsets dramatically improves sample efficiency, but strict filtering can reduce the German web corpus from ~100B to ~28B tokens — a 3.6× reduction. If multi-epoch training on this smaller set degrades due to data memorization and reduced diversity, practitioners are stuck: they cannot afford to waste signal on low-quality documents, but they also cannot safely repeat the good ones. This trade-off is unresolved in the non-English literature; Muennighoff et al. (2023) found returns diminish after 4 epochs on constrained corpora, and nearly all recent German LLMs either use lightly filtered massive multilingual mixtures or rely on cross-lingual transfer from English-centric models. Key Idea: The central insight is that the conventional 4-epoch repetition ceiling is not a property of repetition per se but of data quality: if the corpus is sufficiently information-dense, each re-exposure still delivers net learning signal because the model has not yet extracted all learnable structure from the documents. High semantic density (educational clarity + factual richness + coherence) functions as a substitute for token novelty, making quality a lever that directly trades against quantity under a fixed compute budget. Methodology: The pipeline operates in two stages. First, quality annotation: Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct scores 500K randomly sampled FineWeb-2 German documents on three rubrics — Coherence (syntactic flow, 1–3 scale), Information Value (fact density, 1–4), and Educational Quality (pedagogical clarity, 0–5, using FineWeb-Edu's prompt). Three separate snowflake-arctic-embed-m-v2.0 regression models are fine-tuned on these scores (1K context, lr=1e-4, 10 epochs, batch 64), then deployed over all 496M FW2-DE documents. Dense Core is the intersection of documents passing all three score thresholds (coherence ≥ 3, information value = 4, education ≥ 3), yielding 24.5M documents / 28B tokens. Per-subset BPE tokenizers (32K vocab) are trained to match lexical distributions. Second, training: Llama-2-style decoder-only transformers at 350M (24L × 1024H) and 1B (16L × 2048H) parameter counts, all trained with AdamW (β1=0.9, β2=0.95, wd=0.1), cosine LR schedule (peak 5e-4), batch 0.5M tokens, context 2048 on 8×A100. Experiments compare uniform baselines (single-pass random/coherence), dense repetition (Dense Core at 3.6×, 7.2×), external quality filters (MKC, AA High), and two curriculum variants (SORTED: coherence tokens in ascending educational score order; PHASED: 50B random + 50B Dense Core). Evaluation uses six German benchmarks, including four that were re-translated from English using Tower+72B to fix word-order artifacts. Critical design decisions: (1) separate per-dimension classifiers rather than a joint scorer — justified by allowing independent threshold tuning; (2) embedding regressor rather than generative LLM for inference at 500M-doc scale — computationally justified; (3) per-subset tokenizers — justified by measured fertility improvements on both train and holdout sets. Key Results: Under a 100B-token budget at 350M scale, Dense Core (28B × 3.6 epochs) achieves 39.24 average across MMLU, ARC-C, ARC-E, HellaSwag, LAMBADA, OpenBookQA versus 34.35 for the Random baseline — a 4.89-point gain. Single-filter subsets also outperform Random, with Educational Quality (33B × 3×) reaching 38.62. The curriculum approaches (SORTED, PHASED) lag behind pure Dense Core by 1.6–2.0 points, indicating that early exposure to low-signal data consumes budget capacity. Among external baselines, MKC (35B × 2.9×) reaches 37.28 and AA High (21B × 4.8×) underperforms even Random at 33.14, attributed to weaker annotator models in the AA High pipeline. At 1B scale the Dense Core advantage grows to 5.14 points. Extending to 200B tokens (7.2 epochs), Dense Core 350M reaches 40.16 without plateauing; the 1B Dense Core 200B model reaches 44.05 — competitive with Gemma-3-1B (trained on 2T tokens) and exceeding Llama-3.2-1B (9T tokens). Instruction-tuned DENSE-CORE 350M achieves 253/1000 correct responses under LLM-as-judge versus 178 for Random; 1B Dense Core reaches 338. Baselines (Random, LLäMmlein) are appropriate; the comparison to multilingual models (Gemma-3, Llama, Qwen3) uses models trained on token budgets 10–360× larger, making the absolute metric comparison informative but not a controlled experiment. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The core finding — that aggressive quality filtering + multi-epoch repetition beyond the previously-reported 4-epoch ceiling remains beneficial for non-English high-resource languages — is genuinely novel and challenges a consensus assumption from Muennighoff et al. (2023). The hierarchical classifier pipeline adapts known English techniques (FineWeb-Edu) to German without a novel architectural contribution; it is competent application of existing ideas to a new setting. The corrected German benchmark suite and released BOLDT models are concrete artifacts with community value. Rigor & Reproducibility: No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple training seeds are reported across any experiment — a significant omission for empirical claims resting on benchmark score differences of 1–5 points. The ablation structure is strong: it isolates individual filter dimensions, curriculum variants, external baselines, and parameter/token scaling. A notable methodological concern is that the authors re-translate and clean the evaluation benchmarks themselves (using Tower+72B), then evaluate exclusively on these author-corrected benchmarks — creating a risk that the cleaning choices that benefit their approach are inadvertently incorporated, even if unintentionally. Code and models are released at huggingface.co/Boldt; the filtered datasets and annotated classifiers are not explicitly mentioned as released. This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.28075v2); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Training requires 8×A100 GPUs for pre-training and 1×A100 for instruction tuning — feasible for academic labs and small industry teams. - Efficient: Dense Core training uses 10–360× fewer total tokens than competitive baselines to reach comparable benchmark scores, directly reducing compute cost; the classifier inference over 500M documents requires one full-dataset scoring pass with an embedding model, which is non-trivial but operationally achievable. - Effective: The benchmark evidence directly shows improved zero-shot accuracy and instruction-following correctness attributable to the filtering strategy rather than only to model scale, and the results hold across two model sizes and two token budgets. - Scalable: The paper tests only 350M and 1B parameters up to 200B tokens; behavior at 7B+ parameters or multi-trillion token budgets is claimed to generalize but is not experimentally supported. The authors explicitly acknowledge this gap. Strengths: - The key empirical claim — that Dense Core repetition past 7 epochs still yields gains without overfitting — directly refutes the 4-epoch ceiling from Muennighoff et al. with controlled experiments, extending Fang et al. (2026) to the non-English constrained setting. - The AA High failure case (repeated 4.8× yet underperforming Random) is analytically valuable: it demonstrates that multi-epoch training is conditionally beneficial only when classifier quality is high, providing a calibration point for practitioners choosing annotator models. - The benchmark correction contribution is practically important: the authors identify and document a structural artifact (German verb-final order breaking completion-task targets) that has corrupted existing evaluation harness results, and release fixed versions. - The finding that training curricula transitioning from diverse to dense data consistently underperform pure dense training (contra the intuition that broad warm-up helps) is a clear, actionable result. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) results are German-only; generalization to French, Japanese, Chinese, or lower-resource languages is assumed but untested; (2) model scale is capped at 1B parameters and 200B tokens, with uncertainty about industry-scale behavior; (3) only dense transformer architectures are explored, leaving MoE and alternative attention mechanisms unexamined; (4) no toxicity, bias, or safety evaluation is conducted, and the authors note multi-epoch training on educational content may amplify rather than mitigate encoded societal biases. These acknowledgments are direct and credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The evaluation-on-own-corrected-benchmarks design is an unacknowledged Goodhart's Law risk: since the retranslation choices (which sentences to discard, how to handle word-order in HellaSwag) are made by the authors and applied to their own evaluation suite, there is no independent verification that the corrected benchmarks do not inadvertently favor models trained on educational German (which may more closely match the Tower+72B translation style). No statistical significance tests are reported despite averaging over benchmarks where differences of 1–2 points drive conclusions. The instruction-tuning evaluation uses a judge model (Llama-3.3-70B) that may have stylistic preferences correlated with formal/educational German text — the same register favored by Dense Core — which could inflate the SFT advantage. The AA High baseline uses short documents (avg 296 tokens) while Dense Core uses long documents (avg 1150 tokens), confounding quality and document-length effects without explicit ablation. The paper does not report perplexity on a held-out FW2-DE test set as a proxy for generalization beyond the six benchmark tasks. Bottom Line: A practitioner training a German small language model (≤1B parameters) should read this paper and adopt its hierarchical filtering approach; the evidence that aggressive quality filtering plus multi-epoch repetition dominates diversity-maximization under a fixed compute budget is well-supported at the tested scale, even if the absence of error bars and the self-referential benchmark correction warrant independent replication before treating the specific numbers as definitive.
Recommended 2604.28075 PDF
#67 — Innovative (67)

ProMax: Exploring the Potential of LLM-derived Profiles with Distribution Shaping for Recommender Systems

Yi Zhang, Yiwen Zhang, Kai Zheng, Tong Chen, Hongzhi Yin · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ProMax, a model-agnostic framework that uses LLM-derived user/item profiles as distributional guidance signals — rather than as feature injections — to train collaborative filtering recommenders via two auxiliary losses: supervised distribution reshaping (SDR) and self-supervised distribution reshaping (S²DR). - Why it's important: Existing LLM-profile-augmented recommenders force semantic embeddings through MLP alignment into a low-dimensional ID space, incurring semantic loss and failing to exploit profiles' collaborative signal; ProMax sidesteps that lossy fusion entirely. - How it does it: SDR treats the softmax distribution over all items induced by PCA-compressed profile embeddings as a soft training target for the recommendation model, weighted by per-user entropy; S²DR augments each user's interaction set via profile-based dense retrieval + LLM re-ranking with a sparsity-level-shift budget, then enforces bidirectional consistency between original and augmented views. - The good: On Amazon-Book with LightGCN, ProMax improves NDCG@10 by 8.44% over the best competing baseline (ProEx), with statistically significant gains (p < 10⁻⁸) across all three datasets and all four tested base models. - The missing: The entire pipeline depends on GPT-4o-mini for per-user re-ranking at inference preprocessing time, but runtime comparisons exclude this API cost, making the efficiency claims misleading. - Read it or not: Researchers building LLM-augmented general (non-sequential) recommenders should read it for the distributional guidance framing; practitioners need to budget the hidden GPT API costs before adopting it.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (75) is the strongest dimension — 10 seeds, paired t-tests with p<10⁻⁸, code released, 9 baselines, four backbones tested. Novelty (68) — distributional-guidance framing (profiles as soft targets, not features) is genuinely novel relative to alignment-and-injection paradigm. Non-Obviousness (65) — entropy-weighted SDR is principled. Completeness (55) — no formal limitations section, hidden GPT-4o-mini preprocessing cost excluded from efficiency comparison, profiles reused from RLMRec without quality ablation. Resilience (60) — small datasets (≤23K users), τ=0.2 not validated, augmentation-noise quality unmeasured. Impact (65) — code released but external GPT API dependency limits offline deployment. Weighted ≈ 65.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 55 Resilience 60 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: General collaborative filtering recommenders learn user/item ID embeddings from observed interactions, which causes severe degradation under interaction sparsity (99.57–99.80% in the tested datasets). LLMs can generate rich semantic profiles of users and items, but current approaches either (a) fine-tune the LLM itself on recommendation objectives at high computational cost, or (b) inject profile embeddings via MLP adapters that compress high-dimensional semantics (e.g., 4096-dim) into low-dimensional recommendation spaces (e.g., 32-dim), destroying semantic richness. The gap addressed is: how to exploit LLM-derived profiles without forcing them through dimensionality-destroying alignment modules. Key Idea: The core trick is to never inject the profile representation into the recommendation model's parameter space at all. Instead, profiles serve purely as an external supervisory distribution: the cosine-similarity softmax over PCA-compressed profile embeddings defines a target distribution over the full item set, and the recommendation model is trained to match it via cross-entropy. This decouples the profile's semantic space from the recommendation embedding space, avoiding alignment loss while still transferring collaborative signal. Methodology: ProMax operates in two stages. In preprocessing, LLM-generated text profiles for all users and items are embedded by a text encoder (from RLMRec's pipeline, reused here), then PCA-compressed to κ = 32 dimensions. User-side dense retrieval (cosine similarity over compressed profiles, K=100 neighbors) identifies similar users; item-side retrieval aggregates candidate unobserved items from those neighbors. A sparsity-level-shift mechanism computes per-user augmentation budgets K_u that move each user up to the next interaction-count quartile (Q0.25, Q0.5, Q0.75). GPT-4o-mini re-ranks the candidate items against the user's text profile to produce an augmented interaction set M'_u. During training, two auxiliary losses are added to the base recommendation loss: (1) SDR (Eq. 12) — a cross-entropy from the profile-induced distribution p_LLM(i|u) to the model distribution p_Θ(i|u), with uncertainty weighting w(u) = 1 − H(u)/log|I| that down-weights users with diffuse profile distributions; (2) S²DR (Eq. 14) — bidirectional cross-entropy between model distributions induced by M_u and M'_u, plus homogeneous node contrastive alignment. Critical design decisions: (i) stop-gradient on profile distributions, so profiles never backpropagate into the recommendation model — well-justified to prevent contamination; (ii) entropy-based weighting of SDR — sensible but the entropy is computed on a softmax over all items at temperature τ=0.2, making it sensitive to τ; (iii) LLM re-ranking to filter dense-retrieval candidates — justified by ablation showing slight gain over raw retrieval. Key Results: Evaluated on Amazon-Book (11K users, 9.3K items, 200K interactions), Yelp (11K users, 11K items, 278K interactions), and Steam (23K users, 5.2K items, 526K interactions) with Recall@10/20 and NDCG@10/20. LightGCN is the primary backbone for LLM-method comparisons; MF, LR-GCCF, and SimGCL are additionally tested for model-agnosticism. Against nine LLM-enhanced baselines, ProMax achieves best performance on all 12 metric/dataset combinations: NDCG@10 gains over best baseline (ProEx) are 8.44% on Amazon-Book, 4.75% on Yelp, 3.31% on Steam (p < 10⁻⁷ throughout). Applied to LightGCN, Recall@20 improves 13.3–15.6% over base model. Sparsity analysis shows the largest gains for mid-tier users (U2, U3), consistent with the sparsity-level-shift design. Efficiency comparison (Fig. 6) shows ProMax's per-epoch training time is slightly above RLMRec and below ProEx — but this excludes the GPT-4o-mini API calls in preprocessing. Ablation (Fig. 7) shows both SDR and S²DR each contribute, with their removal degrading performance across all datasets, and LLM re-ranking outperforms raw retrieval marginally. Results are averaged over 10 random seeds. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The distributional-guidance framing — using LLM profile softmax distributions as soft training targets rather than as feature vectors — is genuinely novel relative to the alignment-and-injection paradigm. The sparsity-level-shift budget mechanism is a new heuristic. The profile construction and base datasets are reused from RLMRec [21], so the empirical infrastructure is not novel. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation studies are present and isolate each of the two loss terms plus the LLM re-ranking step (three variants V1–V3). Ten random seeds with averaged results provide reasonable variance reduction, though standard deviations are not reported in the tables. Statistical significance is tested via two-tailed paired t-test with p-values reported (all < 10⁻⁵). Code is released at https://github.com/BlueGhostYi/ProRec. A concern: the paper reuses profile embeddings generated by RLMRec [21] using GPT-3.5-turbo rather than generating new profiles, which means ProMax is not fully self-contained and its performance is partially a function of RLMRec's profiling quality. The GPT-4o-mini re-ranking step is evaluated only via indirect ablation (V3), not by comparing to alternative re-rankers or measuring hallucination rates in the augmented interactions. Temperature τ=0.2 for the SDR softmax is set by default from SimGCL without sensitivity analysis. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (system built and evaluated on real recommendation datasets). - Practical: The recommendation model (LightGCN) runs on standard GPU hardware; the preprocessing requires GPT-4o-mini API access, which is commercially available but adds external dependency and ongoing cost. - Efficient: Training complexity of the two additional losses is O(|B|N·max(d,κ)) and O(|B|²d), both below the base model's encoding cost per the paper's analysis; however, preprocessing involves N×M LLM API calls for re-ranking, which is not measured. - Effective: Evidence on three real-world implicit-feedback datasets with full-ranking evaluation against nine contemporaneous baselines supports the claim that it improves recommendation quality; improvement margins are moderate (3–8% over best baseline). - Scalable: The largest dataset has 23K users and 5.2K items — small by industry standards. No experiment at 10× scale is reported; the O(|B|N) SDR complexity term grows linearly with item count and could be expensive for catalogs with millions of items. The work delivers a PyTorch implementation with released code but requires GPT-4o-mini access for the re-ranking step, limiting fully offline deployment. Strengths: - The distributional guidance framing is conceptually clean and avoids the core weakness (semantic loss from forced alignment) of prior work, and the motivation is backed by the visualization in Fig. 2(a) showing profiles encode collaborative structure. - Model-agnostic plug-in design: demonstrated across four architecturally distinct base models (MF, LR-GCCF, LightGCN, SimGCL), with consistent gains on all three datasets. - Entropy-based uncertainty weighting in SDR is a principled mechanism that automatically reduces influence of LLM profiles when they are underdetermined, addressing the hallucination/noise problem partially. - Rigorous statistical testing (two-tailed paired t-test, 10 seeds) and comprehensive baseline comparison against 9 recent LLM-augmented methods. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors do not dedicate a formal limitations section. They note that SimGCL sees smaller gains because it already provides rich self-supervised signals, suggesting diminishing returns when the base model is already strong. They acknowledge that fine-tuning-based LLM recommenders are excluded from comparison as being orthogonal in objective (sequential vs. general recommendation). No acknowledgment of the GPT API cost, dataset scale limitations, or sensitivity to profiling quality. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Hidden API cost: The per-epoch runtime comparison (Fig. 6) excludes GPT-4o-mini re-ranking preprocessing cost. For 11K users each requiring an LLM call, this can dominate total wall-clock time. The efficiency claim is incomplete without this. - Profile dependency: ProMax reuses profiles from RLMRec without ablating profile quality — it is unclear how much performance is attributable to ProMax's mechanism versus the quality of GPT-3.5-turbo profiles from [21]. - Scale: All datasets have at most 23K users and 11K items, well below industrial scale. The O(|B|N) term in SDR and the full-ranking evaluation protocol both become impractical at 10M+ items. - Temperature sensitivity: τ=0.2 is borrowed from SimGCL and not validated for ProMax's SDR objective, which operates over a fundamentally different distribution (semantic vs. interaction-based). - Augmentation noise: The quality of augmented interactions M'_u is not directly measured (e.g., what fraction of LLM-retrieved items are actually relevant). The ablation only shows marginal gain of LLM re-ranking over dense retrieval, suggesting the LLM adds limited value at extra cost. - Cold-start users: The sparsity-level-shift targets the most-sparse user group (U1) but the visualization and discussion focus on U3; gains for cold-start users (U1) appear smaller, undermining a key claimed motivation. Bottom Line: ProMax is a solid, well-executed contribution to LLM-augmented general recommendation that cleanly sidesteps the alignment-loss problem and delivers consistent empirical improvements, but practitioners should scrutinize the hidden GPT API preprocessing cost and the small-dataset scope before adopting it at scale.
Recommended 2604.26231 PDF
#68 — Innovative (67)

Long-horizon forecasting of electrolysis stability from early-stage operating data through temporal learning

chemrxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ElectroTS, a temporal sequence modeling framework that forecasts electrolyzer voltage evolution, degradation rate, and device lifetime from the first 100 hours of operating data, extending predictions to 10× the input window and beyond 1,000 hours. - Why it's important: Commercial anion-exchange membrane water electrolysis (AEMWE) requires ~100,000 hours of stable operation, but lifetime testing at even laboratory scale is prohibitively slow and expensive, blocking the rapid iteration of materials and operating conditions needed to close the stability gap. - How it does it: A channel-embedding interaction architecture trained autoregressively on a self-collected 25,000-hour AEMWE dataset predicts future voltage increments recursively; few-shot transfer learning from AEMWE to solid oxide electrolyzer cells (SOEC) leverages shared degradation dynamics despite operating in radically different physical regimes. - The good: Lifetime prediction (R² > 0.95, MAPE < 15% across 5%/7.5%/10% voltage-rise thresholds) from 100-hour inputs outperforms LSTM by more than 30% and substantially beats two general-purpose time-series foundation models (Chronos-2 and TimesFM 2.5), with the domain gap—slow monotonic drift plus abrupt loss events—explaining why general pre-training fails. - The missing: The SOEC dataset contains only 15 trajectories, so all SOEC results (including the transfer-learning benefit claim) rest on a very thin statistical base; no significance tests or error bars on R² values are reported for the primary AEMWE results. - Read it or not: Worth reading for electrochemical engineers and ML practitioners interested in applying time-series forecasting to energy devices; less compelling for general time-series researchers, as the architecture description is largely deferred to supplementary material and the design novelty relative to existing temporal models is not made fully explicit.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (70) is genuine — first large controlled AEMWE stability dataset (>25k hours) plus the cross-platform transferability finding. Rigor (65) is held back by no error bars on R², 15-trajectory SOEC base making transfer claims statistically thin, and architecture details deferred to supplementary. Completeness (70) is solid with direct limitation acknowledgment. Practical Impact (70) reflects code release and promised Zenodo dataset, with deployment-aligned lifetime-to-threshold metric. Resilience (60) suffers: only constant-current/temperature protocols tested, no industrial dynamic load cycling. Composite: 0.20*70 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*70 ≈ 67.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Electrochemical devices—particularly AEMWE and CO₂ solid oxide electrolyzers—must sustain operation over tens of thousands of hours to be commercially viable, yet state-of-the-art AEMWE demonstrations reach only thousands of hours in the lab, two orders of magnitude below the ~100,000-hour commercial target. Running full-duration stability tests for each new catalyst, membrane, or operating condition is economically and temporally infeasible. ML-based prognosis has transformed battery lifetime prediction but has not transferred to electrolyzers because (1) electrolyzers are open, dynamic systems with stochastic bubble dynamics and nonstationary voltage signals that differ fundamentally from closed battery cells, and (2) no standardized, publicly available electrolysis stability datasets exist. Prior digitized datasets from literature suffer from unknown smoothing, selective publication bias toward stable results, and insufficient diversity in degradation modes. Key Idea: The core insight is that electrochemical degradation dynamics—despite differing in mechanism and scale across technologies—share a common temporal structure: a voltage-increment signal that can be learned by a multi-channel interaction model trained autoregressively, then transferred with minimal fine-tuning data across physically distinct platforms (aqueous low-temperature AEMWE → high-temperature solid-state SOEC). The specific "trick" enabling long-horizon forecasting is a reference voltage transformation that converts absolute cell voltage into incremental voltage changes, making the slow monotonic degradation signal learnable, combined with autoregressive rollout that chains 25-hour prediction windows to arbitrary horizons. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. (1) Dataset construction: AEMWE voltage–time trajectories are self-collected across 0.5–2 A cm⁻² current densities and room temperature to 80 °C, producing >25,000 hours of data with diversity in degradation rate (14.6–1896.2 μV h⁻¹) and voltage fluctuation amplitude (15.3–485 mV in 10-hour windows). SOEC data comprise 15 trajectories (~10,000 hours total) from in-house high-temperature CO₂ electrolysis setups. (2) Model training: ElectroTS encodes time-series channels along the temporal dimension, feeds embeddings into interaction modules that aggregate them into a global core and redistribute per-channel, and outputs predictions via a linear head after N stacked layers. The objective is to minimize prediction error on future voltage increments. Preprocessing includes temporal alignment, outlier removal, uniform resampling, and the reference voltage transformation. An ensemble of 10 independently trained models provides confidence intervals. Leave-one-out cross-validation is used for AEMWE evaluation. (3) Transfer learning: The AEMWE-pretrained model is fine-tuned on SOEC data; DTW similarity between the two datasets increases substantially after preprocessing, which the authors identify as the enabling condition for successful transfer. Critical design decisions: (a) autoregressive rollout rather than direct long-horizon output—justified by flexibility but introduces cumulative error; (b) reference voltage transformation—well-motivated for making degradation signal stationary; (c) ensemble uncertainty quantification—justified pragmatically but the diversity mechanism among ensemble members is not described. Key Results: On the AEMWE dataset with 100-hour input windows: decay-rate prediction achieves R² = 0.92, MAE = 60.38 μV h⁻¹, MAPE = 24.49%. Lifetime prediction at 5%, 7.5%, and 10% voltage-rise thresholds achieves R² of 0.96, 0.97, and 0.99 respectively, with MAPE below 15% for all thresholds. Voltage MAE remains below 30 mV for prediction horizons under 700 hours and rises to ~60 mV at horizons exceeding 1,000 hours. ElectroTS outperforms linear regression, GPR, SVM, MLP, and LSTM on all metrics; relative to LSTM, improvement exceeds 30% across all metrics. ElectroTS also outperforms Chronos-2 and TimesFM 2.5 on the AEMWE dataset. For SOEC transfer learning with few-shot conditions (1, 4, or 8 training trajectories), transfer learning reduces decay-rate MAPE by more than 50% compared to training from scratch. The claim that full-dataset SOEC transfer reduces MAPE and voltage MAE substantially is stated qualitatively; specific numerical improvements are shown in Figs. 4E–F but exact numbers are not given in the main text. A representative SOEC example forecasts 2,000 hours from 100-hour input with the experimental trajectory falling within the predicted 90% CI. The AEMWE dataset claimed to be the largest publicly available controlled AEMWE stability dataset. Baselines are reasonable (LSTM is appropriate; foundation model comparison is current) but do not include recent transformer-based time-series models like PatchTST or iTransformer, which are architecturally closer to ElectroTS. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuine novelty is the construction and release of the first large-scale, controlled AEMWE stability dataset and the demonstration that standard time-series modeling plus domain-adaptive preprocessing can forecast electrolyzer lifetime 10× beyond the input window with practically useful accuracy. The model architecture is a standard channel-interaction design applied in a new domain; the transfer learning approach is borrowed from battery literature. The empirical finding that degradation dynamics contain cross-platform transferable temporal structure is new. Rigor & Reproducibility: Leave-one-out cross-validation is used for AEMWE, which is appropriate given the moderate dataset size. No significance tests or error bars are reported for R² values on the primary results. Voltage MAE SEM is shown as a function of horizon (Fig. 3C), which is positive. The SOEC evaluation uses 15 trajectories, which is very small; few-shot results with 1 training trajectory are particularly fragile statistically. The ablation of preprocessing components (DTW similarity before/after, Fig. 4D) is qualitative. No ablation is provided in the main text for the number of ElectroTS layers N or the ensemble size choice of 10. The ensemble confidence interval validation (CIs encompass trajectories) is demonstrated on representative examples only, not evaluated systematically across the test set. Code is available at github.com/schwallergroup/ElectroTS. Electrochemical data are promised on Zenodo upon acceptance (not yet available at submission). The supplementary materials reportedly contain full architectural and preprocessing details (Figs. S1–S38), which are not available in the provided main text. This is a preprint; peer-reviewed confidence is not yet warranted. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (systems built and measured experimentally, model trained and evaluated on real data). - Practical: The model runs on standard hardware; training data collection requires a custom electrolyzer testing station, which limits replication outside well-equipped labs. - Efficient: Computational cost of ElectroTS is not stated; inference from 100-hour input is presumably fast (seconds), but data collection takes hundreds of hours, which is the true bottleneck. - Effective: For AEMWE with >25,000 hours of diverse training data, the evidence supports practical degradation-rate and lifetime prediction; for SOEC with 15 trajectories, the evidence is suggestive but statistically thin. - Scalable: The paper claims generalization to new materials and operating conditions when training diversity is sufficient, but tests only two electrolysis platforms; scaling to proton-exchange membrane electrolyzers, alkaline systems, or fuel cells is asserted without evidence. The electrochemical dataset itself is described as a foundation for expansion. The framework delivers a trained model and code (software artifact). Strengths: - The self-collected AEMWE dataset (>25,000 hours, diverse degradation rates spanning two orders of magnitude) is a genuine community contribution that directly addresses the data scarcity bottleneck the authors identify; publicly releasing it via Zenodo adds lasting value. - The demonstration that preprocessing (reference voltage transformation + timestep alignment) increases DTW similarity between AEMWE and SOEC datasets sufficiently to enable effective transfer is a concrete, mechanistic insight, not just an empirical observation. - Comparison with Chronos-2 and TimesFM 2.5 is a current and honest benchmark; the finding that domain-specific modeling outperforms general foundation models for slow-drift scientific time series is practically useful guidance for the field. - The lifetime-to-threshold metric (time to X% voltage increase) is a deployment-aligned evaluation metric that connects model output to actionable maintenance decisions, going beyond generic time-series accuracy benchmarks. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge that voltage MAE and SEM increase with longer prediction horizons, attributing this to both accumulating autoregressive error and the decreasing number of long-duration training samples. They explicitly note that further improvements require expanded dataset collection and model optimization. The SOEC dataset's small size is acknowledged as motivating the transfer learning strategy rather than direct training. These limitations are confronted directly rather than minimized, which is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The model operates on voltage-time signals alone and incorporates no electrochemical features (e.g., impedance spectroscopy, electrolyte composition, temperature history, or current density metadata) that are physically informative about degradation mechanisms—it is purely phenomenological, which limits interpretability and may fail when degradation shifts to an unseen mode. The SOEC transfer-learning evaluation with 15 total trajectories is insufficient to establish statistical robustness; confidence intervals on the reported MAPE reductions are absent, and it is not reported how many of the 15 SOEC runs are held out for testing versus fine-tuning. The model was tested only under constant-current, constant-temperature protocols; real industrial operation involves dynamic load cycling, which introduces nonstationary signals the model has not been trained on and that may invalidate the transferable-structure hypothesis. The 60 mV voltage MAE at >1,000 hours is presented as acceptable, but no context is provided for what error magnitude would actually matter for materials screening decisions in practice. The baseline comparison omits recent channel-independent and patch-based time-series transformers that would be natural architectural competitors. Bottom Line: Practitioners developing or screening AEMWE catalysts and membranes will find the dataset and forecasting framework directly actionable—the ability to infer 1,000-hour stability from 100-hour measurements is a genuine acceleration of the materials development cycle—but the SOEC results should be treated as preliminary given the 15-trajectory dataset, and the framework's validity under dynamic load cycling remains undemonstrated.
Citations
#69 — Innovative (67)

AcademiClaw: When Students Set Challenges for AI Agents (8 ▲ )

Junjie Yu, Pengrui Lu, Weiye Si, Hongliang Lu, Jiabao Wu, Kaiwen Tao, Kun Wang, Lingyu Yang, Qiran Zhang, Xiuting Guo, Xuanyu Wang, Yang Wang, Yanjie Wang, Yi Yang, Zijian Hu, Ziyi Yang, Zonghan Zhou, Binghao Qiang, Borui Zhang, Chenning Li, Enchang Zhang, Feifan Chen, Feng Jian, Fengyin Sun, Hao Qiu, Hao Zheng, Haoran Zhu, Hongyu Liu, Jianbin Deng, Jiaxin Song, Jiaying Chi, Jiayou Shi, Jie Fang, Jinghui Zhong, Jingyu Zhou, Jinze Li, Junfeng Yi, Junyan Yu, Junzhi Xue, Ni Song, Pengyi Chen, Qi Chen, Quansheng Li, Rui Tao, Shenghai Gong, Shenhang Lu, Tianqi Shen, Tianxiang Zhu, Tiehan Kang, Tingyu Li, Wendi Wu, Xiao Shen, Xiao Zhou, Xiaotao Zhang, Xinrong Li, Xuankun Yang, Xun Zhang, Yan Li, Ye Lu, Yi Wang, Yibo Zhou, Yichi Zhang, Yihao Sun, Yijun Huang, Yixin Zhu, Yixuan Wu, Yuchen Sun, Yue Wu, Yuheng Sun, Yukun Li, Yutian Tu, Yuxuan Qin, Yuzhuo Wu, Zeyu Li, Zhengyu Lou, Zhenning Ran, Zizhu He, Pengfei Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 5 May 2026
