The paper delivers a methodology and experimental results only — no software artifact, dataset, or trained model weights are released. Strengths - The dependency-graph structure over sub-claims is a genuine improvement over independent sub-claim verification; the ablation explicitly isolates the value of edges (~2pp macF1) and the qualitative explanation graphs provide traceable reasoning paths absent from prior work. - The core assumption — that gold-consistent evidence yields stronger LLM explanations — is non-obvious and validated across two LLM backbones (GPT-3.5 and LLaMA-3.1) and two prompt formulations in Appendix B, with quantified informativeness/soundness gaps (e.g., GPT-3.5 soundness 4.86 for false-aligned vs. 2.83 for true-aligned on false claims). - Explanation evaluation is multi-faceted: LLM-as-judge (GPT-3.5 Likert), objective discrepancy (label-distance, no LLM), and human evaluation with Krippendorff's α and Kendall's W — more rigorous than most papers in this space. - Backbone agnosticism is demonstrated: G-Defense_LLaMA3.1 outperforms the GPT-3.5 version on LIAR-RAW (32.49% vs. 31.55% macF1), confirming that commercial API dependency is not fundamental. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) G-Defense is better suited for accuracy/explanation-quality-focused scenarios than latency-sensitive real-time systems; (2) evaluation is limited to social media and political claims from Snopes/PolitiFact — they plan future work on scientific, financial, and health misinformation; (3) no multi-modal support for images or videos; (4) graph-aware pretraining (graph foundation models) is not yet adapted to linguistic claim-centered graphs. These are stated directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Graph construction quality is never measured: there is no metric for whether LLM-generated edges correctly capture logical dependencies, so it is unknown whether the graph encodes meaningful structure or LLM hallucination noise. - The "competing wisdom" assumption is weakest for half-true claims — Table 7 shows F/T explanation quality scores converge for "Half" claims — yet LIAR-RAW has four intermediate categories (barely-true, half-true, mostly-true, pants-fire), which likely explains the muted gains on that dataset; this structural mismatch is not discussed. - No latency measurements are reported (only a formula and a qualitative discussion), making it impossible to assess deployment feasibility against any latency budget. - The LLM-as-judge evaluation (GPT-3.5 scoring GPT-3.5-generated explanations) introduces systematic circular bias; while the paper adds an objective discrepancy metric, it does not include a judge-model ablation to quantify the bias magnitude. - The minimum-two-sub-claim constraint is never ablated against an adaptive alternative; the complexity analysis (Figure 6) shows G-Defense slightly underperforms the no-decomposition baseline for 2-sub-claim RAWFC claims, suggesting forced decomposition of simple claims is a known but unaddressed cost. Bottom Line G-Defense is a well-executed, rigorously ablated extension of defense-based fake news detection that delivers meaningful gains on RAWFC and provides genuinely interpretable explanation graphs; practitioners building explainable misinformation pipelines over unverified web reports should read it, but should temper expectations given the modest LIAR-RAW improvements, single-domain evaluation, and absence of any released implementation.
Recommended
#75 — Innovative (65)

Rethinking Reasoning-Intensive Retrieval: Evaluating and Advancing Retrievers in Agentic Search Systems (27 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces BRIGHT-PRO, an expert-annotated benchmark that extends BRIGHT with multi-aspect gold evidence and weighted aspect labels, and evaluates retrievers under both static and agentic (deep-research loop) protocols; it also contributes RTriever-Synth, an aspect-decomposed synthetic training corpus, and RTriever-4B, a LoRA fine-tuned 4B embedding model. - Why it's important: Deep-research agents spend excessive rounds on iterative retrieval because retrievers are trained and evaluated on single-passage relevance, concealing failures in evidence portfolio construction that directly inflate latency and compute cost in agentic workflows. - How it does it: The key mechanism is decomposing an LLM-generated reference answer for each query into 2–3 non-overlapping reasoning aspects, synthesizing one complementary positive passage per aspect, and then generating positive-conditioned hard negatives — passages that share topical cues with the query but deliberately omit the aspect covered by the matching positive. - The good: ReasonIR-8B, despite being reasoning-trained, falls to 11th of 13 retrievers on aspect-aware α-nDCG@25 (41.0), isolating single-positive training as a structural misalignment: a retriever can rank one relevant passage highly while completely failing evidence portfolio coverage. - The missing: RTriever-4B is trained on simple one-positive one-negative triplets sampled from a corpus that already contains complementary multi-positive bundles, so the multi-positive training signal the paper argues is essential is never exploited; no ablation compares this choice against multi-positive objectives. - Read it or not: Researchers building or evaluating retrieval components for agentic/deep-research pipelines should read this; the benchmark construction methodology and the static-vs-agentic rank divergence finding are the most transferable contributions.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) reflects a substantively new benchmark with multi-aspect weighted annotation and the AER metric, plus the sharp finding that ReasonIR-8B underperforms BM25 on aspect-aware metrics. Non-Obviousness (70) is genuine — static-vs-agentic rank divergence and BM25's agentic recovery are non-trivial. Rigor (65) is solid on annotation quality (κ=0.742) but undermined by the GPT-5 judge (proprietary, non-reproducible), 175-query agentic eval with single runs, and no significance tests. Completeness (60) is the main drag: the paper advocates multi-positive training but RTriever-4B is trained on single-positive triplets, with no ablation isolating which design choice drives gains. Impact (65) for benchmark + model release. Weighted ≈ 65.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 60 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Reasoning-intensive queries, as encountered in deep-research agents (systems that iteratively plan, search, and synthesize), require a retriever to surface a diverse set of complementary passages that jointly cover multiple reasoning subproblems — not simply the single most relevant document. The dominant benchmark for this regime, BRIGHT, provides each query with a narrow gold set derived from one or two web pages, measures retrievers in isolation from the agent loop, and supplies no labels indicating which reasoning aspect each passage covers. Synthetic training corpora (e.g., ReasonIR, DIVER) typically pair each query with one positive and hard negatives, training retrievers to rank a single relevant document highly. A retriever optimized this way can score well on NDCG while consistently missing the evidence diversity an agent actually needs across retrieval rounds, causing the agent to compensate with additional iterations at high computational cost. Key Idea The core insight is that evidence portfolios for reasoning-intensive queries decompose into non-overlapping aspects, and both training signal and evaluation metrics should be organized around aspect coverage rather than passage-level relevance. Concretely: generate a comprehensive reference answer for each query, decompose that answer into 2–3 non-overlapping reasoning aspects, realize each aspect as a complementary positive passage, and train with positive-conditioned hard negatives that share topical surface features while deliberately omitting the needed aspect. On the evaluation side, use α-nDCG (which penalizes aspect redundancy) and weighted aspect recall rather than standard NDCG, and embed the retriever inside an LLM agent loop to measure downstream answer quality and interaction efficiency. Methodology BRIGHT-PRO extends the StackExchange subset of BRIGHT (excluding coding and theorem subsets as too syntax-dependent). Field-specific expert annotators decompose each query's information need into a compact set of reasoning aspects (mean 3.74 aspects/query), assign each aspect a Likert importance weight (1–5, normalized to sum to 1), and collect positive documents per aspect by re-auditing original BRIGHT passages and gathering new ones via web and AI-assisted search using a customized FireCrawl interface. A second annotator reviews every example; inter-annotator agreement on aspect importance weights reaches weighted Cohen's κ = 0.742. The final benchmark covers 7 domains (Biology, Earth Science, Economics, Psychology, Robotics, Stack Overflow, Sustainable Living), 739 queries, 526,319 documents, and averages 7.13 positive documents per query. For static evaluation, the primary metric is α-nDCG@k (Clarke et al., 2008) with α=0.5, which penalizes repeated retrieval from already-covered aspects; weighted aspect recall (A-Recall@k) and standard NDCG@k/Recall@k are reported as diagnostics. For agentic evaluation, each retriever is plugged into a single-tool LLM agent (GPT-5-mini-08-07 or Qwen3.5-122B-A10B) with identical prompts, top-5 passages per round, and 2,048-token truncation per document. Fixed-round (R∈{1,2,3}) and adaptive-round (agent self-terminates, hard cap 100 rounds) protocols are both run on a fixed 175-query sample (25/domain). Answers are scored by GPT-5 as LLM-as-Judge against reference answers generated from the aspect-annotated gold passages; per-aspect coverage ∈ {0, 0.5, 1} is weighted and aggregated as reasoning completeness; an overall quality score (1–5) is also assigned. The Efficiency-Quality Reward AER = OQ × e^{−0.05(R−1)} penalizes rounds beyond the first. RTriever-Synth starts from 140K randomly sampled MS MARCO queries, rewrites them into DeepResearch-style long-form queries using persona-conditioned LLM prompting (PersonaHub personas), generates a comprehensive reference answer, decomposes it into 2–3 non-overlapping aspects, and synthesizes one positive passage per aspect plus one hard negative per positive (conditioned on the positive's title and TL;DR, with an explicit avoidance constraint). RTriever-4B is then trained by LoRA fine-tuning Qwen3-Embedding-4B (r=16, α=32, frozen backbone) with INFONCE objective (τ=0.02), one randomly sampled positive and one hard negative per query, 5 epochs, batch 768, 2 NVIDIA B200 GPUs. Key Results On static α-nDCG@25, the ranking is: BGE-Reasoner-8B (68.0) > DIVER-4B-1020 (63.7) > DIVER-4B (59.9) > RTriever-4B (55.3) > INF-Retriever-Pro-7B (53.8) >> Qwen3-8B (49.5), with all general-purpose 7–8B retrievers scoring 39–50. RTriever-4B beats every 7–8B general-purpose embedder despite being 4B. ReasonIR-8B ranks 11th at 41.0 — below BM25's 40.3 — because its single-positive training optimizes the BRIGHT-style metric rather than multi-aspect coverage. On fixed-round agentic evaluation (GPT-5-mini, Round 3), BGE-Reasoner-8B leads (Overall=4.31), RTriever-4B is third (4.25). On adaptive-round AER with GPT-5-mini, BGE-Reasoner-8B leads (AER=3.65, 5.10 rounds), RTriever-4B is second (AER=3.51, 6.01 rounds). BM25, last statically (40.3), climbs to comparable performance in agentic settings (AER=3.53 GPT-5-mini) because agent-issued follow-up queries reduce vocabulary mismatch. DIVER-4B-1020 collapses from second statically to seventh (AER 3.56→3.11) under the Qwen3.5 agent, revealing retriever-agent compatibility effects below the top tier. Five qualitative failure modes are characterized in case studies from 175 adaptive-round traces: early-round efficiency, evidence deprivation, repetition bias, aspect tunnel vision, and hypothesis hopping. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (BRIGHT-PRO), NEW METHOD (aspect-decomposed synthesis pipeline with positive-conditioned hard negatives), NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (static rank diverges from agentic rank; BM25 vocabulary-mismatch recovery under agent-issued queries; single-positive training causes ReasonIR-8B to underperform BM25 on multi-aspect metrics). The synthesis pipeline for multi-positive complementary evidence is a genuine methodological contribution; the benchmark construction methodology builds incrementally on BRIGHT but adds substantively novel annotation structure. The AER metric is a minor formalization of an existing intuition. Rigor & Reproducibility The benchmark has credible quality control (dual annotation, weighted κ=0.742, reference answer validation against human-written answers reaching 4.80 vs. 4.75 on completeness). However, no ablation studies are provided: the paper does not isolate whether the gains from RTriever-4B come from aspect decomposition, positive-conditioned negatives, query rewriting, or simply fine-tuning on any in-domain data. No error bars or significance tests accompany the main tables. The agentic evaluation uses only 175 queries (25/domain), which is modest given the variance in LLM-agent outputs across runs. GPT-5 as a judge is an unreleased proprietary model at the time of this preprint, making the judge scores non-reproducible for external researchers. The paper mentions artifact links in the title (RTriever and BRIGHT-PRO icons) but does not provide explicit URLs in the paper body. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.04018v1). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: RTriever-4B inference is a standard dense embedding model and runs on commercially available GPU hardware; training required 2 NVIDIA B200 GPUs but LoRA keeps the base model frozen. - Efficient: LoRA fine-tuning on 140K examples is lightweight relative to full fine-tuning; inference cost matches any 4B embedding model and is lower than the 7–8B baselines it outperforms. - Effective: RTriever-4B beats all evaluated 7–8B general-purpose retrievers on both static and agentic metrics, demonstrating that training objective alignment matters more than parameter count for multi-aspect retrieval — a specific, well-evidenced claim. - Scalable: Evaluation is limited to BRIGHT-PRO's 526K-document corpora and 739-query benchmark; no test at web-scale retrieval or with larger query loads, and the claim that the approach generalizes beyond StackExchange domains is untested. The work delivers a trained model (RTriever-4B), a benchmark (BRIGHT-PRO), and a training corpus (RTriever-Synth), but reproducibility is constrained by the GPT-5 judge dependency and incomplete artifact disclosure in this preprint. Strengths - The multi-aspect benchmark annotation with weighted aspects and a two-annotator quality protocol produces a genuinely richer evaluation signal than existing reasoning-retrieval benchmarks, and the case studies convincingly illustrate failure modes that flat NDCG misses. - The controlled agentic evaluation design — same agent, prompts, and tool interface across all retrievers, with only the retriever varying — cleanly isolates retriever quality from agent behavior. - The empirical finding that ReasonIR-8B underperforms BM25 on aspect-aware metrics is a sharp, falsifiable claim that directly indicts single-positive training as a design flaw, and it is backed by quantitative tables rather than just qualitative argument. - The AER metric correctly formalizes the deployment-relevant tradeoff between answer quality and interaction cost, and the GTE-7B case (highest overall quality yet seventh by AER) illustrates why this metric surfaces something NDCG and per-round quality scores miss. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that BRIGHT-PRO covers only seven StackExchange domains and that the scope may not capture real-world diversity. The benchmark is small due to the high cost of expert annotation. RTriever-4B uses a deliberately simple training recipe (one-positive one-negative triplets) and the authors identify multi-positive objectives, aspect-aware sampling, and negative curricula as future directions. These are direct, credible acknowledgments. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The paper argues that complementary positives are the crucial training signal but trains RTriever-4B on single-positive single-negative triplets sampled from the multi-positive corpus; the gap between claimed mechanism and actual training procedure is unaddressed and undermines the core training contribution. - No ablation isolates the contribution of (a) query rewriting, (b) aspect decomposition, (c) positive-conditioned vs. generic hard negatives, or (d) base model choice; the gains over the Qwen3-Embedding-4B base could plausibly come from fine-tuning on any BRIGHT-like data. - BRIGHT-PRO uses GPT-5 (proprietary, unreleased) for both reference answer generation and judging; external researchers cannot reproduce or audit the agentic evaluation scores. - The agentic evaluation runs each retriever once per query (no repeated runs with different random seeds or agent temperatures), so answer quality scores may have high variance; the adaptive-round results in particular are sensitive to stochastic agent decisions. - The benchmark is built exclusively on StackExchange, which skews toward hobbyist technical questions; whether the aspect-annotation methodology transfers to professional domains (medicine, law, finance) or truly open-domain queries is untested. Bottom Line The benchmark design and the static-vs-agentic rank divergence finding are genuinely useful contributions to the retrieval community, but the paper's training contribution is weakened by the mismatch between the multi-positive training signal it advocates and the single-positive objective it actually uses, and the results rest on a non-reproducible GPT-5 judge — practitioners should treat the numbers as directional until the judge and artifacts are publicly accessible.
Recommended 2605.04018 PDF
#76 — Innovative (65)

From Skill Text to Skill Structure: The Scheduling-Structural-Logical Representation for Agent Skills (9 ▲ )

Qiliang Liang, Hansi Wang, Zhong Liang, Yang Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 4 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SSL (Scheduling-Structural-Logical), a three-layer typed JSON representation that converts free-text SKILL.md agent skill documents into disentangled layers capturing invocation interface, scene-level execution phases, and atomic action/resource evidence, instantiated via an LLM-based normalizer. - Why it's important: As LLM agent systems scale to large skill registries, text-heavy SKILL.md artifacts force every downstream component — routers, discovery engines, security reviewers — to repeatedly re-parse the same verbose natural-language documents to extract evidence that differs per task, creating an expensive and error-prone representational bottleneck. - How it does it: A four-pass constrained NL2JSON pipeline using DeepSeek-V3.2 extracts three layers with closed vocabularies: a scheduling record (goal, intent signature, I/O contracts, control-flow flags), a directed scene graph (phases typed from a 7-value vocabulary), and a directed logic-step graph (atomic actions typed from 12 act_types with 7 resource_scope categories), with hard structural validation and bounded retry. - The good: On Skill Discovery over 6,184 skills and 431 intent queries, Desc + SSL-Rich raises MRR@50 from 0.649 to 0.729 (95% bootstrap CI: [0.051, 0.111]) over the strongest non-SSL baseline, with consistent gains across all five query types and a source-outline control condition that rules out generic text-compression explanations. - The missing: Gold labels for Risk Assessment were produced by the same class of LLMs (Gemini, Claude, GPT-5) that received the SSL record during labeling, and the evaluation judge (DeepSeek-V3.2) also sees SSL — this structural circularity means the claimed F1 gain (+0.100) lacks an independent human expert ground truth to validate it. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building LLM agent skill registries or pre-execution risk review pipelines; the Skill Discovery result is credible and the schema is practically actionable, but those focused on runtime agent safety will find the Risk Assessment evaluation insufficiently validated.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (72) — the three-layer SSL decomposition mapping cognitive linguistic theories onto skill artifacts is a genuinely new schema; the closed-vocabulary design is practical. Rigor (60) limited by the structural circularity in Risk Assessment (gold labels produced by LLMs that received SSL as input, judge also sees SSL), benchmark stratified by SSL-derived signals, single embedding model, no inter-annotator agreement. The Skill Discovery side is more credible (bootstrap CIs, source-outline control). Non-Obviousness (65) — the layer mapping is reasonable but not surprising. Completeness (70) — limitations honestly enumerated. Resilience (55) — the Risk Assessment claims would not survive adversarial review without independent human labels. Impact (75) — released schema and normalized records for 6,184 skills are concretely usable. Composite ~65.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 70 Resilience 55 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM agent systems increasingly package reusable capabilities as skills — bundles of instructions, control flow, constraints, and callable operations documented in SKILL.md-style files. As skill registries grow to thousands of entries, downstream systems (routers, discovery engines, security reviewers, policy checkers) must repeatedly re-parse the same natural-language artifacts to extract three semantically distinct types of evidence: invocation-level interface signals (when to call, what inputs/outputs), scene-level execution structure (how the workflow is organized), and action/resource-use evidence (what operations and external resources are touched). These are entangled in a single textual surface, forcing every downstream task to independently recover them from verbose, ambiguous prose. No structured intermediate representation existed that disentangles these three evidence types in a machine-usable, reusable, cross-skill-comparable form. The problem is acute for skill discovery in large overlapping registries and for pre-execution risk assessment, where scattered resource-access and side-effect signals are easy to miss in long instructional text. Key Idea The core insight is that Schank and Abelson's three cognitive linguistic theories map cleanly onto the three distinct kinds of evidence embedded in skill artifacts: Memory Organization Packets (goal-oriented contexts) motivate a skill-level scheduling record encoding invocation interface; Script Theory (stereotyped ordered scenes with roles and transitions) motivates a scene graph encoding execution phases; and Conceptual Dependency (primitive action structures) motivates a logic-step graph encoding atomic typed operations with resource boundaries. Encoding these as a typed JSON graph with closed, restricted vocabularies forces each evidence type into a separate, machine-comparable layer that downstream systems can selectively consume without re-parsing the full document. Methodology SSL maps a skill artifact d into a tuple (r_sch, G_str, G_log, R_cont, R_entry). The scheduling layer r_sch is a single record with fields: skill_goal, intent_signature, tags, expected_inputs/outputs, dependencies, control_flow_features (Boolean flags for branching, loops, tool calls, sensitive-resource access), and entry pointers. The structural layer G_str is a directed scene graph whose nodes have scene_type drawn from {PREPARE, ACQUIRE, REASON, ACT, VERIFY, RECOVER, FINALIZE}, with data contracts (input/output), entry/exit conditions, and next_scene_rules routing to named scenes or terminal values (END_SUCCESS, END_FAIL). The logical layer G_log is a directed logic-step graph with nodes having act_type from a 12-value vocabulary (READ, SELECT, COMPARE, VALIDATE, INFER, WRITE, UPDATE_STATE, CALL_TOOL, REQUEST, TRANSFER, NOTIFY, TERMINATE) and resource_scope from 7 categories (MEMORY, LOCAL_FS, CODEBASE, PROCESS, USER_DATA, CREDENTIALS, NETWORK, OTHER). Cross-layer connectivity is restricted to containment and entry pointers. Normalization uses DeepSeek-V3.2 via a four-pass constrained NL2JSON pipeline: (1) extract scheduling record, (2) decompose into 2–5 scenes, (3) expand each scene into grounded atomic logic steps, (4) validate and retry. Hard validation checks globally unique identifiers, valid enum values, valid containment links, valid entry pointers, and transition targets. Initial pass uses DeepSeek-V3.2-Thinking at temperature 0.1; failures retry with DeepSeek-V3.2-NothinkIng. Yield: 6,184/6,300 = 98.16%; 116 skills excluded. Human fidelity audit of 100 skills reports 83% source-grounding accuracy. Two evaluation tasks are deployed: Skill Discovery using a 6,184-skill corpus, 431 intent queries (auto-generated and filtered by realism annotation), FAISS inner-product retrieval over Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B embeddings, with 10 representation variants compared; and Risk Assessment over 252 stratified-sampled skills with 6-dimensional binary labels (data exfiltration, destructive behavior, privilege escalation, covert execution, resource abuse, credential access), gold labels from majority vote of Gemini-3.1-pro-preview, Claude-Sonnet-4.5, and GPT-5, evaluated with a fixed DeepSeek-V3.2 judge across 5 input representations. Key Results Skill Discovery: Desc + SSL-Rich achieves MRR@50 = 0.729, NDCG@5 = 0.748, NDCG@10 = 0.770, Recall@10 = 0.905, compared to Desc + Source Outline (best non-SSL baseline): 0.649/0.670/0.689/0.833. The improvement is statistically supported: 95% bootstrap CI for MRR@50 gain = [0.051, 0.111] over 20,000 resamples. Full SKILL.md (MRR@50 = 0.645) is weaker than Desc + SSL-Rich (0.729), confirming the gain is not explained by embedding more text. Ablation shows monotonic improvement from SSL-Shallow (0.716) to SSL-Rich (0.729) when paired with description only; Full-document + SSL variants consistently underperform Description + SSL variants across all three levels. Safety-oriented queries show the weakest absolute performance (Desc + SSL-Rich: 0.674) versus compositional (0.778) or scenario (0.762). Risk Assessment: Full SKILL.md + SSL achieves macro F1 = 0.509 versus Full SKILL.md = 0.409; 95% CI for improvement = [0.051, 0.152]. Full SSL alone (0.422) only marginally exceeds Full SKILL.md (0.409), confirming SSL is additive rather than substitutive. Largest per-dimension gains: data exfiltration F1 = 0.511→0.699, resource abuse = 0.271→0.419, credential access = 0.695→0.780. Covert execution remains poorly detected across all conditions (best F1 = 0.264). A counterexample (electric-proxy-auth) is reported where SKILL.md alone correctly detects data-exfiltration and credential-access risk but SKILL.md + SSL misses both, because the normalizer emphasized validation and request construction rather than the source prose's explicit discussion of auth token injection and secret handling. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD (SSL schema) + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (6,184-skill corpus, 431-query retrieval benchmark, 252-skill risk benchmark). The three-layer typed decomposition grounded in cognitive linguistic theories is genuinely novel as applied to agent skill artifacts — no prior work explicitly disentangles scheduling interface, execution structure, and action/resource evidence for skills. The retrieval and risk evaluation pipelines are standard applications of dense retrieval (FAISS, sentence embeddings) and LLM-judge frameworks to a new domain rather than methodological contributions in themselves. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations across 10 representation variants for Skill Discovery are systematic and include a critical source-outline control that isolates SSL's contribution from generic source-text expansion. Bootstrap confidence intervals are reported for both main comparisons with 20,000 replicates. A per-query-type breakdown (Table 8) is provided. Human fidelity audit covers 100 skills with explicit binary criteria — adequate methodology but small sample relative to the 6,184-skill corpus. The normalizer's 17% hallucination rate (1 – 0.83) is not broken down by layer or field type, obscuring where errors concentrate. Critical concern: gold labels for Risk Assessment were produced by models that received the complete SSL record as input, and the evaluation judge (DeepSeek-V3.2) also receives SSL — structural circularity may inflate apparent SSL benefit for risk classification. No expert human gold labels exist for Risk Assessment. Skill Discovery benchmark uses automatically generated queries filtered by LLM-mediated realism annotation, not real production user queries. The evaluation benchmark is stratified by risk-relevant signals derived from normalized SSL metadata, creating potential sampling bias favorable to SSL. Code, schema, and data are planned for release at https://github.com/COOLPKU/SSL (per abstract footnote; paper body notes release upon acceptance — a minor inconsistency). No trained model weights are involved; normalization requires external LLM inference per skill. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile) - Practical: Normalization requires standard LLM API access; retrieval uses Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B with FAISS on commodity CPU (25 MB stored embeddings per representation per 6,184 skills) — accessible on commercially available infrastructure. - Efficient: Normalization costs roughly 6,300 LLM calls for an initial corpus; ongoing per-skill cost is one API call per new skill. Local retrieval is lightweight. Per-skill inference cost is not reported in dollar terms. - Effective: Consistently improves MRR@50 (+0.080) and macro F1 (+0.100) over text-only baselines with statistical support, but evaluation is on LLM-generated benchmarks with potential circularity in risk labels — real-world effectiveness against expert-curated ground truth is unvalidated. - Scalable: Tested at 6,184 skills — a realistic mid-sized registry. Normalization is embarrassingly parallelizable. No experiments beyond this scale. The 17% normalizer hallucination rate may compound for diverse or poorly documented skills at scale. The paper delivers a schema (SSL guidelines), normalized SSL records for 6,184 public skills, Skill Discovery queries and labels, and Risk Assessment rubrics and labels — concrete artifacts usable by others. No trained model; SSL generation requires an LLM inference call per skill. Strengths - The three-layer decomposition cleanly maps distinct evidence types to distinct downstream needs, and the ablation in Table 1 confirms monotonic retrieval gains as richer SSL layers are added — the design rationale is validated empirically, not just asserted. - The source-outline control condition (deterministic text excerpts from SKILL.md without SSL fields) decisively rules out the hypothesis that SSL gains are explained by generic source-text expansion, a confound most retrieval papers in this space do not address. - Bootstrap confidence intervals on paired comparisons provide statistical grounding absent from many IR and agent-systems papers; the qualitative counterexample (Case 3, electric-proxy-auth) is reported honestly without minimization. - The closed vocabulary design (7 scene types, 12 act types, 7 resource scopes) makes normalized records cross-skill comparable without open-ended summarization, which is a practical systems contribution for registry indexing. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly enumerate four limitations: (1) SSL is extracted from static artifacts and cannot capture dynamic runtime behavior such as downloaded payloads, constructed commands, or conditional resource access; (2) normalizer fidelity depends on LLM extraction that may omit relevant facts, over-regularize, or assign coarse enums to underspecified or obfuscated skills; (3) evaluation scope covers only Skill Discovery and Risk Assessment and does not assess impact on actual agent planning, execution, or monitoring; (4) the Skill Discovery benchmark uses automatically generated rather than human-authored queries; (5) risk labels should not be interpreted as substitute for expert security audit or runtime safety evaluation. These are confronted directly, which is creditable. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The most significant gap is circularity in Risk Assessment: gold labels were co-produced by models (Gemini, Claude, GPT-5) that received the complete SSL record as labeling evidence, and the evaluation judge (DeepSeek-V3.2) also sees SSL at test time. The gold standard may be systematically biased toward SSL's ontology of risk, inflating the apparent benefit of SSL-augmented inputs — an independent human expert gold-standard subset is necessary to validate the F1 claims. - The Risk Assessment benchmark is stratified by risk-relevant signals derived from normalized SSL metadata (tool calls + network/credential resources), which likely makes SSL-augmented inputs perform better on that benchmark than they would on a naturally distributed population of skills. - All retrieval experiments use Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B exclusively; it is unknown whether SSL gains transfer to stronger embedding models or are specific to a relatively weak model that may depend more heavily on structural/lexical cues. - Safety-oriented queries show the weakest SSL gains (Desc + SSL-Rich: 0.674 vs. 0.548 baseline), but the paper does not analyze why — this is the most security-critical query category and the failure mode most relevant to the paper's risk-assessment motivation. - No inter-annotator agreement is reported for the human fidelity audit or for the manual audit of risk labels, making it impossible to assess annotation reliability. Bottom Line SSL is a well-motivated, cleanly designed intermediate representation with credible retrieval gains on a large-scale benchmark, but the Risk Assessment evaluation's structural circularity — gold labels co-produced by LLMs that received SSL as input, evaluated by a judge also receiving SSL — leaves the security use case incompletely validated, and practitioners should treat the F1 improvements as provisional until confirmed against expert human labels.
Recommended 2604.24026 PDF
#77 — Innovative (65)

Decoding Scientific Experimental Images: The SPUR Benchmark for Perception, Understanding, and Reasoning

Junpeng Ding, Zichen Tang, Haihong E, Mengyuan Ji, Yang Liu, Haolin Tian, Haiyang Sun, Pengqi Sun, Yang Xu, Yichen Liu, Haocheng Gao, Zijie Xi, Ruomeng Jiang, Peizhi Zhao, Rongjin Li, Yuanze Li, Jiacheng Liu, Zhongjun Yang, Jintong Chen, Siying Lin · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SPUR, a benchmark of 4,264 expert-curated multiple-choice QA pairs from 1,084 multi-panel scientific experimental images, organized into a three-stage evaluation hierarchy (Perception → Understanding → Reasoning) across seven tasks. - Why it's important: Existing MLLM benchmarks severely underrepresent multi-panel experimental images — the dominant format in biomedical literature — and lack cross-panel relation understanding and quantitative reasoning tasks needed to assess AI readiness for science automation. - How it does it: SPUR filters 5,000+ PMC papers by impact factor and panel count (≥7 panels), uses GPT-4o to generate candidate QA pairs, eliminates text-only-solvable questions via a 10-trial statistical test, and validates survivors through multi-tier domain expert review. - The good: The decoupled MCoT analysis (Table 5) is the most insightful finding: when perception is correct, MCoT methods improve reasoning accuracy by 7.99%–26.93%; when perception fails, MCoT amplifies hallucinations and reduces reasoning accuracy by 2.10%–23.08%, establishing perception quality as the decisive bottleneck. - The missing: No human expert baseline accuracy is reported, so the claim that models fall "significantly short of expert-level requirements" lacks a quantitative anchor — the 60% threshold as an expert proxy is asserted, not measured. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or evaluating MLLMs for AI-for-Science applications; less relevant for those focused on general visual reasoning benchmarks where the biomedical experimental image domain is peripheral.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) — new benchmark in genuinely underserved domain with hierarchical task taxonomy. Rigor (60) reduced — no human expert baseline, no ablations on benchmark design choices, no error bars, GPT-4o both generator and evaluatee, preprint. Non-Obviousness (72) — decoupled MCoT analysis showing perception bottleneck is non-obvious. Completeness (60) — missing expert baseline is a major hole. Resilience (58) — life-science only despite broader claims, near-saturation risk. Impact (68) — dataset publicly released. Weighted ≈ 64.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 60 Resilience 58 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Automating scientific literature comprehension requires MLLMs to interpret multi-panel experimental images (Western blots, staining images, kinetic charts) that arrange experimental processes and results across many panels with implicit cross-panel dependencies (e.g., dose-response trends, control vs. treatment comparisons). Existing benchmarks either aggregate isolated figures from broad scientific domains without panel-level granularity (ScienceQA, MMMU, M3CoT), target low-complexity images averaging fewer than eight panels (MMSci, SciAssess, SFE), or omit quantitative reasoning entirely (MicroVQA). No benchmark simultaneously requires fine-grained panel perception, cross-panel relation understanding, and expert-level quantitative inference — the three capabilities that define how a domain expert reads an experimental figure. Key Idea: The core insight is that interpreting a multi-panel scientific image is a strictly hierarchical cognitive process: accurate panel-level perception is a prerequisite for cross-panel relation understanding, which is in turn required for correct expert-level reasoning. SPUR operationalizes this hierarchy as a benchmark evaluation pipeline, and the key empirical payoff is demonstrating that perceptual failures cascade — numerical misreading of a bar value propagates through cross-panel comparison and produces a wrong quantitative conclusion even when the reasoning chain itself is logically sound. Methodology: Dataset construction proceeds in three stages. First, 5,000+ open-access PMC papers published within the last decade with source journal impact factor > 3.0 are manually curated; a YOLO-based panel detector (pre-trained on a proprietary biomedical dataset) segments images and discards those with ≤6 panels (removing 77.6% of candidates); domain experts then verify experimental workflow completeness (removing a further 14.2%). Second, domain experts write prompt templates aligned with the task hierarchy and use GPT-4o to generate 7,608 candidate QA pairs. Third, quality control applies: (a) a statistical shortcut filter — GPT-4o answers each question from text only 10 times, and items answered correctly ≥5 times are discarded (removing 21.2%); (b) expert review for biomedical validity and task alignment rejects a further 28.9%, leaving 4,264 QA items. The seven tasks span Numerical Perception (NP), Morphological Perception (MP), Information Localization (IL), Trend Analysis (TA), Heterogeneous Integration (HI), Qualitative Reasoning (Qual.), and Quantitative Reasoning (Quant.). No formal objective function; the benchmark is evaluated by MCQ accuracy. Key Results: Twenty MLLMs (8 proprietary, 12 open-source) were evaluated. Only Gemini 3 Pro Preview exceeds 60% overall (60.57%); Claude 3.7 Sonnet (thinking) scores 59.52%, Gemini 2.5 Pro Preview 59.00%, GPT-5.1 57.68%, GPT-4o 53.95%. The weakest task is Numerical Perception (NP): Gemini 3 Pro Preview leads at 61.26%; most open-source models score below 55%. Information Localization (IL) is similarly weak (top score 59.67%). Quantitative Reasoning lags Qualitative Reasoning by 12.76%–31.41% across models. Trend Analysis (TA) accuracy for Claude 3.7 Sonnet (thinking) drops from 60.70% to 34.00% as the number of cross-panel relations increases from 1 to 4. Among open-source models, GLM-4.5V (59.87%) outperforms InternVL3-78B (51.94%) and Qwen2.5-VL-72B (48.21%) despite comparable or smaller size. MCoT methods fail to provide consistent gains: on Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct, Cantor raises perception accuracy from 49.50% to 52.02% but VIC drops it to 41.12%; when perception is correct, all MCoT methods except VIC boost reasoning by 7.99%–26.93%, but when perception is incorrect all except Cantor reduce reasoning accuracy by 2.10%–23.08%. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The genuine novelty is (1) the focus on multi-panel biomedical experimental images as a distinct and underserved image type, (2) the three-stage hierarchical task taxonomy that decouples perception, understanding, and reasoning, and (3) the textual-shortcut elimination protocol. The overall benchmark construction methodology (GPT-4o-assisted generation + expert review) is standard and applies existing techniques to a new domain. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations on benchmark design choices (e.g., panel-count threshold, IF cutoff, shortcut filter threshold). No error bars or significance tests on model accuracy scores. The dataset (CC BY 4.0) and code (Apache 2.0) are publicly released at BUPT-Reasoning-Lab/SPUR on GitHub. The YOLO-based panel detector is trained on a proprietary dataset, so the image-filtering step is not fully reproducible. GPT-4o is used for both question generation and shortcut filtering, and GPT-4o is also one of the evaluated models — while visual grounding requirements reduce direct contamination risk, the question style may favor GPT-family models. Human expert accuracy is not reported, which is a significant omission for a benchmark claiming to measure the gap to expert-level performance. This is a preprint; results have not been peer reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile — the paper builds and evaluates a benchmark dataset; no trainable system is proposed. - Practical: Evaluation requires only standard inference APIs or locally hosted MLLMs; no special hardware beyond what model inference demands. - Efficient: The benchmark contains 4,264 questions — evaluation cost is proportional to model inference latency and is comparable to other MLLM benchmarks of similar size. - Effective: The benchmark successfully discriminates among models (range 34.47%–60.57% overall) and reveals task-specific failure modes, supporting its validity as a diagnostic tool. - Scalable: The benchmark is fixed at 4,264 items across seven life science disciplines; no mechanism for automated expansion to new experimental paradigms or non-biological domains is described. Given that Gemini 3 Pro Preview already approaches 60%, saturation may occur within 1–2 model generations without expansion. The paper delivers a publicly released dataset; the associated codebase is available but the proprietary panel-detection model limits full replication of data collection. Strengths: - The hierarchical Perception → Understanding → Reasoning framework cleanly maps to how experts actually read experimental figures, and the task decomposition enables diagnostic attribution of failure modes rather than a single opaque accuracy score. - The textual-shortcut elimination filter (10-trial text-only answering, ≥5 correct = discard) is methodologically principled and operationally credible — 21.2% of candidates were removed by this step, indicating real filtering work. - The decoupled MCoT analysis (Table 5) is the paper's strongest empirical contribution: isolating cases where perception is correct vs. incorrect reveals that the bottleneck is visual perception, not reasoning capacity — a non-obvious and actionable finding for model developers. - The dataset's structural complexity (14.3 panels per image on average, six fine-grained panel types, five experimental paradigms) substantially exceeds existing benchmarks and matches the complexity of real published experimental figures. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that the MCQ format provides limited visibility into internal reasoning processes. They also acknowledge that while SPUR covers seven life science disciplines from PMC, extending findings to non-biological scientific fields may require additional adaptation and dataset expansion. These limitations are stated directly and briefly without minimization, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: No human expert baseline is provided — the benchmark is titled around "expert-level" requirements, but expert human accuracy is never measured, making it impossible to calibrate how far models actually are from human performance. The benchmark's domain is entirely life science (Molecular Biology, Cell Biology, Oncology, Neuroscience, Immunology, Physiology/Pharmacology, Microbiology) despite claims of cross-disciplinary generalization — physics, chemistry, and materials science experimental images are absent. GPT-4o is used to generate questions and is also one of the evaluated models; while the shortcut filter mitigates the risk, question style may still favor GPT-family models. The YOLO panel detector is trained on a proprietary dataset that is not released, limiting reproducibility of the curation pipeline. With Gemini 3 Pro Preview already at 60.57%, the benchmark may saturate within one or two model generations, and no ceiling analysis or difficulty-scaling strategy is discussed. The paper does not investigate whether fine-tuning or targeted training on experimental images could close the gap — all evaluated methods are inference-only, leaving the training-time question unanswered. Bottom Line: SPUR fills a real gap — multi-panel biomedical experimental images are genuinely underserved and the three-stage task decomposition is the right framework — but the absence of a human expert baseline undermines the central evaluative claim, and the benchmark's domain restriction to life sciences limits generality beyond AI4S applications in biology.
Search Results 2604.27604 PDF
#78 — Innovative (64)

Contextual Linear Activation Steering of Language Models

Brandon Hsu, Daniel Beaglehole, Adityanarayanan Radhakrishnan, Mikhail Belkin · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CLAS (Contextual Linear Activation Steering), a method that replaces the single fixed scalar coefficient in linear activation steering with per-block, context-dependent coefficients computed by a learned sensing vector cℓ ∈ R^(k+1) trained via next-token prediction loss on as few as 10 prompt-completion pairs. - Why it's important: Fixed steering coefficients in standard LAS require expensive grid search over model generations and apply uniformly to all prompts regardless of context, causing systematic under- or over-steering that limits deployment reliability. - How it does it: At each transformer block ℓ, CLAS modifies the forward pass as h̃ℓ,t = hℓ,t + (cℓ · [hℓ,t 1])dℓ, where dℓ is an RFM-probe-derived steering direction (fixed) and cℓ is the learned sensing vector; only cℓ is trained, decoupling interpretable direction extraction from adaptive coefficient learning. - The good: CLAS achieves 86.3%, 88.4%, and 83.5% average steering accuracy on Qwen2.5-7B, Llama-3.1-70B, and Llama-3.1-8B respectively (vs. 64.0%, 75.1%, 74.6% for LAS) while reducing tuning time from 1–3 hours to under 4 minutes. - The missing: CLAS collapses to 8% on the MNMT task for Llama-3.2-1B (vs. 57–66% for LoRA/ReFT), revealing a systematic failure mode under-discussed by the authors; the evaluation also relies on 50 test prompts per task and o3-mini as judge for 6 of 11 tasks, creating potential evaluation dependency. - Read it or not: Researchers and practitioners working on lightweight, interpretable LLM specialization with limited labeled data should read this paper; those seeking large-scale or task-specific fine-tuning may find LoRA or ReFT at higher ranks more practical.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (60) — context-dependent sensing-vector parameterization is a modest but principled extension of rank-1 ReFT with a different direction source. Rigor (55) — preprint, 50-prompt test sets, no error bars/seeds, and o3-mini both generates training completions and judges 6/11 tasks (circular evaluation). Non-Obviousness (65) — decoupling RFM-derived directions from learned coefficients is a clean architectural insight. Completeness (65) — clean ablations isolate context-dependence vs. NTP objective, but the MNMT 8% collapse on Llama-3.2-1B is unexplained and missing few-shot/system-prompt baselines. Resilience (60) — small test sets and judge dependency would not survive adversarial review. Impact (75) — strong data efficiency (10 examples, <4 min), code released, surprisingly robust on JailbreakBench where LoRA/ReFT collapse. Composite: 0.20*60 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*75 ≈ 61.8.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Linear activation steering (LAS) inserts a fixed-direction, fixed-magnitude perturbation into a model's residual stream at inference time to elicit latent behaviors without modifying weights. The practical bottleneck is that the scalar steering coefficient α must be selected by grid search over generated outputs — evaluated by humans or an LLM judge — which is computationally expensive and yields a context-independent value applied uniformly to every input token and every model block. This rigidity causes under-steering on prompts that weakly activate the target concept and over-steering on prompts where the concept is already present, degrading generation quality and limiting reliable deployment across diverse real-world inputs. Key Idea: The core insight is that the scalar steering coefficient in LAS can be parameterized as a linear function of the current activation: αℓ,t = cℓ · [hℓ,t 1], where cℓ is a k+1 dimensional sensing vector learned by minimizing next-token prediction loss on a small labeled dataset. This replaces a single hand-tuned scalar with L learned vectors (one per transformer block), each of which reads the activation at that block to decide how strongly to apply the pre-extracted steering direction — requiring no generation, no evaluator calls, and no grid search. Methodology: The pipeline has two decoupled stages. First, block-specific steering vectors {dℓ} are extracted via Recursive Feature Machine (RFM) probing: a dataset of N=200 prompt-concept label pairs is passed through the frozen LLM, last-token residual stream activations are collected at each block, and an iterative kernel ridge regression (KRR) is fit using a Mahalanobis Laplace kernel whose data-dependent metric M is updated via the Average Gradient Outer Product (AGOP). The steering vector dℓ is the principal eigenvector of the final AGOP matrix, oriented by Pearson correlation with the labels. This stage is backpropagation-free and completes in minutes. Second, sensing vectors {cℓ} are learned by freezing the entire LLM, modifying the forward pass with the CLAS update, and minimizing standard next-token prediction loss on n=10 prompt-completion pairs via AdamW (up to 8 gradient steps, early stopping on validation loss). The three critical design decisions are: (1) decoupling direction extraction from coefficient learning, which preserves RFM's interpretability properties; (2) using NTP loss rather than evaluator-based scoring, enabling gradient-based optimization without generation; and (3) using a bias term in the sensing vector (the concatenated constant 1), which allows the coefficient to be nonzero even at zero activations. CLAS shares functional form with rank-1 DiReFT but differs fundamentally in how the steering direction is obtained — RFM probe vs. jointly trained — resulting in nearly orthogonal directions (unsigned cosine similarity ≈ 0.01–0.04 across all tasks and models). Key Results: On 10 steering tasks (excluding JailbreakBench), CLAS achieves average accuracy of 86.30 / 88.40 / 83.50 / 58.30% on Qwen2.5-7B / Llama-3.1-70B / Llama-3.1-8B / Llama-3.2-1B, versus LAS at 64.00 / 75.10 / 74.60 / 52.50%, LoRA at 78.80 / 77.90 / 82.90 / 62.40%, and ReFT at 76.80 / 84.90 / 73.70 / 65.10%. CLAS is best on 3 of 4 models; ReFT leads on Llama-3.2-1B. JailbreakBench is a notable outlier: CLAS achieves 88 / 84 / 84 / 65% while LoRA and ReFT collapse to near 0% due to repetition failure from partial steer dataset completions. Tuning time is 3–4 minutes for CLAS vs. 35 minutes to 3 hours for LAS grid search. Concept monitoring accuracy with RFM directions averages 96.14–97.19% (Avg over blocks) vs. LoRA 70.63–74.41% and ReFT 68.85–73.77%. Test sets are 50 prompts per task — a small scale that limits statistical confidence. Increasing LoRA/ReFT to rank-4 or rank-8 does not consistently improve results. This is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is context-dependent activation steering via sensing vectors trained with NTP loss, decoupled from probe-derived steering directions. The use of RFM probing for steering is drawn from prior work by two co-authors (Beaglehole et al., Science 2026); the NTP-based coefficient learning and the sensing vector formulation are the new contributions. This is not a straightforward combination of existing ideas — the decoupling is a deliberate architectural choice with interpretability consequences — but the method is a modest extension of rank-1 ReFT with a different direction source. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations test rank-4 and rank-8 variants of ReFT and LoRA (Appendix C.1), confirming that higher rank does not consistently help. A coefficient-baseline ablation (Appendix C.2) confirms that per-block LAS tuned via NTP loss underperforms grid search, demonstrating that the advantage of CLAS is the sensing vector's context-dependence rather than the NTP objective alone. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported; the 50-prompt test sets and o3-mini judge evaluations are not replicated across seeds or evaluators. For 6 of 11 tasks, o3-mini is both the completion generator (for the steer dataset) and the evaluator, which creates a potential circular dependency: the judge may favor outputs stylistically similar to its own generations. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/bhdsc/CLAS. No model weights or fine-tuned checkpoints are released. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL — methods evaluated via inference experiments on standard GPU hardware. - Practical: Sensing vector learning requires only one forward-backward pass per example on consumer-grade GPUs (experiments use NVIDIA A40 for models under 70B and H200 for 70B), making deployment accessible. - Efficient: CLAS adds L(k+1) parameters — roughly half of rank-1 ReFT's L(2k+1) and far less than rank-1 LoRA's L(k+q) (q > k by 4×) — and trains in under 4 minutes on 10 examples, a 15–45× speedup over LAS grid search. - Effective: CLAS convincingly outperforms LAS on 10 of 11 tasks and matches or exceeds ReFT/LoRA on 3 of 4 models; it fails on MNMT for Llama-3.2-1B (8% vs. 57–66%) in a way the paper does not adequately explain. - Scalable: Tested up to Llama-3.1-70B; no experiments beyond 70B; test sets of 50 prompts do not establish scalability of evaluation robustness, and the 10-example steer dataset generalization to substantially out-of-distribution inputs is not evaluated. The work delivers a software library (code released) but no trained model weights or production-grade tooling. Strengths: - Decoupling direction extraction from coefficient learning preserves the full interpretability of RFM probe directions: RFM achieves 96–99% concept monitoring accuracy (max-block metric) versus 88–97% for LoRA/ReFT directions, meaning CLAS steering vectors remain semantically meaningful probes unlike fine-tuning directions. - Exceptional data efficiency: learning from only 10 labeled completions to adapt coefficient behavior across all blocks is a practically significant result, directly enabling low-resource deployment scenarios. - JailbreakBench result is genuinely surprising: LoRA and ReFT at rank-1 completely fail (near 0%) on anti-refusal steering due to degenerate repetition, while CLAS achieves 65–88%, demonstrating that activation-space methods have qualitative advantages for certain behavior types that gradient-based weight modifications cannot replicate. - The ablation comparing scalar LAS-NTP (per-block or per-model) versus sensing-vector CLAS-NTP (Table 13) cleanly isolates the contribution of context-dependence from the objective function, which is methodologically honest. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that CLAS underperforms ReFT on Llama-3.2-1B (the smallest tested model), that per-task accuracy varies and CLAS occasionally underperforms (Figure 1 shows a few negative Δ vs. LAS), and that JailbreakBench behavior is attributed to partial completions in the steer dataset. These are stated directly. The authors do not acknowledge evaluation scale concerns, o3-mini judge dependency, or the MNMT failure mode on small models. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - MNMT accuracy for CLAS on Llama-3.2-1B is 8% — far below LoRA (57%) and ReFT (66%) — with no investigation of why sensing vectors fail on multilingual generation for the smallest model; this is not a corner case but a systematic failure on one of 11 tasks. - The o3-mini judge evaluates outputs for 6 tasks, and o3-mini also generated the completions used as training targets for those same tasks; this shared provenance may inflate CLAS scores if outputs that resemble o3-mini's style receive higher judge scores. - No comparison to few-shot prompting or system-prompt baselines, which can often achieve comparable behavioral steering without any parameter modification; the "Non-Task Prompt" accuracy column in Table 6 shows the gap CLAS must exceed is sometimes small (e.g., Alpaca, ToxicChat). - The 50-test-prompt evaluation scale is small; per-task accuracy differences of 5–15 percentage points between methods correspond to 2.5–7.5 example differences and are not statistically tested. - Generalization to out-of-distribution prompt distributions is not evaluated — all test prompts come from the same source dataset as probe/steer data, so robustness to domain shift is unknown. Bottom Line: CLAS is a practically useful, well-motivated improvement to linear activation steering that any practitioner exploring lightweight, interpretable LLM specialization with limited labeled data should read — but the 50-prompt test sets and o3-mini evaluation dependency mean the reported accuracy gains should be treated as indicative rather than definitive.
Recommended 2604.24693 PDF
#79 — Innovative (64)

Odysseus: Scaling VLMs to 100+ Turn Decision-Making in Games via Reinforcement Learning (6 ▲ )

Chengshuai Shi, Wenzhe Li, Xinran Liang, Yizhou Lu, Wenjia Yang, Ruirong Feng, Seth Karten, Ziran Yang, Zihan Ding, Gabriel Sarch, Danqi Chen, Karthik Narasimhan, Chi Jin · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 4 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Odysseus, an open RL training framework that fine-tunes an 8B vision-language model (Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct) for 100+ turn long-horizon decision-making in Super Mario Land using PPO with a lightweight CNN-based turn-level critic and positive-advantage filtering. - Why it's important: Existing RL methods for VLMs (GRPO, Reinforce++) stagnate past 20–30 turn horizons due to unstable credit assignment; this bottleneck blocks progress on embodied AI agents that require sustained sequential reasoning and perception. - How it does it: A small CNN value network (DQN-style backbone, hidden dim 512) provides turn-level advantage estimates in place of a second full VLM critic, decoupling temporal credit assignment from token generation and cutting the compute overhead of actor-critic training roughly in half. - The good: Odysseus achieves 1,511.9 average game progress across five training levels vs. 512.9 for the best frontier model (GLM-4.6V at 106B parameters) — a 2.95× gain — and 5.59× over the base Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, with standard errors computed over 256 independent evaluation runs. - The missing: Training and evaluation are confined to a single game family (Super Mario Land / Super Mario Bros.); whether the CNN-critic recipe generalizes to genuinely different domains (web navigation, 3D open worlds, different action semantics) is entirely untested. - Read it or not: RL researchers working on VLM-based embodied agents should read this — the turn-level CNN critic design and the rigorous 7-method ablation are concretely useful; practitioners outside the game-agent niche can skim the recipe and skip the game-specific results.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (75) and Novelty (65) are the strongest dimensions — the cross-modal actor-critic (small CNN turn-level critic for VLM RL) is a genuinely non-obvious design that demonstrably stabilizes training where critic-free methods plateau. Rigor (75) — unusually thorough seven-method × two-seed ablation with unsmoothed run plots, 256-run evaluation with SE for open-source comparisons; weakened by only 8-run proprietary comparisons and no significance tests. Completeness (60) — no human baseline, no full-level completion metrics, generalization reported as relative gains. Resilience (50) is the main drag — entire evaluation is a single game family (Mario), so the 'long-horizon RL recipe' claim is structurally fragile; transfer to Super Mario Bros. is near-trivial. Impact (60) — code release status unclear, recipe is concrete but domain-narrow. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*60 = 65.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 60 Resilience 50 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Building VLM agents capable of sustained interactive decision-making faces a critical RL training barrier: critic-free methods that have driven progress in short-horizon reasoning tasks (GRPO, Reinforce++) exhibit unstable dynamics and negligible performance gains in environments where episodes span 100+ turns. Supervised fine-tuning on human demonstrations is data-hungry and difficult to scale. Even the largest proprietary frontier models (GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, GLM-4.6V) achieve very limited zero-shot progress in Super Mario Land (310–512 average x-coordinate progress out of a maximum ~2316) because accurate spatial perception and precise motor timing across extended trajectories remain hard without task-specific training. The research gap is practical: no prior work has demonstrated stable, improving VLM RL training beyond ~30-turn horizons. Key Idea The core insight is that temporal credit assignment in long-horizon game environments can be handled by a small separate CNN operating on game frames, rather than by a second full VLM critic. Game visual states are compact and regular enough that a DQN-style convolutional network can learn accurate turn-level value estimates, enabling PPO's advantage computation without the 2× memory and compute penalty of large-model critics. Pairing this lightweight critic with positive-advantage filtering (zeroing negative-advantage samples) further stabilizes gradient updates in a regime where the policy improvement signal is noisy across long trajectories. Methodology Odysseus operates as a three-stage pipeline. (1) SFT Initialization: ~5,058 game frames are sampled across 10 Super Mario Land levels from walkthrough videos; GPT-o3 generates structured chain-of-thought responses (perception → reasoning → answer XML tags), and the base VLM is fine-tuned with cross-entropy loss for 1 epoch at LR=1e-7. (2) Multi-task RL: PPO with a turn-level CNN critic (Nature CNN backbone, hidden dim 512, SmoothL1 loss against discounted return-to-go with γ=0.95). The reward is forward x-coordinate progress per turn — dense, minimal, and read directly from game RAM. Advantages are batch-normalized and, under positive-advantage filtering, negative advantages are zeroed and normalization is applied only over positive samples (Eq. 1 in the paper). An auto-curriculum sets per-level sampling weight ω_k ∝ 1/N_k (inverse average trajectory length) to equalize gradient contributions across easy and hard levels. RL training runs for 190 steps with 1,024 trajectories per batch, up to 400 turns per trajectory, at LR=1e-6. (3) Interaction Protocol: each turn, the VLM receives one upsampled game frame (160×144 → 1280×1152) plus a fixed instruction prompt; it outputs perception/reasoning/answer tags; the parsed action is applied with frame-skipping (15 frames for jumps, 5 otherwise). Critical design decisions: turn-level vs. token-level critic (well-justified by cost and game reward structure); positive-advantage filtering (empirically justified, but the paper concedes this is an open question); minimal scaffolding with single-frame input (intentional, to isolate RL's contribution). All VLM components — vision encoder, projector, language backbone — are trained end-to-end. Key Results Odysseus (RL on SFT init) achieves average level progress of 1,511.9 (±8.95 SE) across five training levels compared to 270.2 for the base model, 512.9 for GLM-4.6V (106B), 348.2 for Claude Sonnet 4.6, and 310.5 for GPT-5.4. Odysseus-Zero (RL without SFT) achieves 1,354.9 — the RL stage does the overwhelming majority of the work; SFT alone (261.8) produces no reliable gain over base. The algorithmic ablation across seven RL variants (GRPO outcome/process ×2, Reinforce++, PPO ×2) shows all critic-free methods plateau or oscillate, while PPO with the CNN critic reaches ~800 progress by 0.38M samples. VLM-based RL reaches comparable converged performance to classical deep RL (CNN policy from scratch) at roughly 2× fewer training samples, and does so without engineered action spaces. Generalization: +32.2% over base on off-policy held-out states from training levels; +41.5% on five entirely unseen Super Mario Land levels; +23.1% on all 32 levels of Super Mario Bros. General-domain benchmarks (MMMU, MathVision, RealWorldQA) are preserved to within 1% of base model scores. Open-source model statistics use 256 runs; proprietary model statistics use only 8 runs (SE for GPT-5.4: ±25.95). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the cross-modal actor-critic design — pairing a VLM actor with a lightweight CNN value network for turn-level advantage estimation in long-horizon RL. The auto-curriculum (inverse trajectory length weighting) is a sensible adaptation of known curriculum ideas. The SFT → multi-task RL pipeline structure is standard in the VLM alignment literature, applied here to a harder horizon regime. The paper does not claim a new problem, but meaningfully extends demonstrated RL stability from 20–30 turns to 100+ turns. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough for the core algorithmic comparison: seven methods, two independent runs each, with individual (unsmoothed) run plots in the appendix. Final Odysseus evaluation uses 256 runs (open-source) with mean ± SE reported — commendably robust for this literature. Hyper-parameters are fully documented across four tables. Code is built on a "substantially modified VeRL framework"; a project page is listed (odysseus-project.github.io/) but explicit code/weight release is not stated in the paper text. Methodological concern: the reward signal (x-coordinate delta) is a proxy for game completion, and the agent is never evaluated on full-level completion rate. The comparison against proprietary models (8 runs each) is statistically underpowered given the spread — the 5× gain claim over GPT-5.4 rests on estimates with ±25.95 SE on a mean of 310. No significance tests are reported for any comparison. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.00347v1, May 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper trains and evaluates a system end-to-end in a game environment. - Practical: Inference requires a single 8B VLM, feasible on one A100/H100; training requires multi-GPU infrastructure with parallel game emulation to generate 1,024 trajectories per batch. - Efficient: The CNN critic adds negligible overhead relative to the VLM actor (contrast to prior work using a second full VLM as critic); tens of millions of interaction samples are required total, a meaningful but manageable compute budget with parallel emulators. - Effective: Strong evidence for the game-playing objective; the agent demonstrably learns perception and timing skills the base model lacks; generalization is positive but modest (+23–41%), with no data on full level completion rate. - Scalable: Tested only at 8B VLM scale; no experiments at larger model sizes. Auto-curriculum handles multi-task within-game scaling. Cross-game transfer is limited to the Mario franchise. The work delivers a training framework (Odysseus, built on VeRL) and trained model; explicit open-source release status is not confirmed in the paper. Strengths - The turn-level CNN critic design is practically impactful: it makes PPO-based VLM RL affordable by avoiding a second large-model critic, and the paper demonstrates empirically that it outperforms all critic-free alternatives in this setting. - The algorithmic ablation is unusually thorough for this subfield — seven RL variants × two seeds × individual run plots make the PPO superiority claim credibly established rather than cherry-picked. - The three-tier generalization evaluation (off-policy same levels → unseen same-game levels → different game) is well-structured and provides a principled difficulty ladder rather than a single transfer result. - Preservation of general-domain VLM capabilities (within 1% on MMMU, MathVision, RealWorldQA after tens of millions of game interactions) is a meaningful result showing the RL training does not catastrophically forget. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper acknowledges that SFT teacher responses (from GPT-o3) do not produce optimal game actions, and that the SFT stage alone does not improve performance — RL is required to correct for this. The role of positive vs. negative advantage samples in RL training is described as "an actively studied question." No further limitations are stated; the paper does not acknowledge the single-game-family evaluation scope, the low statistical power on proprietary model comparisons, or the gap to human-level performance. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The cross-game generalization test uses Super Mario Bros., which shares nearly identical visual vocabulary, physics, and action semantics with Super Mario Land. Transfer to structurally different games (e.g., open-world 3D, turn-based strategy, text-driven) is untested, making it impossible to know whether the CNN-critic recipe is broadly applicable or Mario-specific. - Proprietary frontier models are evaluated with 8 runs vs. 256 for open-source models. The ±SE on GPT-5.4 (±25.95) and Claude Sonnet 4.6 (±22.61) is substantial relative to the gap between frontier models; the headline "5× better than GPT-5.4" claim is statistically shakier than the paper implies. - No human baseline is reported. The paper states "a human player with no prior experience can readily achieve non-trivial progress" but does not quantify this, leaving Odysseus's absolute capability gap to humans uncharacterized. - Generalization improvement (+32–41%) is reported as relative improvement over the base model, not as absolute level completion rate. An agent improving from 5% to 7% completion would show a large relative gain but demonstrate limited practical capability. - There is no ablation on SFT data quantity or annotation quality; it is unclear whether the GPT-o3-generated annotations are necessary or whether random walkthrough frames with no CoT would suffice as initialization. - The single-frame, no-history design is a deliberate simplification, but the paper does not test whether providing observation history (standard in POMDP settings) would further improve performance or alter the RL dynamics. Bottom Line Practitioners building VLM agents for long-horizon interactive tasks will find the turn-level CNN critic design actionable and the algorithmic ablation convincing; the game-specific scope limits immediate generalization, but the recipe is concrete enough to transfer to other dense-reward embodied settings.
Recommended 2605.00347 PDF
#80 — Innovative (64)

Natural language ontology : diachronic restructuring of nominal classification in Dagaare

degruyterbrill.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a 10-class THING TYPE nominal classification system for the Lobr dialect of Dagaare (a Mabia/Gur language of Ghana and Burkina Faso), arguing the system is semantically transparent and ontologically grounded, organized around the central notions of humanness and diminution. - Why it's important: Prior consensus held that Mabia (Gur) classificatory systems are only partially semantic — this mischaracterization obscures the ontological principles structuring one of West Africa's major language families and distorts the typological record of nominal classification in Niger-Congo. - How it does it: Applies a form–meaning pairing approach from Systemic Functional Linguistics (SFL), treating THING TYPE as an ideational grammatical system of the nominal group, then uses grammaticalization theory and internal reconstruction to trace each suffix to a lexical source. - The good: The demonstration that a single noun (e.g., yɛ́b 'sibling') can take three distinct plural suffixes — each encoding a different construal of the referent (non-human, discrete human, familiar kin) — directly refutes the claim of semantic inconsistency and is the paper's strongest empirical move. - The missing: The ~600-noun classification rests substantially on native-speaker introspection by the sole analyst, with no inter-rater reliability reported for suffix alternation interpretations, raising circularity risk for the claim of semantic transparency. - Read it or not: Worth reading for specialists in African linguistics, grammaticalization, and nominal classification typology; general linguists will find the ontological framing stimulating but must tolerate the absence of quantitative exception rates and formal verification.
Why this rank: Theoretical profile. Novelty (70) — genuine new formulation of Dagaare nominal classification with five specific grammaticalization pathways, refuting prior consensus. Rigor (55) — peer-reviewed, supplementary 600-noun DB released, but sole-analyst-as-native-speaker introduces circularity and no exception-rate quantification. Non-Obviousness (75) — the referent-construal reanalysis (same noun, different suffix encoding distinct construals) is genuinely surprising and explanatory. Completeness (70) — limitations confronted candidly. Resilience (60) — diachronic reconstructions explicitly hypothesized pending Oti-Volta comparative data. Impact (40) — descriptive linguistics, no system, but typological influence likely. Composite: 0.25*70+0.20*55+0.25*75+0.10*70+0.15*60+0.05*40 = 64.
theoretical Novelty 70 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Prior descriptions of Dagaare (Bodomo 1997; Bodomo and Marfo 2006; Miehe 2012a) and related Mabia languages (Abubakari and Issah 2023) consistently concluded that their nominal classificatory systems are only partially semantically motivated: although nouns can be grouped by shared number suffixes, the mapping between inflectional morphology and referent semantics is irregular. This conclusion is largely an artifact of two methodological choices. First, analysts adopted a form-first approach, assigning nouns to classes based on shared affix patterns rather than asking what meaning each suffix encodes. Second, they relied on a "compounding test" to identify abstract noun roots, producing a tripartite root–singular–plural model; the present study argues this test is invalid because compound-internal reduction in Dagaare is a general morphophonological process applying even to mass nouns that lack NUMBER distinctions. The consequence was that the largest class — non-characterised entities, marked by the plural suffix -rɪ and its 24 phonetic allomorphs — appeared semantically incoherent, with different allomorphs treated as separate morphemes rather than conditioned variants of a single underlying form. The gap is the absence of a meaning-first, form–meaning-pairing analysis that reads suffix distributions as encoding categories of referents (thing types) rather than categories of nouns. Key Idea The core insight is that Dagaare plural suffixes mark classes of referents construed by nominal groups, not inherent properties of nouns. The same noun root can take different plural suffixes in different discourse contexts, with each suffix encoding a distinct ontological construal of the referent — for instance, fàarà 'priest' takes -rɪ when the priests are unknown (non-characterised), or -mɪǹɛ̀ when they are familiar associates. This referent-construal view transforms what appeared to be morphophonological arbitrariness into a semantically motivated network with Aristotelian genus-species structure: a top-level distinction between named and common entities, then between countable and non-countable, then between diminutive and non-diminutive, then between human and non-human, with further splits within each branch based on social or material criteria. Methodology The analysis integrates three frameworks. (1) SFL system network: THING TYPE is modeled as an ideational grammatical system of the nominal group, formalized as a system network in which choices ramify in increasing delicacy (from COUNTABILITY down through humanness, social valuation, characterization, etc.). The nominal group structure is: (Deictic^)(Deictic^)(Classifier^) Thing (^Epithet)(^Numerative)(^Deictic)(^Qualifier). (2) Form–meaning pairing: every posited thing type must be realized by a unique morphological or phonological reflex; this constraint prevents positing semantically unmotivated classes and disciplines the analysis. (3) Grammaticalization and internal reconstruction: since Dagaare lacks written historical records, fossilized items and synchronic bridging contexts (forms ambiguous between lexical and grammatical readings) are treated as residues of earlier stages, applying the four canonical mechanisms of grammaticalization (extension, desemanticization, decategorization, erosion). Data consist of approximately 600 noun entries from the Lobr variety, drawn from a text archive (folktales, casual conversations, radio broadcasts, Bible translations) supplemented by elicitation and native-speaker introspection. Categorization continued to saturation — defined as no new affix patterns emerging.
