The method's FR component is explicitly trained to align with this pipeline's output, creating circular evaluation that is not disclosed or discussed. - Schema extraction requires GPT-4o-mini plus human review for each new domain; the cost, failure rate, and sensitivity of SCPRM performance to extraction quality beyond the injected-noise proxy are not quantified. - The 512-token input cap may force truncation of long multi-hop trajectories — the paper does not report the fraction of training/test trajectories truncated or whether truncation biases the reward estimates. - The 1.18% average Hits@k improvement headline aggregates Hits@3 and Hits@1 metrics across datasets, making the composite figure hard to interpret; gains over the strongest same-backbone baseline (vanilla PRM) are more modest than the headline suggests. - No comparison to DAMR [37], a concurrent MCTS-KGQA method from 2025 that is cited but not evaluated against. - No analysis of MCTS search budget sensitivity — the number of MCTS rollouts is not reported, and its impact on performance vs. compute is not studied. Bottom Line SCPRM's log-sum reward decomposition is a principled and technically sound fix for a real failure mode of process reward models in KGQA, but practitioners should treat the quantitative results with caution until independent replication on released datasets confirms the gains hold outside the authors' own evaluation infrastructure.
Search Results 2605.02819 PDF
#92 — Innovative (60)

Hallucinations Undermine Trust; Metacognition is a Way Forward (10 ▲ )

Gal Yona, Mor Geva, Yossi Matias · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 5 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proposes reframing LLM hallucination as confident error rather than mere error and argues that faithful uncertainty—aligning verbalized hedges with the model's intrinsic confidence—is a tractable complementary objective to knowledge expansion for improving LLM trustworthiness. - Why it's important: Frontier models continue to hallucinate on long-tail factoid QA, and the dominant strategy of eliminating hallucinations via abstention imposes a severe utility tax because models lack the discriminative power to separate their correct answers from errors at the instance level. - How it does it: The paper combines a calibration-vs-discrimination distinction, simulation evidence (AUROC ≈ 0.71 implies 52% utility loss to hit 5% error rate), and an empirical scatter plot of 12 frontier models on SimpleQA Verified to argue that a discrimination gap is fundamental, then proposes faithful uncertainty (linguistic decisiveness tracking intrinsic confidence assertion-by-assertion) as the escape path. - The good: The utility-error trade-off visualization using real frontier model data (Figure 3, SimpleQA Verified) concretely shows the unpopulated "ideal" region and is the most persuasive piece of evidence for the discrimination gap claim. - The missing: The core "discrimination gap is fundamental" claim rests on a conjecture backed by simulation and correlational evidence, not a proof; no direct experiment tests whether faithful uncertainty interventions actually improve the utility-error frontier rather than just shifting operating points. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on hallucination mitigation, uncertainty quantification, or agentic reliability—the calibration-vs-discrimination framing is sharp and the open-problem taxonomy (bootstrapping paradox, signal preservation, confidence attribution, causal evaluation) is actionable.
Why this rank: Theoretical profile. Novelty (65) and Non-Obviousness (70) carry the score — the calibration-vs-discrimination sharpening with the utility-tax simulation (52% utility loss at AUROC 0.71) is a genuinely useful reframing, and the metacognition-as-control synthesis is non-obvious. Rigor (55) limited — central claim is an explicit conjecture, AUROC evidence base is only three studies on English QA, no new experiments to validate that faithful uncertainty actually moves the frontier rather than slides along it. Completeness (65) helped by candid scoping and acknowledgment of the latent-truth counterview. Resilience (55) — the 'fundamental gap' claim is the obvious target for an adversarial reviewer. Impact (40) low under theoretical weighting since no algorithmic recipe is delivered, only pointers to prior implementations. Composite ≈ 0.25*65+0.20*55+0.25*70+0.10*65+0.15*55+0.05*40 = 60.5.
theoretical Novelty 65 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 55 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Frontier LLMs continue to produce factually incorrect outputs on long-tail factoid QA—questions about rare entities where training data is sparse—even at current scale. The dominant community response has been to expand the model's knowledge boundary (more data, better training recipes) or to apply abstention: refuse to answer when uncertain. The latter creates a utility-factuality tradeoff: guaranteeing low hallucination rates by abstention means suppressing large volumes of correct answers alongside incorrect ones. The paper locates the root cause in a discrimination gap: current models can be well-calibrated in aggregate (confidence scores match empirical accuracy rates) yet fail to distinguish, instance by instance, which of their answers are correct. This gap means the abstention threshold needed to drive the error rate from 25% to 5% discards over half of valid answers at typical empirical AUROC values (0.70–0.85). Neither calibration-based metrics (ECE) nor AUROC as currently reported in the literature make this cost visible. Key Idea: The key insight is that calibration and discrimination are orthogonal properties: a model can assign perfectly calibrated confidence scores (correct 60% of the time when it says 0.6) while having zero discriminative power (assigning 0.6 to both correct and incorrect answers indiscriminately). Hallucination mitigation research has optimized for calibration while ignoring discrimination, masking a fundamental barrier to utility-preserving factuality. By redefining hallucination as confident error rather than any error, a third path emerges: expressing hedged answers that preserve information value while reducing harm from overconfidence. Feasibility rests on the observation that matching output language to internal confidence is a closed-loop problem over the model's own states—unlike verifying external ground truth, which requires solving a harder open-world discrimination problem. Methodology: This is a position/argumentative paper, not an experimental paper. The methodological contribution is analytical and definitional. The argument proceeds in four moves: (1) formalize the distinction between calibration and discrimination using the Utility-Error Trade-off curve; (2) ground the discrimination gap claim empirically by reviewing AUROC values from 30+ model×task combinations in the literature (clustering at 0.70–0.85) and showing via simulation (N=25,000 synthetic data points with Beta-distributed confidence scores, calibrated via Isotonic Regression to smECE ≈ 0.014) that AUROC = 0.71 requires 52% utility loss to reach 5% error; (3) visualize the empirical tradeoff for 12 frontier models (GPT-5, o3, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude Opus 4, etc.) on SimpleQA Verified; (4) formally define faithful uncertainty using the framework from Yona et al. (2024): intrinsic confidence conf_M(A) = 1 − fraction of repeated samples contradicting A; linguistic uncertainty via a decisiveness score dec(A;R,Q) = Pr[A is True | R, Q] judged by an LLM; faithfulness as mean absolute deviation between decisiveness and intrinsic confidence per assertion; and cMFG (conditional mean faithful generation) as the calibration-normalized aggregate. The paper then extends the argument to agentic systems, where faithful uncertainty is recast as the control layer governing when to invoke retrieval tools. Key Results: The paper does not present new primary experiments. Its empirical content consists of: (1) a simulation showing that at AUROC = 0.71 (consistent with the literature average), reducing error rate from 25% to 5% requires discarding 52% of valid answers; even at the AUROC = 0.85 ceiling the utility tax is ~28%; the tax only falls below 5% at AUROC ≥ 0.95, which no reported method reaches; (2) a reanalysis of Table 7 from SimpleQA Verified (Haas et al., 2025) showing 12 frontier models—including GPT-5, o3, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude Opus 4, Claude Sonnet 4—all either cluster near the diagonal (high utility, high error) or move left (low utility, low error) when prioritizing factuality, with the top-right "ideal" region unpopulated; (3) cited cMFG scores of 0.5–0.7 for current models (from Yona et al. 2024), indicating expressed uncertainty is weakly correlated with intrinsic confidence. The baselines and benchmarks cited are current (2024–2026 publications). Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The calibration-vs-discrimination distinction is not entirely new (AUROC has been used in hallucination literature), but the paper sharpens it into a precise argument about why calibration is insufficient for utility-preserving hallucination mitigation. The utility-error trade-off curve framing and its application to directly visualize the cost of discrimination-limited abstention is the genuine analytical contribution. The faithful uncertainty objective builds on Yona et al. (2024) and Ghafouri et al. (2024)—the paper cites and extends that prior work rather than introducing it from scratch. The agentic framing of metacognition as a control layer (storage vs. control problem) is a new synthesis. Rigor & Reproducibility: No new experiments are run; no ablations exist by nature of the paper type. The simulation supporting Figure 2 is described in Appendix A with enough detail to reproduce (Beta(1.8,1.0) and Beta(1.0,1.3) distributions, N=25,000, Isotonic Regression calibration). Figure 3 reanalyzes published data from Haas et al. (2025)—the underlying numbers are from an external benchmark. The central claim—that the discrimination gap is fundamental rather than a current-generation artifact—is stated as a conjecture, which is scientifically honest. The paper's AUROC review draws on 3 studies (Farquhar et al. 2024: avg 0.79 across 30 model×task; Savage et al. 2025: GPT-4 tops at 0.79 in medical QA; Kang et al. 2025: GPT-4o-mini 0.68–0.72 on biography generation). This literature sample is thin for the weight placed on the AUROC clustering claim; other domains or tasks could yield different numbers. No code is released (nothing to release for a position paper). This is an arXiv preprint (May 2026); not yet peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: THEORETICAL (the paper argues for a new objective and formalism; no system is built or deployed). - Sound: The core conjecture (discrimination gap is fundamental) is explicitly a conjecture, not a theorem; the formal definitions (faithfulness metric, cMFG) are internally consistent and borrowed from prior peer-reviewed work (EMNLP 2024). The simulation methodology is sound but relies on distributional assumptions about LLM confidence scores. - General: The argument focuses on extrinsic hallucinations in parametric LLMs on factoid QA with clear ground truth; the paper explicitly sets aside long-form generation, contested claims, intrinsic hallucinations, and reasoning errors—these scope limitations are stated but significant. - Tight: No formal bounds are proven; the AUROC threshold argument (AUROC ≥ 0.95 needed for <5% utility tax) follows mathematically from the simulation but is derived from distributional assumptions, not a model-independent lower bound. - Implementable: The faithful uncertainty objective has been operationalized in Yona et al. (2024) and follow-up work cited (Eikema et al. 2025, Ji et al. 2025, Liu et al. 2025). The paper stops at paper form but points to concrete implementation directions. The work delivers a paper-form argument and formal definitions; no software artifact, dataset, or trained model is released. Strengths: - The calibration-vs-discrimination distinction is articulated more precisely and with more quantitative force than prior presentations. The simulation making the utility tax concrete at specific AUROC values (52% at AUROC=0.71, 28% at AUROC=0.85) gives reviewers and practitioners a number to argue against. - The Figure 3 scatter plot reusing SimpleQA Verified data to show all frontier models occupying only the "tradeoff diagonal" or "abstention left" regions—with the ideal top-right corner empty—is empirically grounded and visually compelling. - The agentic extension (metacognition as control layer, storage problem vs. control problem) is a coherent and novel synthesis, not merely appended but architecturally motivated. - The open-problem taxonomy (bootstrapping paradox, signal preservation through post-training, confidence attribution, causal evaluation) is specific and actionable—each identifies a concrete unsolved methodological challenge. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly frame the central claim about the discrimination gap as a conjecture, not a theorem, and acknowledge the alternative view that better probes of latent truth representations may eventually close the gap (Section 7.3). They state the argument is scoped to extrinsic hallucinations in parametric models on factoid QA, excluding long-form generation and creative domains. They acknowledge that faithful uncertainty does not eliminate "honest mistakes" (confident errors) and that continued knowledge expansion remains necessary. These are direct engagements with limitations, which strengthens the paper's credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The primary unacknowledged gap is that the paper does not demonstrate, even with a preliminary experiment, that any method actually moves the utility-error frontier (rather than sliding along it) by targeting faithful uncertainty—the claim that it offers a path out of the tradeoff remains theoretical. The AUROC evidence base is thin: three cited studies, all on English QA tasks, covering a narrow band of model families and domains; the claim that AUROC "clusters at 0.70–0.85" for realistic factual QA may not generalize to structured or specialized domains. The dismissal of the "latent truth" counterargument (Section 7.3) is not fully resolved—the authors acknowledge it is a valuable pursuit but characterize it as requiring a strong assumption about universal truth representations, without engaging with the mechanistic interpretability evidence they themselves cite (Stolfo et al. 2024, Ravfogel et al. 2025) that partially supports that view. Finally, the paper does not address how faithful uncertainty interacts with multi-turn conversation, instruction following, or system prompt constraints that may force decisive tone regardless of intrinsic confidence. Bottom Line: Researchers working on hallucination mitigation, uncertainty quantification, or agentic system reliability will gain a sharper analytical vocabulary and a concrete set of open problems from this paper—but should treat the core "discrimination gap is fundamental" claim as a well-motivated conjecture pending direct empirical validation of the faithful uncertainty approach on held-out benchmarks.
Recommended 2605.01428 PDF
#93 — Innovative (60)

MiA-Signature: Approximating Global Activation for Long-Context Understanding (37 ▲ )

Yuqing Li, Jiangnan Li, Mo Yu, Zheng Lin, Weiping Wang, Jie Zhou · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MiA-Signature, a compact, query-conditioned representation of the global activation pattern over a structured memory space (the "mindscape"), used to condition both retrieval and generation in RAG pipelines and iterative agentic systems. - Why it's important: Standard RAG systems retrieve locally plausible chunks but fail when answers require synthesizing evidence dispersed across a large, overcomplete memory space (e.g., merged multi-book series), causing high false-confidence errors on the locally plausible wrong option. - How it does it: A greedy submodular algorithm selects a compact set of session-level GPT-4o summaries that jointly maximize query relevance, chunk coverage, and diversity, producing a signature that conditions a specially trained mindscape-aware retriever and optionally the generator. - The good: Conditioning retrieval on the MiA-Signature improves average R@10 by 10.9% and average task performance by 3.8% over the same retriever backbone without signature conditioning across four long-context benchmarks. - The missing: The custom mindscape-aware retriever (MiA-Emb-8B) was developed by the same group in prior work and was presumably trained to accept query+signature input, making it unclear whether the gains reflect the signature concept or a trained retriever advantage; no simple query-expansion baseline is compared. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building long-context RAG or agentic systems over narrative or multi-document corpora; less compelling for those in non-narrative domains, given the narrow evaluation scope and reliance on the authors' own retriever stack.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (60) is moderate — submodular query-conditioned global activation is a clean reframing, but the retriever architecture is inherited from prior work by the same group. Rigor (60) is hurt by no error bars, no seeds, and reliance on the authors' own MiA-Emb-8B without a general-retriever-plus-naive-signature baseline that would isolate the signature's contribution. Completeness (60) and Resilience (55) suffer from narrative-only evaluation and the missing ablation against simpler query-expansion alternatives. Impact (55) is limited — open retriever weights but no released library, custom non-released benchmark splits, GPT-4o dependency. Weighted ≈ 60.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Standard RAG systems assume relevant information can be captured by a small set of locally retrieved chunks, but this assumption breaks down when correct answers require binding together evidence dispersed across a large document collection — a character's identity revealed in chapter 2 that determines the interpretation of an event in chapter 18, for example. This problem is acute in multi-book series QA, long legal corpora, and any setting where memory is overcomplete and redundant. Existing improvements — iterative retrieval (IRCoT, FLARE, DeepRAG), structured indexing (RAPTOR, HippoRAG), and memory-augmented agents (ReadAgent, MemAgent) — either make retrieval more iterative or enrich the static index, but all still frame memory access as local evidence lookup. The cognitive science literature on Global Neuronal Workspace theory motivates a different framing: global activation over distributed memory followed by compression into a tractable representation that guides downstream computation. Key Idea The core insight is that a query activates a broad semantic region of a memory space, and that a compact, submodularly-selected set of session-level summaries — the MiA-Signature — can approximate this global activation well enough to re-steer retrieval away from locally plausible but globally incorrect evidence, without requiring the model to process the full activated context. The signature is not a document summary; it is query-induced and serves as a dynamic conditioning signal that evolves as the agent accumulates evidence. Methodology The pipeline has three stages. First, offline memory construction: each document is split into non-overlapping windows of W=20 chunks, each summarized once with GPT-4o into session summaries cached per document. Second, signature initialization at step 0: a query-only retriever (SFT-Emb-8B) retrieves the top-50 chunks, which are mapped to their session summaries. A greedy algorithm then selects up to K=5 summaries by maximizing a weighted marginal gain: (a) query relevance fQ — a modular sum of cosine similarities between the query and candidate summaries; (b) chunk coverage fC — a weighted MAX-COVERAGE term over candidate chunks, which is monotone submodular with a (1−1/e) ≈ 0.632 greedy approximation guarantee for the fQ+fC sub-objective; and (c) diversity fD — penalizes angular similarity to already-selected summaries (non-monotone, acts as a tie-breaker). Default weights are λQ=0.3, λC=0.4, λD=0.3. Third, downstream use: a mindscape-aware retriever (MiA-Emb-8B) scores chunks as s(c|q,σ) = (1−α)·s_qry(c|q) + α·s_sig(c|σ) with α=0.5, interpolating between query-only and signature-conditioned scores using separate embeddings of the query's first and second </s> tokens (causal masking isolates the two signals). In static RAG, σ₀ is fixed; in the agent, DeepSeek-V3.2 acts as update model Mupd to iteratively refine the signature σt, rewritten query qt, and evidence memory Et over at most three steps. Critical design decisions: (1) using a dedicated mindscape-aware retriever rather than appending the signature to a general retriever — prior work showed the latter blurs query focus; (2) using First-K (rather than coverage-aware) submodular initialization in the agent because online refinement compensates for a simpler initial state; (3) separating the global signature from the local evidence memory as distinct states encoding complementary information. Key Results Evaluation covers four long-context benchmarks in a harder series-book setting (multiple related books merged into a single retrieval index): DetectiveQA (multi-choice, EN/ZH), NarrativeQA (open-ended F1), NovelHopQA (multi-hop F1), and NoCha (claim verification, PairAcc). Baselines include Qwen3-Emb/Qwen-14B, Qwen3-Emb/DS-V3.2, and MiA-Emb/DS-V3.2 (without signature). In static RAG, MiA-Emb with signature conditioning (+sig) achieves DetectiveQA EN/ZH accuracy of 70.7/78.0 vs. 59.3/76.0 without signature, NarrativeQA F1 of 45.1 vs. 41.1, and NoCha PairAcc of 58.7 vs. 61.9 (a mixed result). Full MiA-RAG (DS-V3.2 generator also sees signature) achieves average Perf. of 56.0 vs. 47.8 for Qwen3-Emb/DS-V3.2. In the agent, MiA-Agent (Chunks+SIG.+EVI.) reaches NoCha PairAcc 71.4 vs. 57.1 for Agent w/o Sig., and NovelHopQA R@10=39.3 vs. 33.9. Counter-intuitive finding: exposing the signature to the generator helps on NoCha (where local factual continuity is critical) but hurts on NarrativeQA and NovelHopQA when retrieved chunks already contain a direct evidence path — additional memory state can distract the generator. The series-book setting is confirmed harder than single-book: R@10 drops 2–8 points when merging books (Table 5), validating the evaluation choice. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The MiA-Signature formalism — framing query-induced memory access as global activation followed by submodular approximation — is a genuine conceptual contribution distinct from prior RAG work. The dual-memory decomposition (global signature + local evidence memory) and the finding that these two states encode non-interchangeable information are empirically novel. The cognitive science framing is analogical rather than mechanistically derived; the retriever architecture and training builds directly on prior MiA-RAG work [22] by the same group, so the signature construction is the primary novel element rather than the full system. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations isolate the signature's contribution at retrieval vs. generation time (Table 2), compare Coverage-aware vs. First-K initialization (Table 3), and ablate query rewriting (Table 6) — these are meaningful and well-structured. However, no error bars, multiple seeds, or statistical significance tests are reported across any table. The two core retrievers (SFT-Emb-8B, MiA-Emb-8B) are published on HuggingFace (huggingface.co/MindscapeRAG/), supporting partial reproducibility. Session summaries require GPT-4o API access (proprietary, costly, non-deterministic), and no ablation tests summary quality sensitivity. The full pipeline code and the series-book benchmark splits are not stated to be released. The series-book evaluation construction is custom-made by the authors and not a standard benchmark, making comparison to the broader RAG literature indirect. This is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The method runs on commercially available hardware; the retrievers are open (8B parameters), but offline session summary construction requires GPT-4o API access, and the agent uses DeepSeek-V3.2. - Efficient: Submodular selection adds O(K·N·M) set operations after a single BGE-M3 embedding pass, described as negligible relative to the retrieval call. However, the pipeline runs two retrieval passes (E1 then E2) per query step, and the agent runs up to three such steps, multiplying latency vs. a single-pass query-only RAG; no wall-clock or token-count analysis is provided. - Effective: Consistent retrieval-side gains across four benchmarks, with task-performance gains most clear on DetectiveQA and NarrativeQA; weaker and inconsistent generation-side gains, task-dependent. - Scalable: Tested on series-book aggregations of up to ~50 books; no test beyond this scale and no timing analysis at larger collection sizes; scalability is claimed but not demonstrated. The paper delivers open retriever weights but no standalone library, CLI, or benchmark release. Strengths - The two-memory-state decomposition (global signature σ + local evidence memory E) is principled: Table 2 empirically demonstrates these states carry non-interchangeable information — the best NoCha configuration uses both, while the best NarrativeQA/NovelHopQA configurations use retrieved chunks alone — revealing a clear, actionable design rule. - The submodular objective is formally analyzed with proofs of monotonicity and a (1−1/e) approximation guarantee for the dominant fQ+fC sub-objective, providing theoretical grounding for the greedy algorithm. - The series-book evaluation stress-test (merging related books into one index) is a more realistic long-context challenge than standard single-book QA, and the sanity check in Appendix C.2 quantitatively confirms the added difficulty. - The finding that retrieval benefits from the signature more consistently than generation does is a nuanced empirical observation, actionable for practitioners deciding when to expose the signature to the generator. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) evaluation is restricted to literary/narrative domains — transfer to code repositories, scientific literature, or multimodal interaction is untested; (2) the signature is not optimized end-to-end with the retriever, generator, or task objective — it is training-free over precomputed summaries; (3) adaptive control over when to expose the signature to the generator is unresolved. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The mindscape-aware retriever MiA-Emb-8B was developed by the same group in prior work [22] and was trained to accept query+signature conditioning. Its advantage over Qwen3-Emb (a general-purpose retriever not trained for this interface) may partly reflect training setup rather than the MiA-Signature concept — no ablation uses a general retriever with the signature naively appended to the query. - No simple query-expansion baseline (e.g., prepend the top-1 session summary as context to the query before standard dense retrieval) is compared; it is unclear whether a naive approach achieves similar gains at lower implementation cost. - Session summary quality depends on GPT-4o; no ablation tests sensitivity to the summarizer (e.g., open alternatives at lower cost), making it hard to judge robustness or reproducibility at scale. - No computational cost analysis (latency, API tokens per query, preprocessing cost) is provided, preventing a cost–benefit assessment vs. simpler baselines. - The series-book benchmark splits are a custom construction by the authors and are not stated to be released, limiting external reproducibility and comparison. Bottom Line Practitioners building long-context RAG or agentic systems over narrative corpora will find MiA-Signature's retrieval-side gains convincing and the two-memory decomposition design insight useful, but the evaluation's reliance on the authors' own trained retrievers, custom benchmark construction, and restricted domain coverage means the method's generality remains unestablished.
Recommended 2605.06416 PDF
#94 — Moderately Innovative (59)

The Knowledge-Coherence Framework for Narrative Extraction: An Empirical Study on Scientific Literature

mdpi.com · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the Knowledge-Coherence Framework (KCF), which integrates OpenAlex concept metadata into embedding-based narrative path extraction for scientific literature, and empirically demonstrates that such knowledge augmentation does not improve—and slightly degrades—LLM-judged narrative quality. - Why it's important: Literature review assistants, technology-surveillance pipelines, and curriculum generators that sequence scientific documents need to know which design choices actually move the needle on perceived narrative quality, not just geometric coherence scores. - How it does it: KCF blends Narrative Trails bottleneck-coherence pathfinding with an OpenAlex knowledge-similarity layer via a single tunable weight α, then evaluates paths with 13 LLM judges across 600 paired endpoint comparisons using Wilcoxon tests and Cohen's d effect sizes. - The good: The dissociation finding—SPECTER paths score 0.94 on mathematical coherence yet only 5.36 on LLM quality, while MiniLM paths score 0.81 and 5.87—is a statistically rigorous result showing the two metrics are not interchangeable optimization targets, supported by large effect sizes (Cohen's d = 1.11–2.37). - The missing: All evaluation is confined to a single domain (IEEE visualization, 1990–2022); there is no human annotation; and only two older embedding models (MiniLM, SPECTER) are tested, leaving open whether more recent encoders (SPECTER2, BGE, E5) would preserve or flip the dissociation. - Read it or not: Worth reading for IR/NLP researchers building document-sequence or narrative systems who need a grounded caution against equating embedding-space coherence with reader-perceived narrative quality; of limited value to practitioners outside scientific literature retrieval.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Practical Impact (35) and Resilience (55) pull this down — no code release, single narrow corpus (IEEE visualization, curated by the author's own group), and only two older embedding models (MiniLM, SPECTER) tested, leaving the negative result vulnerable to the obvious objection that newer encoders (SPECTER2, BGE, E5) might flip it. Novelty (50) is moderate: the framework is a simple α-blend, but the controlled negative result on knowledge augmentation plus the mathematical-vs-perceived-quality dissociation is a non-obvious empirical contribution (Non-Obv 65). Rigor (68) and Completeness (70) are respectable — pre-registered α sweep, Wilcoxon + Bonferroni + Cohen's d, 13 cross-provider LLM judges, honest acknowledgment of no-human-eval and single-domain scope. Weighted: 0.20·50 + 0.25·68 + 0.15·65 + 0.15·70 + 0.15·55 + 0.10·35 ≈ 59.
empirical Novelty 50 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 70 Resilience 55 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Researchers entering a new subfield, analysts tracing method evolution across conferences, and curriculum designers need tools that extract coherent ordered sequences of documents from large scientific corpora. Current methods—Narrative Trails, Connect-the-Dots, Narrative Maps—rely solely on embedding similarity to score consecutive document transitions and ignore rich structured metadata such as the hierarchical concept taxonomies available in scholarly databases like OpenAlex. Prior IR work shows that knowledge graph augmentation improves document retrieval and recommendation, creating a natural hypothesis: injecting concept-level domain knowledge into narrative pathfinding should improve the conceptual continuity of the resulting sequences. This assumption has not been tested directly in the narrative extraction setting, where the unit of evaluation is a sequence rather than an individual document. Key Idea The core move is to use a single scalar blending weight α to interpolate between pure embedding-based bottleneck coherence (inherited from Narrative Trails) and an OpenAlex concept-similarity score, then test across multiple α values and two embedding models whether the knowledge signal actually improves LLM-judged narrative quality. The paper's contribution is the empirical test, not a new pathfinding algorithm. The surprise—that α > 0 does not help and measurably hurts—is the intellectual payload. Methodology KCF operates in four stages. (1) Base coherence: the geometric mean of angular cosine similarity and Jensen–Shannon-divergence-based topic similarity in UMAP+HDBSCAN cluster-membership space, computed for every document pair using MiniLM (384-dim) or SPECTER (768-dim). (2) Knowledge extraction: OpenAlex concept metadata is filtered through a sequential three-stage pipeline—retain specificity level ≥ 2, drop concepts in > 50% of papers, and cap high-confidence level-2 concepts at score < 0.95—leaving domain-discriminative terms; 99.4% of the 3549-paper VisPubData corpus has OpenAlex coverage. (3) Knowledge similarity θ_knowledge: sum of exact concept-label overlap (weighted by confidence-score products, w=1.0), partial token overlap (≥6-char tokens, ratio ≥ 0.5, w=0.5), and semantic cosine similarity of concept-name MiniLM embeddings (threshold 0.70, w=0.3), renormalized to [0,1]. (4) Combined scoring θ_combined = (1−α)·θ_base + α·θ_knowledge, with max-min (bottleneck) pathfinding via modified Dijkstra on a sparse MST-threshold-filtered graph. Critical design decisions: the bottleneck objective is inherited from prior work and well-justified (weakest link determines narrative quality); filtering thresholds are set from concept-frequency distributions before any LLM evaluation, protecting against Goodhart's Law; α is fixed at 0.5 for the main comparison and swept over {0.0, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0} for sensitivity. Main evaluation: 100 endpoint pairs × 6 configurations (2 embeddings × 3 path types), Friedman test + pairwise Wilcoxon with Bonferroni correction, Cohen's d reported; LLM quality scores averaged over 3 random seeds per narrative. Key Results Main six-method comparison (structured LLM scoring, 100 endpoint pairs): SPECTER achieves 0.94 ± 0.02 average mathematical coherence vs. MiniLM's 0.81 ± 0.04 (+16%), but MiniLM receives 5.87 ± 0.82 LLM quality score vs. SPECTER's 5.36 ± 0.92. Knowledge augmentation degrades LLM scores for both models: MiniLM drops from 5.87 to 5.25 (−10.6%, Cohen's d = 1.11, p < 0.0001) and SPECTER from 5.36 to 4.66 (−13.1%, Cohen's d = 2.37, p < 0.0001). Random baselines score ~3.65 regardless of embedding, confirming structured methods meaningfully outperform random ordering. Alpha sensitivity analysis (500 MiniLM pairs, direct scoring): mathematical coherence declines monotonically from 0.815 (α=0.0) to 0.685 (α=1.0); LLM scores remain flat at 7.81–7.91 for α ∈ [0.0, 0.7] and dip to 7.57 only at α=1.0. Path length shrinks from mean 18.5 to 6.3 documents as α increases from 0 to 1.0—knowledge augmentation creates shorter shortcuts, not richer paths. Cross-model evaluation: median Pearson r = 0.71 across 78 pairwise comparisons among 13 judges from OpenAI and Anthropic (range: r = 0.28 for GPT-5-nano vs. Claude Opus 4.5, to r = 0.98 for Claude Opus 4.1 vs. Claude Opus 4). Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. KCF itself is a straightforward α-weighted combination of Narrative Trails pathfinding and OpenAlex metadata—not an algorithmic novelty. What is genuinely new is the first controlled empirical test of OpenAlex-style concept augmentation for narrative extraction, yielding a negative result, and the demonstration that mathematical coherence and LLM-judged narrative quality are dissociated optimization targets in this setting. The multi-model LLM evaluation protocol is applied, not invented. Rigor & Reproducibility The α sweep spanning {0.0, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0}, including both α=0 (pure embedding) and α=1 (pure knowledge), functions as a thorough ablation. All six configurations are compared within-subjects on the same 100 endpoint pairs, ruling out endpoint-sampling confounds. Wilcoxon tests with Bonferroni correction and Cohen's d are reported with large effect sizes, and LLM evaluation noise is mitigated by averaging three random-seed calls per narrative. The VisPubData corpus is public and the raw CSV is included as supplementary material; however, no code repository is mentioned or linked, which limits exact reproducibility. Evaluation relies entirely on LLM judges with no human annotation—a meaningful weakness acknowledged by the authors. Filtering thresholds are set from concept distributions before any LLM metric is computed, providing mild protection against benchmark overfitting. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: All components (embedding models, HDBSCAN clustering, Dijkstra pathfinding) run on commodity hardware; the paper reports that a single path extraction on the 3549-paper corpus completes in under a second, with the 600-pair experiment dominated by LLM API latency rather than graph computation. - Efficient: The O(|D|²) coherence matrix is a one-time offline cost; online pathfinding is O(|E| + |D| log |D|) per endpoint pair on the sparse MST-filtered graph; the LLM evaluation cost (13 judges × 3 seeds × 600 pairs) is the practical bottleneck, not the framework itself. - Effective: KCF at α=0 matches Narrative Trails for LLM-judged quality on this corpus; the knowledge layer does not add value, so the added OpenAlex integration complexity is not justified by outcomes. The framework delivers a working pathfinding system, but its distinguishing feature (knowledge augmentation) is the part that does not work. - Scalable: Tested only at 3,549 documents; the O(|D|²) coherence matrix precomputation would become prohibitive at hundreds of thousands of papers without approximation. No empirical test beyond the single corpus is provided, and the claim of scalability via MST sparsification is not stress-tested. The paper delivers a dataset (supplementary CSV) and a framework description but no released code or software artifact. Strengths - The negative result is explicitly hypothesized in advance, rigorously tested with proper non-parametric statistics and effect sizes, and honestly reported—uncommon discipline in a field where positive-result bias dominates publication. - The α sweep (5 levels, 500 pairs) is a clean ablation of the knowledge weight with a striking, consistent finding: LLM quality is essentially invariant to α up to 0.7, while mathematical coherence degrades monotonically—making the conclusion robust to parameter choice. - The dissociation between SPECTER's mathematical advantage and MiniLM's LLM-quality advantage is a concrete, counter-intuitive empirical finding with a mechanistically coherent explanation (citation-clustering produces tight but redundant paths). - Using 13 judges from two independently trained provider families (OpenAI and Anthropic) materially reduces the risk that the LLM-quality finding is an artifact of a single model's scoring idiosyncrasies. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) evaluation is restricted to a single scientific domain (IEEE visualization), limiting generalizability; (2) no human evaluation is included—LLM judges are used throughout; (3) narratives are presented to judges as titles and abstracts only, not full text; (4) the knowledge source is limited to auto-generated OpenAlex concepts; and (5) only MiniLM and SPECTER are compared, with newer encoders excluded by design to maintain comparability with prior narrative extraction literature. The authors confront these limitations directly and flag a targeted human pilot study as the natural next step—a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The entire study uses one corpus from a single narrow domain (IEEE visualization), curated and previously used by the first author's research group. It is unknown whether the negative finding holds in domains where structured knowledge resources have higher semantic precision (e.g., MeSH for biomedical literature or legal ontologies), which could flip the result. - No code or implementation is released, so reproducing the results requires reconstructing the pipeline in full from the paper description. - The two LLM scoring protocols (direct vs. structured) produce different absolute scales and are never cross-validated against each other; it is unclear whether method rankings are protocol-sensitive, since only one protocol is applied per experiment. - The paper proposes salience-weighted concept representations as the key fix for future work, but does not include even a lightweight pilot (e.g., TF-IDF-weighted or abstract-position-weighted concepts) that would test whether the granularity mismatch hypothesis is actually the cause. - Evaluation independence is mildly compromised by the fact that VisPubData was introduced and is maintained in the same research community as Narrative Trails and KCF; while the dataset is public, results on a held-out domain by independent authors would be more convincing. Bottom Line IR and NLP practitioners building document-sequence systems for scientific literature should read the dissociation finding—that SPECTER's strong mathematical coherence advantage does not translate to better LLM-judged narrative quality—as it directly and credibly challenges the default assumption that better embedding-space geometry produces better user-facing outputs; the single-domain evaluation and absent code release limit how far the conclusion should be generalized.
Search Results
#95 — Moderately Innovative (59)

Developing a Fundamental Theoretical Definition for Athletic Injury: Metaphysics, Logic, and Mathematics: JT Kalkhoven

link.springer.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proposes a new theoretical definition of athletic injury grounded in Aristotelian essentialism and Carnapian explication, formalizing injury as the condition D > Dc (tissue damage exceeding a critical threshold), and derives a unified mathematical ontological framework for severity, recovery, and rate of recovery. - Why it's important: Existing definitions — including the IOC's widely adopted standard — conflate essential properties of injury with contingent correlates (pain, time-loss, functional complaints), producing constructs that are vacuous, unfalsifiable, and unsuitable for predictive mathematical modeling. - How it does it: Uses classical thought experiments and boundary tests (modus tollens, reductio ad absurdum, refutation by counterexample) to strip accidental properties (symbebekós) from the concept's essential nature (to ti ēn einai), then maps the residual construct to continuum damage mechanics variables. - The good: The logical argument for excluding pain and time-loss as necessary or sufficient conditions is rigorous and driven by compelling counterexamples (an anaesthetized athlete with a fracture still has an injury; Tiger Woods competed with a torn ACL), making the conceptual disentanglement both precise and defensible. - The missing: The critical damage threshold Dc — the sole criterion separating injury from non-injury in the formalization — is entirely unspecified for any tissue type, leaving the operationalization problem largely unsolved and the framework empirically untestable as presented. - Read it or not: Worth reading for sports medicine researchers and biomechanical modelers seeking a rigorous conceptual foundation before building formal injury prediction models; practitioners focused on clinical or epidemiological work will find limited immediate utility.
Why this rank: Scored using Theoretical profile. Novelty (65) reflects a genuine new formulation applying Carnapian explication and CDM to sports medicine. Rigor (60) is moderate — the syllogistic logic is sound but the sufficiency condition ("not part of normal adaptation") reintroduces vagueness, and Dc remains a free parameter with no estimation procedure, making the formalization vacuous as presented. Non-Obviousness (60) and Resilience (55) are pulled down by the unformalized sufficiency clause. Impact (30) is low because nothing can be predicted, classified, or falsified without tissue-specific Dc operationalization. Weighted composite: 0.25*65 + 0.20*60 + 0.25*60 + 0.10*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.05*30 ≈ 59.
theoretical Novelty 65 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 30
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: In sports science and medicine, no consensus theoretical definition of athletic injury achieves the logical coherence required for scientific formalization. The IOC's leading definition lacks sufficient conditions (any tissue damage qualifies, which would mean every athlete is injured shortly after training), while widely used operational definitions import concepts — pain, swelling, athlete availability, time-loss — that are neither necessary nor sufficient for injury to exist. This definitional incoherence undermines testability, falsifiability, predictability, and reproducibility of injury research. Without a logically coherent theoretical anchor, operational definitions deviate arbitrarily from the construct they intend to measure, predictive models have no principled target to estimate, and conflation with subjective states introduces irreducible bias. The problem is not primarily about semantics in practice but about scientific rigor: a concept that permits no falsifiable boundary collapses into tautology. Key Idea: The core insight is that applying Carnapian explication — the systematic transformation of an imprecise pre-scientific concept into a precise, empirically meaningful one — together with Aristotelian necessity/sufficiency analysis, allows athletic injury to be reduced to its essential nature: tissue damage exceeding a critical mechanical and physiological tolerance threshold during sports participation from mechanical energy transfer. This maps directly onto the continuum damage mechanics (CDM) variable D ∈ [0, 1], yielding the mathematical condition D > Dc. The trick is that once accidental properties are formally excluded using deductive counterexamples, the remaining essential structure is already expressed in engineering damage mechanics literature, enabling immediate mathematical formalization. Methodology: The paper proceeds in three phases. Phase 1 performs metaphysical analysis: thought experiments and boundary tests are used to evaluate whether each candidate property (pain, swelling, functional impairment, athlete availability, time-loss, tissue damage) meets necessity and/or sufficiency criteria. The method is Aristotelian syllogism with refutation by counterexample and modus tollens — for example, the existence of a fractured athlete with anaesthetic-suppressed pain falsifies the claim that pain is a necessary condition. Phase 2 constructs the new definition (Definition 6): tissue damage exceeding mechanical and physiological tolerance during sport from singular, sustained, or repetitive mechanical energy transfer, where the damage is not part of normal positive adaptation. The threshold condition is the critical design decision, introduced to convert tissue damage (necessary but insufficient) into a sufficient condition. Phase 3 formalizes the construct mathematically via CDM: injury ≡ D > Dc; severity Sev = (D − Dc)/(1 − Dc) (normalized [0,1]); recovery R = (Dt1 − Dt2)/Dt1 (normalized); recovery from injury Rinjury = (Dt1 − Dt2)/(Dt1 − Dc); with corresponding rates defined as time derivatives. No experiments are run. The paper is pure theoretical/conceptual work. Key Results: The paper produces no quantitative empirical outcomes. The principal results are definitional and formal: Definition 6 (the verbal definition), Definition 7 (D > Dc), and Equations 1–8 defining severity, recovery, rate of recovery, recovery from injury, and rate of recovery from injury as D-derived quantities. The mathematical framework is internally consistent and coherent. The key conceptual results are the logical refutations: pain is shown neither necessary nor sufficient; time-loss is shown neither necessary nor sufficient; tissue damage is shown necessary but insufficient; adding the tolerance-threshold condition provides sufficiency. The discussion of Tiger Woods completing the 2008 US Open with a torn ACL and a double stress fracture is the cleanest empirical anchor for the time-loss refutation. No baselines are compared; no datasets are tested. This is a theoretical position paper, peer-reviewed and published in Sports Medicine. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION + NEW METHOD (conceptual). The application of Carnapian explication and Aristotelian esssentialism to the definition of athletic injury is genuinely novel within sports medicine. The synthesis of that analysis with continuum damage mechanics to produce a unified mathematical ontological framework (injury, severity, recovery, rate of recovery as D-derived quantities) is a real contribution. The underlying tools — classical logic, CDM, thought experiments — are all established; what is novel is their disciplined integration and application to resolve a long-standing definitional problem in sports science. Rigor & Reproducibility: Profile: THEORETICAL. Sound: the logical structure is valid and the premises are well-grounded; core claims rest on classical counterexample logic (refutation by single case) which is the appropriate tool. The philosophical references (Aristotle, Carnap, Popper, Kripke) are correctly applied. The primary weakness in soundness is the sufficiency condition — "not part of normal physical training and positive adaptation process" — which introduces a contextual judgment that is not itself formally defined and may reintroduce vagueness at the boundary. General: the framework is explicitly scoped to mechanical athletic injuries; it excludes radiant, chemical, electrical injuries and psychological injuries by ontological commitment rather than logical necessity, which weakens universality claims. The restriction to mechanical energy is pragmatically justified but less rigorously argued than the exclusions of pain and time-loss. Tight: Dc is a free tissue-specific parameter with no specified estimation procedure, bounds, or measurement protocol; the formalization is therefore vacuous without tissue-specific operationalization, and no lower bounds on how difficult calibration is are provided. Implementable: the CDM framework is directly implementable in biomechanical models (finite element, analytical fatigue models); Edwards (2018) already operationalized D > Dc for overuse injury, and Firminger & Edwards (2021) applied related methods to tendon. The present paper extends this to a general definitional framework. No code, dataset, or software is released; the paper acknowledges a preprint at SportRxiv.org (doi:10.51224/SRXIV.474). Published peer-reviewed in Sports Medicine (Springer). Strengths: - The logical scaffolding is tighter than typical conceptual papers in sports medicine: each exclusion is backed by a formally stated syllogism with named logical principles (modus tollens, reductio ad absurdum), not merely rhetorical argument. - Genuine conceptual advance: disentangling pain, symptoms, and time-loss from injury's essential nature is important for the field regardless of whether the mathematical formalization is adopted, because it clarifies what operational definitions actually measure versus what they claim to measure. - The mathematical ontological framework (equations 1–8) provides a unified, internally consistent semantic network for injury, severity, recovery, and rate of recovery — terms used inconsistently across the literature — anchored to a single latent variable D. - The paper is exceptionally well-scaffolded for a conceptual work: Tables 1–6 define every term, provide historical context, and summarize contributions, making the argument independently verifiable. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge: (1) Dc thresholds are tissue-specific and require empirical triangulation (stiffness degradation, observable damage manifestations, recovery trajectories); (2) D is a latent variable not directly observable, requiring inference from mechanical property proxies; (3) the condition D > Dc is scale-dependent — at microscopic scales it identifies local fibril failure, while at whole-structure scale it marks functional transition; (4) CDM necessarily simplifies heterogeneous biological tissue behaviors (viscoelastic, plastic, brittle failure modes across structural layers); (5) full practical operationalization is deferred to future works. These limitations are confronted directly and honestly, which is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The sufficiency condition — damage "not a normal part of the physical training and positive adaptation process" — imports a teleological judgment (what is "normal" adaptation?) that is athlete-specific, context-specific, and training-history-dependent, and is not itself formalized. This risks reintroducing definitional vagueness at exactly the boundary the paper claims to have resolved. The argument for restricting athletic injury to mechanical energy is weaker than the arguments for excluding pain: it is presented as a pragmatic ontological choice (mechanical injuries are the primary concern of sports medicine) rather than a logical necessity, yet the exclusion is embedded in the proposed definition, not demoted to a scope qualifier. The mathematical framework for severity and recovery (equations 1–8) is largely a presentational reformulation of Edwards (2018) into normalized form; the theoretical advance over that prior work is not explicitly quantified. No engagement with measurement validity frameworks (convergent/discriminant validity, interrater reliability for Dc estimation) that would be needed before any empirical operationalization. The paper's sole authorship and absence of empirical case studies means the adequacy of Dc for distinguishing "injury" from "normal adaptation" in ambiguous cases (e.g., stress reactions that become stress fractures) remains untested. Bottom Line: Sports medicine researchers and biomechanical modelers will gain a rigorous, citable conceptual foundation from this paper, but the most consequential open problem — how to determine Dc for any specific tissue in any specific athlete — is deferred to future work, meaning the framework cannot yet be used to predict, classify, or falsify a single injury claim.
Search Results
#96 — Moderately Innovative (58)

DISC: Dynamic Decomposition Improves LLM Inference Scaling

proceedings.neurips.cc · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DISC (Dynamic decomposition Improves Scaling Compute), a recursive inference algorithm that adaptively partitions LLM solution traces into variable-size steps at runtime, concentrating sampling compute on steps the model finds difficult. - Why it's important: Static decomposition strategies — single-step, token-level, or sentence-level — uniformly distribute compute across easy and hard steps, wasting budget on trivial regions and undersampling the decision forks that actually determine solution quality. - How it does it: At each iteration, DISC proposes a candidate prefix (first α fraction of the best rollout suffix), computes z-scores of reward rollout distributions at both the base and candidate prefix, and accepts the candidate if its z-score is lower (higher tail probability of improvement), contracting the step size geometrically on rejection. - The good: On LiveCodeBench DISC reduces pass@10 error by 10.5% over the best static baseline, achieves over 85% relative improvement on DeepSeek-R1 with 10 samples, and adds negligible runtime overhead (~9.1% auxiliary vs. ~9.4–9.8% for baselines). - The missing: All main experiments compare only against weak static baselines (BoN, LineSplit, TokenSplit); no comparison against PRM-guided MCTS, which is the current competitive standard for inference-time compute scaling. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on inference-time search and compute allocation — the z-score difficulty estimator is a clean, model-agnostic idea — but practitioners should note the modest absolute gains and absent strong baselines before adopting.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Composite pulled down by Rigor (55) — no error bars on small convenience-selected subsets, and weak baselines (BoN/LineSplit/TokenSplit) with no PRM-guided MCTS comparison undermine the claimed frontier position. Completeness (50) suffers from the missing strong baselines and the misleading '4x over base' framing. Novelty (65) and Non-Obviousness (65) are reasonable — the z-score difficulty proxy is a clean training-free idea — but absolute gains are modest (1–2.5pp on small subsets). Impact (55) is moderate with code only promised. Composite ≈ 0.20*65 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*55 = 58.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 50 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Inference scaling methods improve LLM output quality by decomposing solutions into steps, sampling multiple continuations at each step, and selecting the best. The critical design choice — step granularity — is currently made statically: token-level decomposition is fine-grained but slow, sentence/newline-level is faster but may miss important decision points, and single-step (Best-of-N) treats the entire generation as one action. All three impose a fixed decomposition policy independent of the problem or the model's actual difficulty profile. A deeper issue is that LLMs may treat semantically insignificant tokens (e.g., "therefore", "which") as high-consequence decision forks due to their autoregressive training objective — humans cannot identify these forks a priori. The result is systematic misallocation: easy regions are oversampled (wasting budget) and hard regions are undersampled (missing improvements). Key Idea The core trick is using the z-score of the reward distribution of rollouts from a given prefix as a difficulty proxy. Specifically, z = (max_reward − mean_reward) / std_reward measures how surprising the best observed rollout is relative to the distribution — a low z-score means the prefix has high tail probability, i.e., many rollouts are already near the best, so committing to this prefix is likely fruitful. A high z-score means the best rollout is an outlier and future improvement is uncertain. By comparing z-scores at a base prefix and a candidate prefix (formed by appending the first α fraction of the current best suffix), the algorithm decides whether to advance (accept the candidate, extend further) or contract (reject, try a shorter step). This requires no process reward model, no domain annotation, and no prompt engineering — only a scalar outcome reward. Methodology The greedy-DISC algorithm (Algorithm 1) maintains a base prefix pb, an initial partition fraction α0, and a sample budget N. At each outer iteration: (1) sample rollouts from pb until cumulative reward exceeds threshold σ; (2) compute z-score zb and identify best suffix s; (3) form candidate prefix pc = pb · split(s, α); (4) sample rollouts from pc, compute zc; (5) if zc < zb, accept — set pb ← pc, reset α = α0; else reject — contract α ← α·α0. The geometric contraction implements recursive binary-search-like refinement of the hardest step. The algorithm terminates on budget exhaustion or correct solution. Three critical design choices: the z-score criterion (ablated against mean-based, random, and inverted alternatives); partition fraction α0 = 0.15–0.25 (conservative proposals reduce commitment errors); and low temperature τ = 0.2 (lower variance improves z-score estimate reliability, opposite of BoN's optimum at 0.6–0.8). For plug-and-play with beam search and MCTS, the ExpandNode operator generalizes the greedy variant by computing candidate prefixes from each node's local suffix. DISC is evaluated using ground-truth test cases (APPS, LiveCodeBench) and a pretrained ORM from RLHFlow with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (MATH500). A Wiener process motivating example and formal optimality proof (Theorem 2 in Appendix G) establish that under location-scale reward distributions, accurate empirical moments, and slowly-contracting variance, the algorithm terminates at a correct solution with probability 1. Key Results All main experiments use gpt-4o-mini. On APPS competition-level (200 problems), pass@10 error falls from 0.50 (best baseline) to 0.475 — a 5.0% reduction. On MATH500 (500 problems), from 0.15 to 0.14 — 6.7% reduction. On LiveCodeBench (108 problems, Oct–Dec 2024 to avoid contamination), from 0.57 to 0.51 — 10.5% reduction. Token efficiency curves show DISC reaches a given accuracy level with consistently fewer tokens across all three benchmarks. Open-source model results on APPS: LLaMA-3.1-8B pass@10 improves from 0.01 to 0.04 (300% relative); Mistral-7B from 0.0 to 0.02; Qwen-2.5-7B from 0.095 to 0.17 (79% relative). With DeepSeek-R1, DISC achieves over 85% relative improvement vs. base R1 pass@1 using 10 samples, and 33% relative improvement under a token budget matched to a single R1 sample. Self-generated validation tests (no ground truth) yield 54% relative improvement over the base model on APPS. MCTS > greedy > beam search in both pass@k and pass@token under the same DISC decomposition. The baselines (BoN, LineSplit, TokenSplit) are the standard static alternatives — no PRM-guided methods or trained verifiers are compared. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The use of z-score comparison of outcome reward rollout distributions at consecutive prefixes to drive adaptive step sizing during inference is genuinely new. The broader idea of adaptive inference compute allocation exists (e.g., Snell et al. 2024, Manvi et al. 2024), but DISC's mechanism — requiring no PRM, no domain knowledge, and no prompt engineering, discoverable automatically from rollout statistics — is a distinct contribution. The plug-and-play integration with MCTS and beam search is straightforward given the decomposition abstraction. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough: temperature (6 levels), acceptance criterion (5 variants: z-score, mean, negative z, negative mean, random), partition fraction α0 (5 values), and model family (5 models). The z-score ablation clearly dominates all alternatives. No error bars or confidence intervals are reported on main benchmark curves — the paper cites "standard field practice" in its checklist, but single-run curves on 108–500 problem subsets leave result variance unquantified. Evaluation subsets are chosen by convenience (first N problems, specific date range) with no demonstrated representativeness. The theoretical proof (Appendix G) rests on Assumption 5 (accurate estimates of µ and σ from finite M samples), which can fail badly when M is small and reward variance is high. Code was not released at submission; promised upon publication at disc-search.github.io. The Gaussian reward distribution assumption (Appendix C.5) is validated on one representative MATH500 problem — generalization across task types is asserted but not demonstrated. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Runs via standard API calls to any autoregressive LLM; no GPU required for GPT-4o-mini experiments; open-source experiments use a single A100 80GB. - Efficient: Auxiliary overhead is ~9.1% of runtime (vs. 9.4–9.8% for baselines); effective token cost per correct solution is lower than baselines due to better pass@token scaling. - Effective: Outperforms all three static baselines on all three benchmarks under both pass@k and pass@token metrics, with gains consistent across multiple model families including reasoning models. - Scalable: Tested on models from 7B parameters to gpt-4o-mini and DeepSeek-R1; no experiments beyond k=30 samples or beyond the tested benchmark scales; behavior at very high budgets is not characterized. No software artifact is released; code is promised but not yet available. Strengths - The z-score difficulty estimator requires only scalar outcome rewards — no PRM training, no domain annotations, no prompt engineering. This is a significant reduction in deployment complexity compared to competing approaches. - The empirical observation that LLMs assign critical importance to semantically trivial tokens ("therefore", "which") as autoregressive decision forks, and that DISC discovers these automatically, is a concrete and interesting insight into LLM reasoning structure. - The plug-and-play operator design cleanly separates decomposition from search, enabling the same algorithm to operate with greedy, beam, or MCTS without modification. - The temperature inversion finding — DISC improves at lower temperatures while BoN degrades — is a counter-intuitive and reusable insight: lower variance in rollouts makes z-score estimation more reliable, shifting the optimal operating point. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note: (1) DISC requires access to a scalar outcome reward model, which is unavailable in many domains and must be approximated by self-generated tests or LLM critics; (2) self-generated tests can be incomplete, inconsistent, or narrow, potentially biasing performance estimates; (3) the current formulation is limited to single-turn generation and does not handle multi-turn or interactive settings; (4) gains diminish on trivially solvable or non-compositional tasks where early reasoning does not constrain outcomes. The theoretical guarantees rely on an instance-dependent policy assumption acknowledged to require empirical validation. The authors engage directly with these limitations, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The primary baselines (BoN, LineSplit, TokenSplit) are weak: no comparison against PRM-guided MCTS (the current competitive frontier for inference scaling) or self-consistency methods, making it impossible to gauge where DISC sits in the broader landscape. - Temperature was ablated for DISC but not for baselines at their own optimal settings; the comparison in main results uses τ=0.2 (DISC's optimum) which may disadvantage baselines that prefer τ=0.6–0.8. - Absolute gains are modest: on APPS the pass@10 improvement is 2.5 percentage points (0.50 → 0.475); on MATH500 it is 1 percentage point (0.15 → 0.14). On MATH500, all decomposition methods (DISC, LineSplit, TokenSplit) achieve nearly identical pass@k, with DISC's advantage visible only in token efficiency curves. - The "up to 4x increase over the base model" claim conflates the effect of using k=10 samples (any method) with the DISC decomposition benefit, misleadingly framing the search budget effect as a DISC contribution. - Evaluation subsets are small (108 LiveCodeBench problems, 200 APPS problems) and selected by convenience rather than stratified sampling; results could be sensitive to subset selection. Bottom Line DISC provides a clean, training-free mechanism for adaptive inference compute allocation that consistently outperforms static decomposition baselines with negligible overhead, but the absence of PRM-guided MCTS comparisons and the modest absolute gains mean practitioners cannot yet determine whether DISC is competitive with the strongest inference-time methods.
Search Results
#97 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Taming Request Imbalance: SLO-Aware Scheduling for Disaggregated LLM Inference

arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Kairos, an SLO-aware scheduling system for disaggregated LLM serving that combines urgency-based prefill scheduling and slack-guided adaptive decode batching to jointly improve TTFT and TPOT SLO attainment. - Why it's important: Production LLM services suffer severe SLO violations under long-tail request distributions — head-of-line blocking on prefill and straggler-induced synchronization waste on decode — degrading user experience and underutilizing expensive GPU capacity. - How it does it: Kairos predicts prefill completion times to compute a per-request urgency score (SLO slack normalized by input length) that reorders chunked prefill without starvation, and exploits the gap between per-step decode time and the TPOT SLO budget to selectively pack short requests into slack windows during active decode. - The good: At QPS=3.0 on a production trace with MiniMax-M2.5 (229B MoE) on 8×H200 SXM GPUs, Kairos achieves 89.6% combined SLO attainment versus 55.8% for DistServe, a 33.8% improvement. - The missing: No ablation isolates the contribution of the prefill and decode schedulers independently, only one baseline (DistServe) is compared, and the evaluation covers a single model, a single SLO configuration (TTFT=8s, TPOT=50ms), and a single 1000-request production trace on one server. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems practitioners working on disaggregated LLM serving who need SLO-aware scheduling without output-length prediction; those seeking broader experimental validation should wait for a more complete evaluation.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Non-Obviousness (75) is the bright spot — reframing TPOT as a length-agnostic delivery deadline that exposes a slack window for selective batching is a genuine insight, and the urgency-normalized-by-length formula avoids SJF starvation without output-length prediction. Novelty (65) is solid for the joint prefill+decode SLO scheduler. Rigor (40) is the dominant drag: no ablation separating the two mechanisms, single baseline (DistServe), single model, single SLO setting, single 1000-request proprietary trace, no error bars, no code release, preprint. Completeness (45) and Resilience (45) are weak — the TPOT=50ms slack assumption is exactly the regime where the decode scheduler works; tighter SLOs would collapse the contribution but are untested, and FastServe/SageSched/PARS are cited but excluded. Practical Impact (55) — production-scale model and real trace help, but no artifact released. Weighted: 0.15·65+0.15·40+0.10·75+0.15·45+0.15·45+0.30·55 = 53. Bumped to 58 reflecting the strong real-model deployment context.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 45 Resilience 45 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Production LLM serving on prefill-decode disaggregated architectures faces two structurally distinct inefficiencies caused by long-tail request length distributions. On the prefill side, FCFS scheduling causes head-of-line blocking: a single 128K-token request takes ~8.8s to prefill on MiniMax-M2.5, blocking queued 8K requests (400ms prefill time each) and violating their TTFT SLO even at low request rates. On the decode side, continuous batching paces the entire batch at the speed of its longest request — a 128K-token request incurs 40.3ms per decode step versus 11.0ms for an 8K request, forcing short requests to idle for 29.3ms per step while waiting at the synchronization barrier. Prior systems (DistServe, vLLM, SGLang) either adopt FCFS for prefill or continuous batching for decode, neither of which adapts to this imbalance. Shortest-job-first is impractical because it indefinitely starves long requests. The problem affects any operator running an interactive LLM service with diverse prompt lengths. Key Idea Two structural properties of disaggregated LLM serving can be jointly exploited: (1) prefill completion time is predictable from input length and online-profiled throughput, enabling SLO-deadline-aware reordering of chunked prefill without indefinite starvation by normalizing urgency by request length; and (2) the TPOT SLO is a per-token delivery deadline that is length-agnostic and typically much larger than the per-step decode time, creating a slack window that can be filled by executing short requests without delaying longer requests past their own deadlines. Methodology Kairos adds two scheduling algorithms atop a standard 4P+4D disaggregated architecture using chunked prefill on the prefill side and a modified decode scheduler. Urgency-Based Prefill Scheduling (Algorithm 1): Before each prefill step, Kairos simulates FCFS execution order to estimate each queued request's finish time using a running estimate of profiled prefill throughput (tokens/sec, updated online). It computes a normalized urgency score: `urgency = (SLO_TTFT − (estimated_finish_time − arrival_time)) / (SLO_TTFT × input_length)`. Requests are sorted by urgency (lowest = most urgent) and greedily packed into the fixed chunk budget. The length normalization ensures short requests at similar deadline risk rank above long ones, preventing starvation of long requests while protecting short ones from head-of-line blocking. The finish-time estimator re-runs at every step to account for prefix cache hit variability. Slack-Guided Adaptive Decode Batching (Algorithm 3): A lookup table (LUT) maps (batch_size, sequence_length) → mean per-step decode time, profiled offline over 100 runs and updated online with running means after each actual decode step for robustness to hardware variability. Before each decode step, Kairos computes per-request slack = `(N+1) × TPOT_SLO − elapsed_time − LUT[1, seq_len]`, where N is tokens generated so far. The minimum slack across all active requests sets the budget. Requests are sorted by sequence length; those whose projected per-step time (including their addition to the batch) fits within minimum slack and improves throughput (tokens/step) are selected; others are delayed. If no requests qualify for selective batching, all are decoded together. Three critical design decisions: (1) normalizing urgency by input length — justified to prevent pure SJF starvation; (2) keying on minimum slack across all requests rather than per-request slack — conservative, ensures no individual request misses its TPOT deadline; (3) requiring a throughput gain condition before adding a request to the decode batch — prevents marginal additions that increase batch size without improving tokens-per-second. Key Results All experiments run on a single server: 8×NVIDIA H200 SXM, 192-core Intel Xeon Platinum 8558, 2 TB RAM, NVLink 900 GB/s. Model: MiniMax-M2.5 (229B MoE, FP8 precision, TP4 for each of 4 prefill + 4 decode GPUs). Dataset: proprietary production serving trace, 1000 requests with long-tail length distribution. SLO: TTFT=8s, TPOT=50ms. Sole baseline: DistServe (FCFS prefill + continuous batching). At QPS=3.0 (inflection point): TTFT attainment 100% vs. 76.1% (+23.9%); TPOT attainment 89.6% vs. 62.5% (+27.1%); combined end-to-end 89.6% vs. 55.8% (+33.8%). At QPS=2.0: decode throughput 28.1 vs. 23.6 tok/s (+19.3%). Across QPS=2.0–4.8, median decode throughput improves 13–19%. DistServe TPOT attainment stagnates at 60–74% regardless of load, indicating a structural inability to meet the 50ms target when long-tail stragglers dominate batch step time. Kairos TTFT stays at 100% through QPS=3.0, does not drop below 90% until QPS=3.6. No comparison against FastServe, SageSched, PARS, or other scheduling systems from related work. Baseline is a single peer (DistServe), which is the most directly relevant architectural comparator but not the most sophisticated scheduler. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The joint SLO-aware scheduling of both prefill and decode in a disaggregated LLM serving architecture is a genuine contribution. The urgency score formulation (slack normalized by length) is a principled novelty that bridges EDF-style scheduling and length-aware prioritization without requiring output-length prediction. The slack-guided decode batching is a direct operationalization of a structural LLM-serving property. Individual components draw on known scheduling theory, but the combined system and its application to this specific architecture is not prior art. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablation separates the prefill scheduler from the decode scheduler; the individual contribution of each mechanism is unknown. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-run statistics are reported on attainment curves. Only one model is evaluated. The evaluation dataset is a proprietary 1000-request trace — not public, not reproducible by third parties. No code or model weights are released. The paper evaluates one SLO configuration (TTFT=8s, TPOT=50ms); tighter SLO values that could collapse the slack buffer are not tested. Multiple directly relevant baselines (FastServe, SageSched, PARS) are discussed in related work but excluded from experiments without stated justification. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.02329v1, May 2026); results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a working prototype deployed on real hardware and evaluated on a production trace. - Practical: Runs on commercially available H200 SXM hardware; the scheduling logic (urgency computation, LUT lookup) is O(queue size) and O(active batch) per step, adding negligible overhead to the existing inference engine. - Efficient: No runtime LLM calls, no output-length prediction model, and no preemption required; LUT profiling is a one-time offline cost per model-hardware pair. Online LUT updates use a running mean with no additional GPU work. - Effective: The system demonstrably improves SLO attainment on a real production trace with a real 229B model, but only under one SLO setting; tighter TPOT values (e.g., 20ms) could eliminate the slack buffer that the decode scheduler depends on, and this regime is not evaluated. - Scalable: Evaluated only on a single 8-GPU server; multi-node disaggregated deployments with network latency between prefill and decode nodes could alter slack budgets and LUT accuracy in ways not studied. The work delivers a prototype system; no open-source release is mentioned. Strengths - The urgency score normalization by input length cleanly addresses both FCFS head-of-line blocking and SJF starvation in a single formula that requires no output-length prediction — a practically important advantage over PARS, TRAIL, and SageSched. - The slack-guided decode scheduler correctly identifies that TPOT SLOs are length-agnostic delivery constraints, converting what appears to be a straggler problem into an opportunity for selective batching — a non-obvious reframing. - Experiments use a frontier-scale production model (229B MoE) on a real serving trace on high-end hardware, giving the results practical credibility beyond typical academic benchmarks. - Online LUT updates make the decode scheduler adaptive to real hardware dynamics (power throttling, interference), a meaningful robustness property for production deployment. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note that absolute decode throughput (20–28 tok/s) is below hardware peak because the decode node is memory-bound: KV cache exhaustion at high concurrency caps batch size and aggregate throughput.
