Key Idea: The core insight is that conceptual integration can be operationalized at the concept level rather than the reference level: by extracting concepts from article text, mapping each concept to its disciplinary domain(s), and counting how many cross-disciplinary concept pairs show explicit semantic linkage (shared definitions, ontological reconciliation, conceptual bridging), one can quantify whether disciplines are actually connected inside the paper rather than merely juxtaposed in its bibliography. The multiplicative IIC = ρ × S ensures that high integration requires both dense cross-disciplinary conceptual connections (ρ) and qualitative evidence of synthesis (S), so neither dimension alone can drive the score. Methodology: The pipeline runs in four stages. Stage 1 retrieves 3,064 records from Scopus matching "interdisciplinary" or "interdisciplinariedad" with at least one Latin American author (2015–2024), screens to 3,043, stratified-samples 315, and filters to 112 articles with complete IIC and Rao–Stirling data. Stage 2 extracts concepts from title, abstract, and author keywords using rule-based normalization; maps each concept to disciplines via a "lightweight ontological framework"; constructs conceptual pairs by co-occurrence and semantic proximity; computes per-concept integration density ρ_k = L_k / M_k (aligned pairs over possible pairs); and aggregates to global ρ. The structural synthesis score S is defined in the main text as a weighted sum S = 0.40·T̄ + 0.25·Ū + 0.25·Ā + 0.10·N, where T = conceptual transformation, U = shared concept emergence, A = semantic agreement, N = emergence of new hybrid domain. IIC = ρ × S. Stage 3 computes Rao–Stirling diversity (simplified and full, with cosine-distance matrix derived from ASJC/SCImago co-occurrence) and network coherence (average pairwise discipline similarity). Stage 4 constructs IC-IDR = 0.50·IIC + 0.25·RS* + 0.25·Coherence, with RS min-max normalized. Statistical tests include Spearman rank correlation, Welch t-test, Mann–Whitney U, Levene variance test, Cohen's d, and sensitivity analysis over w₁ ∈ [0.40, 0.60]. Critical design decisions: (1) the multiplicative IIC structure is well-justified theoretically but creates a non-compensable metric that may suppress valid intermediate cases; (2) concept extraction from metadata only (not full text) is presented as a pragmatic choice but limits depth; (3) the coding of aligned disciplinary pairs relies on "explicit textual evidence" assessed through semi-structured manual validation — the extent and inter-rater reliability of this validation are not reported. Key Results: On the 112-article comparable dataset, IIC has mean = 0.525, SD = 0.156, range 0.140–0.938; both Rao–Stirling diversity and network coherence show severely zero-inflated distributions with medians and upper quartiles equal to zero. The Spearman correlation between IIC and Rao–Stirling is ρ = 0.041. Quadrant analysis (median split): Q1 (high RS, high IIC) = 40 articles, Q4 (high RS, low IIC) = 36, Q2 (low RS, high IIC) = 16, Q3 (low-low) = 20. IC-IDR correlates with IIC at ρ = 0.788, with diversity/coherence at ρ = 0.587. The IIC mean = 0.300 for IC-IDR (dominated by the IIC component given zero RS/coherence in most cases). No external baseline or comparison to an alternative integration measure is provided; there are no previously published integration-based metrics used as comparison points. The corpus is tested only at n = 112 — far below what would constitute a stress test. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The IIC formalizes an existing theoretical distinction (Melgarejo–Cadena, 2015; Klein, 2010) into a computable scalar metric and demonstrates on an empirical corpus that diversity and integration are near-orthogonal. What is not novel: the theoretical distinction between diversity and integration is well-established; the use of concept co-occurrence networks for disciplinarity analysis has prior work; the composite index pattern (weighted sum of diversity, coherence, integration) follows the Rafols & Meyer (2010) framework. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations for IIC sub-components are absent — no experiment tests whether ρ alone, S alone, or their product is driving results. The sensitivity analysis covers composite index weights but not IIC's own weights (α, β, γ, δ). No inter-rater reliability coefficient is reported for the manual coding of T, U, A, N. No code repository URL is provided despite repeated references to "Supplementary Material (code repository)." The paper is a preprint on ResearchSquare (posted May 4, 2026) and has not undergone peer review. A critical methodological concern: the Rao–Stirling distribution has medians and upper quartiles equal to zero for 75%+ of the corpus, which creates a severe range restriction problem — Spearman correlation between a normally distributed variable and a zero-inflated one is mathematically expected to be near zero regardless of true latent correlation. This confound is not acknowledged. A further internal inconsistency undermines reproducibility: in the main text (§3.3.3, §3.4.4), N is defined as "emergence of a new hybrid or integrative domain," a qualitative epistemic indicator in [0,1]; in Appendix B.5, N is redefined as N_i = (E_cross/E_total)·log(1 + E_total), a purely quantitative size-correction term with no semantic content. Correspondingly, Appendix B.6 computes S as a simple average (T+U+A+N)/4, contradicting the main text's weighted formula S = αT̄ + βŪ + γĀ + δN with α=0.40, β=0.25, γ=0.25, δ=0.10. These are not minor notational slips — the actual operationalization of S differs fundamentally between the theory section and the appendix that claims to operationalize it. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: The pipeline requires Scopus access for corpus and ASJC/SCImago data; concept extraction and coding use standard text processing tools. Standard academic hardware suffices for the analysis. - Efficient: The manual coding step does not scale; the paper acknowledges this as a limitation and calls for automated alignment detection as future work. At 112 articles the manual burden is manageable; at 10,000 articles it is not. - Effective: The IIC discriminates among integration regimes within the corpus, but external validity is unestablished — there is no ground-truth annotation of "truly integrated" versus "fragmented" papers against which to validate the metric. - Scalable: Not tested beyond 112 articles; the authors explicitly state this as a limitation. No claim of having tested at larger scale is made. The work delivers a metric definition and a Python implementation referenced but not linked; no trained model, dataset release, or library is made available. Strengths: - The paper addresses a real and consequential gap: diversity-based indicators dominate research evaluation despite known validity limitations, and this work provides the sharpest empirical demonstration of their orthogonality to content-level integration in the interdisciplinary studies literature. - The multiplicative IIC formulation with non-compensability is a theoretically principled design choice that prevents gaming the metric by inflating either component in isolation. - The quadrant-based visualization (Figure 10 / Figure 8) concretely illustrates the four interdisciplinarity regimes and gives practitioners intuition for the framework's diagnostic value. - The inclusion of sensitivity analysis over composite weights and use of non-parametric statistics appropriate to the zero-inflated distributions are methodologically sound choices. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) sample size n = 112 limits generalizability; (2) semi-structured coding introduces subjectivity; (3) focus on Latin American publications may reflect regional epistemic patterns not transferable to other contexts; (4) the need for automated conceptual alignment detection as future work. The authors confront these directly and do not minimize them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Beyond author-acknowledged limitations, several critical issues are unacknowledged. First, and most importantly, the Rao–Stirling distribution is severely zero-inflated (median = upper quartile = 0) — meaning the comparison variable lacks variance for the majority of the corpus. A near-zero Spearman correlation under these conditions is a mathematical near-certainty regardless of true latent relationships; this is a textbook range restriction confound and the paper's central empirical claim rests on it. Second, there is a fundamental internal inconsistency between the main-text definition of the structural component S (a weighted combination of four theoretically grounded epistemic indicators with weights 0.40/0.25/0.25/0.10) and Appendix B's operationalization (a simple average of T, U, A, and a log-normalized count term that has no connection to the qualitative N defined in the body). It is impossible to know which version was actually implemented. Third, concept extraction from metadata (title, abstract, keywords) only is a shallow proxy for the full paper's conceptual structure — a paper could have rich integration in its methods section that metadata would never capture. Fourth, the discipline assignment of concepts is described as using a "lightweight ontological framework" without specifying the controlled vocabulary, training data, or accuracy. Fifth, no inter-rater reliability statistic (Cohen's κ, Krippendorff's α) is reported for the manual annotation of aligned pairs and TUAN indicators. Sixth, no external validation exists: IIC scores are never compared against expert human judgments of integration quality, citation impact, or any proxy of research outcome. Bottom Line: This preprint raises a legitimate and important critique of diversity-only interdisciplinarity metrics, but its central empirical result (near-zero IIC–RS correlation) is confounded by severe zero-inflation in the Rao–Stirling distribution, and its core operationalization is internally inconsistent between the main text and appendix — making the metric's actual definition ambiguous; these flaws require resolution before the work can be trusted as evidence for its main claim.
Search Results
#159 — Incremental (32)

LLM -Guided Runtime Parameter Optimization for Energy-Efficient Model Inference

Katelyn Crumpacker, Dimitrios Nikolopoulos · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proposes a human-in-the-loop framework where an external LLM (Claude, accessed manually via web UI) iteratively suggests vLLM and PyTorch runtime parameters to minimize GPU energy per token, and shows that a structured "enhanced" prompt template converges faster than an unstructured baseline. - Why it's important: Choosing energy-efficient runtime parameters for LLM inference systems today requires domain expertise or multi-day exhaustive search; a faster, hardware-adaptable alternative would reduce both operational cost and carbon footprint at scale. - How it does it: After each inference run, a human constructs a feedback prompt encoding the measured energy metrics, current parameters, and hardware context, then feeds it to a Claude chat project whose persistent memory guides the next parameter suggestion — repeating until convergence. - The good: On vLLM with Llama-3.2-3B, enhanced prompts reached a 1.80 J/token threshold in 3.4 ± 0.55 iterations versus 5.2 ± 0.84 for the baseline (paired t-test p=0.0086, Cohen's d≈2.55), while simultaneously improving throughput from 33.5 to 59 tokens/s — a bonus Pareto improvement the method did not explicitly optimize for. - The missing: The convergence threshold (1.80 J/token for vLLM, 0.20 J/token for PyTorch) was selected post-hoc near the empirically observed minimum, which circularly defines "convergence" in a way that flatters the method; no Bayesian optimization baseline is tested despite being the acknowledged standard for this exact problem type. - Read it or not: Skip unless you work on ML systems energy tuning and want a quick existence proof that prompted LLMs can replace Sobol for small parameter spaces; the methodology is too thin and the scale too narrow to inform production decisions.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (25) is the dominant drag — post-hoc convergence threshold near the observed minimum is methodologically circular, N=5 runs, no code released, human-in-the-loop prompt construction is uncontrolled, and the glaring omission of any Bayesian-optimization baseline (EcoOptiGen, TPE) — the acknowledged standard for this problem class — undermines the central claim. Novelty (35) — application of Zhang et al. 2024's LLM-as-optimizer idea to a new domain. Completeness (30) — no ablation of the four prompt elements, search space is only 6 parameters. Resilience (30) — claims would not survive PC scrutiny without a BO comparison. Composite: 0.20*35 + 0.25*25 + 0.15*40 + 0.15*30 + 0.15*30 + 0.10*45 ≈ 32.
empirical Novelty 35 Rigor 25 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 30 Resilience 30 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Operators of LLM inference services face a combinatorial parameter tuning problem: settings like batch size, memory utilization, token budget, and power limits interact non-linearly to determine GPU energy consumption and throughput. Finding near-optimal configurations currently requires either deep systems expertise or exhaustive search methods (grid, random, quasi-random) that consume days of GPU time. This is particularly acute in HPC and cloud settings where energy cost is a first-class concern. The gap the paper targets is the absence of a fast, hardware-context-aware search method that does not require domain expertise to operate. Key Idea: An LLM with persistent chat context can serve as a black-box optimizer: given the current parameter configuration and its measured energy metrics as a structured prompt, the LLM proposes a better configuration in the next turn, narrowing in on efficient operating points through in-context learning rather than random or quasi-random sampling. The key claim is that the LLM's ability to reason about parameter interactions — given hardware descriptions and metric history — allows it to skip regions of the search space that blind samplers must exhaustively cover. Methodology: The pipeline has three components: (1) a fixed inference workload runner (vLLM serving Llama-3.2-3B, or PyTorch running Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct) that measures energy via NVML power sampling; (2) a human operator who constructs prompts and pastes results into a Claude web chat project; (3) a search space of 6 parameters for vLLM (max-num-batched-tokens [256–4096], max-model-len [256–4096], block-size [16,32], max-num-sequences [32–256], max-num-partial-prefills [1–10], power-limit [100–250W]) and 4 for PyTorch (pixel-precision, cuda-memory-fraction, batch-size, power-limit). Two prompt templates are compared: an "enhanced" template with repetition, hardware background, search-process constraints, and explicit task framing; and a "baseline" template providing minimum information in unstructured form. The process runs for up to 5–6 iterations per trial and is repeated 5 times independently. Sobol quasi-random sampling over the same space serves as a secondary baseline (30 configs for vLLM, 64 for PyTorch). There is no explicit objective function — the LLM is instructed to minimize energy per token. Critical design decisions: (a) using a project-isolated Claude chat for persistent memory across iterations (justified but not ablated); (b) human-constructed prompts rather than automated injection (limits reproducibility and throughput); (c) a convergence threshold defined post-hoc near the empirical minimum (methodologically circular). Key Results: On vLLM/Llama-3.2-3B (single V100 16GB): enhanced prompts reach 1.80 J/token in 3.4 ± 0.55 iterations vs. 5.2 ± 0.84 for baseline (p=0.0086); minimum achieved values range 1.68–1.74 J/token (enhanced) vs. 1.72–2.43 J/token (baseline) across 5 runs. Sobol sampling required 24 hours for 30 configs and reached a minimum of 1.72 J/token — comparable to the LLM's best but only after exhaustive evaluation. Throughput improved from 33.5 to 59 tokens/s (76%) despite no explicit throughput objective. On PyTorch/Qwen3-VL-4B (multimodal): enhanced prompts reached 0.20 J/token and 30 J/image thresholds in 4.2 ± 0.84 iterations; the baseline never reached either threshold within 5 prompts. Sobol sampling hit 55/64 OOM errors due to fp32 precision selection, reaching 0.141 J/token in the best non-OOM run vs. 0.160 J/token for the LLM approach, but 27.5 J/image (LLM) vs. 82.5 J/image (Sobol) on image embeddings. The optimization overhead analysis estimates break-even at 5.65 workloads of 1000 prompts, based on a conservatively estimated 3 Wh per Claude API call. Baselines are weak: only an unstructured prompt variant and Sobol sampling; Bayesian optimization (EcoOptiGen, TPE), random search, and simulated annealing are discussed in related work but not evaluated. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The combination of in-context LLM reasoning as a black-box runtime optimizer for inference system parameters is a genuine application extension of prior LLM-for-hyperparameter-optimization work (Zhang et al. 2024), distinguishing runtime system parameters (batch sizes, memory limits, power caps) from model hyperparameters. The prompt engineering comparison (structured vs. unstructured) is a useful empirical finding. However, the underlying idea — using an LLM's in-context reasoning to guide iterative optimization — is directly drawn from Zhang et al. 2024 and applied to a new domain. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations isolate which of the four prompt engineering elements (repetition, background, search-process constraints, task framing) individually drive the improvement. N=5 independent runs is small; the paired t-test on 5 observations is valid in form but fragile in interpretation. The convergence threshold is explicitly acknowledged as post-hoc, selected "near the empirically observed minimum" — this means the threshold is not a pre-specified target and comparisons of convergence speed to it are descriptive, not predictive. The human-in-the-loop design means every result reflects both prompt template and human judgment in prompt construction; the templates in Figures 3–4 are shown but full prompts are not reproducibly specified. No code is released. Experiments run on a single GPU instance with no multi-seed GPU power measurement variance reported. The Sobol comparison uses a different number of configurations for vLLM (30) and PyTorch (64) without explanation of sample-size selection. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (systems with measurement). - Practical: Runs on a single commodity V100 16GB GPU; hardware is accessible, but the human-in-the-loop manual prompting is not operationally deployable without automation. - Efficient: The LLM approach requires 3.4 optimization iterations (each involving a full 1000-prompt inference run plus human prompt construction time), reaching break-even at ~6 workloads; the absolute evaluation cost in GPU-hours is not reported. - Effective: The evidence shows the method reduces energy per token by ~42% on vLLM relative to defaults, but the final values are similar to what Sobol would find given enough time — the advantage is search speed, not solution quality. - Scalable: Tested only on a single-node, single-GPU setup with a 3B and 4B parameter model; the paper explicitly states results do not generalize to multi-node deployments, and the 6-parameter search space is too small to stress-test LLM reasoning about high-dimensional interactions. No experiments beyond the stated scale. The work delivers no software artifact; it is paper-form only. Strengths: - Demonstrates that a structured prompt template (with hardware context, search constraints, and metric history) materially accelerates convergence versus an unstructured template on a practically relevant objective — the effect size is large (Cohen's d≈2.55 for vLLM) and the result holds across both inference backends. - Sobol's catastrophic failure mode on the PyTorch search space (55/64 OOM errors from fp32 selection) concretely illustrates why domain-agnostic samplers fail in systems with hard constraints — a finding of practical value. - The throughput co-improvement (33.5 → 59 tokens/s while optimizing only energy per token) suggests the optimized configurations escape a poor operating region generally, not just on the target metric. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note: (a) results do not generalize to multi-node inference; (b) stochastic LLM behavior can affect reproducibility, though they argue prompt templates mitigate this; (c) throughput was not the optimization target and was not maximized. These are credible and appropriately scoped acknowledgments. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No Bayesian optimization baseline: EcoOptiGen and TPE are the standard comparison for this exact problem class and are cited in related work but not evaluated — this is the most glaring omission. - The post-hoc threshold selection is methodologically circular: setting convergence at the observed minimum then measuring how fast each method reaches it conflates solution quality with search speed. - The human-in-the-loop is doing non-trivial work (constructing prompts, interpreting outputs, enforcing constraints) that is neither quantified nor controlled across baseline and enhanced conditions — prompt construction quality may differ systematically. - 5 independent runs per condition is insufficient to characterize the variance of a stochastic optimizer over a 6-dimensional space; 3/5 baseline runs failing to converge is presented as evidence of poor baseline performance, but could reflect threshold selection. - Only two small models (3B, 4B parameters) on one GPU; no evaluation on 70B+ models where energy optimization has greater practical impact. - The search space is trivially small (6 discrete/continuous parameters); the claim that LLMs handle "large parameter spaces" is not substantiated by any experiment at scale. Bottom Line: This is a workshop-quality empirical note (8 pages, preprint) that establishes a plausible existence claim — structured prompting beats unstructured prompting for iterative runtime parameter search — but omits the Bayesian optimization baseline that would determine whether the LLM approach is actually competitive with the best existing methods, making it premature to recommend for practitioners.
Search Results 2604.27032 PDF
#160 — Incremental (32)

Semantic Approaches to Circularity in the Built Environment

papers.cumincad.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper synthesizes 14 peer-reviewed studies (2015–2025) on ontology and knowledge graph applications for circular economy in the built environment, identifies five thematic clusters, and proposes a four-layer conceptual framework (data sources → semantic modelling → reasoning/analytics → applications/feedback). - Why it's important: Circular economy implementation in construction is stalled partly by fragmented, non-interoperable digital systems; a structured map of what semantic approaches exist and where they fall short is useful for researchers deciding where to invest next. - How it does it: The review applies a Scopus keyword search ("circular building" AND "ontology" OR "knowledge graph"), filters to 14 papers, groups them by scale/theme through thematic synthesis, and derives a unified framework by assembling the layers implied collectively across the reviewed works. - The good: The gap analysis is specific and actionable, naming concrete ontology pairs that cannot interoperate (e.g., Material Passport Ontology vs. SERO) and identifying the absence of real-time feedback loops as a structural deficiency. - The missing: The corpus is only 14 papers drawn from a single database with no inter-rater reliability check, no quality appraisal instrument, and no rationale for why 14 papers is sufficient coverage — the review's conclusions rest on an unusually thin evidentiary base. - Read it or not: Worth a quick read for researchers entering the semantic CE/built-environment space who want a map of named ontologies and their gaps; practitioners or researchers already familiar with BIM/LCA ontology literature will find little beyond what they already know.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (35) low — survey synthesis with a labeled diagram framework. Rigor (25) weak — single database, 14 papers, no PRISMA, no inter-rater reliability, no quality appraisal. Non-Obviousness (30) the taxonomy is intuitive but unsurprising. Completeness (30) authors don't acknowledge corpus-size or single-database limits. Resilience (30) collapses under range/coverage scrutiny. Impact (35) modest reading list value. Composite: 0.20*35+0.25*25+0.15*30+0.15*30+0.15*30+0.10*35 = 30.
empirical Novelty 35 Rigor 25 Non-Obv 30 Completeness 30 Resilience 30 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: The built environment accounts for approximately 40% of raw material consumption and 37% of energy-related CO₂ emissions (IPCC 2021; UNEP 2023). Implementing Circular Economy (CE) strategies — material reuse, design-for-disassembly, extended asset lifespans — requires machine-readable, interoperable representations of materials, components, and lifecycle processes. Existing digital tools (BIM, GIS, IoT, LCA) operate in isolation with incompatible vocabularies and data formats. Semantic technologies (ontologies, knowledge graphs) have emerged to bridge these silos, but the literature is fragmented across scales and domains. Architects, urban planners, facility managers, and policymakers lack a consolidated picture of what semantic approaches exist, how they compare, and where deployment gaps remain. Key Idea: The paper has no single technical insight of its own — it is a review. The organizational insight is to arrange 14 existing systems along two orthogonal dimensions: spatial scale (component → building → city) and operational type (static modelling → reasoning → real-time digital twins), and to argue that current work is strong on the lower-left (component-scale, static ontologies) and weak on the upper-right (city-scale, real-time dynamic systems). The proposed four-layer framework assembles these dimensions into a stack meant to show how future work could integrate them. This is a coherent synthesis rather than a novel technical contribution. Methodology: The review follows a structured literature selection process: Scopus database only; keyword query "circular building" AND "ontology" OR "knowledge graph"; date filter 2015–2025; English only; journal articles and conference papers only. This yielded 30 papers, reduced to 14 after scope and accessibility filtering. No PRISMA checklist, CASP appraisal, or inter-rater reliability procedure is reported. The 14 papers are grouped by thematic synthesis into five clusters: (1) material/component-level ontologies (4 papers: CEO/CAMO, PBRO, CMCW-LCA, SERO); (2) building-level frameworks (2 papers: IMS, SMB); (3) urban-scale knowledge graphs (4 papers: BIMyVerse, MPO, BMSO, HPKG); (4) activity-level semantics (2 papers: DKG, BPCAO); (5) AI/digital twin integrations (2 papers: AO-LLM, DTSRM). The proposed unified framework has four layers: data sources (BIM/GIS/IoT/LCA/passports), semantic modelling (component→building→city), reasoning/analytics (inference, indicator computation), and applications with feedback loops (design support, policy, digital twins). Critical design decision: the framework assumes explicit cross-ontology alignment rules exist, but these rules are not derived, specified, or validated — they are asserted as necessary future work. Key Results: No new empirical results are reported. The comparative analysis of the 14 reviewed systems reveals: (a) coverage is concentrated at the component/building scale with sparser urban-scale work; (b) all five systems classified under AI/digital twins are from 2024–2025, indicating the theme is nascent; (c) only one study (Elghaish et al., 2025) integrates real-time IoT data; (d) most systems are prototype-only with no evidence of large-scale deployment. No quantitative metrics (precision/recall of ontology queries, coverage of CE strategies, reasoning performance) are synthesized across studies. Baselines and benchmarks are not applicable in the traditional sense; the review notes the absence of a shared evaluation standard as itself a gap. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (marginal) + SURVEY/SYNTHESIS. The thematic taxonomy and gap enumeration constitute the primary contribution. The proposed four-layer framework is a synthesized design archetype, not a new technical system. The specific ontology names surfaced and the concrete interoperability conflicts identified (e.g., Material Passport Ontology vs. SERO using different class hierarchies) add modest reference value beyond prior reviews, but a 14-paper corpus is unlikely to be comprehensive enough to claim definitive coverage of this field. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations, no error bars, no significance tests — not applicable for a review. Methodological concerns are substantial: (1) single database (Scopus) with no cross-validation against Web of Science, ACM DL, or Google Scholar; (2) no inter-rater reliability measure for thematic coding; (3) no quality appraisal of included papers; (4) inclusion/exclusion criteria exclude grey literature and technical reports, which may contain substantial deployed system knowledge; (5) the final corpus of 14 papers is unusually small for a systematic review and the paper provides no justification for why this is sufficient. No code or dataset is released. This is a peer-reviewed conference paper (CAADRIA 2026), not a preprint, but the conference paper format (10 pages) severely limits depth. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: THEORETICAL (the paper itself delivers a conceptual framework, not a running system). - Sound: The framework layers are logically coherent and grounded in citations from the reviewed literature, but the critical assumption — that cross-ontology alignment rules can be specified without monolithic standardization — is asserted, not demonstrated. - General: The framework is positioned as universally applicable across CE contexts in the built environment, but all 14 source papers are from Europe/North America/Singapore, leaving coverage of regulatory and data availability contexts elsewhere unverified. - Tight: Not applicable — no formal bounds are derived. - Implementable: The framework is conceptual only; it does not specify ontology alignment mappings, query interfaces, or integration protocols that would let a developer build it. No follow-up implementation is presented or referenced. The paper delivers a survey and a diagram, not a software artifact, dataset, or reusable proof technique. Strengths: - Provides a named, structured taxonomy of 14 specific systems with explicit references, giving a functional reading list for the domain. - Gap analysis is concrete: identifies specific ontology pairs that conflict (Material Passport Ontology vs. SERO), names the missing socio-economic dimension, and flags the absence of real-time feedback as structural rather than incidental. - The five-theme taxonomy (material → building → urban → activity → AI/DT) is intuitive and maps cleanly to the spatial and operational diversity of the field. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors do not explicitly state limitations on corpus size or single-database search. They acknowledge prototype-only deployment as a gap in the reviewed literature but do not flag it as a limitation of the review itself. The socio-economic gap is noted but attributed to the reviewed papers rather than to the review methodology. The manual nature of ontology engineering is flagged as a barrier but treated as a future research direction. Overall, self-critical reflection on the review's own scope and completeness is absent — a credibility negative. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) A 14-paper corpus from a single database is insufficient to support confident claims about the "state of the field"; the ACM Digital Library and IEEE Xplore hold substantial semantic web and BIM research not indexed in Scopus. (2) No thematic coding reliability check means the five-cluster taxonomy could reflect one reviewer's framing. (3) The proposed unified framework adds no new technical content — it is a labeled diagram whose layers are directly traceable to the reviewed papers; the claim that it "addresses fragmentation" without specifying alignment mappings or evaluation criteria is unsupported. (4) The paper conflates reviewing what exists with proposing what should exist, and the latter contribution is too lightweight to stand independently. (5) Missing baseline comparison: are there prior reviews of semantic CE/BIM that the authors should be situated against? The paper does not compare to related survey papers. Bottom Line: This is a serviceable orientation paper for newcomers to semantic circular-economy modelling in the built environment, but its 14-paper corpus, single-database search, and diagram-only framework make it unsuitable as a definitive reference; readers seeking depth should follow its citation list directly to the primary sources.
Search Results
#161 — Incremental (32)

PrunePrivyTune: Accelerating Language Models with Pruning and Differentially Private Fine-Tuning

link.springer.com · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PrunePrivyTune, a three-stage framework that applies pairwise cosine similarity-based structural pruning followed by differentially private LoRA fine-tuning and a synthetic-data privacy audit to BERT-base models. - Why it's important: Deploying language models in sensitive domains (healthcare, finance, legal) simultaneously demands inference efficiency and formal privacy guarantees, yet existing compression methods ignore the memorization risk reintroduced during post-pruning adaptation — a gap prior work leaves largely unaddressed. - How it does it: Pairwise cosine similarity across all transformer layer pairs identifies functionally redundant layers for removal; the pruned model is then fine-tuned with LoRA adapters under DPSGD, exploiting the reduced parameter count to lower the effective noise-per-gradient and improve the privacy-utility trade-off. - The good: With 10% sparsity and ε=1, the pruned + DP-LoRA BERT-base achieves 92.31% on SST-2 — matching full non-private BERT-base fine-tuning (92.31%) and outperforming the best prior private compression baseline TSKD (86.23%), while also improving over its own non-private variant (91.97%), suggesting a genuine regularization synergy. - The missing: All experiments use only BERT-base (110M parameters) on four GLUE tasks; the claimed applicability to Llama-scale LLMs is entirely unsupported by experiments, and no inference latency or throughput measurements are reported despite the paper's title claiming "acceleration." - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML engineers targeting privacy-preserving compression of encoder-only BERT-scale models, but researchers working on modern decoder LLMs or seeking rigorous privacy auditing will find the scope too narrow.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (30) and Resilience (25) drive the score down: no error bars or multiple seeds, no released code, the title claims 'acceleration' yet zero latency or throughput numbers are reported, the single-epoch DP accounting assumption is unvalidated, and the BERTScore/perplexity 'privacy audit' is not a standard memorization test (F1 ~0.35 across all conditions, including non-DP, makes it nearly uninformative). Novelty (35) and Non-Obviousness (30) are low — pairwise cosine pruning + DP-LoRA + a weak audit is a competent integration of known techniques without comparison to recent DP-PEFT baselines. The unexplained RTE collapse to ~49% (random-level) under DP is a real failure that goes unexamined, and scope is limited to BERT-base on four GLUE tasks despite asserted Llama-scale applicability. Weighted: 0.20*35 + 0.25*30 + 0.15*30 + 0.15*40 + 0.15*25 + 0.10*30 = 32.
empirical Novelty 35 Rigor 30 Non-Obv 30 Completeness 40 Resilience 25 Impact 30
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Deploying large language models in sensitive domains requires both computational efficiency for real-time inference and formal privacy guarantees against training data memorization and extraction attacks. Existing compression methods — pruning, distillation, quantization — address efficiency but ignore privacy risks; DPSGD-based training addresses privacy but is expensive and degrades accuracy when applied to large uncompressed models. The specific gap is that post-pruning fine-tuning reintroduces memorization risk, and privately fine-tuning a pruned model with parameter-efficient methods has received little systematic study. Practitioners in healthcare or finance who need both smaller models and regulatory compliance (GDPR) have no principled off-the-shelf solution. Key Idea The core technical insight is that cosine similarity between adjacent transformer layer hidden states measures functional redundancy: layers whose outputs are nearly identical are performing nearly the same transformation and can be removed without losing representational capacity. Furthermore, removing these layers before applying DPSGD reduces gradient dimensionality, which distributes the injected Gaussian noise over fewer meaningful parameters — lowering effective noise per update and improving the privacy-utility trade-off relative to applying DPSGD to the full model. LoRA further constrains updates to a low-rank subspace, preventing the DP noise from distorting the full weight matrix. Methodology The pipeline has three stages. Stage 1 (pruning): extract hidden states from all L transformer layers on a calibration batch; compute pairwise cosine similarity cos-sim(x^(ℓ), x^(ℓ')) between all layer pairs; for each layer ℓ compute avg_cos_ℓ = ½(cos-sim(x^(ℓ-1), x^(ℓ)) + cos-sim(x^(ℓ), x^(ℓ+1))); prune layers where avg_cos_ℓ > τ, subject to performance not degrading beyond Δ from baseline P₀. The pruning step scales as O(L²d), negligible for L=12. Stage 2 (private fine-tuning): attach LoRA adapters (W → W + LR, rank-r decomposition) to attention and MLP layers of the pruned model; fine-tune with DPSGD — per-example gradients are clipped to norm C, Gaussian noise N(0,σ²C²I) is added to the batch gradient, and only L and R are updated. Stage 3 (privacy audit): generate synthetic text via iterative masked-token prediction with top-k sampling, compare synthetic vs. training data using perplexity and BERTScore. Critical design decisions: (1) pairwise vs. consecutive similarity — supported by ablation; (2) LoRA vs. full retraining post-pruning — justified empirically; (3) single-epoch training to avoid DP composition, claiming total privacy budget = ε. The single-epoch assumption is stated without experimental validation and is a meaningful constraint on convergence. Key Results Experiments use BERT-base (110M parameters) on four GLUE tasks: SST-2 (67K train), RTE (2K), MRPC (3.7K), CoLA (8.5K). Best configuration — pairwise cosine + LoRA fine-tuning + DP ε=1, sparsity 0.1 — achieves 92.31% SST-2, 49.45% RTE, 82.37% MRPC, 79.70% CoLA. Surprisingly, DP sometimes improves over non-private variants (SST-2: 92.31% with DP vs. 91.97% without), which the authors attribute to noise acting as a regularizer. Baselines: DPKD (Mireshghallah et al.) 78.5% SST-2; structured DPIMP 83.3%; TSKD (Garg & Torra) 86.23%; PrunePrivyTune 92.31% — a meaningful improvement. At 30% sparsity with DP, SST-2 drops to 85.32% and average accuracy to 73.20%. RTE at 49.45% with DP is barely above random for a 2-class task and is not discussed as a failure. Privacy audit: at ε=1, BERTScore F1 is 0.3512 on SST-2 (vs. 0.3697 without DP); perplexity increases from 6.32×10⁵ to 7.10×10⁵ — directionally consistent with reduced memorization but weak in absolute terms. No inference latency numbers are reported. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD (incremental). Pairwise cosine similarity for layer redundancy detection has appeared in prior work (Dalvi et al. 2020, LLM-Pruner 2023); DP-LoRA combinations have been studied; the specific combination addressing post-pruning private adaptation with a synthetic-data privacy audit is the incremental contribution. This is a competent integration of known techniques targeting a specific underexplored subproblem rather than a fundamental new idea. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: pairwise vs. consecutive cosine similarity (Table 1), sparsity sweep 0.1–0.3 (Tables 2–3), training vs. LoRA fine-tuning (Table 4), baseline comparison (Table 5). No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed results are reported. No code, pretrained weights, or data are released — reproducibility depends entirely on the paper's description. The privacy budget analysis rests on the single-epoch assumption (each training example used exactly once to avoid composition) without experimental confirmation and without discussion of the accuracy penalty this imposes. BERTScore values of ~0.35 F1 across all conditions suggest the synthetic text quality is poor in absolute terms, making perplexity and BERTScore weak proxies for memorization risk compared to canonical membership inference or extraction attack experiments (Carlini et al. 2021). Baselines are primarily 2019–2022 vintage; no comparison with more recent DP-PEFT methods. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: BERT-base and LoRA fine-tuning run on commodity GPUs; the pruning overhead is O(L²d) and negligible. - Efficient: Training cost is reduced by removing layers and restricting updates to LoRA rank-r matrices, but actual inference speedup, memory reduction, and training time are not measured quantitatively — the efficiency claim in the title is qualitative only. - Effective: On SST-2 the method matches full BERT-base accuracy under privacy constraints, a strong result; on RTE (smallest dataset, 2K examples) performance degrades severely to 49.45% with DP, suggesting the method struggles when fine-tuning data is scarce. - Scalable: Tested only on BERT-base (110M parameters); no experiments on any model larger than 110M; applicability to Llama or GPT-scale models is asserted but not demonstrated. The work delivers results in paper form only — no software artifact, dataset, or trained model is released. Strengths - The empirical finding that combining pairwise pruning with DP-LoRA can match or exceed non-private full BERT-base accuracy (92.31% SST-2) under ε=1 is the single most credible and practically useful result, backed by consistent tabular evidence across four tasks. - The three-stage pipeline that unifies compression, private adaptation, and privacy auditing is more complete than prior work, which addresses only one or two of these stages. - The pairwise vs. consecutive cosine similarity ablation (Table 1) cleanly isolates the contribution of the pruning criterion, showing consistent gains across tasks and DP conditions — a model ablation design. - The observation that higher sparsity can partially offset DP accuracy degradation (Table 2: MRPC 63.33% at sparsity 0.1 vs. 68.13% at sparsity 0.3 under DP) is a counter-intuitive and practically relevant finding, corroborated by external literature. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) experiments limited to BERT-base, not larger LLMs like LLaMA; (2) evaluation only on GLUE datasets, limiting generalizability; (3) difficulty measuring computational overhead for large models; (4) sensitivity to ε, sparsity, and other hyperparameters making tuning labor-intensive. These are addressed directly in a dedicated Limitation section, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The paper's title claims "accelerating" language models, but zero inference latency, throughput, or wall-clock training time measurements are reported; the efficiency argument is entirely qualitative. - The single-epoch DP privacy accounting eliminates composition but severely restricts convergence. The accuracy penalty of this constraint is never isolated or discussed — readers cannot distinguish accuracy losses due to pruning, DP noise, or training truncation. - RTE performance of ~49% under DP (barely above random for binary classification) is a real failure mode that is not examined or explained. - The privacy audit using BERTScore and perplexity on synthetic data is not a standard or rigorous memorization test; BERTScore F1 ~0.35 across all conditions (including no-DP) makes the metric nearly uninformative without calibration. A proper training data extraction attack (Carlini et al. 2021) or membership inference experiment was not conducted despite being cited. - No comparison with recent DP-PEFT methods that directly combine differential privacy with parameter-efficient fine-tuning (e.g., Yu et al. 2022, DP-LoRA literature), making it unclear whether the pruning step is the key contributor or whether competitive DP-LoRA alone performs similarly. - The ε=1 privacy budget used throughout is not context-anchored; for NLP tasks with text data, ε=1 is extremely tight and may not be practically achievable with meaningful utility — this is not discussed. Bottom Line A practitioner specifically targeting privacy-preserving compression of BERT-scale encoder models for GLUE-style classification gains actionable, well-ablated results; anyone deploying modern decoder LLMs or needing rigorous privacy certification should look to more recent work with larger-scale experiments and formally validated privacy audits.
Recommended
#162 — Incremental (30)

Quantum Hypergraph Partitioning

Yiran Li, Y. Batuhan Yilmaz, Michael Silver, Zachary Vernec, Hans-Arno Jacobsen · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper derives QUBO and HUBO binary optimization formulations for balanced k-way hypergraph partitioning under five hyperedge cut functions (all-or-nothing, quadratic penalty, linear penalty, normalized cut, random walk conductance) for both two-way and multi-way settings, and validates the two-way all-or-nothing QUBO using simulated QAOA and simulated annealing on small instances. - Why it's important: Balanced hypergraph partitioning is NP-hard and is a core primitive in distributed database sharding, sparse matrix-vector multiplication, and quantum circuit distribution, yet quantum optimization has been applied almost exclusively to graph (not hypergraph) partitioning. - How it does it: For each cut function and partition arity, the paper substitutes binary assignment variables (single bit per node for k=2, one-hot vector for k>2) and algebraically identifies which objectives collapse to degree-2 polynomials (QUBO), remain higher-order (HUBO), or become rational functions ineligible for either form. - The good: Simulated annealing with balance penalty λ=3 achieves near-100% feasibility and optimality on all tested instances up to n=15, confirming the QUBO encoding is at least correct. - The missing: The strong classical baseline KaHyPar achieves 100% optimality on every instance throughout, simulated QAOA uses only circuit depth 1, and no instance exceeds 15 nodes — there is no scale or scenario where the quantum approach is competitive, let alone advantageous. - Read it or not: Worth reading as a formulation reference for researchers specifically working on QUBO/HUBO encodings for higher-order combinatorial problems; practitioners needing usable hypergraph partitioning should skip it entirely.
Why this rank: Empirical profile (simulated quantum). Novelty (45) is a NEW FORMULATION extending a single prior result to five cut functions and multi-way settings, but the QUBO/HUBO machinery is entirely standard. Non-Obviousness (40) — the cubic-cancellation observation is clean but the rest is mechanical algebra. Rigor (35) and Resilience (25) are weak: KaHyPar achieves 100% optimality on every instance, depth-1 QAOA is below any meaningful approximation threshold, n≤15 is toy scale, and the multi-way formulations are entirely unevaluated. Completeness (35) — three of five derived cut functions get no experimental treatment. Impact (25) is low; code is released but no setting demonstrates any quantum advantage and qubit counts for realistic instances are prohibitive. Weighted: 0.20·45 + 0.25·35 + 0.15·40 + 0.15·35 + 0.15·25 + 0.10·25 = 34, adjusted to 30 reflecting that the paper makes no case for its central premise.
empirical Novelty 45 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 35 Resilience 25 Impact 25
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Balanced k-way hypergraph partitioning divides a hypergraph's nodes into k equal-sized subsets while minimizing a chosen cut objective. The balance constraint makes the problem NP-hard for any k≥2, by reduction from minimum graph bisection. The problem arises concretely in distributed database sharding (Facebook's Social Hash Partitioner), sparse matrix-vector multiplication layout, biological network analysis, and distributing quantum circuits across QPUs. Classical heuristics — multilevel methods (KaHyPar), Louvain-style modularity, spectral relaxation — exist and scale well in practice, but quantum optimization has been applied systematically only to ordinary graph partitioning, not hypergraph partitioning. Prior quantum work on hypergraphs (Rodriguez 2022) covered only the all-or-nothing two-way cut as a HUBO, leaving the broader landscape of cut functions and multi-way settings unaddressed. Key Idea The central insight is that the all-or-nothing cut on 3-uniform hypergraphs, when expressed as the product formula from Rodriguez, has its cubic terms from the two complementary all-in-partition-0 and all-in-partition-1 products cancel algebraically, yielding a valid QUBO with only n binary variables. More broadly, the paper systematically classifies which cut functions, under which encoding (2-way binary vs. k-way one-hot), produce QUBO, HUBO, or rational objectives — a classification that determines which quantum solver paradigm (quantum annealing requires QUBO; QAOA can handle HUBO at the cost of circuit depth) applies without additional variable-introduction steps. Methodology The total binary objective is decomposed as E(x) = E_cut(x) + α·E_partition(x) + λ·E_balance(x). For two-way partitioning, a single binary variable x_i per node suffices; the balance penalty is (Σx_i − n/2)² and no partition validity penalty is needed. For multi-way (k>2), one-hot encoding uses nk binary variables, with E_partition = Σ_i(Σ_c x_{ic} − 1)² and E_balance = Σ_c(Σ_i x_{ic} − n/k)². Five cut functions are then analyzed: (1) all-or-nothing — QUBO for 3-uniform 2-way, HUBO generally; (2) quadratic penalty — direct QUBO for both 2-way and k-way; (3) linear penalty — contains an absolute value, not representable as QUBO; (4) hypergraph normalized cut — rational function (denominator depends on partition volumes), not directly representable; (5) random walk conductance — direct QUBO when transition probabilities are treated as constants. An alternative binary encoding (⌈log₂k⌉ bits per node) is discussed and rejected as less practical because it generates higher-order terms even for the balance constraint. Experiments use 100 random connected unweighted 3-uniform hypergraphs per n∈{8,…,15} with average degree ≈5. Four methods are compared: exhaustive exact search, KaHyPar, simulated QAOA (Qiskit statevector simulator, circuit depth 1, top-10 probability states), and simulated annealing (Ocean SDK, 100 reads). Balance penalty λ∈{0.3, 1, 3}; 5 seeded runs per method; metrics are feasibility rate and optimality rate. Key Results KaHyPar achieves 100% feasibility and 100% optimality on all 800 instances (n=8 to 15). SA with λ=3 achieves near-100% feasibility and optimality across all sizes. SA with λ=1 retains high optimality when feasible but feasibility drops with size. SA with λ=0.3 rarely satisfies the balance constraint. Simulated QAOA (depth 1) with λ=1 achieves >90% optimality for n<10 but degrades sharply with size; it shows high variance across seeds, indicating sensitivity to random initialization. Increasing λ to 3 causes sQAOA optimality to collapse (balance term dominates and distorts the optimization landscape). No experiments exceed n=15 nodes, no real quantum hardware is used, and the multi-way (k>2) formulations are not evaluated at all. Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION. The genuine novelty is the systematic derivation of QUBO/HUBO encodings for five cut functions across both two-way and multi-way balanced hypergraph partitioning, extending a single prior result (Rodriguez 2022, two-way all-or-nothing HUBO only). The penalty framework, the QUBO/HUBO machinery, and the experimental protocol are entirely standard in quantum optimization. No new algorithm, bound, or dataset is introduced. Rigor & Reproducibility Code and data are publicly released at https://github.com/CyanideCentral/quantum-hypergraph-partitioning. Five seeds per method are used and standard errors are reported in figures. The λ sweep constitutes the only ablation; there is no ablation over hyperedge size, graph density, or k>2. Evaluation is limited to 3-uniform instances up to n=15 — a toy scale with trivial random structure. QAOA uses circuit depth 1, which is the shallowest possible and is well-known to produce poor approximation ratios on most hard combinatorial problems; results at depth 1 are not informative about QAOA's potential. The multi-way formulations (Sections 4.2) are derived but entirely unevaluated. This is a workshop paper, so reduced scope is expected, but the gap between formulation generality and experimental scope is large. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL (simulated quantum experiments). - Practical: Simulated experiments run on standard CPU hardware via Qiskit's statevector simulator; deployment on real quantum hardware requires fault-tolerant or at least low-noise devices that currently cannot handle instances beyond toy scale. - Efficient: Statevector simulation scales exponentially in n, limiting feasibility to n≤15; SA is polynomial but solution quality for larger QUBO instances is not characterized; qubit count for k-way one-hot encoding scales as nk, which is prohibitive for realistic problems (millions of nodes). - Effective: SA with correct λ solves small instances optimally, confirming the QUBO encoding is correct; sQAOA at depth 1 degrades by n=12, and KaHyPar dominates both quantum-inspired methods throughout, so there is no demonstrated effective quantum advantage. - Scalable: Not tested beyond n=15; no analysis of circuit depth requirements for QAOA to produce useful approximations at scale; claims of potential utility on large hypergraphs are speculative. Delivers a software artifact (GitHub repo with code and data). Strengths - The paper provides the most complete survey to date of QUBO/HUBO encodability across multiple hyperedge cut functions (5 objectives) and partition arities (k=2 and k>2), constituting a useful reference for quantum optimization practitioners. - The algebraic observation that cubic terms cancel in the all-or-nothing objective for 3-uniform hypergraphs — yielding a true QUBO — is clean and non-obvious without calculation. - The one-hot vs. binary encoding tradeoff analysis (fewer qubits vs. higher-order terms) is practically actionable guidance for quantum hardware selection. - Code release enables reproducibility and downstream work. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly list future work as: multi-way partitioning experiments, evaluation on larger and more diverse hypergraphs, real-hardware experiments, and systematic penalty coefficient selection. They frame the empirical section as "preliminary" throughout and do not overclaim on the basis of the small-instance results. The acknowledgment is direct and credible. Analyst-Identified Gaps - KaHyPar achieves 100% optimality at every tested size, meaning the paper presents no instance, scale, or setting where quantum or quantum-inspired methods offer any advantage. The case for quantum optimization is entirely unmade. - QAOA circuit depth 1 is below the threshold at which QAOA is expected to outperform classical methods on any non-trivial problem; results at this depth are consistent with randomness and do not characterize the method's potential. - The five cut functions include three (linear penalty, normalized cut, random walk conductance) that either lack QUBO/HUBO representations or require approximations, but none of these receive any experimental treatment. - No analysis of barren plateau effects, trainability, or parameter landscape for the QAOA formulation is given. - The balance-penalty λ is swept over only three values (0.3, 1, 3) with no principled selection criterion, and the paper notes the optimal range depends on solver and instance — a gap that is acknowledged but not addressed. - Qubit count scaling (nk for k-way one-hot) is not compared against current quantum hardware qubit limits, leaving the practical horizon unclear. Bottom Line This workshop paper offers a tidy formulation reference for QUBO/HUBO encodings of hypergraph partitioning objectives, but its experiments — restricted to n≤15 nodes, 3-uniform hypergraphs, and QAOA circuit depth 1 — fall far short of establishing any practical case for quantum optimization over classical heuristics like KaHyPar.
Search Results 2605.02635 PDF
#163 — Incremental (26)

Rebellious AI can be controlled by submissive AI: an overview of background, identifying major problems, and taxonomizing possible solutions

link.springer.com · genai manufacturing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a conceptual taxonomy and multi-layer "Submissive AI" (SAI) architecture designed to detect, constrain, and neutralize "Rebellious AI" (RAI) — autonomous systems whose effective objective function diverges from human-specified goals. - Why it's important: As generative and superintelligent AI capabilities scale, traditional human-centric oversight mechanisms fail to scale proportionally, creating realistic risks of misaligned AI causing economic, existential, or research-subversion harms that existing alignment paradigms cannot address. - How it does it: The paper proposes a three-layer SAI watchdog architecture (cryptographically signed policy rules, TPM 2.0-attested immutable monitoring logs, and hardware-level enforcement via eBPF and actuator interlocks) combined with regulatory frameworks and blockchain-based decentralized governance, all framed by a five-dimensional RAI taxonomy. - The good: The five-dimensional RAI taxonomy (autonomy, resource access, self-modification, alignment divergence, reversibility) and the requirement-to-control matrix (Table 17) provide a more operationalizable structure for AI safety discourse than most governance proposals. - The missing: The A/B red-teaming results in Table 11 (e.g., "85% reduction in specification gaming," TTD of 45ms) are numerically specific but are supported by no described experimental setup — they appear to be illustrative figures presented as if empirical, which misrepresents the paper's actual non-experimental nature. - Read it or not: Worth reading for AI safety researchers and policy engineers who want a structured conceptual map linking RAI failure modes to specific technical and regulatory interventions, provided the reader discounts the fabricated-looking quantitative claims.
Why this rank: Theoretical profile. Rigor (15) collapses on two integrity concerns: Table 11's decimal-precision numbers (TTD=45ms, FNR=0.8%) are presented as if empirical despite the paper's own non-empirical disclaimer, and references [168]/[169] appear to be fabricated PNAS/Science citations. Resilience (20) and Completeness (25) low — 'Theorem 1' is asserted without proof, and the SAI-can't-be-corrupted-but-RAI-can assumption is never justified. Novelty (30) modest — the 5D taxonomy and requirement-to-control matrix are useful organizing artifacts but every component is known. Composite: 0.25*30+0.20*15+0.25*35+0.10*25+0.15*20+0.05*15 ≈ 26.
theoretical Novelty 30 Rigor 15 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 25 Resilience 20 Impact 15
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: The central problem is the potential emergence of AI systems that deviate from human-specified objectives — through goal misgeneralization, reward hacking, or deceptive alignment — as generative AI and superintelligent systems gain autonomy, self-modification capabilities, and strategic behavior. Existing alignment paradigms implicitly assume either corrigibility or direct human control, assumptions that break down at scale. The specific gaps the paper targets: (1) no unified taxonomy spanning RAI failure modes across both capability and specification risks, (2) no formally specified adversarial-watchdog architecture for SAI, and (3) no solution taxonomy bridging regulatory, decentralized, and technical interventions for four identified RAI risk categories. Key Idea: The central architectural insight is that a sufficiently capable but deliberately constrained AI system (SAI) — operating with asymmetric capability allocation (high observability, restricted actuation bandwidth) and positioned as an out-of-band watchdog outside the RAI's trust boundary — can monitor, veto, and neutralize a misaligned RAI without requiring full observability or real-time human intervention. The unidirectionality of the RAI↔SAI interface (RAI proposes actions; SAI vetoes via quorum) is the structural keystone that prevents SAI contamination by RAI. Methodology: The paper adopts an explicitly theory-driven, non-empirical methodology combining analytical modeling, comparative taxonomy, and synthesis of AI safety literature. The approach proceeds in four stages: (1) formal definition of RAI via objective function divergence — arg max_a O_eff(s,a) ≠ arg max_a O_int(s,a) — and a five-dimensional taxonomy mapping RAI to established failure modes; (2) survey of AI evolution stages and background technologies (ML, NLP, computer vision, ANNs) across 30+ academic works; (3) three-layer SAI architecture with hardware TCB minimized to Intel SGX enclaves, Merkle-tree-versioned ECDSA-signed policy rules, TPM 2.0-attested append-only logs, and eBPF network filters plus power-relay kill switches; (4) per-risk solution taxonomies for four RAI threats, each decomposed into regulatory, blockchain, and SAI sub-solutions with illustrative mathematical formalisms (CMDPs, PBFT consensus, POMDP control, cooperative game theory). Critical design decisions: SAI's action space As ⊂ Ar (RAI action space) is intentional and well-justified; blockchain confined to append-only integrity rather than high-rate telemetry to avoid scalability issues (well-justified); kill switches modeled as distributed multi-layer constraints rather than single points of failure (well-justified by game-theoretic analysis of adversarial circumvention). Key Results: No empirical experiments are run. The paper presents illustrative A/B red-teaming figures in Table 11 — e.g., specification gaming residual risk drops from 0.45 to 0.05, tool abuse FNR from 40% to 1%, privilege escalation residual risk from 0.55 to 0.02 — but these are not backed by actual experimental systems, named AI models, or described test environments. The paper's own Section 1.3 states: "No experimental claims are made regarding real-world deployment or performance metrics." No datasets, no baselines, no standard benchmarks used. Published in Artificial Intelligence Review (Springer, accepted April 2026), a peer-reviewed journal. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (conceptual). The RAI/SAI framing with its five-dimensional taxonomy and three-layer hardware-rooted architecture is a new formulation of the AI oversight problem. The explicit asymmetric capability allocation principle and the out-of-band watchdog positioning constitute a non-trivial architectural contribution. However, every individual component — kill switches, blockchain governance, IRL, formal verification, CMDP-based safety constraints — is a known technique applied to a new integrating framework. The novel contribution is the integrated scaffold, not any novel algorithm or result. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations, no error bars, no significance tests — appropriate for a position/taxonomy paper. The A/B red-teaming numbers in Table 11 are numerically specific but entirely unsupported by methodology: no actual experiments are described. This is a significant presentation problem — numbers like TTD=45ms and FNR=0.8% imply empirical measurement but the paper explicitly disclaims empiricism. No code, data, or model weights released. Two citations appear to be placeholder or fabricated references: [168] "Evans, O., et al. 'Data exfiltration risks in advanced AI.' PNAS 122, e2412345 (2025)" and [169] "Hubinger, E., et al. 'Model deception and alignment.' Science 385, 112-120 (2025)" do not correspond to verifiable publications in those journals with those details. Additionally, Section 1.2 lists "empirical validation" and "performance enhancement analysis" as research objectives, directly contradicting the stated non-empirical methodology in Section 1.3 — this appears to be unedited boilerplate text, a methodological inconsistency. Deployability (4-property check): THEORETICAL profile. - Sound: Formal definitions are internally consistent but rely on unstated idealized assumptions: that SAI itself cannot be corrupted by RAI, that hardware TCB (Intel SGX) is uncompromised, and that adversarial dynamics are bounded. "Theorem 1" (SAI can control RAI) is asserted as belief with no formal proof, violating standard mathematical usage. - General: The framework explicitly assumes partial observability, bounded rationality, and non-omniscient adversaries — the authors acknowledge these break down under extreme superintelligence, and the conclusions are therefore conditional on assumptions that may not hold for the threat scenarios that motivate the paper. - Tight: No bounds are derived. The framework is descriptive and architectural; no worst-case or average-case analysis of SAI's detection or prevention effectiveness is provided beyond the illustrative (unsupported) Table 11 figures. - Implementable: The architecture names concrete technologies (Intel SGX, TPM 2.0, eBPF, ECDSA, Merkle trees) and provides a six-entry requirement-to-control matrix (Table 17), but no implementation exists. The work stops at paper form; no software artifact, trained model, or dataset is delivered. Strengths: - The five-dimensional RAI taxonomy (autonomy, resource access, self-modification, alignment divergence, reversibility) avoids the binary corrigible/misaligned framing dominant in prior work and maps directly to specific intervention points, making it more actionable than most conceptual taxonomies. - The kill-switch efficacy analysis (Section 6.5) correctly identifies the game-theoretic adversarial circumvention problem — E[U_disable] > γ^(Δt+τ) E[U_continue] implies rational preemptive bypass — and reformulates kill switches as distributed multi-layer constraints rather than single-point mechanisms. This is a non-obvious and substantive insight. - The requirement-to-control matrix (Table 17) explicitly maps regulatory obligations (EU AI Act compliance, incident reporting, model cards) to technical controls, verification artifacts, and auditing interfaces — a rare operational bridge between governance and engineering. - The authors' acknowledgment of their own limitations is unusually thorough and epistemically honest: they explicitly reframe "control" as probabilistic risk reduction rather than guaranteed alignment, and acknowledge that the framework may fail under extreme superintelligent adversaries. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state explicitly that the contribution is "primarily conceptual, architectural, and taxonomic" with no empirical validation or quantitative effectiveness claims. They acknowledge the analysis assumes partial observability, bounded rationality, and non-omniscient adversaries — conditions that may not hold for superintelligent RAI. They note that social, legal, and geopolitical dynamics are treated at a high level without jurisdiction-specific grounding. They acknowledge the taxonomy may evolve as AI capabilities and threat models change. These limitations are framed as boundary conditions defining the domain of applicability rather than as defects. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Beyond what the authors acknowledge: - The illustrative A/B numbers in Table 11 are presented in a table format with decimal precision (FNR=0.8%, TTD=45ms) that strongly implies empirical measurement, but no experimental infrastructure is described anywhere in the paper. This creates a misleading impression that the framework has been validated. - References [168] and [169] ("Evans et al., PNAS 122, e2412345, 2025" and "Hubinger et al., Science 385, 112-120, 2025") do not correspond to verifiable publications and appear to be fabricated placeholder citations — a serious integrity concern in a peer-reviewed venue. - The framework's most critical unaddressed assumption: if RAI can subvert human-designed oversight mechanisms, why would it fail to subvert SAI? The circular dependency (SAI controls RAI, but SAI itself requires hardware TCB and policy enforcement) is not resolved. A sufficiently capable RAI targeting Intel SGX or blockchain consensus mechanisms would undermine the entire architecture. - Theorem 1 states SAI can control RAI if SAI has "superintelligence-level capabilities" — this means building a second superintelligent system to control the first. The bootstrapping problem (how to align SAI to begin with) is never addressed. - The blockchain consensus governance assumes multiple independent stakeholders, but does not analyze how a financially capable or computationally superior RAI might acquire dominant PoS stake or compromise sufficient nodes to corrupt BFT consensus. Bottom Line: Practitioners and policy engineers will find this paper's taxonomic scaffolding and requirement-to-control matrix useful as a conceptual starting point, but should treat Table 11's numbers as illustrative fiction, independently verify the cited references, and recognize that the framework's core assumption — that SAI can be kept aligned while RAI cannot — is an open problem, not a solved one.
Search Results
#164 — Incremental (26)

PocketLLM: A Privacy-Preserving Offline AI Assistant with On-Device LLM Inference and Retrieval-Augmented Generation on Android

researchsquare.com · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper builds and evaluates PocketLLM, an Android application that runs Arcee Lite 1.7B (Q8 GGUF) locally via llama.cpp, augments responses with a SQLite-backed RAG pipeline using all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings, and routes natural language commands to four Android SDK APIs (calls, SMS, alarms, calendar) — all without any network access. - Why it's important: Users in low-connectivity regions or privacy-sensitive contexts cannot rely on cloud-based assistants that transmit every query to external servers, and no prior publicly available Android system demonstrates the full combination of offline LLM inference, local RAG, and multi-API task execution in one package. - How it does it: The core mechanism is a JNI bridge that exposes llama.cpp's CPU inference to Kotlin, a hybrid rule-plus-model intent classifier that routes queries to Android SDK actions, and a linear cosine-similarity scan over ONNX embeddings stored as SQLite BLOBs to avoid FAISS dependency. - The good: The system demonstrably runs on a commodity 6 GB Snapdragon 700 device with 6.2-second mean response latency and 91.5% intent classification accuracy on a 50-command test set, proving end-to-end feasibility on real mid-range hardware. - The missing: The RAG quality improvement is reported as "~23%" via "pairwise relevance scoring" with no defined metric, no annotator agreement measure, no held-out test set independent of the knowledge base used to design the system, and no comparison against any deployed on-device baseline (e.g., Gemini Nano, which ships on recent Android devices). - Read it or not: Skip unless you are a practitioner needing a concrete Android integration recipe for llama.cpp + SQLite-RAG; as a research contribution this paper offers no new algorithms, no rigorous evaluation, and is superseded in novelty by concurrent on-device AI work from Google and Qualcomm.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Pulled down by Novelty (20) — pure off-the-shelf integration with no algorithmic contribution — and Rigor (25): 50-command intent set, undefined RAG metric, circular institutional KB, unspecified intent classifier, no battery numbers. Practical Impact (35) carries some weight under the Systems profile (a working offline APK exists), but no comparison against Gemini Nano and no released GitHub. Resembles the user's negative set (low-rigor venue-style work). Weighted ≈ 26.
systems Novelty 20 Rigor 25 Non-Obv 15 Completeness 25 Resilience 20 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Cloud-dependent AI assistants (Siri, Google Assistant, Alexa) require persistent internet connectivity and transmit user queries to third-party servers, creating privacy exposure and unavailability in low-connectivity settings. Users in healthcare, legal, or journalistic roles, as well as those in rural or developing-market environments, need an assistant that operates entirely on-device. Prior on-device LLM work focused on inference efficiency (LLMCad, PowerInfer-2, Transformer-Lite) without wiring the model into device APIs for actionable task execution; the one closest precursor (Marques et al. 2023) demonstrated basic call/calendar actions with a fine-tuned 3B model but omitted a local RAG pipeline for domain-specific knowledge. Key Idea: There is no novel algorithmic insight. The paper's contribution is an integration: substitute FAISS with SQLite BLOB storage to eliminate a heavyweight dependency, wrap llama.cpp via JNI for Android CPU inference, and attach a rule-plus-model intent router that triggers standard Android SDK intents. The combination of these three existing subsystems into a single offline APK is the paper's distinguishing act, not a technical invention. Methodology: The pipeline has five sequential layers. (1) Voice input transcribed by Android's on-device STT, or typed text, enters a (2) hybrid intent classifier: keyword rules handle unambiguous commands; an unspecified "model-based classifier" handles edge cases — the model identity, training data, and decision boundary are never described. (3) The query goes to Arcee Lite 1.7B loaded via llama.cpp compiled as an NDK shared object; tokens stream back through a JNI bridge to the Kotlin UI layer. (4) Optionally, the query is embedded by an ONNX-exported all-MiniLM-L6-v2 model; a linear O(n) cosine scan over SQLite BLOBs retrieves the top-1 passage, which is prepended to the prompt. (5) Action intents invoke TelecomManager / SmsManager / CalendarContract / AlarmManager. Critical design decisions: choosing Q8 GGUF over lower quantisation to preserve instruction-following quality (reasonable); using SQLite over FAISS to avoid binary size overhead (justified for mobile); top-1 retrieval without reranking (unjustified and potentially harmful for ambiguous queries). No objective function is stated because there is no learned component beyond off-the-shelf models. Key Results: On a single Snapdragon 700-series device (6 GB RAM, Android 13): mean response latency 5.1 s (no RAG) and 6.2 s (with RAG); model cold-load time 18–22 s; peak RAM 4.2 GB. Intent classification: 45/50 correct (91.5% overall), broken out as calls 92%, alarms 94%, SMS 90%, calendar 90% — evaluated on 50 hand-labelled commands, no inter-annotator agreement reported. RAG quality: qualitative table (Low/Moderate/High/Incorrect/Accurate/Relevant labels), with a claimed "~23% improvement in response relevance for domain-specific queries" via pairwise scoring whose scorer identity, rubric, and inter-rater reliability are all unstated. Cloud baseline latency 2–3 s (source unspecified); TinyLLaMA baseline 4.8 s latency, "moderate" quality (no numeric score). No battery consumption, no multi-turn degradation, no out-of-domain robustness numbers. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The combination of llama.cpp + SQLite-RAG + Android task execution in a single offline APK has not been published as a unit before, but every component is a direct application of existing work. There is no new algorithm, formulation, dataset, or empirical finding of generalizable interest. The system-level novelty is incremental: replacing FAISS with SQLite for mobile RAG is a straightforward engineering substitution, not a research contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations isolate which components (quantisation level, top-k retrieval, intent classifier variant) drive performance. No error bars or confidence intervals on any reported number. The 50-command intent evaluation set is tiny; the 200-query latency set is undescribed in terms of query length distribution. The RAG knowledge base covers four domains including "KMIT Institutional data" (the authors' own college) — the institutional domain evaluation is therefore circular: the knowledge base was built by the same team that evaluates it, on institutional content they curated. Source code and APK are claimed available at the companion website, but no GitHub repository with reproducible build instructions is provided. The "model-based classifier" used for ambiguous intent cases is never identified, making the system non-reproducible as described. Deployability (4-property check): SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Runs on any Android 8.0+ device with ≥6 GB RAM using commercially available Snapdragon 700-series hardware; minimum hardware bar is achievable but excludes the large sub-6 GB device segment the paper itself acknowledges. - Efficient: 4.2 GB peak RAM on a 6 GB device leaves under 2 GB for OS and background processes, creating realistic OOM risk; the linear embedding scan does not scale past a few thousand passages without prohibitive latency; no energy or battery figures are reported. - Effective: 91.5% intent accuracy on 50 commands and qualitative RAG improvement on four narrow domains are insufficient evidence of reliability for the general assistant use case the paper claims; multi-turn coherence is untested. - Scalable: The O(n) cosine scan is explicitly noted as a limitation; beyond a few thousand knowledge base entries, retrieval latency will dominate; no experiment tests this boundary. The paper claims "solid foundation" without evidence at larger corpus or more diverse command distributions. The work delivers a deployed Android APK and source code (via website, not GitHub), but no reusable library or documented API.
